description: Deep comparative analysis of every FX design doc section against state of the art. Each entry answers: what does FX do, who does it better, who does it worse, what's missing, and could we improve.

verdict_scale.frontier: FX is at or beyond the state of the art — no known better approach
verdict_scale.strong: FX is competitive, minor improvements possible from specific systems
verdict_scale.adequate: FX works but known systems do significant parts better
verdict_scale.weak: FX's approach has clear deficiencies vs established alternatives
verdict_scale.missing: Section is too thin/handwavy to evaluate — needs more design work
verdict_scale.novel: No direct comparison exists — FX is attempting something new

confidence_scale.high: Agent did thorough web search and found definitive comparisons
confidence_scale.medium: Agent found partial comparisons, some gaps in research
confidence_scale.low: Agent relied mostly on general knowledge, limited web search results

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for §1 Introduction, announcing FX as a dependently-typed graded modal language with 19 orthogonal dimensions unifying software, hardware, and cross-boundary contracts under a deny-by-default capability model.

closest_prior_art.approach: Dependently-typed proof-oriented language using Hoare-style pre/post and ~5 effects; has refinements but no graded modal theory at same scale
Quantitative Type Theory with three multiplicities {0,1,ω}, one semiring; no hardware or contract integration

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unifies more concerns in one typing judgment than any published system: linearity, effects, security, sessions, hardware clocks, and contracts all share one grade-checking algorithm. The ambition to cover software-to-Verilog from one source is genuinely broader than F*+Low*/Vale, Dafny, or Lean+Mathlib ecosystems.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: This is a chapter header with no proven implementation. Granule is the closest academic relative and is a 'research project' with three grading dimensions; jumping from 3 to 19 is unprecedented in actual deployable implementations. Metatheory for the full composition is not cited anywhere.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No chapter-level statement of soundness theorem, no reference to a mechanized proof of the joint type system, no acknowledgement that Idris 2/Granule have yet to scale QTT/graded modalities to industrial use.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Open the chapter by explicitly citing Atkey 2018, McBride 2016, Wood/Atkey 2022, and Granule; name a target mechanized-metatheory milestone (e.g., Agda formalization of the core calculus) instead of implying the design is self-evidently sound.

analysis.what_fx_does: Lists the 21 graded type dimensions (Type, Refinement, Usage, Effect, Security, Protocol, Lifetime, Provenance, Trust, Representation, Observability, Clock domain, Complexity, Precision, Space, Overflow, FP order, Mutation, Reentrancy, Size, Version) in a canonical reference table with default (restrictive) setting and grant syntax per dimension. Each dimension is a graded algebraic structure instantiated from one parameterized grade-checking kernel dispatched by tier (S/L/T/F/V, §6.3). Post Proposal 13 clarification: dimensions compose pointwise on the grade vector but are NOT orthogonal — §6.8 catalogs ten soundness collisions (classified × Fail, borrow × Async, CT × Async, ghost × runtime, monotonic × concurrent, etc.) with error codes.

closest_prior_art.approach: Graded modal types over user-extensible semirings; ships with ~3 built-in grade modalities (linearity, security lattice, IO possibility); extensions via user-defined semirings
Exactly one semiring {0,1,ω} for usage; no other orthogonal dimensions at grade level
~5 'dimensions' encoded separately (types, refinements, effects via Dijkstra monads, ghost, termination) — not a uniform graded semiring framework
6 capabilities (iso, trl, ref, val, box, tag) forming a deny-matrix for concurrent aliasing — fewer and domain-specific
Lifetimes + borrow check + Send/Sync marker traits + unsafe escape hatch; effects inferred rather than graded

analysis.where_fx_is_better: No other published language claims a unified semiring framework for 21 dimensions. Granule's framework is user-extensible but in practice ships only three modalities; FX commits to the superset up front and gives each dimension an explicit default and grant. The canonical reference table is a concrete contribution to PL documentation.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Cognitive load and error-message explosion: a single call site can fail on any of 19 axes simultaneously, producing worst-case O(19) diagnostics to triage. No prior system has successfully presented >5 graded dimensions ergonomically; Granule acknowledges baseline performance issues from computing grade contexts often. The claim that Protocol and Representation are 'semiring instances' is suspicious — §6.3 itself admits Protocol is 'not a simple semiring' and Representation is 'not a semiring in usual sense'. The uniform-framework claim is already soft in its own spec.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of compile-time cost of checking 19 grade vectors per binding; no statement of decidability of the composed system; no acknowledgement that Wood/Atkey 2022 corrected-Lam rule is for a single semiring, not a product of 19. No story for how error messages are prioritized when multiple dimensions fail simultaneously. No cognitive-load study, no IDE integration plan beyond §24.
analysis.could_we_do_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 1 + rigor-first] Verdict raised from 'novel' to 'strong' after Proposal 1 adopted Dimension 20 (Size) with 'with Productive' effect + guardedness check for §3.5 codata, plus the tier-system refactor of §6.3 that classifies each dimension as Tier S (semiring), L (lattice+validity), T (typestate), or F (foundational). Rigor-first eliminates all inference sites with explicit surface syntax per Appendix E. Earlier note preserved below.

Reduce to ~7 core dimensions that are provably semirings (Usage, Effect, Security, Lifetime, Precision, Space, Clock) and treat the others (Protocol, Representation, Provenance, Observability, Mutation, Reentrancy, Trust, Complexity, FP-order, Overflow) as either refinement predicates, module-level attributes, or lattices rather than claiming uniform semiring treatment. Alternatively: explicitly separate 'graded' from 'annotated' dimensions in the table with different checking algorithms.

analysis.what_fx_does: States nine design principles: deny-by-default / grant-by-permission capabilities, correct-by-construction, effects-as-capabilities, resources-as-types, protocols-as-types, security-as-types, types-are-expressions, every-behavior-defined, complexity-opt-in but safety-not-opt-in, and compiler-as-proof-partner.

closest_prior_art.approach: Deny capabilities for safe, fast actors: every reference capability is about denying capabilities of aliases; a concrete instantiation of deny-by-default for data-race freedom
Object-capability language built around POLA (Principle of Least Authority); no ambient authority, references are unforgeable, capabilities must be passed explicitly
Systems language with linear types and capability security — closest to FX's resources-as-types principle
Effects-as-capabilities via row-polymorphic effect types; Tot (total) default, with annotations to grant IO/div/etc.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Combines Pony's deny-by-default ethos, E's object capabilities, Koka's effect rows, and dependent refinements in one coherent story. The explicit 'every annotation is a grant, never a restriction' framing is cleaner than Pony (which uses a 6x6 deny matrix) and more rigorous than Rust (where safety requires unsafe escape).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Principles are aspirational — no proof that, for example, 'incorrect programs do not compile' is achievable when the constraint system is the product of 19 semirings. Pony's principles are backed by a formal proof of data-race freedom; FX's are proclamations. E's capability model has 20+ years of attack-surface analysis; FX claims the benefits without the scar tissue. 'Types are expressions' dependent-typing claim is shared with Idris, Agda, Lean, Coq, F* — not novel.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No concrete mapping from each principle to a mechanized theorem. No acknowledgement that 'deny by default' in a graded system requires a bottom element per semiring — what is the bottom for Protocol (no-channel?) or Representation (opaque?)? No threat model for when the compiler-as-proof-partner fails (timeouts, unknowns).
analysis.could_we_do_better: For each principle, cite the specific theorem (e.g., 'effects-as-capabilities ⇒ noninterference for declared effect sets') and the paper it's adapted from. Replace 'mathematically guaranteed' (rhetoric) with 'proved in §27 Metatheory' (pointer). Acknowledge that 'complexity opt-in, safety not' is tension-laden when safety proofs themselves have complexity (Z3 timeouts).

analysis.what_fx_does: Gives one canonical example (encrypt_and_send) exercising seven dimensions simultaneously — type safety, refinement (length>0), ownership (linear plaintext, borrowed key), lifetime, effects (IO+Crypto+Async+eff), protocol (session send-then-end), and security (secret key flow) — showing the compiler checks them together.

closest_prior_art.approach: Verified cryptographic library using F*'s Pure+ST+refinements; equivalent multi-dimensional checking but without session types or clock domains
Pre/post/decreases + ghost + method-level verification; lacks session types and capability-level effects
Contract-based programming with compile-time dimensional units, Pre/Post/Type_Invariant aspects; production-deployed (glider autopilots, avionics)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Shows channel session types inline with refinements and effects — HACL* does not express session types; SPARK does not have linear sessions. The single example covering seven axes simultaneously is didactically stronger than most spec presentations.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The example is hypothetical syntax with no implementation; §1.3 shows the ambition but not the ergonomics. HACL* exists, ships, is verified, and is deployed in Firefox/Linux kernel — FX's example is a pitch. The annotations on the signature (four type parameters, one refinement, two clauses in body, secret mode, region mode, effect row) are already dense — this is the ergonomic ceiling, not the median.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No shown error message when a dimension is violated. No indication of how long this function takes to type-check. No indication whether Z3/SMT is invoked and how often. No comparison example in F*/Dafny/Koka for the reader to benchmark cognitive load against.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Show three versions side-by-side: F* equivalent, FX version, Rust+crypto-crate version — so the reader can judge whether the extra dimensions are worth the annotation weight. Show the compiler error for one dimension violation (e.g., forgetting secret mode on key) to demonstrate the diagnostic quality promised in §10.10.

analysis.what_fx_does: Three-column table of bug classes × mechanism × scope-of-guarantee. Rewritten under Proposal 22 (external review critique) to be precise rather than marketing. Rows distinguish 'elaboration' (unsafe program cannot typecheck — use-after-free, double free, null deref, uninitialized read), 'compile-time SMT proof' (buffer overflow via refinement, requires programmer to carry the refinement), and 'declared in signature' (integer overflow with wrap/trap/saturate surfaces the behavior rather than eliminates it; stack growth via Alloc(Stack, bound: N) is opt-in per §19.5 not default). Integer overflow split into two rows (unbounded int/nat vs fixed-width with overflow mode). Stack growth split into termination (decreases proves finite depth) vs stack size (Alloc(Stack, bound) is the actual guarantee, opt-in). Typed resource leak row explicitly notes user-level 'resources' not expressed as linear values are not tracked. Deadlock mechanism covers only session-typed channels, not arbitrary mutex waits. The 'no unsafe keyword' is moved to §1.6 (Two-Tier Safety Model) with its rationale.

closest_prior_art.approach: Memory safety without GC via borrow checker; unsafe escape hatch exists; data races prevented in safe code only
Formally verified compilers for constant-time crypto; timing side channels addressed at compiler/ISA level, not just source
Deadlock freedom guaranteed only for restricted classes (e.g., acyclic communication topology, non-interleaved sessions); classic MPST has known consistency issues
Decreases clauses prove termination but fail on Collatz-like problems, recursion through maps, higher-order recursion through datatype function fields

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Explicit commitment to no-unsafe, no-bypass — stronger than Rust whose soundness proofs exclude unsafe. Combining taint + session types + classified-by-default into one language is broader than any single competitor covers.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Several claims are overblown. (1) 'Deadlock impossible via session types with priority' — multiparty session types with interleaving have open soundness issues (see POPL 2024 dlfactris paper); priority annotations help but do not give you deadlock freedom in the presence of general delegation. (2) 'Stack overflow impossible via decreases' — Dafny admits decreases* escape hatch for programs like Collatz; higher-order recursion through map-valued fields is a known incompleteness. (3) 'FP non-determinism impossible' — exact decimal default is true, but the moment you opt into f32/f64 the usual IEEE gremlins return, and 'strict' ordering depends on the compiler actually preserving it (Jasmin proves compiler preservation; FX spec does not yet). (4) 'Undefined behavior impossible: every reduction step defined' — C++ committee has tried this; the real UB in modern languages lives at FFI, concurrency memory models, and speculative execution (Spectre) where FX is silent. (5) No timing side-channel bug class is listed, yet §12.5 CT effect exists — the table is incomplete.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No mention of Spectre / speculative constant-time (see POPL/Jasmin work); no discussion of which bug classes become impossible only when release-mode bans sorry/axiom; no citation of counterexamples from prior systems that motivate each claim; no clause about FFI / unsafe-by-linking (extern C).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Qualify each claim with the proof that supports it and the assumption under which it holds. For deadlock, restrict the claim to 'binary sessions and acyclic multiparty topologies'. For stack overflow, say 'when decreases proves terminating; non-terminating programs must declare with Div'. Add timing-channels row, cite Jasmin/FaCT, and say 'impossible within with CT, best-effort otherwise'.

analysis.what_fx_does: Table separating erased (refinements, usage grades, security labels, protocol states, lifetimes, provenance, trust levels, observability markers, clock domains, complexity/precision/space bounds, Version labels since P25) from what survives in the binary (machine code, ADT tags, vtable pointers for §16.5 existentials, effect operations, memory layout, trust boundary validators). Supports §1.5's compile-time erasure claim. Composes with dead-effect elimination in §21.4 — program declaring no Network effect never links the Network service module.

closest_prior_art.approach: Erasure via multiplicity 0 — values with grade 0 cannot be pattern-matched (unless inferrable) and are erased; formal guarantee via QTT semantics
Prop universe erased on extraction but issues remain (indexes from Type still present); separate sigma types etc.
Graded Modal Dependent Type Theory formalized in Agda with a proven-sound extraction function for erasable grades; ICFP 2023
Iterators, generics, lifetimes erased at codegen; empirically validated; reliance on optimizer

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Erasure of 12+ dimensions in one framework is broader than Idris 2's single usage dimension or Coq's Prop-universe. Coupling erasure with compiler-inserted secure_zero for classified values is a concrete capability most languages need libraries for.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Claim is made without citing any proved erasure theorem. ICFP 2023 Abel et al. prove erasure soundness for a specific graded modal dependent type theory — FX inherits none of that machinery explicitly. 'Zero runtime cost' for complexity/precision/space bounds is trivially true because they are specifications, not code; framing this as an erasure win inflates the claim. Recent 2025 work (arXiv:2505.21225) notes that even Idris 2 erasure sometimes depends on partial evaluation 'that does not always fire' — so 'zero cost' is not a given in practice.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No erasure theorem statement; no account of refinements that leak into residual code (runtime validators at trust boundaries are acknowledged but their size budget is not); no discussion of how Representation dimension (layout) affects runtime code paths through specialization / monomorphization; no interaction with profiles that forbid heap.
analysis.could_we_do_better: State erasure as a theorem ('extraction(program) preserves observable behavior when all grade-0 bindings are removed'), cite ICFP 2023 formalization, and list the sub-theorems per dimension. Add a column 'erasure proof locator' pointing to §20.2 (FX→FXCore erasure) and a section proving it.

analysis.what_fx_does: NEW SECTION added under Proposal 22 in response to external-review critique (effectfully, 2026-04-20). Articulates FX's answer to 'no unsafe keyword': the language provides escape hatches but typed and per-definition, with the trust dimension propagating cost through the call graph. Sketch mode (§22) is TypeScript-permissive for exploration — missing annotations inferred with warnings, proof obligations become runtime assertions, linear cleanup becomes runtime drop, effects inferred. Release mode requires every reachable function to reach trust Verified except on paths explicitly marked Assumed. Three per-definition escape hatches: sorry (unfinished proof, rejected in release), axiom (acknowledged assumption, trust propagates non-Verified), with Div on rec (non-termination declaration). These are FX's typed unsafe: scoped to a single definition, tracked in trust dimension, surfaced in diagnostics via fxc --show-axioms, audited at package publish time (§25.11). Supply-chain rationale explicit — agent-written packages cannot ship unverified assumptions silently.

closest_prior_art.approach: 'unsafe' as a blanket keyword with no finer-grained accounting — opt into all five unsafe superpowers together (deref raw ptr, unsafe fn, union, mutable static, unsafe trait)
'unsafePerformIO' is the archetypal typed-but-risky escape, isolated to one function
'--safe' flag toggles primitive blessing; terms using '--unsafe' features are marked in the type system
'Axiom' and 'Admitted' are tracked per-definition via 'Print Assumptions'
'--admit_smt_queries' for skipping specific proofs with flag-controlled auditability
'#print axioms' shows per-definition axiom dependencies; 'noncomputable' + 'opaque' mark specific looser-checking paths

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Converts 'no unsafe' from an absolute prohibition into a design model. The two-tier split (sketch permissive / release strict) lets FX match TypeScript ergonomics for exploration while matching Lean/Coq rigor for shipped code. Per-definition escape hatches with trust-dimension propagation are more granular than Rust's blanket unsafe keyword — an agent reading 'fxc --show-axioms myfn' sees exactly which unverified assumptions myfn transitively depends on. Ties into supply-chain (§25.11): §14.10 contract diff computes semver bump when new axiom / new effect ships, so unverified assumptions cannot hide silently in a published package.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The three escape hatches (sorry, axiom, with Div) are narrower than Rust's unsafe. A programmer needing to do raw pointer arithmetic, memory-mapped IO bit manipulation, or low-level FFI does not have a direct 'unsafe' equivalent — they must go through §18 hardware-layout types, §19 systems-programming effects, or extern 'C' (§5.10). For genuine low-level experimentation on unverified code, the path is less direct than Rust unsafe. Sketch mode is a TypeScript-strictness level — permissive but not untyped. For prototyping that wants TYPE-UNSAFE code (type-confused casts, etc.), FX does not provide a path.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Concrete examples of when each escape hatch is the right choice. Guidance for when 'axiom' is acceptable in a library (foundational math lemma, cited external specification, axiomatized hardware semantics) vs when it's a code smell. Cross-reference to §10.6 trust dimension propagation rules for the detailed flow of how Sorry / Axiom / Assumed trust levels interact in the call graph.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add a subsection enumerating canonical uses of axiom (Hardware semantics per §18, Cited crypto standards per NIST SP 800-90A, Foundational math lemmas per Mathlib). Add linter rule warning on new axiom / new sorry introduced in a PR. Document the interaction with §25.9 workspace policy — teams can forbid axiom entirely via 'max_axioms = 0' in their policy.fxpolicy.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for §2 Lexical Structure — covers source encoding, identifiers, keywords, literals, comments, operators, and 26 disambiguation rules.

closest_prior_art.approach: Detailed lexical spec with UAX 31 identifiers, NFC normalization, confusable detection; production-grade
Full Unicode identifiers, precedence groups, custom operators

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Keeping lexical rules close to the design narrative (one file, one chapter) is cleaner than most language specs where lexical syntax is in an appendix.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Rust has a separate lexer spec with UAX 31, NFC, confusable detection; FX's lexical chapter is briefer and lacks the rigor expected of an LLM-first language spec.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No chapter-level summary of what is tokenized and what is transformed in a later phase; no normalization rules; no statement of BOM handling, UTF-8 well-formedness, whether lexer is stateful.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Since fx_lexer.md already exists as a 598-line companion, the chapter should reference it explicitly at the top and leave only the conceptual motivation here.

analysis.what_fx_does: Source is UTF-8, file extension .fx, one file = one module, no separate interface files — visibility is controlled by pub.

closest_prior_art.approach: One file = one module with paired .ml/.mli (optional interface file); inspired the ML module system
File = module, integrates with .NET; optional signature files (.fsi)
File = module via mod system; pub controls visibility; separate impl/trait/pub conventions
Directory = package; files within a package share namespace; no separate interface files

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Choosing one-file-one-module without optional .mli removes the OCaml duplication pain and keeps visibility choices at declaration site (Rust-style). No BOM, no platform file-encoding ambiguity.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No ability to give a module an abstract signature separately (OCaml-style). Loses module functors' signature-first design, though §5.5 hints at their preservation. Compared to Go's directory-granular packages, FX forces one file per module regardless of file size — large modules become unsplittable without refactoring.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: How file path maps to module name; what happens with namespacing (nested modules in one file?); encoding errors (BOM handling, invalid UTF-8 sequence behavior); case sensitivity on case-insensitive filesystems.
analysis.could_we_do_better: State the path-to-name mapping rule explicitly (e.g., 'src/foo/bar.fx' ≡ module foo.bar) and define behavior for invalid UTF-8 (reject at lex), BOM (strip or reject), line ending (LF canonical).

analysis.what_fx_does: Identifiers match [a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*; ASCII only in identifiers/operators/keywords; Unicode allowed in strings and comments only; single quote is not an identifier character; backticks escape any token; naming conventions (PascalCase/snake_case/SCREAMING_SNAKE) are compiler-enforced.

closest_prior_art.approach: Non-ASCII identifiers via UAX 31 with NFC normalization and confusable detection (UTS 39); ASCII-only in extern crate; less_used_codepoints lint
Unicode identifiers since Python 3.0 via UAX 31
Full Unicode identifiers including math symbols and emoji
ASCII identifiers plus Unicode letters (Lu, Ll, Lt, Lm, Lo); no confusable detection

analysis.where_fx_is_better: ASCII-only removes the homoglyph/bidi attack surface that Rust's RFC 2457 has to mitigate with UTS 39 confusable detection; a security win by simplicity, not vigilance. Compiler-enforced case convention eliminates a whole class of style-lint bikeshedding. Backtick escape gives full token-as-identifier access without reserving syntax.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: In 2026, ASCII-only is a hard sell for internationalization — Rust, Python, Swift, Java, C#, Go all allow Unicode identifiers. For scientific computing the Math Compat extension proposed in Rust RFC 3840 (∇, ∂, ∞, subscripts) is specifically useful. FX's claim that the primary user is an LLM does make ASCII defensible (BPE-tokenizer efficiency) but the spec does not state this rationale here.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No NFC normalization statement (trivially unnecessary given ASCII but should be explicit); no rationale for choosing ASCII over UAX 31 (the LLM-tokenization argument is in CLAUDE.md, not the spec); no rule for identifier length limit.
analysis.could_we_do_better: State the ASCII-only rationale explicitly — 'single BPE token per identifier, zero homoglyph surface, stable line-by-line diff' — and cite Unicode Security Mechanisms (UTS 39) as the attack-class avoided. Alternatively relax to Rust-like UAX 31 with mandatory confusable detection to widen accessibility without losing security.

analysis.what_fx_does: Reserves 92 global keywords plus many contextual keywords (for machine, register, contract, hardware, effect, class, test blocks).

closest_prior_art.approach: 25 keywords — minimalist, simplicity-focused
~52 keywords including reserved-for-future
~35 keywords, some contextual (match, case)
~109 keywords — highest among mainstream, reflects feature accretion
~89 keywords plus contextual

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The split between global and contextual keywords is principled — machine-only keywords like state/transition/reachable don't pollute global scope, a cleaner solution than C++'s everything-is-reserved model. 85 global + backtick escape is a documented, bounded surface.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: 92 globals is closer to C++ (109) than Go (25) or Rust (52); this is keyword bloat by mainstream standards. Contextual keyword bloat is present though trimmed in later proposals (Proposal 2 removed ~13 machine-block LTL keywords in favor of std/ltl library; Proposal 4 collapsed polysemy). Each contextual keyword is still a potential parser corner case.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No rationale per keyword (why 'defer' AND 'drop' AND 'end'?), no future-reserved list, no migration plan if a keyword needs to be added post-1.0, no documented convention for when to prefer contextual over global keywords. Global list is slightly inconsistent (e.g., codata appears in global list between continue and contract, out of alphabetical order).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Audit the 85 for redundancy. Candidates for removal/merging: `axiom` (could be an attribute), `calc` (could be a macro), `code`/`lift` (staging can use syntactic alternatives), `hint` (could be `#[hint]`), `sanitize`/`tainted` (could be attributes). Target: ~50 globals like Rust. Reorder list alphabetically and fix codata placement.

analysis.what_fx_does: Integer literals (decimal/hex/binary/octal with underscores), sized suffixes (42u8), default unsuffixed int = arbitrary precision; decimal literals (3.14) are exact decimals by default (0.1+0.2==0.3); IEEE 754 floats require explicit suffix f32/f64; string literals ("...", f"{}", r"...", b"...") — only double quote; bit literals with Nb prefix (8b11110000); balanced ternary literals 0t10T with trit values -1/0/1.

closest_prior_art.approach: Arbitrary-precision int by default, float is IEEE 754 double; no exact decimal default (decimal module required explicitly)
Int is machine-width, traps on overflow by default, no arbitrary precision; no decimal default
decimal type is first-class 128-bit DFP (IEEE 754-2008 style), but not default
Unsuffixed integer literals infer type but default to i32; no arbitrary-precision default; no exact decimal
Balanced ternary (Soviet ternary computer, 1958); 6-trit trytes; pioneering language support for balanced ternary
Decimal literals via M suffix (1.1M), rationals built-in (1/3); exact by default only via bigint/ratio

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Dotted literal = exact decimal by default is genuinely unusual: Python, Rust, Swift, Java, C, C++ all default to binary FP. 0.1+0.2==0.3 by construction is a real win for financial/measurement code. Balanced ternary literals t6/t12/t24/t48 are unique among modern general-purpose languages; Setun/DSSP are historical. Nb bit literal prefix is cleaner than Verilog's 4'b1010.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Performance cost is significant: software decimal arithmetic can be 100× slower than binary FP (even IBM POWER6/z9 DFP hardware is not ubiquitous). Making exact decimal the default and making f32/f64 explicit opt-in means the naive code path is slow — for scientific/ML workloads this is backwards. The 1Nb literal grammar (rule 16 conflict with record braces) has known grammar issues (gap #116). Balanced ternary in a language targeting Verilog synthesis is exotic — FPGAs are binary. No octal 'o' prefix rationale vs C's legacy ambiguity. Unsuffixed integer = arbitrary precision int defaults callers into heap allocation if they don't provide refinement bounds — see §3.1 warning N001.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No statement of which operations on `int` require Alloc (addition? comparison?); no rounding-mode spec for cross-family coercion; no BOM/normalization spec for string literals; no explicit rule for how f-strings nest or escape braces; no spec for byte string encoding (is b"\u{...}" allowed?); no spec for trit arithmetic rounding (Knuth's truncation-rounding equivalence claim not mentioned).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Reconsider default: either keep exact decimal but provide compiler diagnostic showing the allocation/perf cost, or switch default to rational (frac) which has closed arithmetic without decimal's conversion quirks. Add Ada-style dimension suffixes (3.14_m for meters) for a genuine differentiation. Document the Nb-prefix grammar conflict with bits { ... } braces.

analysis.what_fx_does: // line comments, /* */ block (non-nesting), /// doc comments attach to the following declaration.

closest_prior_art.approach: /// outer doc comments, //! inner doc comments, Markdown conventions from RFC 505/1574, rustdoc with testable code examples
/** */ with @param/@return/@throws tags, HTML markup, javadoc tool
/// triple-slash doc comments with XML tags
/** */ markdown or /// for attached doc comments

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Non-nesting block comments match C++ and avoid scanner state complexity.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No inner-doc analog (//! in Rust) for module-level docs; FX relies on the 'attach to following declaration' rule which doesn't work for the top of a module. No statement about whether doc comments support Markdown, runnable examples, @since tags, or any structured tags. Javadoc, Rustdoc, D-Ddoc, and C# XML all offer more structure.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Module-level documentation convention; doc comment format (Markdown? plain text? embedded FX for examples?); testable examples; cross-references (§24 mentions agent discovery — does /// support linked symbols?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add //! for inner/module doc comments (Rust-proven), specify Markdown-only for rendering, mandate that FX code fences in doc comments are runnable tests (Rustdoc-style), and explicitly link to the §24 REST daemon's /symbol/Foo endpoint to clarify how docs are discoverable.

analysis.what_fx_does: Arithmetic/comparison/bitwise use standard symbols; logical uses keywords (not/and/or) not symbols (no !/&&/||); is is infix constructor test; pipe |>; arrow -> (type) vs => (lambda/match body); range .. and ..=; spread ...; dot for field/method.

closest_prior_art.approach: not/and/or as keywords, readability-focused; also 'is' for identity; no custom operators
Custom operators, && || as Bool ops, `and`/`or` as list fold functions; flexible but error-prone
|> pipe (popularized by ML), not/&&&/||| (mixed), custom operators
|> pipe operator, and/or/not as short-circuit keywords, && || as strict Bool
!/&&/|| for booleans, no custom operators, .. for ranges

analysis.where_fx_is_better: not/and/or as the only boolean operators (not in addition to !/&&/||) removes a whole class of ambiguity (C programmer confusion between | and ||, bitwise vs logical). Dropping single-quote character literals eliminates the lifetime/char confusion Rust has. No custom operators avoids Haskell's readability problems.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Removing ! is unusual — Python keeps `not` but doesn't ban a bang operator because it has no bang operator for booleans anyway; Ruby has !, && and also not/and/or with different precedence. Losing the visual distinction makes dense expressions like `not (x and y) or z` wordier than `!(x && y) || z`. The `is` keyword conflicts conceptually with Python's identity-test `is` (FX means constructor test) — confusing for migrants. No statement of operator precedence table anywhere in §2.6 (precedence is kept somewhere else but not flagged).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Full precedence/associativity table; rules for operator overloading (§16.8 says 'no operator overloading' but is + overloadable for user-defined types via built-in structures? §16.6 algebraic structures imply yes); rules for chained comparison (Python-style `0 <= x <= 10`?); bitwise on bit-vectors vs integers.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add an explicit precedence table in §2.6 (Rust, Swift, and Haskell all have one). Clarify `is` ambiguity with Python by renaming to `matches` or `:::`. Allow `!` as a shorthand for `not` to aid migration (keyword-first parse, symbol-second).

analysis.what_fx_does: Lists 26 disambiguation rules to eliminate parsing ambiguity (semicolons after conditions, angle brackets for intro vs parens for application, typed end closers, prefix modes, keyword-only logical ops, .set() for mutation not :=, context-sensitive { } meaning, three with positions, ref two meanings, etc.) — 'every token has one meaning, every ambiguity is a compile error'.

closest_prior_art.approach: The most vexing parse: ambiguity between function declaration and object initialization forces the former; unbounded lookahead required for declarations vs expressions
Automatic Semicolon Insertion with restricted productions (return/throw/continue/break/postfix++) that trap programmers; three core ASI rules
Layout-sensitive rule plus 'extends as far right as possible' metarule for lambda/let/if ambiguity; expression/pattern disambiguation in GHC via DisambECP
Turbofish ::<> for generic application to disambiguate from < comparison; parser sees ident<T> in expression context as comparison, forcing f::<T>()
Automatic semicolon insertion but with a much simpler rule (after certain tokens at end of line); no most-vexing-parse-style ambiguity

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Explicit 'every ambiguity is a compile error' policy beats JS's silent ASI trap and C++'s silent declaration-over-expression default. Typed closers (end fn, end match) give human-readable error recovery and match programmer intent better than C-family braces. Choosing angle-at-definition / parens-at-use avoids the Rust turbofish ::<> wart entirely. Mandatory `;` on every statement sidesteps ASI catastrophes.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: 26 disambiguation rules is a lot — Go has essentially 1 (ASI), Rust has a handful. Each rule is a cognitive tax; rule 17 (three `with` positions) and rule 16 (three meanings of `{}` depending on surrounding context) indicate keyword/symbol overloading that parser-level discipline has to fix after the fact. Rule 26 (ref two meanings disambiguated by `ref IDENT COLON` vs `ref LPAREN`) is exactly the kind of positional-disambiguation C++ committee stopped adding after MVP. The existence of gap #115 (lifetime-region syntax contradiction) and #116 (Nb bit literal grammar gap) in your own tracker shows this list is incomplete. Rule 8 (prefix modes own/ref/affine with no-mode=linear) creates a silent default that IDE/hover must reveal — same category of footgun as ASI, just in a different place.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No proof that the 26 rules make the grammar LALR(1) (fx_grammar.md claims LALR(1)-compatible but this chapter doesn't reference it); no mention of error-recovery strategy when rules interact badly; no rule ordering / precedence (what if two rules could apply?); rules 20-26 feel tacked on (out of numbering flow with 1-19 which are §2.7-core, while 20-26 are semantic-pass). Rule 19 ('Contradictory dimensions are compile errors') is not a parser rule at all.
analysis.could_we_do_better: [Resolved per Proposal 4 + 1] Verdict raised from 'strong' to 'frontier' after Proposal 4 closed every polysemy rule: rule 14 (ref has one meaning: borrow), rule 15 (format is contract-only, hardware uses layout), rule 16 (record update is spread {...base, field: v}), rule 17 (with is effect-only), new rule 26 (ref has one meaning) and new rule 27 (region uses kind form, no apostrophe). Closed gaps #115, #116, #117, #118, #119, #120, #81, #82, #77, #130. Appendix F now contains the formal precedence table. Every token in FX now has exactly one meaning; grep over source is reliable. Earlier Proposal 1 note: Dimension 20 (Size) with 'with Productive' + guardedness check for §3.5 codata, plus tier-system refactor of §6.3. Rigor-first eliminates all inference sites per Appendix E. Still open: grammar LALR(1) proof statement.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for §3 Types: declares FX has no separate type grammar — every type is an expression and every expression can appear in type position, with the type checker disambiguating.

closest_prior_art.approach: Dependent type theories where types and terms share syntax, with universe hierarchy (Type : Type_1 : ...) managing paradox
Types as expressions with refinement and effects, ancestor of FX's approach
closest_prior_art.reference: Swamy et al., 'Dependent Types and Multi-Monadic Effects in F*', POPL 2016

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The chapter cleanly announces the unified grammar principle, which avoids duplication seen in languages like Java/Rust where type syntax and expression syntax are maintained separately (e.g., Rust `Fn(T) -> U` vs `|x| y`).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The two-paragraph header does not address universe hierarchy, Type-in-Type avoidance, or predicativity — Lean/Agda/Coq treat this rigorously and FX's handling is implicit at this point.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No statement about universes or stratification; no statement on decidability of type-checking given types = expressions; no reference to elaboration ordering between types and values.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add an explicit sentence about universe/sort stratification and cite the elaboration rules that make type/term disambiguation decidable (referencing §6 would be enough).

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines a large primitive-type menagerie: signed/unsigned fixed-width integers from i8 to i1024, platform pointer types, arbitrary-precision int/nat, exact fractions (frac64/128/256/frac), exact decimals (dec32..dec1024/decimal), f32/f64, unit, never, with the key policy that unsuffixed literals are `int` (arbitrary-precision, Alloc-requiring, N001-warned).

closest_prior_art.approach: Native arbitrary-width integers u0..u65535, i0..i65535 as a first-class language feature; allows u7, i38 in packed structs with no library
Library-based i256/i512/i1024 as crates with multi-word arithmetic; native only up to i128/u128
Single arbitrary-precision int for all integers (no fixed-width int in core); heap-allocated bignum throughout; pidigits benchmark shows ~10-100x overhead vs GMP C
closest_prior_art.reference: Wilfred Hughes, 'The Fastest BigInt In The West' https://www.wilfred.me.uk/blog/2014/10/20/the-fastest-bigint-in-the-west/

closest_prior_art.approach: Native decimal128 hardware (DFP) in POWER6+ and z9+; x86 has no hardware decimal support — IEEE 754-2008 decimal on x86 is software (Intel BID library) with 10-100x overhead
Hierarchical Integer ⊂ Rational ⊂ Real ⊂ Complex with automatic widening; exact/inexact contagion
closest_prior_art.reference: St-Amour et al., 'Typing the Numeric Tower', PADL 2012 https://www2.ccs.neu.edu/racket/pubs/padl12-stff.pdf

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX is more principled than Rust (crates-only) by making i256..i1024 first-class with statically known register allocation for multi-word. The fixed-width fraction types frac64/128/256 with auto-widening and stack-only allocation are novel — no mainstream language offers closed rational arithmetic on registers. The N001 warning that disappears when a refinement bounds the range is cleverer than Scheme's dynamic-tower (compile-time register sizing instead of runtime dispatch) and better than Rust's blanket `i128` cap. Single unified decimal type family (dec32..dec1024) with IEEE 754-2008 semantics is more comprehensive than Java `BigDecimal` or C# `decimal` (single 128-bit).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The 'dec96 is 4x faster than dec128 on SSE' claim is almost certainly false on x86: x86 has NO native decimal float hardware, the Intel BID library software-emulates all widths including 96-bit, so a 96-bit decimal is not a hardware sweet spot on x86. IBM POWER has DFP hardware that operates on 64/128/extended; 96 is an odd non-standard size. The doc conflates register size with arithmetic cost. Zig beats FX on generality — Zig lets you declare u7, i38, u2048 as needed, FX only ships fixed power-of-2-ish widths. FX's automatic widening of frac64→frac128→frac256 mid-expression is underspecified: where is the rule? When does it narrow? What about NaN-like behavior when not fitting?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No spec for frac auto-widening algorithm (when widens, when narrows, compiler's bit-growth tracking algorithm). No normalization algorithm spec for frac (GCD cost dominates — every operation is O(log min(a,b)) in bignum-land). No specification of decimal arithmetic traps/flags (IEEE 754-2008 has invalid/divide-by-zero/overflow/underflow/inexact — which does FX preserve?). Platform-availability matrix missing: does dec1024 compile on FPGA (§18.8 says no)? Literal-resolution rule is clean but doesn't discuss ambiguous contexts like `let x = 42; f(x)` where f expects u8 vs where x later used as int. No mention of saturating vs trapping vs wrapping defaults at the per-type level — everything routes through `with overflow(...)`.
analysis.could_we_do_better: (1) Drop the dec96 claim or clarify it as a proposal rather than fact. (2) Adopt Zig-style arbitrary-width integer syntax for bits(n)/signed_bits(n) unification so users can declare bits(7) / i38 seamlessly (already done for bits, not for i). (3) Specify the frac auto-widening as worst-case-bit-growth tracking through ANF (a well-known technique in interval arithmetic). (4) Add an explicit table of which primitive types are available on which target (x86/ARM/GPU/FPGA). (5) Specify NFC normalization requirement for string at this level rather than defer to §3.10.

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines three bit-vector family: bits(n) unsigned, signed_bits(n) two's-complement, trits(n) balanced ternary with digits {-1,0,1}; bit slicing insn[6:0], concatenation bits{hi,lo}, literals with optional Nb prefix and 0t-prefixed ternary literals 0t10T.

closest_prior_art.approach: Bit-vector as first-class polymorphic across widths, SMT-backed verification, translates to VHDL for FPGA synthesis; the de facto industry standard for crypto bit-vector DSL
closest_prior_art.reference: Erkök & Matthews, 'Pragmatic Equivalence and Safety Checking in Cryptol', PLPV 2009; http://cryptol.net

closest_prior_art.approach: `logic [N-1:0]` and `std_logic_vector(N-1 downto 0)` as hardware-synthesizable bit-vector types with slicing `sig[7:0]` and concatenation `{a,b}`
Hardware balanced-ternary 18-trit words (Setun 1958), modern 24-trit RISC on FPGA; digit-encoded 1.58 bits/trit, useful for certain signed-symmetric operations
closest_prior_art.reference: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Setun ; 'It's not a binary choice: Boffin builds ternary CPU' https://www.theregister.com/

closest_prior_art.approach: Arbitrary-width integers serve as bit-vectors via u1..u65535 with native slicing/concat via @truncate, @bitCast

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unified bit-vector story with both unsigned (bits) and signed (signed_bits) as distinct types avoids Verilog's sign-annotation-on-wire confusion. Explicit balanced-ternary `trits(n)` with T digit is novel in mainstream PL — Cryptol has no ternary, SystemVerilog doesn't, Haskell/Rust don't. Concatenation via `bits{a,b}` reuses the record syntax cleanly. Integration with §18 (format, register state machines) gives bit-vectors a home that Cryptol lacks (Cryptol doesn't target drivers/register files).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: trits(n) inclusion is aesthetic more than pragmatic — no modern hardware uses balanced ternary (Setun was 1958; modern FPGA ternary is a research curiosity). Carrying trits through the whole type system (§6 dimensions, §13 machines, §18 hardware) imposes complexity cost for near-zero user value. Cryptol's polymorphism across bit-widths (number 5 can be Integer or 32-bit vec or 12-million-bit vec) is more flexible than FX's monomorphic bits(n). SystemVerilog's 4-state logic (0/1/X/Z) is missing — hardware verification without X-propagation is weak; FX claims synthesizable subset in §18.8 but §3.2 has only 2-state bits. Slice syntax `insn[6:0]` has no spec for 0-indexed vs MSB-first vs inclusive endpoints.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No 4-state logic (0/1/X/Z) for HW simulation / X-propagation. No unsigned-vs-signed interaction rules for bits+signed_bits. Slice bounds direction (is [6:0] MSB:LSB like Verilog, or [low:high]?). Bit literal `8b11110000` is mentioned but `0b1010` = bits(4) rule is not: what happens for `0b1010` in a context wanting bits(8) — zero-extend silently? No endianness discussion (only mentioned as `repr(big_endian)` in §8.5). No discussion of bit-vector arithmetic overflow (does bits(8) + bits(8) wrap, saturate, grow to bits(9)?). Ternary literals 0t10T — ordering of digits (MSB first or LSB first?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Drop trits(n) from core; move to a library codata-based extension since real ternary hardware is vanishingly rare. Add 4-state logic(n) type as required for HW simulation (Verilog/VHDL both have it). Adopt Cryptol's width-polymorphism story (quantify over n: nat) for building generic operations like popcount<n>(x: bits(n)) : bits(log2(n+1)+1). Specify slice endpoints unambiguously with one example per direction. Add a bit-vector arithmetic table mirroring §3.8's numeric tower: what does bits(8) + bits(8) produce?

analysis.what_fx_does: Sum types declared with `type Name  Constructor;  ... end type;` — PascalCase constructors, payloads with parens `Circle(radius: f64)`, no `of` keyword, no `|` separators, each constructor on its own line terminated by `;` inside a `type..end type` block.

closest_prior_art.approach: `data Maybe a = Nothing | Just a` — pipe-bar separators, single-line or multi-line with layout rule
`enum Option<T> { None, Some(T) }` — braces, comma separators, Typename::Variant access syntax
`type shape = Circle of float | Rect of float * float` — `of` keyword, pipe bars, optional inline polymorphic variants `[\`Circle of float]`
`enum Shape: case Circle(radius: Double); case Rect(w: Double, h: Double)` — colon-case syntax close to FX's style

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Consistent with FX's overall semicolon-terminator rule and typed-closer aesthetic (`end type;`). No `of` vs `:` vs `=` inconsistency (OCaml has `of` for payloads but `;` for records). The one-constructor-per-line layout is readable and diff-friendly compared to Haskell's `|` list which often lives on one line. PascalCase rule enforced by lexer (§2.2) matches Rust/Scala convention. Payload syntax matches application syntax (`Circle(3.0)`) which is tidy.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: GADT syntax is unspecified at this level — fx_grammar.md §8 supports GADT-style constructor patterns with named fields but §3.3 is silent on GADT declarations. Rust has explicit `Typename::Variant` qualified access at use site; FX doesn't clarify whether constructors are imported into scope or require qualified `option::None`. Haskell/OCaml support polymorphic variants (Scala 3's `enum` doesn't but `case class` hierarchies do); FX doesn't. Example shows three constructors but no discriminated-union refinement types (§10 refinements aren't shown here to narrow `shape` to just Circles).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: GADT declaration syntax (can a constructor introduce type equalities?). Constructor qualified-access rules. Whether constructors can share field names (Rust allows, OCaml 4.x didn't). How constructor payloads with named fields relate to record types (e.g., `Lit(value: int)` — is `value` a record field or just a name?). Exhaustiveness-checking spec for pattern matches on these types (§4.3 pattern matching references this but doesn't formalize). No mention of recursive types and productivity/coinductive distinction (deferred to §3.5 codata).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Scala 3's qualified-access default (`Color.Red`) with import to enable bare form, resolving ambiguity without verbose Rust-style `std::result::Result`. Add GADT example (type `expr<t>`) showing how constructor-level refinement works. Specify constructor-namespace rules explicitly. Syntactically the choice is fine; semantic specification is thin.

analysis.what_fx_does: Records use `type config { host: string; port: nat; tls: bool; };` declaration, `config { host: "x", port: 80, tls: true }` construction, `cfg.host` access, and `{ ...cfg, port: 9090 }` spread update per Proposal 4 (the `with` keyword is now effect-annotation-only per rule 17); `:` always separates field name and value/type, never `=`.

closest_prior_art.approach: `type config = { host : string; port : int }` with `{ cfg with port = 9090 }` functional update; `;` separators; uses `=` not `:` in construction
`struct Config { host: String, port: u16 }` with `Config { port: 9090, ..cfg }` spread syntax; RFC 2528 extends to type-changing
`data Config = Config { host :: String, port :: Int }` with `cfg { port = 9090 }` — base-name-and-braces update

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Using `:` consistently in type and value position (vs OCaml's `:` in type but `=` in construction) reduces cognitive load. `{ x with field: value }` is closer to OCaml's readable form than Rust's `..base` spread (which has to go last and has awkward type-changing restrictions). Semicolon separators match the rest of FX rather than Rust's commas.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Extremely thin — 12 lines for a major feature. No spec for: (1) anonymous/structural records (OCaml has them via object types; Rust doesn't; TypeScript makes them central). (2) Field punning (Haskell's `Config { host }` shorthand, Rust's `Config { host, port }`). (3) Optional fields / default values in construction. (4) Record field update with computed names (usually impossible in nominal systems but worth saying so). (5) Row polymorphism (OCaml objects have it, Haskell RecordWildCards has sugar, PureScript has proper row types). No mention of whether records are nominal or structural.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Field punning syntax. Default values for fields. Whether records are nominal (OCaml/Rust) or structural (TypeScript, row polymorphism). Relation to §3.3 constructor-with-named-payloads (is `Lit(value: int)` the same as `type lit { value: int }`?). Whether `cfg.host` triggers linear consumption or borrows (§7 mentions mode but records aren't specifically addressed here). Pattern-matching binding syntax for records (fx_grammar.md §8 mentions `{field, ...}` rest patterns but §3.4 doesn't).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add 3-5 more paragraphs covering: punning (`config { host, port: 80, tls: true }`), nominal-vs-structural stance, interaction with ownership (consumption semantics of `cfg.host` when cfg is linear), rest patterns `{host, ..}`, and default values. 12 lines is too skimpy for the primary data-grouping construct.

analysis.what_fx_does: 18-line codata section: declares `codata stream<a> fn head(self):a; fn tail(self):stream(a); end codata;` and values via `unfold head => n; tail => nats_from(n+1); end unfold;`, representing potentially infinite destructor-defined structures.

closest_prior_art.approach: Records as codata with copattern matching; fields are destructors; productivity via syntactic guardedness or sized types
closest_prior_art.reference: Abel, Pientka, Thibodeau, Setzer, 'Copatterns: Programming infinite structures by observations', POPL 2013

closest_prior_art.approach: Native CoInductive declarations with `cofix` and guardedness checker; known brittleness with nested destructors
All data is potentially codata by laziness; no productivity checking — non-productive definitions just loop
Codata-first language designs where destructor-style is primary
closest_prior_art.reference: Rendel et al., 'From Object Algebras to Attribute Grammars', OOPSLA 2014 (Uroboro successor)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Explicit codata declaration (distinct from data) — better than Haskell's "everything is lazy" which silently accepts non-productive definitions. Unfold syntax with arrow clauses is cleaner than Coq's `cofix f := CoFix_constructor (f ...)` pattern. Integration with the broader type system (complexity dimension, effects) potentially gives productivity guarantees Haskell lacks.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: THE SECTION IS DANGEROUSLY INCOMPLETE. 18 lines for codata is not enough: (1) No productivity-checking spec — Agda needs either syntactic guardedness OR sized types, both of which have known soundness issues (Agda issue #1209 showed the naive check was unsound and let users prove False). (2) No mention of sized types or modal `▷` guarded recursion (Atkey's productive coprogramming). (3) The spec FX admits this gap itself in §3.5: 'productivity checking is not explicitly addressed in §3.5; this is a gap.' (per CLAUDE.md). (4) No bisimulation for equality (how is stream equality checked?). (5) No eta rules (Agda copatterns famously disallow eta for coinductive types due to typechecker looping).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Productivity checking algorithm — the entire crux of codata correctness. Bisimulation equality. Sized-type or guarded-modality machinery for advanced productivity reasoning. Interaction with grading (§6) — is codata consumption tracked as usage grade 0 for unused fields? Destructor modes (can head(self) borrow rather than consume?). Termination of codata recursion (fx_design.md §3.5 is completely silent).
analysis.could_we_do_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 1 + rigor-first] Verdict raised from 'missing' to 'strong' after Proposal 1 adopted Dimension 20 (Size) with 'with Productive' effect + guardedness check for §3.5 codata, plus the tier-system refactor of §6.3 that classifies each dimension as Tier S (semiring), L (lattice+validity), T (typestate), or F (foundational). Rigor-first eliminates all inference sites with explicit surface syntax per Appendix E. Earlier note preserved below.

Either expand §3.5 to 3-5 pages with a concrete productivity story (sized types per MiniAgda, or guarded modality per Atkey/Nakano), or admit codata is deferred and cite the gap in §27 (metatheory). The current treatment is below-adequate and would generate type-safety bugs in any real implementation. Adopt sized types (Abel/Pientka) as the most mature automatic approach.

analysis.what_fx_does: Shape-indexed tensor types `tensor(f32, [batch, seq, dim])` with natural-number shape parameters, compile-time shape arithmetic, `fn matmul<a,b,c>(x: tensor(f32, [a,b]), y: tensor(f32, [b,c])): tensor(f32, [a,c]) with Alloc;` as signature, and the claim 'Broadcasting follows standard rules and is checked at compile time.'

closest_prior_art.approach: Index sets as first-class types; shape-polymorphic functions with compiler-checked annotations; ragged arrays via existentials
closest_prior_art.reference: Paszke et al., 'Getting to the Point: Index Sets and Parallelism-Preserving Autodiff for Pointful Array Programming', ICFP 2021; OpenReview Dex paper https://openreview.net/pdf?id=rJxd7vsWPS

closest_prior_art.approach: Haskell type-level Nat lists for tensor shape; GHC DataKinds lifts shape to type level with compile-time checking
Runtime-checked shape annotations that trigger during JIT-tracing in JAX, so zero runtime cost after compilation
`TypeVarTuple` allows Array[Batch, Channels, Height, Width] with type-level shape parametrization

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Tensor types in core language with compile-time shape checking is stronger than Python's runtime-checking (jaxtyping) or JAX's trace-time checking. Having shape parameters as natural numbers integrated with refinements (§3) means shape constraints compose with ordinary value predicates. If the full FX story works, it's on par with Dex/Hasktorch but accessible in a general-purpose language rather than a research DSL (Dex) or a type-level obstacle course (Hasktorch needs DataKinds, TypeFamilies, TypeInType).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: 'Broadcasting follows standard rules' is HANDWAVING. 'Standard' means NumPy broadcasting which is notoriously subtle — when dimensions are 1 or absent, they expand; mismatches are errors only sometimes. The broadcasting rule has five-step algorithm that needs formal specification. Dex solves this via its index-set story (explicit broadcasting via reshape/expand). Hasktorch forces it into the type system with explicit shape manipulation. FX declares by fiat with no algorithm. Silent bugs from broadcasting errors are one of the top-5 ML bug sources; FX cannot compile-time-check something it doesn't specify. Also no: ragged/dynamic shapes (Dex has existentials), in-place ops, stride info, memory layout (row-major vs column-major), slicing/view semantics, autodiff interaction (tensor with gradient type?), einops/einsum notation.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Broadcasting algorithm specification. Dynamic-shape story for cases where shapes are not statically known (batch size from runtime data). Stride/layout in the type (row-major vs column-major matters for BLAS). Interaction with §3.10/§6 (classified tensors? provenance-tracked tensors?). Tensor element-type restrictions (can tensor(string, ...) exist?). Indexing refinement (tensor(f32,[n])[i] requires i < n — how expressed?). Interop with §26 (std BLAS, FFT). Dispatch to GPU (.gpu() mentioned in §11.12 but not specified here). No dot product, matmul, elementwise op operators. No einsum.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand §3.6 from 8 lines to several pages: (1) Adopt Dex's index-set approach as primary — this is state of the art in 2026 for rank polymorphism. (2) Specify broadcasting algorithm or drop the claim. (3) Add stride/layout as a type parameter. (4) Show einsum-style matmul: `A[i,k] * B[k,j]` with implicit reduction. (5) Address dynamic shapes with existentials. Currently §3.6 is aspirational, not a specification.

analysis.what_fx_does: Quotient types `quotient type rat = (int, int { x != 0 }) by fn((a,b),(c,d)) => a*d == b*c;` with compiler-verified reflexivity/symmetry/transitivity and respect-preserving functions.

closest_prior_art.approach: `Quot` as primitive kernel construct with sound/trusted axioms; integer construction via Int := Nat x Nat / equivalence; complete QIT machinery in Mathlib
Higher inductive types (HITs) with native path constructors; quotients compute judgmentally via cubical type theory
Setoid rewriting with per-type equivalence relations; no primitive quotient — work with (T, ≈) as pair and manual congruence proofs
closest_prior_art.reference: Gonthier et al., Mathematical Components — https://math-comp.github.io/

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Syntactic first-class quotient type declaration avoids Coq's boilerplate setoid approach. Auto-verified reflexivity/symmetry/transitivity conditions are a clean ergonomic win. Direct specification 'functions must respect equivalence' is correct and standard.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Proposal 11 specifies function-respect via an SMT `pre` obligation on `Quot.lift`, resolving the main handwave. (1) Judgmental vs propositional equality distinction is resolved by design choice: set-quotients (not HIT); FX's §31.4 explicitly excludes cubical/HIT machinery. (2) Dependent elimination (pattern match escape to representative) explicitly forbidden — §3.7 says `Quot.lift` is the SOLE eliminator, preserving the abstraction barrier. (3) Computation rules at the kernel level (when `(0,1)::rat` reduces to `(0,2)::rat`) still not specified — the Quot-lift axiom (Appendix H.7) states typing but not reduction. (4) No HIT path constructors by deliberate choice.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Computation/reduction rules — when does `(0,1)::rat ≡ (0,2)::rat` hold definitionally vs propositionally? Treatment of dependent functions on quotients (the Lean Quot.ind-equivalent principle). Connection to §27 metatheory proof for well-definedness preservation.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the reduction rules: likely propositional equality with SMT discharge at each use site (matching FX's bidirectional + SMT discipline). Document explicitly that this is set-quotients and cite Lean 4 Quot as the model. Cross-reference Appendix H.7 Quot-lift axiom.

analysis.what_fx_does: Documents implicit lossless widening chains (u8→u16→...→u1024→nat→int; dec32→...→dec1024→decimal; frac64→...→frac; bits zero-extension), cross-family coercions (iN→frac(2*N), fracN→decimal), requires `with Alloc` for arbitrary-precision widening, requires explicit narrow() with `with Fail(range_error)`, and mandates widened result type in mixed-width arithmetic (signed wins over unsigned).

closest_prior_art.approach: Integer ⊂ Rational ⊂ Real ⊂ Complex hierarchical widening with exact/inexact contagion; Typed Racket preserves at type level
closest_prior_art.reference: St-Amour et al., 'Typing the Numeric Tower', PADL 2012 https://www2.ccs.neu.edu/racket/pubs/padl12-stff.pdf

closest_prior_art.approach: Implicit widening for primitives (int→long→float→double), explicit narrowing, but FP-integer conversions are surprisingly permissive (int→double lossy above 2^24)
NO implicit widening at all — every conversion requires `as` or `.into()`; considered a safety feature vs C/C++'s implicit promotion
Usual arithmetic conversions — integer promotion, signed-unsigned mixing has pitfalls (unsigned wins silently, negative ints turn huge)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Explicit chain up to int/nat with Alloc-warning is cleaner than Scheme (dynamic) or Java (allows lossy int→float silently). Cross-family lossless rule (iN→frac(2*N)) is explicit and sound. Signed-wins rule avoids C's famous unsigned-wins bugs (where a + 1 < a is unexpectedly false). Narrow explicitly requires `with Fail` — no silent truncation like Java's (int) cast.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Some rules are undercooked. (1) 'Widening from any fixed-width type to an arbitrary-precision type is implicit but introduces heap allocation' — if implicit, how does the programmer even KNOW to add `with Alloc`? Usually you would see a call site, do widening at arithmetic, and suddenly need Alloc without a local cause. Rust's explicit-only rule avoids this entirely. (2) `iN → frac(2*N)` is LOSSLESS but creates a wider type silently — in crypto/embedded where bit-width matters, this is surprising. (3) No clarification on how widening interacts with overflow dimension (§6 dim 16) — if a has overflow(wrap) and b has overflow(exact), mixing produces what? (4) `signed wins when mixed` rule can lose information: u64 + i32 → i64 may not fit i64 if u64 is 2^63+.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Interaction rules with overflow dimension when widening. Interaction with refinement types (widening u8 {x<=10} to u16 — does refinement survive?). Interaction with representation dimension (repr(C) u8 widens to repr(Native) u16?). Integer↔float coercion rules (iN → f64 lossy above 2^53 for i64 — not addressed). Precision dimension interaction: widening f32→f64 — does precision grade change? Mixed decimal/fraction/float interactions (what's dec64 + frac128?). Mixed-signedness rule at boundary: u64 + i64 → ? (C would say uint64, FX claims signed wins but u64 may not fit i64).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Replace 'signed wins when mixed' with explicit widening to a common supertype that's always wide enough: u64+i32 → i65 (well, wider signed) OR promote to int with Alloc. Disambiguate FP vs integer widening (don't claim integer→float is lossless — it isn't). Spec the interaction with all other dimensions explicitly or cite §6.

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines 'never' as the bottom type (kernel empty inductive Ind with no constructors, Appendix H.4, 'absurd<a>(x: never): a'). Auto-imported from Std.Prelude. Uses: divergent-function return type ('fn loop() : never with Div'), exhaustiveness in irrefutable match, let-else diverging branch type. Proposal 12 REMOVED 'Any' top type and 'dyn Trait' entirely from the language — three native patterns replace them: (1) closed enums for tagged data (JSON, YAML, config — compiler checks exhaustiveness), (2) §14 contract decode at boundaries (produces typed value with validators), (3) §16.5 explicit existentials 'exists (T: type), { val: T; method: fn(...) -> ... }' for opaque types with behavior. Rationale for removal: Any would require runtime type descriptors contradicting §1.5 erasure; linearity soundness issues on failed narrow. Rust users pay a verbosity cost at definition sites but gain vtable visibility; stdlib catalog of named existentials mitigates common cases.

closest_prior_art.approach: Three siblings: `any` (unsafe top, any↔any coercion), `unknown` (safe top, requires narrowing), `never` (bottom); TS3.0 added unknown because any was unsafe
`Any` (top, includes all value types), `AnyRef` (reference top), `AnyVal` (value top), `Nothing` (bottom, subtype of all)
`!` never type for divergence (panic, loop forever); no top type at all — uses trait objects for dynamic dispatch
`Any` top (all types extend), `Nothing` bottom (subtype of all, used for throw/infinite loop)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Removing Any preserves the §1.5 erasure claim unconditionally — types are erased, full stop. Three principled native replacements give better signals at definition sites than a universal top with reflection: closed enums force enumeration (compiler-checked exhaustiveness), contracts force boundary validation (typed outputs not dynamic bags), explicit existentials force writing out the vtable (the record's shape IS the dispatch surface). Rigor-first onboarding: 'what methods does this accept?' is answered by reading the existential record, not by consulting a separate trait declaration. Kernel stays smaller: no runtime-type-descriptor axiom needed.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) Rust users migrating lose `dyn Trait` ergonomics; `exists (T: type), { val: T; method: fn(...); }` is ~60 chars vs `dyn Trait`'s 12. Named existentials at stdlib level mitigate common cases (`type Closeable = exists (T: type), { val: own T; close: fn(own T) -> unit with IO };`). (2) TypeScript users lose `unknown` — but §14 contracts do the same job with boundary validation. (3) Genuinely dynamic interop (reflection libraries, dynamic plugin loaders) is harder; authors write explicit closed enums or extern C ABI. (4) No direct analog for serialization frameworks that want `Map<String, Any>` — must use a closed JsonValue-style enum. These are real ergonomic costs; rigor-first judges them worth paying.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: A stdlib catalog of named existentials so common cases are one-line to use (Closeable, Readable, Drawable, etc.). Performance characterization: is `exists T. { val: T, method: fn(T) -> U }` as fast as `dyn Trait`'s fat pointer? (Should be identical at emit time since the record layout is the vtable.) Migration guidance for Rust developers (when to use closed enum vs existential vs contract). Interaction with type classes (§16.4): can a type class automatically produce an existential pack when asked?
analysis.could_we_do_better: (1) Provide compiler sugar for the common 'pack a value with its type class instance' pattern — a type class method `Trait.pack(x)` that produces the canonical existential. (2) Auto-derive existential constructors from type classes via `@[existential]` attribute. (3) Document the full stdlib catalog of named existentials alongside common prelude types. (4) Provide a migration guide in the docs for Rust/Scala/Java users arriving with `dyn`/`Any`/`Object` habits.

analysis.what_fx_does: UTF-8 strings with THREE unit choices: `string(Grapheme)` (default — 'cafe\u0301' has length 4), `string(Codepoint)` (length 5), `string(Byte)` (UTF-8 byte count). Explicit conversion via `as_codepoints`/`as_bytes`. Grapheme-level comparison is NFC-normalized, so 'e\u0301' == '\u00E9' is true.

closest_prior_art.approach: `String` is a Collection of extended grapheme clusters (Characters) since Swift 3; indexing is O(n); widely criticized for performance (hidden heap allocations, no O(1) index, potential O(n²) with look-behind)
closest_prior_art.reference: Tony Allevato, 'Strings, characters, and performance in Swift — a deep dive' https://medium.com/@tonyallevato/strings-characters-and-performance-in-swift-a-deep-dive-b7b5bde58d53

closest_prior_art.approach: `String` is UTF-8 byte sequence, no Unicode-aware default length; `.chars()` returns codepoints, `.graphemes()` from unicode-segmentation crate for grapheme clusters; explicit opt-in
`str` indexes by codepoint (not grapheme); length is codepoint count; grapheme requires `regex` or `grapheme` package
`string` is UTF-8 byte sequence; `len()` is bytes, `range` iterates runes (codepoints); no grapheme-level default

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Typed units make the unit explicit — you can't accidentally measure bytes when you meant graphemes. This is cleaner than Rust's method-based approach where len() is bytes and you have to remember to use .chars().count() or .graphemes(). NFC normalization by default on comparison beats Python/Rust/Go which all do byte-identity comparison (so 'café' with precomposed vs combining accents fails ==). The triple-unit story is well-considered.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Swift made grapheme-default the official 'Character' and has been criticized relentlessly: (1) O(n) indexing — no way to have str[7] in constant time. (2) Hidden heap allocations for Character values (Allevato's benchmark: 2 seconds for 1.6MB text tokenization). (3) Unicode version upgrades change grapheme boundaries (your length changes between Unicode 14 and 15). (4) Potential O(n²) with look-behind rules for emerging emoji sequences. FX inherits all these problems. NFC comparison requires either: (a) normalizing on every operation (huge cost), (b) normalizing at construction (surprises on non-NFC input from I/O), or (c) lazy normalization (complex, potentially cache-busting). FX doesn't say which. Real-world text comparison in tight loops will be 10-100x slower than Rust's byte-wise == on identical NFC inputs due to normalization overhead unless FX skips the check (unsound under hash-table lookup).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: When does NFC normalization happen? At I/O? At comparison? At construction? Hashing behavior — are two NFC-equal strings guaranteed equal-hash? (Yes if normalized at input; no if compared lazily.) Fast-path spec — ICU's Quick_Check property lets NFC verification hit ASCII/Latin-1 in constant time; FX should mention this. Mutation semantics — does mutating a string re-normalize? Unicode version bound — which Unicode version does FX pin to? String indexing — is str[i] defined for grapheme-indexed strings, and if so, what's the cost? Substring/slicing with grapheme boundaries. Interaction with bytes (FFI, file I/O, network).
analysis.could_we_do_better: (1) Make byte-level the default storage but grapheme the default LENGTH/COMPARISON semantic (like Swift but backed by UTF-8 byte storage per Rust). (2) Adopt ICU4X's approach of Quick_Check fast paths explicitly. (3) Pin a specific Unicode version per release and document that upgrading causes observable semantic changes. (4) Make NFC normalization a one-shot at construction (e.g., when reading from I/O, when constructing from literal) rather than on every comparison — this preserves O(1) equality. (5) Document the cost tradeoff explicitly: '~5% overhead typical ASCII, 10-50x on heavy combining-mark text'.

analysis.what_fx_does: `f32`/`f64` opt-in (default fractional is `decimal` exact). Per-function FP controls via `with NaN(propagate)`, `with Denormals(flush_to_zero)`, `with Rounding(toward_zero)`, `with FMA`. Default is strict IEEE 754: no NaN propagation (refinements prevent creation), denormals preserved, round-to-nearest-even, separate mul+add.

closest_prior_art.approach: Legacy `strictfp` keyword (obsoleted in Java 17 — now always strict); no per-function rounding mode control
`fesetround()`/`fegetround()` at runtime — not per-function; `#pragma STDC FENV_ACCESS` required for compiler to respect changes; widely broken in practice (GCC often ignores)
No rounding mode control at all — relies on LLVM default round-to-nearest-even; NO way to change rounding mode portably
Standard recommends language support for per-block precision control; no language has implemented all five rounding modes per-block
closest_prior_art.reference: IEEE 754-2019 Std; Differences Among IEEE 754 Implementations https://grouper.ieee.org/groups/msc/ANSI_IEEE-Std-754-2019/background/addendum.html

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Per-function rounding/NaN/denormal/FMA control is beyond anything mainstream. C's fesetround is process-global and brittle; FX's effect-tagged per-function control is the right granularity. `decimal` as default fractional is a major quality-of-life improvement — the 0.1+0.2==0.3 trap that bedevils finance/math code vanishes. Strict IEEE default with opt-in relaxations inverts the bad C/C++ default (which reassociates, contracts FMA, fuses 1/sqrt(x) silently).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) 'No NaN propagation (refinements prevent NaN creation)' — this is a DANGEROUS claim. Refinements check preconditions, but `1.0 / x` where x is refined `x != 0` can still produce NaN via 0/0 if x is simultaneously zero via some aliasing (no — but more subtle: `sqrt(-epsilon)` after rounding, `inf - inf`, `0*inf` in FMA, etc.). Proving NO NaN from arbitrary FP computation is as hard as termination. FX glosses over this. (2) FMA changes numeric results — enabling/disabling per-function means a function's result depends on caller's `with FMA` decision. Not addressed — does a callee inherit caller's FMA setting? (3) IEEE 754 has FIVE rounding modes (nearest-even, nearest-away, toward-zero, toward-+inf, toward--inf); FX lists only `toward_zero` — incomplete. (4) No mention of exception flags (invalid, divide-by-zero, overflow, underflow, inexact) — IEEE 754 requires these. (5) No mention of signed zero (`-0.0 == 0.0` but `1/(+0)` vs `1/(-0)` differ). (6) No mention of IEEE 754 totalOrder.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: All five rounding modes. Exception flag handling. Signed zero semantics. Subnormal handling spec (default 'preserved' — but `with Denormals(flush_to_zero)` per-function — does caller's setting propagate? Is this an effect? Not clear). totalOrder for sorting FP with NaN. fma spec details — FMA fuses and reduces rounding count, but fma(x,y,z) != x*y+z in general; which result does FX's FMA produce? Interaction with reassociate dimension (§6 dim 17). Reproducibility guarantees across architectures.
analysis.could_we_do_better: (1) Add all five rounding modes. (2) Replace 'refinements prevent NaN creation' with 'refinements prevent DIVISION BY ZERO and SQRT OF NEGATIVE'; NaN can still arise from 0*inf, inf-inf, so there should be a with NoNaN effect that forbids inputs that could lead to those. (3) Specify exception flag propagation. (4) Address subnormal inheritance through call graph (function A with denormals-on calls B with denormals-off — what happens?). (5) Per-function rounding mode should be an EFFECT, not just syntactic sugar — otherwise callers can silently change callee semantics. Consider making rounding mode a phantom parameter that propagates through types.

analysis.what_fx_does: Default sort is stable (equal-elements preserve input order); `with Unstable` is opt-in for faster algorithms. Hash maps default to unordered iteration; compiler rejects code that depends on iteration order on unordered maps. Typed variants: hash_map (unordered default), ordered_map (insertion), sorted_map (by key ordering).

closest_prior_art.approach: `sorted()` and `list.sort()` are always stable (since 2.3); TimSort (now Powersort in 3.11+); dicts are insertion-ordered since 3.7
`Arrays.sort()` stable for objects (TimSort), unstable for primitives (Dual-Pivot Quicksort); `Collections.sort()` stable; HashMap unordered, LinkedHashMap insertion-ordered, TreeMap sorted
`std::sort` UNSTABLE (introsort); `std::stable_sort` stable; `std::unordered_map` unordered, `std::map` sorted by key; no insertion-ordered in standard
`sort.Sort` NOT stable; `sort.Stable` stable; `map[K]V` intentionally-randomized iteration to prevent order-dependence (Go 1.0 design)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Rejecting order-dependent code on unordered maps at COMPILE TIME is genuinely novel — Go randomizes iteration at runtime (which is itself ahead of C++/Java), but no mainstream language statically prevents this. Stable default matches Python/Java objects; surpasses C++/Go unstable defaults. Three typed map variants (hash/ordered/sorted) make the tradeoff explicit in the type system rather than in documentation.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) HOW does the compiler detect order-dependence? This is undecidable in general — need either syntactic restriction (e.g., banning `hash_map.first()` / `hash_map.iter().take(1)`) or dependent analysis. FX doesn't say. (2) 'Hash maps default to unordered iteration' — for security, this should also include iteration-order randomization (HashDoS defense). Not mentioned. (3) Stable sort is O(n log n) worst case with guaranteed extra space O(n) for merge-based; some languages use mergesort, some use TimSort/Powersort, some use Wikisort (in-place stable). No commitment. (4) No mention of unstable alternative's actual guarantees — is it Θ(n log n) worst case? Or introsort with O(n log n) average, O(n log n) worst (due to heapsort fallback)?
