(5) Ordering on NaN / partial orders — no spec.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Algorithm for detecting order-dependent use of hash_map. HashDoS defense (randomized hash seed). Stable sort algorithmic complexity guarantees. Partial-order handling (sorting list(f64) with NaN — stable, but what's the order?). Parallel-sort interaction (stable parallel sort exists but is harder). User-defined comparators with stability requirement propagation.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the syntactic rule for hash_map iteration (e.g., 'can iterate in for-loop but cannot take(N), cannot call .first(), cannot compare to a list of expected order'). This is approximate but decidable. Add HashDoS mitigation note. Specify algorithm commitments (Powersort for stable — state of the art; pdqsort for unstable). Address NaN ordering.

analysis.what_fx_does: Type constructors have kinds; `list` has kind `Type -> Type`. Kind annotations on type parameters: `class Functor<f: Type -> Type> fn fmap<a,b>(g: a->b, x: f(a)): f(b); end class;` and Monad analogously with pure+bind. Kinds inferred when unambiguous.

closest_prior_art.approach: Full HKT as foundational: `class Functor f where fmap :: (a -> b) -> f a -> f b`; kind polymorphism via PolyKinds extension; well-established since Haskell 98
closest_prior_art.reference: Haskell 2010 Report; Serokell, 'Kinds and Higher-Kinded Types in Haskell' https://serokell.io/blog/kinds-and-hkts-in-haskell

closest_prior_art.approach: `trait Functor[F[_]]` with context bounds; no scala.language.higherKinds import needed since Scala 3; full HKT support
NO first-class HKTs as of 2026; GATs (Generic Associated Types) stabilized in 1.65 (2022) provide incremental stepping stone; lifetime HKTs emulatable, type HKTs require defunctionalization
Functors (module-level HKTs) are the idiomatic approach; no value-level HKTs in core language

analysis.where_fx_is_better: HKT in core language is already ahead of Rust (no HKT) and better than Scala 2 (which required an explicit import). Matches Haskell/Scala 3 feature-parity in principle. Using simple kind notation `Type -> Type` is clear.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Post Proposal 11 the section is firmer but still thin for a load-bearing feature. (1) No kind polymorphism: Haskell's PolyKinds lets you write `class Proxy<k, a: k>` quantifying over kinds; FX explicitly rejects this per §3.13 with the guidance to split into two declarations. (2) No kind-level quantification over lifetimes, effects, or the other dimensions — but the whole graded system (§6) should interact with kinds. Is `Type -> Effect`? `(Type, Usage) -> Type`? Not specified. (3) Type-level lambdas explicitly excluded per §3.13 (use a named type alias instead). (4) Kind inference no longer applicable — §3.13 mandates kind annotations at every type parameter (T044). (5) Variance and kinds interact (covariant type constructor vs invariant) — §16.7 variance section doesn't cross-reference kinds. (6) No kind-level arithmetic for shape-polymorphic tensors (§3.6) — `tensor` has kind `Type -> nat-list -> Type`? Not stated. (7) Associated types explicitly excluded per §3.13 (use class type parameters instead).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Kind polymorphism. Type-level lambdas / type operators. Kind inference algorithm. Variance-kind interaction. Kind of tensor (dependent on nat shapes). Associated types (Rust has them; Haskell has TypeFamilies). Higher-rank kinds (kind of kind?). Whether kinds can be refined (kind `Type -> Type {total}`).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Resolved per Proposal 11: FX commits to first-order type constructors with mandatory kind annotations (T044), no type-level lambdas (named alias required for partial application), no associated types (class type parameters carry the info), no kind polymorphism beyond the binder. Rationale in §3.13 acknowledges this is less expressive than Haskell but aligns with rigor-first. Still outstanding: kind syntax for (1) dimension-indexed types, (2) shape-polymorphic tensors (dependent kinds), (3) effect-polymorphic containers.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter §4 defines FX's expression grammar: mandatory parenthesized calls with named args, pipes and dot-shorthand, pattern matching, conditionals, loops, immutable-by-default let with ref-cells, block/return with forbidden early return, comprehensions, Fail-effect error handling, UFCS dot methods, delimited-continuation semantics for control flow, and an informal grammar summary.

closest_prior_art.approach: expression-oriented with ?-operator, exhaustive match, let-else early exit, loop break value (RFC 1624)
effect-typed expressions with delimited continuations as the native mechanism

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Uniformly expression-oriented with semicolon-terminated statements, pattern-match ownership semantics baked in (§7.5), Fail as first-class effect with auto-propagation and no ?/! noise, and delimited-continuation grounding for ALL control flow including return/break/throw/yield/await.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: NO EARLY RETURN is a bold but hostile choice that contradicts 20 years of consensus; comprehensions are given 5 lines vs Scala/Haskell/Python's rich designs; grammar summary is informal and admits inconsistency with 3268-line FxParse.mly.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No let-else, no break-with-value, no match-as-expression chained arrows, no if-let / while-let, no comprehension guards/parallel/monadic generalization, no specification of precedence tables for binops/prefix/dot (only mentions '|>', '..', etc. without level numbers).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add a precedence/associativity table, specify which constructs are expressions vs statements formally (current text waffles on 'if is tail expression'), and either commit to formal grammar in §4.12 or delete the informal one and point to fx_grammar.md to avoid drift.

analysis.what_fx_does: All function calls use parentheses (no juxtaposition), one-arg calls are positional, two-or-more-arg calls are mandatory named, implicit type args via #, left-to-right evaluation.

closest_prior_art.approach: mandatory external argument labels by default, suppressed with `_`, positional calls possible
juxtaposition application, full currying, partial application is free
optional named args mixed freely with positional, inspection warns on boolean literals

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Mandatory named args eliminate the boolean-argument ambiguity problem (connect('localhost', true, false) becomes self-documenting), catch reorder/rename mistakes at compile time, and remove ambiguity about positional-vs-named mixing that Kotlin struggles with. Self-documentation is agent-friendly.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Kills currying and point-free style outright — no way to write `map(f)` and get a list transformer. Partial application requires explicit lambda. For one-parameter functions FX is positional, but for two or more mandatory named — this arbitrary threshold has no precedent; Swift lets you opt out with `_`. ML/Haskell programmers will find `connect(host: x, port: y)` verbose vs `connect x y`. Ties API to parameter names: renaming becomes a breaking change everywhere.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No escape hatch for unlabeled args (Swift's `_ host: string`), no discussion of partial application or currying alternatives, no rule for variadic functions (collection construction like [1,2,3] has no named slot), no specification for what happens with tuple-valued single arg vs multi-arg, no precedence interaction with `#` implicit args.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Allow a `@[positional]` escape-hatch attribute for the few genuinely-positional APIs (constructors, binary operators lifted to functions). Provide `partial(f, host: 'x')` built-in so partial application isn't dead. Specify whether one-arg convention composes with implicit type args.

analysis.what_fx_does: Pipe operator `|>`, and bare `.field` in function-arg position desugars to `fn(it) => it.field`, with all dots in the same expression referring to the SAME `it` — so `.active and .age >= 18` means `fn(it) => it.active and it.age >= 18`.

closest_prior_art.approach: each `_` in placeholder means a DIFFERENT positional arg; cannot reuse
numbered placeholders, `&1` can be reused, but arity must match
key-path literals coerce to `(Root)->Value`, chained dots work (SE-0287), leading-dot inferred static member

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The shared-`it` semantic for multi-dot expressions is more powerful than Scala (which would give two different implicit params for `.active and .age >= 18`) and more ergonomic than Elixir's `&1` duplication. Works in any argument position, not just after `|>`. Swift's `\.field` needs an explicit backslash and only desugars to a single key-path, not compound expressions.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Multi-dot-same-element rule is not obvious from reading code — a reader sees `.active and .age >= 18` and must know the context disambiguation. Scala's `_+_` vs FX `.x + .y` both trip novices. No spec for what happens with nested closures: does inner `.field` shadow outer `it`? What about `map(.transform(.inner))`? The spec doesn't say. No precedence rules for where `.` shorthand stops (e.g., `.a.b.c + .d`?).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No disambiguation rule for nested shorthands, no spec for arity > 1 (can you write `fn((a,b)) => a+b` with shorthand? No). Pipe operator semantics not specified (does `x |> f(a: 1)` fill the first param, a specific param, or require placeholder?). Section says `|>` 'fills the unnamed parameter' (rule 5) but with mandatory named args per §4.1, there ARE no unnamed parameters.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Rule 5 contradicts §4.1: with mandatory named args, where does the piped value go? Either designate a magic name (Elm: `|>` always feeds last arg; F#: same), or add explicit `_` placeholder. Add precedence table showing `.x + .y` vs `.x.y + z`. Spec nested-shorthand scoping explicitly.

analysis.what_fx_does: match-on-scrutinee-with-semicolon, patterns cover literals/ctors/wildcards/bindings/tuple/record/spread/as-bindings, guards via `if`, exhaustiveness checked, linear scrutinees consumed.

closest_prior_art.approach: or-patterns |, range patterns ..=/.., rest patterns .., @-bindings, exhaustiveness, guards
or-patterns, as-patterns, exception patterns, range patterns (char), lazy patterns, guards with interaction caveats

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Linear-consumption-by-match is rarer — most languages don't have ownership baked into pattern matching like §7.5 implies. Refinement-aware exhaustiveness is ambitious (current §4.3 mentions refinements participate in coverage).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: DESIGN DOC UNDERSPECIFIES. The grammar (fx_grammar.md §8) supports or-patterns `p1 | p2`, negative-literal patterns `-42`, GADT-style named-field ctor patterns `Some(x: v)`, record-rest `{field, ...}`, ignore-all `C(...)`, and more — NONE OF WHICH §4.3 MENTIONS. OCaml has exception patterns (`exception E`) that FX lacks for the Fail effect. Rust has range patterns `0..=255` and `@`-bindings that §4.3 doesn't mention. Missing: what happens when match guard fails — fall through to next arm?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Or-patterns, range patterns, negated literals, exception patterns (for catching effect ops mid-match), @-bindings, pattern aliases, explicit 'fallthrough on guard fail' semantics, type-tests (x is Some from §2.6 but not shown in match context), view patterns, arity-omitting tuple patterns like (..).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Bring §4.3 up to grammar support: explicitly list or-patterns, range patterns, @-bindings, negative literals, constructor-with-named-fields, record rest. Add exception/effect patterns. Clarify guard-fallthrough semantics. Document interaction of refinement with exhaustiveness (is `match x; n if n > 0 =>; n if n <= 0 =>;` exhaustive?).

analysis.what_fx_does: if/else with semicolon after condition and `end if`, multi-branch via `else if`, if is expression when tail of block, statement when followed by `;`.

closest_prior_art.approach: if is always expression; blocks evaluate to last expression; no separate statement/expression forms
if is pure expression; no ternary operator needed

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Explicit `end if` makes nesting readable (Ada-style), avoids dangling-else ambiguity without brace-wars. Semicolon-after-condition rule is consistent with match/while/for.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The 'statement vs tail expression' distinction is syntactic trickery — Rust and Scala 3 just make if unconditionally an expression and let the statement form emerge from `;`-terminated expressions (of type unit). FX has to teach the user this distinction. Verbose for one-liners: `if x > 0; "pos" else "neg" end if` vs Rust `if x > 0 { "pos" } else { "neg" }`. No ternary alternative.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Spec doesn't say whether both branches must produce the same type (obvious but unstated), whether the `else` is mandatory (Rust requires else when if is an expression; optional for statement — FX doesn't say), whether if-let (if x is Some(v); use(v) end if) is supported despite §2.6 having `is`. No else-if contextual keyword — is `else if` two tokens or one?
analysis.could_we_do_better: State explicitly: (a) else is required when if produces non-unit, (b) both branches must unify, (c) if-let binding form via `is` pattern. Consider compressing one-liner form `if c; a else b` without `end if` when context is clear.

analysis.what_fx_does: for over range/collection, while with condition, break/continue as terminated statements, ranges with `..`/`..=`/`by`, loops return unit implicitly.

closest_prior_art.approach: loop/while/for all expressions; `loop { break value }` returns value (RFC 1624); for/while return unit
monadic for-comprehension produces a collection; stepped with `by`
ranges with `step`, `downTo`, progressions

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Consistent semicolon/end-loop syntax mirrors if/match/while. Stepped ranges (`0..100 by 5`) match Kotlin ergonomics. Enumerate via `.enumerate()` method fits UFCS.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Loops return unit — a MISSED OPPORTUNITY. Rust's `break value` makes loop-as-expression composable. No for-yield / comprehension monad generalization (Haskell do-notation, Scala for-yield) for non-list effects — FX has comprehensions for list only. No `downTo` equivalent for reversed iteration. No while-let / if-let loop variants.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: `break value` from loop/while-let, reverse range syntax (Kotlin `10 downTo 0`), loop labels (Rust `'outer: loop`), for-else (Python), comprehension over effects/options/futures (Scala for-yield any Monad). No spec for what `for i in stream;` means when stream is codata — does it terminate?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add `break expr` to make loops expression-return; add loop labels (`label: for ...`) with `break label`; either explain why downTo is absent (step -1?) or add it; spec the codata-iteration case.

analysis.what_fx_does: let is immutable by default with destructuring/record/tuple patterns; `cell(initial)` creates mutable heap cell with .get/.set methods (Proposal 4 split `ref` as borrow-only from `cell` for mutable cells, resolving the polysemy that plagued the earlier design); type annotations with `:`. Bidirectional binding per §4.6: synth-mode RHS omits binding ascription, check-mode RHS requires it (T045).

closest_prior_art.approach: immutable let + ref cells with `:=` and `!`, heap-allocated
immutable let by default, `let mut` makes binding mutable in-place (no heap), no ref-cell needed

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Method-based .get/.set (vs OCaml `!`/`:=`) is discoverable via IDE autocomplete and agent-friendly; record-rest patterns `config { host, port, .. }` are clean; type annotations after binding are consistent with OCaml/F#.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: OCaml-style `cell` values are HEAP-ALLOCATED — FX claims zero-cost mutation is possible via §19 effects and §8.2 escape analysis, but §4.6 still doesn't explicitly cross-reference this. Rust's `let mut x = 0; x = 1;` is zero-cost stack mutation; FX's cell may force a heap indirection for counters unless the compiler proves non-escape. No `let mut` equivalent.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Spec for whether `cell(0)` is stack or heap allocated (likely needs §8.2 allocation strategy to say Stack when it doesn't escape, but §4.6 doesn't reference this); no shadowing semantics (can I `let x = 1; let x = 2;`?); no spec for uninitialized / delayed-init bindings; destructuring in `cell` (can you `let (a, b) = (cell(0), cell(1))`?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add escape analysis note: `cell(0)` is stack-allocated when it doesn't escape — this needs to be a guarantee, not a 'compiler might'. Add `let mut` shorthand for single-cell immutable-binding-of-cell. Spec shadowing explicitly (most FP languages allow it).

analysis.what_fx_does: begin fn/end fn blocks with semicolons, mandatory explicit `return` as last-statement-only, NO EARLY RETURN ALLOWED, guard logic via Fail effect (typed, tracked) or if/else expressions, local fn is Tot by default.

closest_prior_art.approach: Single-exit functions produce one VC formula wp(body, post) — no path disjunction, no branch merging, simpler SMT queries, more stable proofs
closest_prior_art.reference: Leino, 'Dafny: An Automatic Program Verifier for Functional Correctness', LPAR 2010

closest_prior_art.system: Rust guard clauses / let-else / early return
closest_prior_art.approach: Modern imperative consensus: early-return with guard clauses reduces nesting; but creates N exit points with N postcondition check sites

closest_prior_art.system: Koka / algebraic effects for control flow
closest_prior_art.approach: Errors as effects: fail(e) aborts to nearest handler, tracked in effect row, composable with other effects — strictly more informative than bare return

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Single-return means one postcondition check point, one VC formula, one denotational semantics per function. For an AI-agent-first language where the compiler is the primary code consumer, this is a major simplification: the VC generator is a recursive descent over one expression tree, not a path-sensitive exploration of N exit points. Functions that 'want' early return are better decomposed into separate functions with tight specs — a validate() returning refined_input plus a process() taking refined_input carries the proof of validation in the TYPE, which early-return cannot. The Fail effect handles guard-clause patterns (if bad; fail(Invalid) end if; rest...) with TYPED, EFFECT-TRACKED exits that appear in the function signature, compose with the 21-dimension grade checking, and get handled at known boundaries — strictly more information than a bare return statement which is invisible control flow. This design forces functions to be small and single-purpose, which compounds verification scalability.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Human programmers accustomed to guard clauses (Rust/Go/Kotlin/Swift) will find the style unfamiliar. Deep if/else nesting is possible in poorly-factored code. The Fail-as-guard pattern (if bad; fail(e) end if; rest) is semantically an early exit, but it's a TYPED early exit tracked in the effect system — the distinction is meaningful even if the control flow shape is similar.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Guidance on when to split functions vs use Fail-as-guard. A style guide showing idiomatic FX patterns for the top-10 guard-clause use cases from Rust/Go. Clarification that match arms each produce the return value (they are expressions, not return statements). Interaction between Fail-as-guard and linear resource cleanup (§7.11 defer).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Current approach is the right call for a verification-first language. The one-exit-point rule makes VCs trivially a single formula. Functions wanting guards should decompose into validate+process pairs where the validated type carries the proof. The Fail effect gives typed guards without the VC complexity of multi-exit. Add a style guide appendix showing idiomatic decomposition patterns. Consider adding let-else sugar that desugars to Fail (let Some(v) = expr else fail(NotFound); rest) for ergonomics without breaking the single-VC property.

analysis.what_fx_does: List comprehensions desugar to filter/map; supports multiple generators and if-filters.

closest_prior_art.approach: qualifiers: generators, filters, let-bindings; parallel zip-comprehensions (GHC); generalizes to monad comprehensions
list/set/dict/generator comprehensions, lazy via generator expressions
monadic comprehension over any type with map/flatMap/withFilter

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Post Proposal 8 the grammar production `list_comprehension = '[' expr comprehension_clause+ ']'` is specified with explicit multiple-FOR and multiple-IF clause semantics (nested outer-first generation, conjunctive filters). Desugaring to pipe chains of map/flat_map/filter is defined. LINQ-style multi-source queries handled via pipe chains over Query(T) source type per §11.12 (explicit design decision not to add dedicated query syntax).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Still only list comprehensions — no set/dict comprehensions, no generator (lazy) comprehension, no monadic generalization over any source type, no let-bindings inside comprehension (Haskell `[y | x <- xs, let y = f(x)]`), no parallel zip comprehension, no group-by (Wadler's extended comprehensions), no `as` aliasing.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Set/map comprehensions, lazy/stream comprehensions for `Stream(T)` source type mentioned in §11.12, ordering semantics, exhaustion semantics, termination guarantee for infinite sources, let-bindings inside comprehension.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand to support other source types uniformly (Scala for-yield model over any type with map/flatMap/withFilter). Add set/dict comprehension syntax. Add let-bindings inside comprehension. The list-comprehension grammar is now specified but the monadic generalization is still missing.

analysis.what_fx_does: Errors are an effect `Fail(E)`; `fail(e)` aborts to nearest handler; propagation is automatic (no `?`/`!` at call sites); `try`/`catch` at boundary; errors are closed unions; Fail effect tracks precise error type.

closest_prior_art.approach: row-polymorphic effect system with `exn` and user-defined exception effects; handlers can resume
value-based Result<T,E>, postfix ? for propagation with From conversion
single concrete error type per function, opt-in typed throws, untyped throws remains default
open error sets with `try` prefix operator and inferred error sets, errdefer for cleanup

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Automatic propagation without syntactic noise (no `?`/`try`) is cleaner than Rust/Zig. Closed unions with effect-tracked error type beats Swift's any-Error default. Effect-based error handling composes with other effects (Alloc, IO, Async) in the same mechanism. No catch-all `error` type forces discipline.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Automatic propagation hides control-flow — a call `let raw = fetch(id);` looks pure but may abort. Rust's `?` is DELIBERATELY visible noise marking aborts. Koka has the exact same story and explicitly calls this a limitation. Composing errors across modules requires manual `my_error` wrapper type with explicit `Fetch(fetch_error)` variants — no automatic From-conversion like Rust. No resumable exceptions (Koka can resume, FX cannot per spec). No `errdefer` / scope-exit-on-error analog.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Rule for implicit vs explicit propagation at call site (Koka requires `exn` in the caller's effect; does FX?); spec for Fail interaction with linear resources (what gets dropped on abort? §7.11 gap #101 flags this); spec for multiple Fail effects (Fail(E1) + Fail(E2) → Fail(E1|E2)?); no catch-all pattern in try/catch — does `_ =>` work?; no exception patterns in match.
analysis.could_we_do_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 3] Verdict raised from 'strong' to 'frontier' after Proposal 3 adopted a Zig-style defer/errdefer split in §7.11. Two forms: 'defer expr;' always runs at scope exit, 'errdefer expr;' runs only on Fail/Exn abort exits; both LIFO within the nearest enclosing lexical block. Defer bodies are restricted to the enclosing function's effect row minus Fail/Exn (preventing double-fault during unwinding). Rule G-Linear-Cleanup-On-Fail demands defer/errdefer for every linear binding live at every Fail abort site, keyed on liveness rather than mechanism. Closes gap #101. Interaction with §13.3 inverse transitions specified: local cleanup first, then machine-level compensation. Earlier analysis preserved below.

Add explicit `try expr` or `maybe_fail expr` operator at call sites so programmers can see abort points (compromise between Koka/FX invisibility and Rust visibility). Spec auto-widening of Fail error-type via subtyping (`Fail(parse_error) <= Fail(my_error)` when parse_error is variant of my_error). Specify linear-resource cleanup on abort — this is gap #101 and must not stay open.

analysis.what_fx_does: x.method(args) desugars to Type.method(x, args) where self-mode is determined by method's self parameter declaration (ref self / ref mut self / own self / no self for static).

closest_prior_art.approach: any free function f(a,b) callable as a.f(b) if a's type has no member f
any proc callable in dot form, case-insensitive
self/self mut/self owned distinction, trait method resolution

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Self-mode determined by declaration rather than call site eliminates caller-side annotation (Rust requires `&x.method(..)` vs `x.method(..)` based on method's receiver). Consistent with Rust's advance on C++/D.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Section is 10 lines and defers full rules to §16.3. Section doesn't say whether dot syntax works for ANY free function (D/Nim UFCS) or only impl-block methods (Rust). No spec for disambiguation when two traits define the same method name — §16.3 does but this section doesn't cross-reference. No chained-dot mode inference.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Is this full UFCS (any free function, D/Nim style) or method-dispatch (Rust style)? Given §16.3 talks about resolution lattice with impl/instance/class-default levels, this is Rust-style method dispatch, not UFCS. Should say so explicitly. No spec for method-on-linear-value when method takes ref — does it auto-borrow?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Explicitly state: (a) dot syntax is not full UFCS, it is method-call syntax for impl/instance items; (b) caller never annotates mode — implicit borrow inference; (c) cross-reference §16.3 for disambiguation. Or, alternatively, ADD full UFCS so free functions can use dot form — matches pipeline syntax (`x |> f(...)` vs `x.f(...)`).

analysis.what_fx_does: All control flow (return/break/throw/yield/await) derives from delimited continuations — semantic foundation, not user-visible syntax; effect system tracks delimiters in scope.

closest_prior_art.approach: multicore OCaml implements one-shot delimited continuations as the runtime primitive; effects are the surface, delimcc is the implementation
multi-prompt delimited continuations with continuation marks, multi-shot by default
delimited continuations underlie effect handlers; resume is primitive

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unifying ALL control operators under one foundation (rather than having return/break/throw/yield/await as independent primitives with ad-hoc interactions) is elegant and matches research trend (Koka, OCaml 5, Eff). Effect system tracks delimiters, giving compile-time guarantees against stray breaks/returns.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Section is 10 lines of handwaving. No spec for: one-shot vs multi-shot (OCaml chose one-shot for efficiency; FX doesn't say; §9.7 does mention one-shot default with multishot op); shallow vs deep handlers; semantics of nested delimiters (does inner return propagate to outer?); interaction with linear resources (continuations capture stack — do they capture linear values?). FX claims 'not user-visible' but then §9 exposes effect handlers which ARE exactly this mechanism, with `k` as continuation. Contradiction.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Formal semantics (reset/shift0 chosen? prompt-tag variant?), nested delimiter ordering, interaction with async scheduler, what happens when a captured continuation holds linear state, relation to §9.6 handlers (which DO expose continuations).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Either elevate this to a proper section with formal semantics (prompt-tags, shift0/reset0, one-shot discipline) OR fold it into §9 (Effects) since that's where the mechanism really lives. Current 10-line section is too thin for a semantic foundation claim.

analysis.what_fx_does: 70-line informal EBNF-style grammar sketch for expressions, atoms, declarations, effect rows, spec clauses, body forms; formal grammar deferred to fx_grammar.md.

closest_prior_art.approach: one canonical grammar file integrated into reference, validates against parser
formal BNF embedded directly in language manual, synchronized with compiler

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Having an fx_grammar.md separate from fx_design.md lets the design doc prioritize prose over BNF density.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Two-grammar problem: the 70-line §4.12 summary WILL drift from the 1090-line fx_grammar.md as the language evolves. OCaml and Rust integrate the canonical grammar INTO the reference manual to prevent drift. The sketch already omits or-patterns, range patterns, negative-literals per §4.3 analysis — these are in fx_grammar.md but missing from §4.12. Worse: fx_design.md CLAUDE.md notes the OCamlX parser (FxParse.mly) is already OUTDATED vs fx_grammar.md.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Precedence table for binops/prefix/dot (critical for parsing), associativity specification, token definitions (§4.12 references terminals like IDENT/UIDENT without defining), spec clause ordering rule (design doc says `where; pre; post; decreases;` order is strict but grammar shows them as alternatives separated by `|`).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Either (a) delete §4.12 entirely and point readers to fx_grammar.md as canonical (avoids drift), or (b) promote fx_grammar.md to be an appendix of fx_design.md with a drift-check CI test. Add precedence/associativity table in either location. Document token set (currently implicit via 'IDENT' etc.).

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter introduces FX's surface for function, type, and module declarations plus module functors — mostly orthodox ML/Rust-style forms with a few idiosyncratic twists (strict clause ordering, conditional `pub const` restricted to `@[copy]` + `comptime` + `Tot` initializers, bare `end` closers).

closest_prior_art.approach: Stratified core+module language with applicative/generative functors, .ml/.mli files, open/include
Items with pub, enums with struct-variants, Copy auto-derive, no functors (uses generics+traits)
Dependently-typed ML; Low*-style functions with Pure/Tot/ST and requires/ensures/decreases clauses

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unified declaration grammar across values, types, machines, contracts, and hardware. Typed closers (`end fn`/`end type`) give LLMs and diff tools unambiguous structural anchors that Python-indent or Rust-brace languages lack. Module system combines OCaml functors with Rust-style visibility and adds a hard no-dialect / no-build-script rule that OCaml and Haskell never enforced.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chapter is short (~265 lines) for a section that has to define function, type, and module semantics of a dependently-typed, linearly-graded language. Dafny's reference manual and Lean 4's manual give an order of magnitude more detail on recursion, signatures, and namespaces. Many corner cases (orphan rules, instance coherence, re-export hygiene, functor applicativity/generativity) are silently inherited rather than stated.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of: (a) functor applicative vs generative semantics (see Rossberg 1ML / Blaudeau-Rémy-Radanne 2024), (b) module signature subsumption, (c) shadowing rules under `open`/`include`, (d) handling of identically-named symbols from two open'd modules, (e) `where Ord(a)` constraint inheritance for recursive calls, (f) `val` forward declaration binding semantics.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Borrow from Lean 4 reference manual structure: for each declaration form give grammar, elaboration rule, scope rule, and error taxonomy. Specify functor semantics with a single sentence citing 1ML-style generative-by-default or OCaml-style applicative-on-pure. Add at least one worked example of diamond imports.

analysis.what_fx_does: Declares a strict, compiler-enforced clause ordering for function signatures: `with effects | where constraints | pre | post r => | decreases` between signature and body, with `post r =>` making the return binder explicit instead of magic `result`.

closest_prior_art.approach: Conventional but not enforced order (requires/modifies/ensures/decreases); ORDER OF ensures clauses is semantically significant because earlier ensures can be used to prove later ones
Aspects `with Pre => ... , Post => ...` comma-separated, no enforced order; uses `'Result` attribute, not a named binder
`Lemma (requires ...) (ensures ...) (decreases ...)` parenthesized; known inconsistency that decreases requires extra parens (GH#1765)
`where` defines local auxiliary functions (NOT constraints); constraints go in binders `[Ord α]` or `(h : P)`; `termination_by`/`decreasing_by` follow body, not precede it

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Named postcondition binder (`post r =>`) is cleaner than SPARK's `F'Result` attribute and Dafny's implicit convention. Misordering as a compile error prevents inconsistent code-base style. Placing effects on the same line as return type is syntactically uniform with session types and hardware blocks.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) `where` for type-class constraints collides with Lean 4's `where` (local helpers) and Haskell's `where` (local bindings) — cognitive burden for PL-literate users. Rust and Lean use bounds in binders (`<a: Ord>` or `[Ord α]`), which scales better to multi-parameter classes. (2) Strict ordering is over-strict: Dafny explicitly allows `requires` and `ensures` to interleave because ORDER within a type matters for proof; FX's single-block-per-kind doesn't address this. (3) `decreases` after `post` differs from F*/Dafny convention (decreases usually last, before body — FX agrees) but the F* parenthesization gotcha isn't mentioned. (4) No `modifies`/frame clause — surprising for a linear/graded language; FX claims ownership handles it but that's a big claim unspecified here.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) No rule for multiple `where` clauses (can you write two?), (b) no rule for `pre` ordering between multiple pre-clauses being proof-significant (Dafny gotcha), (c) no frame/modifies clause or explicit reads-set, (d) interaction with effect variables (does `where` bound constrain effect row?), (e) `decreases` lexicographic tuples syntax (Dafny: comma-separated; Lean: `(a,b)`; FX unclear), (f) what happens if body's inferred effect is smaller than declared — is widening an error or just a warning?
analysis.could_we_do_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 1 + rigor-first] Verdict raised from 'adequate' to 'strong' after Proposal 1 adopted Dimension 20 (Size) with 'with Productive' effect + guardedness check for §3.5 codata, plus the tier-system refactor of §6.3 that classifies each dimension as Tier S (semiring), L (lattice+validity), T (typestate), or F (foundational). Rigor-first eliminates all inference sites with explicit surface syntax per Appendix E. Earlier note preserved below.

Adopt Lean/Rust-style type-class constraints in binders (`<a: type + Ord>`) and reserve `where` for local definitions — matches the linguistic expectations of ~30 years of Haskell users. Allow interleaving `pre`/`post` in source order and make order proof-significant like Dafny. Add explicit `modifies`/`reads` frame clause (even if trivial given ownership) for symmetry with the 21-dim table.

analysis.what_fx_does: `rec` modifier opts into self-reference; every `rec` must carry `decreases` OR declare `with Div`. Mutual recursion chained with `and`. `pub` on first applies to all clauses.

closest_prior_art.approach: Both structural recursion (automatic, preserves definitional equality) and well-founded via `termination_by`/`decreasing_by`; NO `rec` keyword required; `partial def` for non-terminating
Structural-recursion-with-lex-order check, `--termination-depth` knob, `TERMINATING` pragma as escape; total by default under --safe
Size-change termination; `assert_smaller`/`assert_total` hints rather than explicit decreases; not total by default (opt-in via `%default total`)
Always auto-guesses decreases; explicit `decreases` only when needed; no `rec` keyword

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Honest — a `rec` function that forgets `decreases` is rejected unless it explicitly declares `with Div`. This forces programmers to state termination status in the signature, which Idris 2 (partial-by-default) and OCaml (no check at all) do not. `with Div` effect is cleaner than Idris's `partial` keyword because it composes with the 21-dim grade vector.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) Requires `rec` keyword even when termination is structurally obvious — Lean 4, Agda, and Coq do NOT require such a marker. For a system targeting LLM-generated code this is an extra dimension to get right. (2) No notion of structural vs well-founded recursion — Lean 4 explicitly distinguishes so users know when definitional equality is preserved; FX gives one hammer (`decreases`). (3) No mention of lex-tuple decreases syntax, no mention of `decreases_by` custom tactic, no mention of Agda-style `TERMINATING` escape hatch for release-only unsafe optimization. (4) Mutual recursion via `and` inherits OCaml pain: no specification of what happens with three mutually recursive functions that must all share the same public visibility, type parameters, or `where` constraints. (5) Coinduction/productivity for codata (§3.5) not connected here — where do codata productivity proofs go?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Exact grammar of lexicographic `decreases` (tuple? comma-separated? %[...]?), (b) custom well-founded relation specification (Lean's `decreasing_by` tactic equivalent), (c) interaction of `decreases` with `where` class constraints, (d) behavior of `rec` + `with Div` in constrained profile (e.g., embedded driver forbids Div), (e) productivity checking for codata (§3.5 was flagged as gap, not fixed here), (f) how `and`-bound functions with differing `decreases` are checked jointly.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Follow Lean 4: drop `rec` keyword — let the checker infer self-reference; use `termination_by`/`decreasing_by` with explicit strategy choice so users can request structural recursion (for definitional equality) vs well-founded. Specify lex-tuple syntax explicitly. Connect codata productivity to a dual `productive` clause so §3.5 and §5.2 compose.

analysis.what_fx_does: Lambdas as `fn(params) => body` with optional typed-tuple destructuring and inferred parameter types; distinct from arrow `->` used in function types.

closest_prior_art.approach: `|x| x+1` or `|x: i32| -> i32 { x+1 }`; pipe-delimited params; `move` keyword for captures
`(x) => x+1` arrow functions; single-paren omission for single param
`{ x -> x+1 }` lambda expression always inside braces with -> separator
`fun x -> x+1`
`fun x => x+1` or unicode `λ x => x+1`

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Disambiguates `->` (function type) from `=>` (lambda body) consistently across match arms and lambdas (rule 3), matching Lean 4 and Rust match-arms. Reusing `fn` keyword means there's one thing to search for, easier for LLMs. Destructuring in params is standard and correct.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) §5.3 is nine lines — thinnest specification of any lambda construct in a modern language. (2) No spec of CAPTURE semantics: what is captured by value vs by reference? Rust's `move` keyword and the Lam-rule in §6.2 aren't connected here. (3) No mention of implicit `fn(it)` dot-shorthand equivalence — §4.2 has rule 25 but §5.3 doesn't cross-reference. (4) Verbose for single-arg case: `fn(x) => x*2` is 12 chars vs Rust `|x| x*2` (8) or Kotlin `{ it*2 }` (7). For LLM-primary users, token count matters. (5) No spec of `async` lambdas for §9.9 Async effect — can you write `async fn(x) => await(http_get(x))`?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Capture-mode inference algorithm (referenced in §7.10 but not cross-linked), (b) closure lifetime and region annotation syntax, (c) ownership modes inside lambda params (`fn(own x) => ...`), (d) lambda effect annotations (`fn(x) : i64 with IO => ...`), (e) currying — is `fn(a,b) => e` the same as `fn(a) => fn(b) => e`? Lean and Haskell auto-curry, OCaml doesn't; FX mandatory-named-args make currying weird.
analysis.could_we_do_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 1 + rigor-first] Verdict raised from 'weak' to 'adequate' after Proposal 1 adopted Dimension 20 (Size) with 'with Productive' effect + guardedness check for §3.5 codata, plus the tier-system refactor of §6.3 that classifies each dimension as Tier S (semiring), L (lattice+validity), T (typestate), or F (foundational). Rigor-first eliminates all inference sites with explicit surface syntax per Appendix E. Earlier note preserved below.

Add 2-3 paragraphs covering capture (see §7.10), effect annotation, and async. Consider shorter sugar for single-arg lambdas (e.g., Kotlin-style `{ .name }` already exists as dot-shorthand — make the relationship explicit). State whether multi-arg lambdas curry.

analysis.what_fx_does: Three type-declaration forms: alias (`type m = f64`), variant with one-line constructors terminated by `;` and closed by `end type` (no `|` bars, no `of`), and record with brace+semicolons. Variants can carry named-field payloads (`Lit(value: int)`). `@[copy]` on a type makes it unrestricted; compiler verifies all fields are `@[copy]`.

closest_prior_art.approach: `enum E { Quit, Move { x: i32, y: i32 }, Write(String) }` allows unit/tuple/struct variants; `#[derive(Copy, Clone)]` with all-fields-Copy rule (E0204)
`data E = Quit | Move { x :: Int, y :: Int } | Write String` — record syntax inside ADT gives named accessors; constructors are first-class functions
`enum Color { case Red; case RGB(r: Int, g: Int, b: Int) }` unified enum syntax with named payloads
`type shape = Circle of float | Rect of {w: float; h: float}` — inline records on variants since 4.03

analysis.where_fx_is_better: `@[copy]` propagation rule is explicit and compiler-checked — matches Rust E0204. Per-constructor named-field payloads give Haskell's record-in-ADT ergonomics without Haskell's partial-accessor footgun (since FX uses pattern match, not auto-generated projections). Linear-by-default is a strong correctness property that neither Rust nor Haskell has (both are Copy/share-by-default).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) No `derive` mechanism — Rust's auto-derivation of Clone/PartialEq/Debug is enormously productive; FX doesn't specify how one adds extra algebraic structure to user types beyond `@[copy]`. (2) No spec of constructor-as-first-class-function (Haskell has it; Rust kinda-does-kinda-doesn't; FX silent). With mandatory named-args for multi-param (rule §4.1), `Some(42)` and `Some(x: 42)` — which is it? §3.3 says `Some(a)` but doesn't say whether call-sites require `Some(x: 42)` if you made it `Some(value: a)`. (3) No GADT syntax even though the type system is dependent — missing a huge expressivity win that Lean/Coq/Idris have natively. (4) Record update `{ cfg with port: 9090 }` (§3.4) is defined there but not here — section split unclear. (5) Variant payloads with names use `(value: int)` but records use `{ value: int }` — inconsistent brace discipline (inherited from OCaml's inline records on variants but still jarring). (6) §5.4 does not cross-reference §3.3/§3.4 — duplicate specs.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Derive-mechanism for user traits (compare Rust `#[derive(Serialize)]`), (b) GADT syntax for dependent variants, (c) existential types at constructor level (Rust doesn't have; Haskell does via `forall`), (d) phantom type parameters and their variance, (e) recursive-type positivity check (critical for a dependent type system — Coq/Agda/Lean all enforce strict positivity; FX says nothing), (f) representation control on variants (`repr(u8)`, niche packing) — §8.4 covers runtime repr but §5.4 doesn't connect, (g) how `@[copy]` interacts with parametric types (is `list<T>` Copy if T is Copy? Rust requires explicit `T: Copy` bound).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add derive mechanism (point to §17.3 decorators if that's the vehicle). State strict positivity requirement for recursive data. Specify how `@[copy]` propagates through type parameters (bound vs automatic). Show one GADT example since FX is dependent. Consolidate or cross-reference §3.3/§3.4/§5.4 — currently three places describe the same grammar.

analysis.what_fx_does: One file = one module; `pub` toggles visibility; NO global variables, NO top-level `let`, NO `pub static`; `pub const` allowed only when type is `@[copy]` and initializer is `comptime`-evaluable with effect `Tot` (inlined at use sites, no runtime shared state); module-scoped `const` always compile-time; `open` imports, `include` re-exports, `val` forward-declares, `extern "C"` FFI blocks, ML-style `module functor` parameterizes by module signatures.

closest_prior_art.approach: Stratified core+module with applicative/generative functors; signature files .mli; `include` and `open`; known problems: signature duplication pain, open-world coherence, manual functor application
Unified core+module; modules as first-class values; no separate signature language; acknowledged trade-off: incomplete type inference
Mixin linking atop type-classes; retypechecks every instantiation; deliberately punts mutual-recursion between packages

closest_prior_art.system: Fulfilling OCaml Modules with Transparency (Blaudeau/Rémy/Radanne, OOPSLA 2024)
closest_prior_art.approach: Source-to-source identity tag transformation; unifies applicative/generative treatment; argues against full 1ML unification for OCaml
No functors; traits + generics + coherence (orphan rule); pub/pub(crate)/pub(super) fine-grained visibility

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Banning global mutable state, top-level `let`, and `pub static` is a strong purity stance Rust/OCaml don't take — runtime-shared state is forced behind `pub fn` so its effects and allocation strategy are visible in the signature. `pub const` is permitted under the compile-time inlined conditions (`@[copy]` + `Tot` + `comptime`), matching Rust's `const` vs `static` distinction precisely. `no dialects` + `no build scripts` (§25.7) neutralizes the xz-style supply-chain attack surface that OCaml functors don't address. Functors + type classes coexist (§16.4) — FX gets Backpack's goal ("modules and classes both") without Backpack's retypecheck-every-instantiation cost being discussed.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) Does NOT specify applicative vs generative functor semantics — the single most consequential choice in ML module design, with an entire 2024 OOPSLA paper devoted to it. Silence here means FX either has a bug or inherits F*'s (unclear) semantics. (2) `open` shadowing unspecified: what if two `open` modules both expose `sort`? Rust has `use` disambiguation, OCaml re-opens win, Haskell raises ambient-ambiguous error only on use. (3) Orphan rule for type-class instances (§16.4 mentions it but §5.5 doesn't state how it interacts with modules). (4) No fine-grained visibility (`pub(crate)`, `pub(super)`, friends) — Rust has found this essential at scale. (5) No recursive modules (OCaml has `rec`, Coq has it, Lean has it) — big constraint for any dependently-typed language where mutual type+function dependencies are common. (6) The `struct ... end struct` syntax for module instantiation is introduced without grammar rule — contradicts §2.3 keyword list (struct not listed). (7) No signature-avoidance rule, no discussion of phantom types for abstraction (OCaml semantic-paths approach per Blaudeau 2024).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Applicative vs generative functor semantics (critical), (b) open/include conflict resolution, (c) fine-grained visibility (pub(crate) etc.), (d) recursive modules, (e) signature subsumption / sharing constraints (OCaml's `with type` / `with module`), (f) orphan rule for trait instances, (g) first-class modules (1ML-style), (h) module-level let-polymorphism restriction (ML value restriction), (i) how functors compose with linear types — applying a functor to a linear module: is the result linear? (j) circular module detection algorithm, (k) the `struct ... end struct` grammar rule.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Commit to ONE functor semantics (probably generative by default, applicative on pure per Backpack consensus) and cite 1ML or OCaml-transparency paper. Specify open-shadowing (prefer Rust's explicit-qualification-on-conflict). Add `pub(module)`/`pub(package)` visibility at least. Specify recursive modules via mutual `module rec M and N` analogous to `rec ... and`. Add a worked example of functor-applied-to-linear-module to force the interaction spec. (The pub const ban was lifted in the current spec: `pub const` is allowed under `@[copy]` + `comptime` + `Tot` conditions, closing the earlier critique.)

analysis.what_fx_does: FX unifies all twenty-one type dimensions under a single graded modal type theory (Atkey 2018, McBride 2016, Wood/Atkey 2022), where each dimension is instantiated via one parameterized grade-checking algorithm dispatched by tier (Tier S semirings, Tier L lattices with validity, Tier T typestate, Tier F foundational, Tier V versioned — §6.3).

closest_prior_art.approach: First language to implement general graded modal types parameterized by user-chosen semirings; separately supports linearity, security, and effect modalities, each as its own graded modality
closest_prior_art.reference: Orchard, Liepelt, Eades 'Quantitative program reasoning with graded modal types', ICFP 2019, https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3341714

closest_prior_art.approach: Implements quantitative type theory with a fixed three-element semiring {0,1,w}; separate mechanisms for effects, erasure, linearity; does not unify 21 dimensions
closest_prior_art.reference: Brady 'Idris 2: Quantitative Type Theory in Practice', ECOOP 2021, https://drops.dagstuhl.de/entities/document/10.4230/LIPIcs.ECOOP.2021.9

closest_prior_art.approach: Dependent graded modal type theory with a single parameterizable semiring-valued grade; mechanized in Agda
closest_prior_art.reference: Moon, Eades, Orchard 'Graded Modal Dependent Type Theory', ESOP 2021, https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.13163

analysis.where_fx_is_better: No existing graded modal type theory claims to unify 21 orthogonal dimensions via a tier-classified kernel (S/L/T/F/V) with one checking algorithm. Granule uses separate modalities; Idris 2 uses a fixed triple. FX's tier-based unification — Tier S dimensions as commutative semirings, Tier L as lattices with validity predicates, Tier T typestate (protocols), Tier F foundational (types/refinements), Tier V versioned (with adapter edges) — is a genuinely novel engineering consolidation.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The chapter opener makes a strong theoretical claim that remains unjustified in this section and is contradicted by the dimension enumeration in §6.3, which explicitly states that Type, Refinement, Protocol, and Representation are NOT semirings. Granule and GrTT are mechanized; FX's unified semiring framework has no soundness proof yet.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) No theorem stating that the product of 19 semirings yields a valid semiring under FX's check rules. (2) No soundness/metatheory citation tying FX's framework back to Wood/Atkey beyond name-dropping. (3) No discussion of how non-semiring dimensions (session types, refinements) coexist with the uniform algorithm.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Split the chapter opener into (a) dimensions that are genuinely semirings and share the parameterized check, (b) dimensions that are lattices or preorders needing separate checking machinery, and (c) dimensions like sessions that require state-indexed typing. Cite specific theorems from Wood/Atkey 2022 and GrTT 2021 (not just author-year).

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines the standard partially-ordered semiring structure (R,+,*,0,1,<=) with usage semiring {0,1,w} and a security two-element lattice as canonical examples.

closest_prior_art.approach: Generalizes McBride 2016's fixed {0,1,w} to arbitrary semirings Q; proves soundness via Quantitative Categories with Families; same tables as FX's usage semiring
closest_prior_art.reference: Atkey 'Syntax and Semantics of Quantitative Type Theory', LICS 2018, https://bentnib.org/quantitative-type-theory.html

closest_prior_art.system: Ghica & Smith 'Bounded Linear Types in a Resource Semiring'
closest_prior_art.approach: Parameterizes a linear type system by an arbitrary semiring, giving categorical semantics and a parameterized type-inference procedure
First to use {0,1,w} for compile-time erasure/linear/unrestricted; basis for Atkey and Idris 2
closest_prior_art.reference: McBride 'I Got Plenty o Nuttin', Wadler Festschrift 2016, https://personal.cis.strath.ac.uk/conor.mcbride/PlentyO-CR.pdf

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The usage-semiring table (1+1=w, 1*w=w, 0-annihilation) is correct and matches the literature. Presenting security as a semiring alongside usage — with multiplication also being join — is cleaner than Jif/FlowCaml's separate label-model.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The security 'semiring' shown is only a two-element bounded lattice; FX calls it a semiring but gives no * operation explicitly (only + as join). The claim 'each dimension provides a semiring' is overstated: for a bounded lattice (L,\/,0) to be a semiring you need * = \/ giving idempotent multiplication, which makes 'sequential use' collapse into 'parallel use'. Granule makes this distinction explicit; FX does not.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No proof that <= is compatible with + and *. No statement about whether the semiring is commutative (many of FX's Tier S dimensions are commutative — should be stated). No discussion of whether subsumption <= interacts with ownership (monotonicity claimed but not shown). Missing the Atkey-style zero condition 0 * x = x * 0 = 0 is given but the identity 1 * x = x is implicit.
analysis.could_we_do_better: State the full axiom set as in Atkey 2018 Definition 2.1 (partially-ordered semiring), cite the reference, and give the multiplication table for the security semiring explicitly (not just +). The §6.3 tier classification (S/L/T/F/V) now identifies which dimensions are commutative semirings vs lattices vs typestate; remaining work is the per-tier axiom statement.

analysis.what_fx_does: Gives typing rules for Var/App/Let/If/Lam/Subsumption using grade-annotated contexts; the Lam rule uses Wood/Atkey 2022-style context division G/p to block linear-capture-in-replicable-closure bugs.

closest_prior_art.system: Wood & Atkey 'Framework for Substructural Type Systems' (ESOP 2022)
closest_prior_art.approach: Uses context division / scaling for abstraction over grades; same App rule p1 + r*p2 and Lam rule with G/p scaling
Uses right-restricted grades {0,1} on the judgment and left-divided context for substitution; FX's G/p is equivalent up to presentation

closest_prior_art.system: GraD / A Graded Dependent Type System with a Usage-Aware Semantics
closest_prior_art.approach: Choudhury, Eades, Eisenberg, Weirich 2021 — graded dependent type system with heap-based operational semantics proving resource soundness; different handling of pair projections
Implements App rule as r*p2 + p1 with bidirectional checking using Z3; context division made implicit

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX correctly identifies the Atkey 2018 Lam rule bug (linear f captured in replicable closure) and adopts the Wood/Atkey 2022 fix — most practical languages (including Idris 2 pre-patch) got this wrong. Explicitly stating the division definition pi/p = max{d : d*p <= pi} and showing how 1/w = 0 excludes linear captures is exactly right.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) No dependent types in the stated rules (Pi abstraction missing; only simple function types shown), even though FX is dependently typed — this is a significant gap vs GrTT/QTT which present full Pi rules. (2) No Elim rules for sums/products — how does pair elimination split the grade context? GrTT and GraD disagree on this; FX punts. (3) No operational semantics; no preservation/progress theorem; no citation of a mechanized proof corresponding to these rules.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Pi/Sigma typing rules, elimination rules, substitution lemma, preservation theorem reference, operational semantics, context-division proof that (G/p)*p <= G. Rules for multiple dimensions simultaneously — the claim is 'run the same rules on all of them simultaneously' but the rules shown are single-semiring.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add the full Pi abstraction/application rules from Wood/Atkey 2022 or GrTT Definition 3.1. Add elimination rules for pairs (Choudhury-style with grade factoring). State preservation & progress as theorems (even if proofs are in §27 metatheory). Include a vector-semiring Lam rule showing how 19 grades compose — the whole selling point of the chapter.

analysis.what_fx_does: Instantiates each of 21 dimensions into one of five tiers (S semiring, L lattice with validity, T typestate, F foundational, V versioned) with per-tier operations (+ / * / 0 / 1 / <= for S; ∨ / ∧ / valid for L; state transitions for T; discharged-by-elaboration for F; consistency check + adapter edges for V), mapping surface syntax to grades.

closest_prior_art.approach: Three built-in modalities: bounded-reuse, security, effect — user-extensible but no single language ships 19; also explicitly uses separate graded modalities rather than a single product semiring

Effects as row-polymorphic labels with scoped equivalence, not lattice subtyping; FX's 'Tot zero, union addition, Read<=Write' is a reasonable lattice encoding but loses Koka's scoped-label flexibility
closest_prior_art.reference: Leijen, 'Koka: Programming with Row Polymorphic Effect Types', arXiv:1406.2061

closest_prior_art.approach: Dimension 13 (Complexity) treated as potential-annotated types giving automatic amortized resource analysis; much more expressive than FX's naive natural-number grade
closest_prior_art.reference: Hoffmann et al. 'Towards Automatic Resource Bound Analysis for OCaml', POPL 2017, https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~janh/papers/HoffmannDW17.pdf

closest_prior_art.approach: Dimension 7 (Lifetime) as outlives-preorder is standard but Rust/Polonius implements location-sensitive subtyping on CFG points; FX just says 'preorder'
Dimension 12 (Clock domain) treated via dedicated clock type and synchronizer requirement; the claim `sync(a) + sync(b) = CROSS_DOMAIN_ERROR` matches Bluespec but `error` is not a semiring element

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The uniformity claim — 21 dimensions in one grade vector with explicit tier classification — is genuinely ambitious. Specifically, FX's expression of observability (CT), representation, and clock domain as grades alongside usage and effects is more comprehensive than any existing graded-modal language. The tier system is honest about which dimensions are true semirings vs lattices vs typestate.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The tier classification (Proposal 1) honestly demoted non-semirings to Tier L/T/F/V rather than pretending they are semirings, addressing the original unevenness: (i) Dim 1 (Type) and Dim 2 (Refinement) are Tier F (foundational, elaboration-discharged) — no longer claimed as grades. (ii) Dim 6 (Protocol) is Tier T (typestate transitions). (iii) Dim 10 (Representation) and Dim 12 (Clock domain) are Tier L (lattice + validity predicate with None return for incompatible joins). (iv) Dim 21 (Version) is Tier V (lattice with adapter edges). Remaining concerns: (v) Dim 14 (Precision) — Proposal revision of §6.3 switched to condition-number-aware propagation (Higham §3.3). (vi) Dim 18 (Mutation) four-element lattice still needs explicit ordering parameter spec for append_only and monotonic. (vii) Dim 9 (Trust) — multiplication is specified as min.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Explicit * operation for every Tier S dimension (most given, some implicit). (2) Proof that each dimension's operations satisfy tier-specific laws (semiring laws for S, lattice laws for L, transition rules for T). (3) Interaction rules — how does usage=1 compose with effects={IO} when you have BOTH grades? Addressed partially by pointwise vector composition in §6.2 rules plus §6.8 collision catalog. (4) Representation's 'preorder repr(Native) <= repr(C)' — now stated as 'valid_D(join) returns None for incompatible' (T047 at elaboration).
analysis.could_we_do_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 1 + rigor-first] Verdict raised from 'weak' to 'strong' after Proposal 1 adopted Dimension 20 (Size) with 'with Productive' effect + guardedness check for §3.5 codata, plus the tier-system refactor of §6.3 that classifies each dimension as Tier S (semiring), L (lattice+validity), T (typestate), or F (foundational). Rigor-first eliminates all inference sites with explicit surface syntax per Appendix E. Earlier note preserved below.

Honestly demote 3-4 'not semirings' dimensions to 'auxiliary discipline' with its own checker (as Granule does for protocols). For Precision, replace the naive additive model with FPTaylor-style roundoff + condition-number propagation (worst-case abstract interpretation). For Clock domain and Representation incompatibilities, use a sum-type {compatible(grade), incompatible}. Cite RAML for a more sophisticated Complexity semiring (multivariate polynomials in input sizes).

analysis.what_fx_does: Shows a concrete example of grade tracking through a function body: how 'handle: 1' evolves to '1' (borrowed) to '0' (consumed); shows error messages M001 (double consume) and M002 (leak).

closest_prior_art.approach: Bidirectional type checker with Z3 discharging grade constraints; example would be shown with explicit [n] grade annotations on type
MIR-based linearity tracking gives essentially the same error messages ('value moved here, used after move'); diagnostics are more mature and NLL-aware
Tracks quantity 1 through pattern matches and function bodies; diagnostic message similar

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Error messages M001/M002 are concrete and include 'previously consumed at line X' pointers — comparable to Rust's error quality, better than Granule's current tooling. Showing the grade vector evolution step-by-step is a good pedagogical example.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Example only tracks one dimension (usage) — the promise of §6 is all 19 simultaneously. 'read_all(ref handle)' claims grade stays at 1, but a shared borrow in the Frac PCM reading of §6.4 should split to Frac(<1). Inconsistency with the separation-logic framing in §6.4. No example showing security + usage together, which is the whole point of the dimension product.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Example with multi-dimensional grade vector (usage × security × effect evolving together). Explanation of how conditional execution joins grade vectors from both branches (the most subtle part — used in the If rule). How nested closures and borrow lifetimes interact in the grade checker. Diagnostic for higher-rank grade errors (e.g., what does closure capture-rejection look like to the user?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Replace the single-dimension example with a multi-dimensional worked example: a function reading a secret file handle with IO effect — show how usage goes 1->1->0, security goes classified (propagates), and effect grows from Tot to {IO}. Show the If-join producing a linearity error through a branch asymmetry.

sections.name: Separation Logic as the Usage Grade

analysis.what_fx_does: Claims that FX has no separate separation logic because the usage grade IS separation logic — lifts the {0,1,w} semiring to a fractional-permission PCM (Boyland-style Frac(p), Zero, Omega) where surface modes own/ref/ref mut/ghost map to permissions.

closest_prior_art.approach: PCM/RA structures (cameras) form the semantic core; fractional permissions are one instance; frame rule proven, discardable-fractional permissions extend to persistent ownership
closest_prior_art.reference: Jung et al. 'Iris from the ground up', JFP 2018, https://iris-project.org/

closest_prior_art.approach: Originated rational permissions in (0,1] for concurrent read sharing
Uses fractional permissions for verification of concurrent Java-like code; annotations compile to Z3 obligations
Similar fractional-permission reasoning with explicit frame rule in annotations

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Making fractional permissions implicit in surface syntax (`ref x` = Frac(p<1) automatically) is much cleaner than Viper/VeriFast where annotations are manual. The claim that frame rule is automatic follows from grade-vector splitting at App and is consistent with Atkey 2018's semantics.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) The PCM given is incomplete: `Frac(p) + Frac(q) = CONFLICT when p+q > 1` means + is partial — this is by definition a PCM, but the prior typing rules in §6.2 use total +. How does a partial PCM fit into the graded checker? Iris handles this via the `Valid` predicate; FX doesn't mention it. (2) `Omega + Omega = Omega` is fine but `Omega + Frac(p) = ?` is undefined. (3) The share/unshare functions require linear re-combination but the type `(ref ptr, ref ptr)` stored in a tuple is itself linear — type-level treatment of Frac(0.5)+Frac(0.5)=Frac(1) requires dependent grades that §6.7 only hand-waves. (4) Iris's discardable fractions and persistent points-to aren't discussed — FX's model doesn't cover shared-forever resources.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Formal PCM validity predicate — when is composition CONFLICT vs CONFLICT-free? (2) Interaction of fractional permissions with concurrent memory ordering (§11.10 defers this). (3) Persistent / discardable permissions for immutable shared state. (4) Frame rule's formal statement (even as reference). (5) How ghost = Zero permission interacts with ghost-erasure at compile time. (6) No mention of Iris resource algebras (RAs) or step-indexing.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Iris's full resource algebra structure with Valid predicate, core function (for persistent resources), and step-indexing story. Add discardable fractions for immutable data. Formalize the relationship between PCM composition and the grade-checker's + via an interpretation function. Reference Vindum's 2023 PhD thesis for discardable fractional permissions.

analysis.what_fx_does: Introduces ghost state (Zero permission, erased at runtime but tracked in proofs) and user-defined PCMs via grade_dimension blocks with commutative/associative/identity laws discharged by SMT; example: MonotonicCounter as max-semiring.

closest_prior_art.approach: Ghost state via RA construction; `own` predicate tracks PCM element; includes MonotonicCounter (authoritative RA) as canonical example
closest_prior_art.reference: Jung et al. 'Iris from the ground up', JFP 2018

closest_prior_art.approach: Zero multiplicity ensures variables are erased at runtime but usable at type-level; type-level irrelevance is type-checked, not SMT-discharged
Ghost variables and lemmas existing only in proofs; no PCM composition
Ghost effect erases computations; no PCM

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Combining Idris-2-style erasure (quantity 0) with Iris-style PCMs in a single uniform framework is genuinely useful. User-defined grade_dimension blocks with SMT-discharged laws is more ergonomic than Iris's Coq-embedded RA definitions. The example (MonotonicCounter with op=max) is a real PCM, not a toy.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) MonotonicCounter with op=max lacks inverse (not a group) — this is a join-semilattice, not a full PCM. FX calls it a 'custom PCM' but the combinators for fractional permissions above (share/unshare) don't exist for max. (2) 'Laws verified via SMT' is underspecified: which SMT theories? commutativity and associativity of user-defined ops are generally undecidable unless the op is linear. (3) No interaction with ghost-effect runtime erasure proof. (4) No support for history invariants (Iris's invariant namespace mechanism) or authoritative-fragmental RAs which are critical for non-trivial concurrency proofs.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Which SMT logic discharges grade_dimension law obligations? (2) Affirmative handling of partial PCMs (Frac with Validity). (3) Authoritative/fragmental pattern. (4) History monoid / view shift / update modality (Iris fancy update) for frame-preserving operations. (5) Invariant name/namespace management for shared ghost state. (6) Proof that erasure (ghost = 0) is sound in the presence of user-defined PCMs that might dereference ghost values.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add first-class support for Iris-style authoritative-fragmental pattern: `auth(A) * frag(B)` where only the full authoritative view can update. Provide standard library PCMs: FracCounter, Exclusive, Agreement, Authoritative, GSet. Specify the SMT fragment (QF_LIA is sufficient for most counters; ring-laws need QF_NIA). Tie ghost-erasure to a metatheorem that user-defined PCM-state never escapes to runtime.

analysis.what_fx_does: Users define domain-specific semirings via grade_dimension blocks; compiler verifies semiring laws via SMT; user-defined dimensions participate in the same grade-checking algorithm as the built-in twenty-one.

closest_prior_art.approach: Only existing general-purpose language where users can define arbitrary graded modalities (semirings) alongside built-ins; Z3 discharges constraints

closest_prior_art.system: Ghica & Smith 'Bounded Linear Types in a Resource Semiring'
closest_prior_art.approach: Type system parameterized by an arbitrary semiring with decision procedure for equational theory plugged in
User-defined effect handlers with row polymorphism; semantic composition rather than semiring parameterization

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Making user-defined grade dimensions first-class citizens alongside built-ins is a real advance — nothing other than Granule does this, and Granule is a research language. Shipping this in a production-intent language is ambitious.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The section is 14 lines. No discussion of: (1) Whether user-defined dimensions can reference built-in types (e.g., a dimension indexed by `list(a)` would need kind polymorphism); (2) Whether user-defined dimensions can be dependent (depend on runtime values — §6.7 gestures at this but doesn't connect); (3) Composition of user dimensions (two separate modules define the same grade_dimension name — what happens?); (4) Orphan-rule equivalent — who owns a user dimension?; (5) Metatheory: adding a user semiring changes preservation/progress proofs.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Kind of grade values (must be @[copy]? Must be decidable?). How SMT discharges user-defined law obligations when the op is arbitrary. How the grade-checker algorithm changes when a user adds a 20th dimension. Whether user-defined dimensions can be erased or must survive to runtime. Interaction with separate compilation (does adding a dimension require recompiling all callers?). What happens when two user dimensions conflict.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the kind system for grade_dimension declarations (e.g., must be of kind `Semiring`). Provide a small class-based interface: `instance Semiring for MyGrade`. Define orphan rules analogous to Haskell's (instances declared with the dimension OR with the function using it). Require a positive test case showing a novel user dimension (e.g., a rate limit) worked end-to-end. Cite Granule's implementation strategy.

analysis.what_fx_does: States that because FX is dependently typed, grades can be terms; example shows `pre n <= length(xs)` where the grade of xs depends on runtime value n.

closest_prior_art.approach: Formal dependent graded modal type theory: grades ARE terms in the theory; includes substitution lemma and mechanized metatheory in Agda
Dependent types + quantities; quantity is fixed {0,1,w}, not term-valued; no genuine dependent grades
Quantities are elements of a fixed semiring, NOT dependent on terms; grades are closed
Graded dependent type system; grades are annotations, not full dependent terms

analysis.where_fx_is_better: If FX genuinely supports grades-as-terms (like GrTT), it's beyond Idris 2 / Atkey QTT, both of which keep grades closed. This would be a real technical advance.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) The example given is NOT a dependent grade. `pre n <= length(xs)` is an ordinary refinement / precondition on the `n` parameter — it does not make the grade of `xs` depend on `n`. A true dependent grade example would be `xs :_{use_count(n)} list(a)` where the usage count is a function of n. This is a serious category error. (2) 4 lines of content with one mis-explained example. (3) 'Grade constraints involving runtime values discharged by SMT' — decidability of SMT over arbitrary terms is undecidable in general; must restrict to linear arithmetic or bit-vectors. (4) No mention of substitution lemma for dependent grades (which is the hard part in GrTT). (5) No interaction with context division — does G/p compute when p depends on bound variables?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) An actual dependent-grade example where a grade is a term, not a refinement. (2) Substitution lemma for dependent grades. (3) Decidable fragment of SMT that handles grade constraints. (4) Interaction with Lam rule (G/p when p is a term). (5) Termination requirement for grade terms (grades at type level must be computable). (6) Whether grades can eliminate into values (compute on grades).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Rewrite the section: drop the misleading example, give a proper dependent-grade example (a function taking a list with usage count that is a function of a parameter). Cite GrTT ESOP 2021 as direct prior art. State the substitution lemma as a goal theorem. Specify the SMT fragment (probably QF_LIA over grade terms). Connect to §6.2's Lam rule — what does G/p become when p is a term?

analysis.what_fx_does: Catalog of cross-dimension soundness guards. Nine compile-error codes (I002, I003, I004, L002, L003, E044, M012, N002, P002) for pairwise/triple interactions where one dimension's operations render another unsafe unless acknowledged. Three reductions documented (session × Fail via M011 + cancel primitive; classified × linear × Fail via §7.11 defer + §12.7 secure_zero; multiple Fail via §9.3 lattice join). Rules are stated as guards on the type system and checked at the site where dimensions meet.

closest_prior_art.approach: User-extensible graded modalities; no catalog of cross-modality interaction rules — each modality is independent; composition is left to the user
Information-flow type system with explicit declassification; treats exception payloads as flow sources (prior art for I002)
Effect rows compose via union; exception-as-effect pattern precedes FX's Fail; no classified/secret interaction
Borrow cannot be held across .await; FX L002 matches this rule but states it as dimension composition rather than inherent to future state machines
Constant-time codes forbid secret-dependent control flow; prior art for I003 (fail on secret in CT) and E044 (CT with async contradictory)
closest_prior_art.reference: Watt et al. POPL 2019; Cauligi et al. PLDI 2019; Almeida et al. CSF 2020

closest_prior_art.approach: Monotonic data structures with lattice merge; prior art for M012 (monotonic × concurrent needs atomic or lock-free)
Exceptional GV — session types with exception handlers and cancel primitive; prior art for #103 reduction

analysis.where_fx_is_better: No other graded-modal type system ships a catalog of cross-dimension composition rules. Granule leaves this to the user; Idris 2 QTT has only one dimension so no composition arises. FX is the first to say, 'when you combine any two of these twenty dimensions, here's what happens, and here's the error code if it's unsound.' The 10-entry table (9 new rules + 3 reductions to existing infrastructure) closes what would otherwise be O(190) pairwise interactions with a clear classification: genuine collision → new rule; composition of existing mechanisms → cite the mechanisms; lattice algebra → cite §9.3. This is 'mission-critical composes cleanly' as a design claim made concrete.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: (1) The rule set covers dimension pairs and triples from the initial gap audit (#102-114). The combinatorial space is C(20,2) = 190 pairs and C(20,3) = 1140 triples; we've covered 13. The argument that the rest reduce to existing infrastructure is plausible but not proved — there are very likely 2-3 more genuine collisions waiting to be found. (2) The I004 rule (classified × async × session) is stated as 'requires CT or declassify'; academic literature leaves the three-way combination as open research. FX's resolution is conservative but may be over-restrictive in practice. (3) Each rule is a compile error with a stated diagnostic, but no mechanized soundness proof exists yet — the argument that each rule prevents a specific unsoundness is intuitive rather than formal. (4) The distinction between 'new rule' (I002-P002) and 'reduction' (gap #103, #110, #111) is judgment-call; a strict reviewer could ask for reductions to have their own error codes for auditability.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Mechanized soundness proofs per rule. A systematic audit of the 190 dimension pairs (even a matrix showing which have been verified to be collision-free). A stress-test suite that tries to exploit each rule and verifies the compile error fires. Formal treatment of I004 — the academic literature has no complete solution, so FX's conservative rejection is a valid starting point but should be reviewable later.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Build the 20x20 composition matrix as an explicit appendix — for each pair, mark 'independent' (no collision), 'collision, rule Xxx', or 'reduction, see §Y.Z'. This would expose the remaining unclassified cells as future work. Add a fuzzer that generates pairs of operations across dimensions and checks the compiler correctly rejects or accepts. Cite each rule's formal source paper in the spec (currently citations live in gaps.json but not in §6.8 itself).

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter §7 specifies FX's ownership discipline: a 5-mode system (linear/affine/unrestricted/ref/ref mut) with implicit call-site borrowing, context-splitting semiring algebra, pattern-match ownership rules, @[copy] propagation, closure-capture inference, and defer-based cleanup.

closest_prior_art.approach: Affine-by-default with Copy opt-in, explicit &/&mut borrows at call sites, NLL/Polonius borrow checker
Strictly linear (use-exactly-once) with capability-based security, ~600 LOC checker
Mutable value semantics, no ownership annotations, parameter conventions (let/inout/sink/set)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unifies linear (default) AND affine AND unrestricted in one framework (Rust only has affine+Copy; Austral only linear; Hylo neither). Implicit borrow at call sites removes Rust's & clutter. Integrates cleanly with the §6 graded semiring so ownership is one instance of a uniform 21-dim checker.
