analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chapter is 243 lines — less than Rust's Ownership/Borrowing chapters plus NLL RFC plus Polonius doc combined. No specification of the borrow-check ALGORITHM (lexical? NLL? Polonius-style Datalog?). The §7 overview is a high-level feature tour; corner cases like partial moves, two-phase borrows, reborrow, lifetime subtyping under mode aren't detailed. Austral's spec is a 100-page PDF; FX §7 is not even close.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Borrow-check algorithm unspecified. (2) Interaction of five modes with lifetimes not tabulated. (3) No two-phase borrows. (4) No reborrow rules. (5) No partial-move specification. (6) No interaction with panic/divergent effects. (7) No statement of linearity soundness theorem.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Replace this overview-only chapter with a formal operational semantics of ownership (like Oxide/RustBelt), cite a specific borrow-check algorithm (Polonius-style is most modern), and state a soundness theorem: well-typed programs do not access dropped/moved values and consume linear values exactly once.

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines 5 modes (Linear default, Affine, Unrestricted via @[copy], Shared ref, Exclusive ref mut) in an 8-row table with usage/drop/copy columns; linearity is default, @[copy] propagates to all bindings, drop is explicit for linear via drop(x) or let _ = x.

closest_prior_art.approach: 3 modes: affine-move (default), Copy (unrestricted), references (borrow); no true linear type
2 modes: Linear (must-use-once), Free (unrestricted); borrows via capabilities
Multiplicity-polymorphic arrows (%1/%Many); linearity on arrows not types
1 mode (value semantics); parameter conventions let/inout/sink/set replace modes

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unlike Rust, FX has TRUE linear types (must-use-once), so resources like file handles are statically guaranteed to be closed — no Drop-leak escape hatch via mem::forget. Unlike Austral, FX adds affine as first-class mode so values with destructors get ergonomic drop-on-exit. Unified mode table in 8 rows is more readable than Rust's scattered docs.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: 5 modes is a LOT — Hylo argues one mode (value semantics) suffices; Rust shipped with 3 and developers still struggle. No justification for WHY 5 modes are needed beyond listing them. Linear-default is ergonomically punishing compared to Rust's affine-default — every integer-like type needs @[copy] which bleeds through generics (see Rust's own Copy-bound derivation problem). Austral went linear-only and still compiled real programs in ~600 LOC checker; FX adds Affine AND Unrestricted without showing the complexity pays off.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) No formal semiring mapping — table says @[copy]=unrestricted, ref=shared, but §6 Appendix C gives {0,1,w} with ref=w (shared) and ref mut=1 (exclusive). Mismatch: if ref mut is grade 1 (linear exclusive), it shouldn't be listed as 'any uses' in the §7.1 table. (2) What is @[copy] as a semiring element? (3) Is the Linear/Affine distinction just weakening-rule toggle, or a richer grade? (4) No row for Relevant (must-use-at-least-once).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Collapse to Linear-Affine-Unrestricted with borrow as an orthogonal capability (ref, ref mut), like Rust separates Copy/Move from &/&mut. Or adopt Hylo-style conventions (let/inout/sink) which avoid mode-annotation at binding sites entirely. Document the exact semiring mapping and remove the Mode/Grade inconsistency between §7.1 and §6.3 Appendix C.

analysis.what_fx_does: A parameter with no mode annotation takes ownership; the value must be consumed exactly once (pass on, return, or drop); using after consumption or not consuming at all is a compile error.

closest_prior_art.approach: Bare T parameter MOVES (affine — drop implicit if unused); no 'use required'
Linear params — must be consumed exactly once; compiler enforces via linear type checker
No ownership annotation; implicit copy/move based on rvalue reference rules

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Stricter than Rust (must-use-exactly-once; no accidental leak-by-dropping). Explicit drop(x) forces the programmer to acknowledge a leak site, giving auditors a grep-able token — Austral does this too but FX matches.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Single-paragraph specification. Rust's move semantics have entire RFCs and chapters (RFC 2229, chapter 4 of The Book, Rustonomicon, RustBelt papers). No description of: how move interacts with panic unwinding, partial moves from structs/enums, reborrow semantics. Austral's linear checker paper is thousands of lines; FX's is 4 lines.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) What happens if a linear value flows through a divergent expression? Is it 'consumed' by Div/Exn? (2) Partial-moves from records — if I destructure let (a, b) = pair and use a but not b, does b leak or error? (3) No mention of reborrow from owned. (4) What's the interaction with async suspension — can a linear owned value be stored in a future?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand with: semantics for consume-through-panic (likely: fail effect consumes all in-scope linears), partial-move state tracking (like Rust's MIR init flags), async-storage rules, and a formal 'consumed-exactly-once' theorem.

analysis.what_fx_does: Two borrow forms: ref (shared, duplicable, read-only) and ref mut (exclusive, read-write); both cannot coexist simultaneously; overlapping mutable borrows rejected.

closest_prior_art.approach: Non-lexical lifetimes based on CFG liveness, MIR-based borrow checker
Datalog-based next-gen borrow checker handling conditional returns
Formal model of Rust borrow checker with regions and loans (arXiv 1903.00982)
Law of Exclusivity via inout — no lifetime annotations, dynamic detection at call sites

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Matches Rust's safety invariant (shared-XOR-exclusive) with less syntax clutter since call-sites are auto. Hylo-like simplicity at the surface but with static checking unlike Hylo's Law-of-Exclusivity (which is partly dynamic).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: CRITICAL GAP: No borrow-check ALGORITHM specified. Rust's borrow checker spec is the NLL RFC (several hundred pages) + Polonius (Datalog rules). FX just says 'compiler rejects overlapping mutable borrows' — this is the HARD PART of ownership systems. Without specifying NLL vs lexical vs Polonius, nobody can implement the language consistently. Mutable aliasing patterns like get_default (returning from conditional branches) are the well-known edge case where NLL fails but Polonius succeeds; FX is silent.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Borrow-check algorithm choice. (2) Lifetime inference rules for local refs. (3) Two-phase borrow support (needed for method calls that take &mut self and consume an argument). (4) Reborrow rules (ref mut x → can you pass ref x temporarily?). (5) Interior mutability story (ref(mut) behind shared ref?). (6) Interaction with §8 region/lifetime system is cursorily mentioned but not formalized.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Polonius-style Datalog formulation and cite it explicitly. State the set of loan/origin facts and the 'subset' and 'liveness' rules. Add conditional-return patterns to acceptance tests. Without this, §7.3 is unimplementable.

analysis.what_fx_does: Affine mode: value may be used at most once but dropping is permitted; suits resources with destructors; illustrated with if-then consume_token where token is dropped (destructor runs) on the else branch.

closest_prior_art.approach: All non-Copy types are affine by default; Drop runs on out-of-scope
Affine = exchange+weakening; linear = exchange only; formal characterization
Linear only, no affine; every value must be explicitly consumed

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Makes affine an EXPLICIT opt-in, so the default (linear) gives Austral-style safety while `affine` keyword recovers Rust-style ergonomics when destructors are appropriate. This is better than Rust (where affine is default and linear is inaccessible) and better than Austral (where linear is default and affine is inaccessible).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Section is 10 lines. Does NOT address the well-known hard question: what happens when a destructor fails? (Borretti's 'Linear Types and Exceptions' covers this in depth.) How does affine-drop interact with panic unwinding? With the Fail effect? Is destructor called when Div effect body diverges? Rust has entire RFCs on Drop ordering, DropCk, PhantomData — FX has two sentences.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Drop-ordering semantics (LIFO? declaration order?). (2) Destructor signature — does it take self (consume) or &mut self (Rust-style)? (3) Can destructor fail? If yes, where does the error go? (4) DropCk — do destructors see potentially-dangling borrows? (5) Interaction with async — Drop in async function mid-suspend? (6) Explicit 'ManuallyDrop' equivalent?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Austral's approach to exception-free destructors (Borretti's design), specify drop order (matching §7.11 defer LIFO), specify destructor signature as `fn drop(own self) : unit with <effects>`, and forbid panic-in-destructor with a compile check.

analysis.what_fx_does: Pattern match consumes the scrutinee; bound variables receive ownership of parts; three rules: match on linear consumes + arms own parts, match on ref borrows + arms get ref, match on @[copy] copies.

closest_prior_art.approach: Automatic binding mode inference for refs — non-reference patterns on a reference auto-deref and bind-by-ref
Pattern can destructure with some fields moved, others borrowed via `ref`; compiler tracks field-level init state
Pattern match on linear value consumes all parts; can't mix ref and own in one pattern

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Clean 3-rule formulation covers the common cases without Rust's auto-ergonomics (which took an RFC to figure out). Matches Austral's linear-consumption semantics.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: CRITICAL: no treatment of PARTIAL MOVES. If scrutinee is `(a: Linear, b: Linear)` and match binds only `(x, _)`, what happens to `_` — is it dropped, forgotten, error? Rust's answer (field-level init tracking) is intricate and took NLL to get right (see issue #16223). §7.5 doesn't say. Also no treatment of guards that reference moved bindings (Rust's RFC 107 'Pattern Guards with Bind by Move'). No mention of or-patterns with mixed modes (a Linear in one arm and Ref in another).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Partial move semantics. (2) Guard semantics over moved bindings. (3) Or-pattern mode unification. (4) Nested pattern through Box/ref indirection. (5) Whether @[copy] field of linear type gets copied inside a linear-scrutinee match. (6) Match on a ref mut — does the arm receive ref mut on parts?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify pattern match as a formal translation to a core calculus with explicit drop/copy/move insertion. Add the four missing rules above. Look at how Austral handles this (strictly no partial moves allowed) for the simplest spec.

analysis.what_fx_does: When callee's parameter is `ref`, the caller does NOT write `ref` at the call site; the compiler borrows automatically; for linear parameters, the caller transfers ownership. Callee's mode alone determines behavior.

closest_prior_art.approach: Explicit & and &mut at call sites; no auto-ref for arguments, only for method receivers
`&` prefix required ONLY for inout (mutable) args; let/sink inferred
inout/ref keyword required at call site (Swift: &x; C#: ref x)
Fully implicit — pass-by-reference is invisible at call site, known source of bugs

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Less syntactic noise than Rust — no `&` / `&mut` clutter at call sites. Safer than C++ (compiler enforces linearity). More uniform than Hylo (which makes inout special-case).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: IMPLICIT BORROW IS DANGEROUS. C++ teaches: hiding reference-vs-move at call sites is a significant readability and correctness hazard (see 'Safe C++' draft — they explicitly argue for explicit `&` for this reason). Swift had to introduce capture lists because implicit capture led to retain cycles. Reviewing `send(ref conn, data)` vs `send(conn, data)` — is `send` receiving a reference or consuming? Depends on the declaration, which reviewer must chase. Rust deliberately rejected this design.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Resolution rules when a type has methods for both `self` and `ref self` (see Rust's method resolution ambiguity — Rust lists strict precedence rules; FX just defers to §16.3). (2) What if a function takes `ref mut` and caller has `ref` already bound? (3) Auto-reborrow rules. (4) Interaction with overloading (§25 no-overload rule helps, but not enough). (5) Diff from pipe: does `x |> f` move x or borrow x based on f's first param mode?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Keep the ergonomics but add mandatory reader disambiguation: for linear-param call sites, require a visible marker (e.g., `move` or `consume x`) so code review can see consumption. This is what modern C++ does with std::move and why it aids readability. Alternatively, surface consumption in IDE hints but keep the surface clean — document the IDE contract.

analysis.what_fx_does: Five-line section gives three semiring addition rules (0+0=0, 1+0=1, 1+1=w-error-for-linear) governing how contexts split when a function uses multiple owned values.

closest_prior_art.approach: Full graded context splitting — bidirectional rules with input/output contexts, distributive laws for graded modalities
Corrected Lam rule with context division operator; cited by FX in §6.2
Quantitative Type Theory with context addition/scaling; flawed Lam rule FX claims to fix

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Nothing concrete — §7.7 is a summary of §6.1, not independent work.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Section is FIVE LINES. Granule's context-splitting is the heart of its contribution — multiple papers, a PhD thesis, distributive laws, bidirectional check/infer modes with split input/output contexts. FX's §7.7 is a sidebar. The core question of context-splitting for the 5-mode system (how does affine+Copy interact? how does Ref split? what's the distributive law for ref into sums?) is not even hinted at.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Rules for splitting under ref and ref mut. (2) Context division for @[copy] (it's the 'w' grade but dividing by w is context-erasure). (3) Interaction with dependent grades (§6.7). (4) Split rules across pattern-match branches (join vs add). (5) Split across effects. (6) Context split for session-typed channels (can't split a linear channel two ways).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Either delete this section (redundant with §6) or expand to a formal specification matching Granule's rigor — include split rules for every mode, pattern-match join rules, worked examples, and a soundness statement.

analysis.what_fx_does: A type is @[copy] iff all its components are @[copy]; checked at definition site; shown with record, enum, and generic examples (option(i64) is @[copy]; option(connection) is linear).

closest_prior_art.approach: Auto-derive adds T: Copy bound for generics; shared refs are unconditionally Copy; Drop and Copy mutually exclusive
Derived via deriving clause; controls unboxed representation
Compiler-inferred predicate; controls memcpy-safety

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Propagation rule is SIMPLE and closed — no subtrait gymnastics, no Clone-as-supertrait complication. FX treats @[copy] as a closed invariant on the type tree. Checking at definition site gives clearer error messages than Rust's Copy bound errors buried in monomorphization.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Rust's Copy has subtleties FX must also handle: (1) &T is Copy regardless of T — is FX's `ref T` @[copy]? Silent. (2) Drop/Copy exclusion — if T has a destructor (affine), is option(T) @[copy]? No. (3) PhantomData allowing manual Copy override. (4) Generic auto-derive doesn't always match user intent (Rust's derive(Copy) adds unwanted T: Copy bounds). FX doesn't address whether propagation is strict or customizable.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Is ref T / ref mut T copyable? (2) Can user manually mark @[copy] and escape propagation (like Rust's impl Copy for Wrapper<T>)? (3) Interaction with destructor (affine type with drop handler) — cannot be @[copy]? (4) Interaction with classified/secret types — should secret T be @[copy]? (5) Subtyping: can option(@[copy] i64) be used where option(T) is expected with T linear?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add the Rust rules explicitly: (a) ref T always @[copy], ref mut T never, (b) types with destructors never, (c) user cannot override automatic propagation for soundness. Or alternatively allow manual opt-out like Haskell (needed for PhantomData-like markers).

analysis.what_fx_does: Eight-line section showing ownership + effects compose in a function signature; `fn upload_and_close(conn: connection, ref data: buffer) : unit with IO`; the signature conveys: conn consumed, data borrowed, IO performed, terminates.

closest_prior_art.approach: Effect system with borrow annotations (koka borrow) integrated in function signatures
Capabilities (effects-like) and linear types both in function signatures
No effects; ownership modes only at parameter level

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Signature carries modes, effects, pre/post simultaneously — more information-dense than Rust's signatures or Austral's.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: This 'section' is a 10-line cross-reference paragraph, not a specification. The actual integration rules (how does a ref value interact with Fail effect — is the borrow alive across the fail? How do Async effects reorder accesses? How does Div interact with linearity?) are not discussed here OR in §9. Rust's handling of unwinding-and-borrows is subtle; FX is silent.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Fail effect + linear value: is the linear consumed or leaked? (2) Async suspension — can a borrow cross suspension points? (Rust forbids this for Send futures.) (3) Div effect — does it consume in-scope linears? (4) Handler continuations (§9.7) that capture linears — one-shot vs multi-shot. (5) Effect operation that 'consumes' a linear via the handler.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Merge §7.9 into §9 and actually specify the interactions, especially (a) fail + linear → compile-time leak error unless destructor provided, (b) one-shot continuations can close over linears, multi-shot cannot, (c) async borrows cannot cross await.

analysis.what_fx_does: Closure capture mode depends on captured variables: capturing @[copy] gives @[copy] closure, capturing linear gives linear closure (callable at most once); compiler infers borrow vs move per variable by usage; returned closures holding borrows cannot outlive them.

closest_prior_art.approach: Closures Capture Disjoint Fields — precise per-field capture, 2021 edition stabilization
Three function traits based on closure's access pattern (by ref, by mut ref, by value)
Explicit [weak self]/[unowned self]/[var = expr] lists; escaping closures require explicit self
Context division under Lam rule — erases linears from replicable closures

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Grounded in a correct Lam rule (Wood/Atkey 2022) which FX fixes from Atkey 2018. Rust needed YEARS of RFCs (RFC 2229 took 2017-2021). FX's single rule (linear-in, linear-out) covers the common case.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: SIGNIFICANTLY underspecified relative to Rust/Swift. Rust has Fn/FnMut/FnOnce TRAIT HIERARCHY that FX doesn't replicate — is a linear-consuming closure different in TYPE from a shared-capture closure? If both are `fn() -> unit`, how does the caller know which? Swift requires explicit capture lists to reason about retain cycles and escaping; FX has nothing. RFC 2229 specifically was about PER-FIELD precise capture — FX doesn't say if captures are whole-variable or per-field. Inference of move-vs-borrow 'based on how the closure uses it' is underspecified — what about branches where one branch consumes and another doesn't?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Closure type hierarchy — is there an equivalent of Fn/FnMut/FnOnce encoded in modes? (2) Per-field vs whole-variable capture (RFC 2229). (3) Explicit capture lists (Swift) — would help reviewer understand closure ownership. (4) Lifetime inference for returned closures — §7.10 hints but doesn't specify. (5) Capture semantics for mutually recursive closures. (6) async closures (Rust's active design area).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt per-field capture (RFC 2229 is a hindsight gift — start there). Add explicit capture-list syntax `fn[weak = conn]() => ...` for escaping/stored closures. Specify that closure type encodes capture modes (so the caller's linearity is preserved). Cross-reference with §7.3 for borrow-check algorithm.

analysis.what_fx_does: `defer` registers cleanup for scope exit; deferred value remains live and BORROWABLE until scope exits at which point ownership transfers to the defer frame; LIFO execution order; between defer and exit value can be borrowed but not moved.

closest_prior_art.approach: Block-scoped defer (always runs) and errdefer (runs only on error); explicit, no compile-time enforcement of leak-freedom
Function-scoped defer; LIFO; cannot be scoped to blocks
Block-scoped defer; runs on scope exit regardless of cause; no linear-type interaction
Static, type-based; no defer — RAII replaces it; unwind-safe with panic

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Combines Zig-style explicit defer with linear-type enforcement — the compiler can STATICALLY verify that defer registrations exist for every linear value (unlike Zig where errdefer omission leaks silently). LIFO ordering is consistent with Zig/Swift. Borrow-while-deferred is more flexible than Rust's RAII (which consumes at drop-point only).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: GAP #101 is known: defer + linear + fail path interaction underspecified. Section says nothing about what happens if fail() fires between `defer close(handle)` and scope exit — does close() run? Zig's whole point of distinguishing defer/errdefer is that sometimes you WANT different cleanup on error. FX has only one defer — conflates success and error paths. If a Fail effect is raised, is the deferred expression still run? If it has effects of its own, can those conflict? Rust's Drop semantics are simpler (always runs on unwind) but FX's linear-plus-defer-plus-fail is a novel combination that isn't specified.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Fail/Exn path — does defer run? (gap #101). (2) Panic/Div path — does defer run? (3) errdefer-analog for error-only cleanup. (4) Can defer register a partial-consume (e.g., defer a function that takes ref)? (5) Multiple defers in same scope — interleaved execution with other statements? (6) Defer across async suspension — does it fire on task cancellation? (7) Defer capturing the linear value: is the deferred closure linear (callable once) or re-callable?
analysis.could_we_do_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 3] Verdict raised from 'adequate' to 'strong' after Proposal 3 adopted a Zig-style defer/errdefer split in §7.11. Two forms: 'defer expr;' always runs at scope exit, 'errdefer expr;' runs only on Fail/Exn abort exits; both LIFO within the nearest enclosing lexical block. Defer bodies are restricted to the enclosing function's effect row minus Fail/Exn (preventing double-fault during unwinding). Rule G-Linear-Cleanup-On-Fail demands defer/errdefer for every linear binding live at every Fail abort site, keyed on liveness rather than mechanism. Closes gap #101. Interaction with §13.3 inverse transitions specified: local cleanup first, then machine-level compensation. Earlier analysis preserved below.

Adopt Zig's two-track system: `defer` (always) + `errdefer` (error-only). Require every linear value to have an associated defer (or explicit consume). Specify fail-path behavior explicitly (most sensible: defers run in reverse before unwinding past scope). Add `defer |err|` capture for error-conditional cleanup. Close gap #101.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for §8 Memory, covering region-based lifetimes, compiler-selected allocation strategies, a proved allocator, representation inference, and explicit layout control.

closest_prior_art.approach: Lifetimes + borrow checker without allocation strategy selection; programmer picks Box/Rc/Arc explicitly
Full region inference with automatic allocation strategy
closest_prior_art.reference: Tofte and Talpin, 'Region-based memory management', Information and Computation 1997

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unifies region inference (MLKit-style), ownership (Rust-style), niche/SoA/hot-cold layout (HotSpot/Jai-style), and a proved allocator — no other language integrates all five. Integrates platform availability (GPU/FPGA) as a hard compile-time constraint.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chapter is compact (5 subsections, ~155 lines total) vs. the depth Rust/MLKit devote to comparable topics. Several subsections (8.3 proved allocator, 8.4 SoA auto-detection) stake claims that would demand major research contributions, while the chapter does not cite any existing proof artifact or compiler pass.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No cross-region dataflow rules; no discussion of region polymorphism (critical in MLKit); no memory model under weak consistency; no story for pinning (moving GC is absent but pinning semantics matter for DMA/FFI); no discussion of region-reference covariance subtleties caught by Oxide/Polonius.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add explicit region-polymorphism section; specify which subset of MLKit's Tofte/Talpin soundness theorems FX inherits; specify interaction with §7 linearity (who wins when a linear value crosses regions); provide a soundness theorem statement for the combined region+linear system akin to Oxide (Weiss et al.).

analysis.what_fx_does: References carry explicit region parameters on pub fn; lifetime subtyping r1 outlives r2 implies ref(r1,T) <: ref(r2,T); local lifetimes are inferred; region-scoped allocation via with_arena frees all at once; static outlives all.

closest_prior_art.approach: Lifetimes are annotated with 'a syntax; Non-Lexical Lifetime inference; Polonius reframes as origin-based alias tracking. Niko Matsakis's 2012 insight was annotating borrowed refs with lifetimes rather than allocation regions.
Stack regions, lexical regions, heap H, dynamic regions; region subtyping; region-polymorphic functions; about 6% of 8% C-to-Cyclone edits were region annotations
closest_prior_art.reference: Grossman, Morrisett, Jim, Hicks, Wang, Cheney 'Region-Based Memory Management in Cyclone', PLDI 2002

closest_prior_art.approach: Full Tofte/Talpin region inference — no annotations at all; space leaks in 'global region' forced eventual GC coexistence
closest_prior_art.reference: Hallenberg, Elsman, Tofte 'Combining region inference and garbage collection', PLDI 2002

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Explicit-on-pub / inferred-locally is a pragmatic compromise — Rust demands explicit on many local fns, MLKit makes everything inferred and suffers space leaks. with_arena is cleaner than Rust's typed_arena third-party crate.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Far thinner than Rust's treatment: no variance story for ref(r,T) (covariant? invariant for mut?), no mention of higher-ranked lifetime bounds (for<'r>), no region polymorphism syntax (though Cyclone showed this is essential for reusable utilities). 'Local inference' is exactly what Rust/Oxide needed 500+ pages of formalism to get right — FX gives one sentence. Polonius's origin-based formulation is not even referenced.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Variance rules for ref(r,T), ref mut(r,T); lifetime elision rules (who gets fresh 'r when signature is ambiguous); interaction with existential types (§16.5); higher-ranked lifetime quantification (required for callbacks); escape analysis for region inference (MLKit's historical pain point); no soundness theorem statement or reference.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Oxide's region-as-set-of-locations semantics and cite it; spell out variance; add a region-polymorphic fn example; specify whether FX uses NLL-style flow-sensitive regions or Rust 2015 lexical lifetimes; state the interaction with §7 (ownership subtyping vs lifetime subtyping composition).

analysis.what_fx_does: No GC; compiler picks from Stack/Region/Slab/Pool/Rc based on escape analysis and lifetime; programmer can override with Alloc(Stack)/Alloc(Region('r))/Alloc(Rc); platform table restricts available strategies (GPU=Stack+Region, FPGA=Stack only) as compile errors.

closest_prior_art.approach: Explicit allocator interface with vtable (alloc/resize/free); no compiler-selected heap allocation — programmer picks FixedBufferAllocator/ArenaAllocator/GeneralPurposeAllocator/PageAllocator; 'no hidden allocations'
Compiler-selected region allocation via Tofte/Talpin inference; historical evidence that purely inferred region allocation leaks space 2x-150x without GC coexistence
Escape analysis moves stack allocations implicitly; Box/Rc/Arc are explicit heap choices; custom allocator_api still unstable after many years

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Cleaner user-facing model than Zig — user doesn't pass allocators through every API boundary. Platform restriction as a compile error (GPU/FPGA) is genuinely rare; CUDA/OpenCL only catch heap misuse at kernel-compile-time with vendor diagnostics. Exposes override knob (with Alloc(X)) which MLKit lacked.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: MLKit's hard-won lesson is that purely compiler-selected allocation leaks space badly on non-nested lifetimes and higher-order code — FX's 5-strategy pick model doesn't address this failure mode. Zig/Rust's explicit allocator-as-value pattern interoperates better with custom allocators per data structure. 'Slab' and 'Size-classed pool' need runtime state the compiler must link in; not free as implied by the table.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: How does the compiler choose between Slab and Pool at compile time? Cost of Rc vs Arc (single vs multi-threaded) not distinguished. No mention of GPU-specific shared/local/global memory spaces (huge semantic differences in CUDA/OpenCL/HIP). How do user-defined allocators plug in (vs Zig's vtable)? What about TLS/thread-local slab pressure? Fragmentation model.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Zig-style explicit allocator-as-value AT THE MACHINE interface while keeping the inference at the source level — best of both. Document the Tofte/Talpin space-leak pathology and state how FX avoids it (presumably via linear-type consumption acting as early-free). Split Rc into Rc/Arc with concurrency dimension as selector.

analysis.what_fx_does: Allocator is written in FX, bootstraps from mmap/fixed page, and proves four properties: no-aliasing (produces own pointers), alignment, slab-slot-free-XOR-allocated, thread-safety via disjoint per-thread caches.

closest_prior_art.approach: Kernel exports allocation policy to userspace; capability derivation tree enforces 'all derived caps removed before retype'; the allocator mechanism is formally verified in Isabelle/HOL
closest_prior_art.reference: Klein et al., 'seL4: Formal Verification of an OS Kernel', CACM 2010

closest_prior_art.approach: Formalizes TLSF dynamic real-time allocator with functional correctness + global memory invariants
closest_prior_art.reference: Springer LNCS 2019, 'A Verified Specification of TLSF Memory Management Allocator'

closest_prior_art.approach: Memory-aware verified compiler; CompCert's infinite-memory model is insufficient — CompCertS extends it to allow allocation failure and concrete memory consumption
closest_prior_art.reference: Besson et al., 'CompCertS: A Memory-Aware Verified C Compiler', hal-02401182

analysis.where_fx_is_better: If realized, an end-to-end proved allocator in the implementation language itself (not Isabelle/Coq/HOL shim) would be novel. seL4 pushes policy out; FX claims to verify the mechanism in-language. Replaceability via interface contract is cleaner than ad-hoc verification glue.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: 24 lines is handwave for what is a multi-year PhD effort — seL4's allocator proof is thousands of Isabelle lines, TLSF verification is one of the few verified real-time allocators and took a major project. 'Proves four properties' is not a specification — no Hoare triple, no separation-logic statement, no frame rule interaction with §6.4/§7. 'Thread safety via disjoint caches' glosses over memory ordering — which parts use seq_cst/acquire/release?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Which prover/SMT handles this? Functional correctness (does allocate return a pointer covering the requested size?) vs safety (no double-free). Bounded allocation failure semantics (vs CompCert's infinite memory). TLB/cache coherence interaction. Interaction with §11.10 memory ordering. Exhaustion behavior (return null or trap?). Interaction with mmap failure. Thread local cache eviction policy.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Cite CertiKOS and VST and state which subset of their separation-logic-with-concurrent-permissions lemmas FX consumes. Specify the allocator interface as a contract (tie to §14) with pre/post/decreases. Prove the four properties in §10-style verify blocks in the spec itself. State the fault model (when mmap fails).

analysis.what_fx_does: Compiler picks physical representation from type info: tagged-pointer packing (low-3-bits + top-16 bits on 64-bit), niche optimization (nat{x>0}, bits(3)), bit packing in records, 32-bit compressed pointers within regions, zero-sized types, automatic SoA from field-access pattern, hot/cold splitting.

closest_prior_art.approach: Sophisticated niche optimization: Option<&T>, Option<NonZeroU32>, Option<bool>, Option<char> all zero-overhead; computes two layouts and picks niche if strictly smaller; field reordering for niche-at-end; rustc_layout_scalar_valid_range_start attribute
struct SOA vs struct keyword toggle lets compiler auto-convert AoS↔SoA; inspired Go #64926 proposal and exists in Odin (#soa[10]Foo) and ISPC (soa<Foo>)
Structure peeling splits struct {hotA,coldB,hotC} → Hot{A,C} + Cold{B}; machine function splitter gives ~2.33% end-to-end runtime improvement via icache/TLB
closest_prior_art.reference: LLVM 2019 dev meeting 'Hot Cold Splitting Optimization Pass In LLVM'

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX attempts automatic SoA from dot-shorthand usage patterns — no mainstream compiler does this (Jai is closed-beta and commentators note the feature isn't fully implemented). Combines all five layout optimizations under one coherent story. Tagged pointer packing + niche + compressed pointers + SoA + hot/cold splitting in a compiler-default position is rare.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Automatic AoS→SoA changes the physical type — FFI breaks, pointer-into-struct breaks, methods that take ref to the whole record break. Rust's niche optimization needed multiple RFCs and years of design just for enums with data-carrying variants; FX describes niche in one paragraph. Compressed 32-bit pointers with region-base are excellent for array-of-tree but costly for small hot-path reads (one add per deref); FX doesn't discuss when to disable. Hot/cold splitting requires PGO data — FX doesn't say where frequency information comes from.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: How is 'field-at-a-time access pattern' detected statically (whole-program analysis)? What's the escape hatch when SoA breaks an invariant? Interaction with FFI (repr(C)) — if the compiler auto-SoA's a struct, the C view is wrong. Compressed pointer deref cost per operation. Hot/cold threshold (how many accesses = hot?). No PGO hook mentioned.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Treat layout as an effect/dimension grant so user opts in rather than surprise. Cite Rust's detailed niche algorithm (two-layout compute + reorder-for-niche-at-end). For SoA, adopt the Jai/Odin syntactic marker instead of auto-detection — safer and matches existing industry practice.

analysis.what_fx_does: Explicit layout via repr(Native/C/packed/aligned(n)/big_endian/transparent) attribute on types, used at FFI boundaries and wire formats.

closest_prior_art.approach: #[repr(Rust/C/packed(n)/transparent/align(N)/u*/i*)] set is exactly what FX mirrors; packed has well-known UB hazards (unaligned refs are UB); transparent is single-field ABI passthrough
extern struct (C ABI) and packed struct (BIT-level layout with arbitrary-width ints like u12, u1 inside packed) — packed is strictly more powerful than Rust's byte-level packed
Longstanding pragma-driven FFI control with all the associated platform-specific footguns

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Includes repr(big_endian) as a first-class annotation — Rust and Zig require manual swap on read/write or third-party crates. Consistent with §18 bit formats for wire-level control. One unified attribute surface.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Rust's repr(packed) famously causes UB when taking &field — FX does not specify what happens when you create a ref to an unaligned field inside repr(packed). Zig's packed struct is bit-level with arbitrary-width ints — far more expressive than FX's byte-level packed attribute. No repr(u*) primitive for enum discriminant size control (critical for FFI with C enums). repr(transparent) mentioned but rules (non-zero-size, non-align-1 requirement) not specified.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Alignment-down vs bit-level packing distinction. What happens on ref/ref mut to packed field (UB? compile error? auto-copy?). Enum discriminant size control. Union types with repr. Compiler-computed vs programmer-computed offsets. ABI attribute for calling convention (extern C vs extern system-v vs extern stdcall).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Zig's bit-level packed struct semantics (since §18 already has bits(n) this should naturally unify). Make ref-to-packed-field a compile error not UB (FX has no unsafe, so UB isn't an option anyway). Add repr(u8)/repr(u16)/etc. for discriminant control. Specify transparent's zero-size/alignment rules.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for §9 Effects, covering declared-with-with annotations, effect variables (polymorphism), join-semilattice, built-in effect set, user-defined effects/handlers, one/multi-shot continuations, generators as Yield sugar, async/await via Async + task_group, and platform-restricted effect sets.

closest_prior_art.approach: Row-polymorphic effect types with duplicate labels, Tot/Exn/Div/Pure/Ndet/St/IO hierarchy, evidence-passing compilation
closest_prior_art.reference: Leijen 'Koka: Programming with Row Polymorphic Effect Types', Microsoft Research 2014

closest_prior_art.approach: Untyped algebraic effect handlers with one-shot continuations implemented as fibers; effects not tracked in function types
Capability-passing semantics with handler specialization; lightweight effect polymorphism
closest_prior_art.reference: Brachthäuser et al. 'Effekt: Lightweight Effect Polymorphism for Handlers', OOPSLA 2020

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Integrates effects with the 18 other dimensions (security/linearity/precision/etc.); platform-conditional effects (GPU/FPGA) as a hard compile-time restriction is novel; Alloc-as-effect ties allocation into the effect system rather than leaving it ambient like Koka.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Uses lattice rather than rows (Koka's experience was explicitly that subtyping was problematic → switched to rows); chapter commits to one-shot-by-default which is correct for concurrency but limits non-determinism/backtracking idioms; no mention of evidence-passing / CPS compilation strategy.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Compilation strategy for handlers (Koka uses evidence passing, Effekt specializes, OCaml uses fibers — FX says nothing). Interaction with linear types in handler continuations. Effect subtyping beyond Read<=Write lattice.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Pick a compilation model explicitly and cite (Koka evidence-passing seems best fit given FX's static emphasis); reconsider rows vs lattice tradeoff; add a Pure = Exn+Div shortcut like Koka.

analysis.what_fx_does: No annotation = Tot (pure+terminating); effects declared with 'with E1, E2...' after return type; pub fn must declare all effects; body's effects must be ⊆ declared; effects never inferred upward; rec without decreases must declare Div; left-to-right argument evaluation.

closest_prior_art.approach: No annotation = total; effects are tracked automatically with full inference (not just checked); Tot/Exn/Div/Pure/Ndet/St/IO hierarchy built-in
IO effect via monad, other effects via monad transformers or mtl-style classes — not a first-class effect system
Exceptions declared with 'throws' in signature; widely regretted usability precedent for mandatory effect annotation on API boundaries

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Explicit left-to-right eval order is strongly specified (C/C++ historically UB; Java/C# are left-to-right; this matches Java). 'Effects never inferred upward' avoids Koka's row inference being unpredictable for agents. rec+decreases OR Div is a clean decision forced at definition site.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Mandatory effect annotation on every pub fn is exactly Java checked exceptions' usability failure mode — decades of evidence that mandatory annotations on public API create annotation fatigue. Koka's choice to infer automatically (even on API) gives better DX. No effect inference for private functions either (spec says 'effects never inferred upward into signature' for ALL fns, including private?). Missing a Pure = Exn+Div alias.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Effect subtyping at call site (can I pass Tot fn where IO expected?). Interaction with closures — does a closure need an effect annotation? What about lambdas? Effect for memory access (Read/Write of ref) — is it automatic from mode system or explicit? 'ref arg' — does that add Read to the caller's effect?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Allow effect inference on non-pub fns (Rust does this for type inference only on fn bodies). Add a Pure alias per Koka. Specify effect requirements for lambdas in closures. Make Alloc effect automatic when the compiler chooses heap allocation via §8.2 (otherwise user must manually declare Alloc every time stack analysis fails).

analysis.what_fx_does: Effect variables for polymorphism: fn map<a,b,eff:effect>(f: a->b with eff, xs: list(a)) : list(b) with eff; row extension 'with eff, IO' adds IO to whatever eff is.

closest_prior_art.approach: Row polymorphism with duplicate labels — f: a -> b -> <e|ε>; rows are first-class types that can be extended/restricted
Bidirectional effect typing — no explicit effect variables, the type-checker propagates effect context
closest_prior_art.reference: Lindley, McBride, McLaughlin 'Do Be Do Be Do', arXiv 1611.09259

closest_prior_art.approach: Capability-passing with lightweight polymorphism — quantifies over effect rows but avoids 'startled user' of Koka/Frank/Helium sugar

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Syntax 'eff: effect' makes the effect-kind explicit, clearer than Koka's implicit row variables. Extension syntax 'with eff, IO' is readable. Inference example (filter adopts the caller's effect) is standard.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chapter is 32 lines — doesn't describe unification, doesn't explain how duplicate effects combine (Koka's duplicate-label trick is non-trivial), doesn't address whether rows or sets (FX calls it lattice earlier, but polymorphism examples look row-like). 'with eff, IO' — what if eff already contains IO? In rows you get two IO labels with different semantic effects (Koka's exn-chain); in lattices you get idempotence. Spec doesn't clarify. Frank's critique of row-polymorphism syntactic sugar ('masquerade often breaks down in sophisticated use cases') applies.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: What structure does 'effect' variable range over — set, row, lattice? Unification rules. Subsumption at call site for effect variables. Rank-1 vs rank-N effect polymorphism (impredicativity). Can an effect variable appear in a higher-kinded position (effect of effect)? Koka-style scoped effect rows.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Commit explicitly to row-polymorphism semantics (if so, cite Leijen's duplicate-label trick) OR to subset-lattice semantics (simpler but limits ability to distinguish 'catches one kind of exn' vs 'catches all exn'). Add a worked example where the choice between row/lattice matters semantically (handler that catches only one exception instance).

analysis.what_fx_does: Effects form a bounded join-semilattice under set union: Tot is identity; join is idempotent, commutative; Read <= Write is the sole subeffect among built-ins. Proposal 13 additions: (1) explicit 'effect-row-as-union' paragraph clarifying 'with e1, e2, eff' denotes the set {e1, e2} ∪ eff with order / duplication immaterial; (2) Fail-unique-per-row rule — surface 'with Fail(E1), Fail(E2)' is compile error E045 (combine into Fail(E1 | E2)), though the lattice internally normalizes Fail(E1) \/ Fail(E2) = Fail(E1 | E2) when composing effects across call sites; (3) user-declared subsumption edges via 'subsumes X' inside effect declarations integrate into lattice subsumption — E046 on undeclared subsumption. Effect subtyping: e1 <= e2 iff e1 \/ e2 = e2.

closest_prior_art.approach: Rejected effect subtyping explicitly — 'an earlier version of Koka used subtyping over effects, but in practice the constraints often became quite complex and the combination of polymorphism with subtyping can make type inference undecidable'
Lattice-structured effects are used in some DSLs where partial order matters (capability lattices, security lattices)
Set-based effects; no lattice structure; handlers consume specific operations

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Lattice structure is simpler to reason about than rows — idempotence ('e \/ e = e') avoids Koka's duplicate-label complications. SMT decidability of lattice subsumption is trivial. Agent-friendly: lattice join is deterministic.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Koka's well-documented experience was that effect SUBTYPING makes inference undecidable and constraints complex — FX's lattice IS subtyping (e1 <= e2 iff e1\/e2=e2) so inherits exactly the critique Koka rejected. Rows let you say 'handler removes specifically Exn(IOError)' vs 'Exn(ParseError)' — a lattice with just Exn conflates these. Read <= Write is the ONLY built-in subtyping relation — but what about Alloc <= IO? Async <= IO? Chapter is 14 lines and declares subtyping but doesn't describe what the lattice elements are beyond §9.4's 9 built-ins.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Full ordering diagram of built-in effects (is Alloc incomparable to IO? is Async above Div or incomparable?). Which user-defined effects can be subeffects of which built-ins. Whether parameterized effects (State(int) vs State(string)) are in the lattice or how they compose. Decidability statement for subtyping+polymorphism.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Reconsider rows vs lattice — FX's design goal is agent-first and rows with duplicate labels are arguably more informative for an agent planning 'what handler to insert where'. If keeping lattice, publish the full Hasse diagram of built-ins. State decidability of inference+subtyping explicitly.

analysis.what_fx_does: Nine built-in effects (Tot/Div/Ghost/Exn/IO/Alloc/Read/Write/Async) plus parallelization rules derived from them (Tot/Ghost always safe, Read safe, Write requires exclusive, Alloc thread-local-safe, IO must serialize).

closest_prior_art.approach: Seven built-ins: total/exn/div/ndet/alloc⟨h⟩/read⟨h⟩/write⟨h⟩/io with io as top; pure = exn+div alias; Koka has per-heap Read/Write (not FX's global)
No fixed hierarchy — effects are user-defined only; stdlib effects are ordinary library-defined effects, not privileged
IO is privileged, everything else in monad transformer; no parallelization rules derived from effects

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Parallelization rules derived from effects is genuinely useful and is connected to the rest of the type system (ownership ensures Write exclusivity). Ghost as a first-class effect (erased at runtime) matches F*'s Ghost. Async as a distinct effect (not buried under IO) is cleaner than Rust's ambient async.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Koka per-heap Read⟨h⟩/Write⟨h⟩ is strictly more expressive — lets you prove 'this function reads from h1 only, never h2'. FX conflates all reads. No Ndet (non-determinism) — does FX then forbid random/clock reads without a dedicated effect? No Pure alias (Koka's shortcut for Exn+Div). Write-implies-Read as the ONLY subeffect is strange: why isn't Alloc implied-by Write (heap write)? Why isn't Async implied-by IO?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Per-heap region parameterization. Ndet effect. Pure alias. Interaction of Ghost with other effects (is Ghost+IO meaningful or forbidden?). Precise definition of Exn (what exception types belong to Exn vs a user-defined Exception effect?). Does Exn carry the exception type or just mark may-fail? Panic vs recoverable exception.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Koka's per-heap Read⟨h⟩/Write⟨h⟩. Add Ndet. Add Pure = Exn+Div alias. Clarify whether Exn is parameterized by exception type. Add subtyping Alloc <= Write (heap write) and Async <= IO (async performs IO eventually).

analysis.what_fx_does: effect E <params> ... fn op(...):T; ... end effect declares user effects with operation signatures (State/Reader/Exception/FileSystem examples).

closest_prior_art.approach: 'effect E { fun op(...): T; ... }' syntax; operations can be fun (pure) or control (resumptive); per-operation linearity
'type _ Effect.t += Op : args -> ret Effect.t' extends single built-in effect type; untyped; no effect row in fn types
Capability declarations with block-level scoping; operations returned as capabilities to be used only within handler scope

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Parameterized effects (State<s:type>, Reader<r:type>, Exception<e:type>) with standard type-parameter syntax is clean. Closer to Koka than to OCaml 5's extensible-type hack. Ordinary function-signature syntax for ops.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chapter shows 4 examples but doesn't specify: (1) can operations be generic in more type parameters than the effect itself? (2) can operations themselves declare effects (e.g. FileSystem operations perform IO under the hood)? (3) is there a distinction between 'fun' (tail-resumptive, optimizable) and 'control' (full continuation) operations like Koka? Missing multishot marker in the declaration (§9.7 adds it but not here).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Per-operation effect annotations (can a handler operation itself perform other effects?). Operation type parameters separate from effect type parameters. Effect inheritance / subsumption. Handler composition when effect has multiple operations handled by different handlers. Parameterized effect operations (State<s>.put: s -> unit — fine but State<int>.put and State<string>.put are separate effects?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Koka's fun/control distinction explicitly — tail-resumptive operations can compile to ordinary function calls. Specify operation-level effect rows. Unify with §9.7's multishot marker in the declaration syntax.

what_fx_does: Handlers provide implementations for effects, removing them from the body's effect row. Typing rule: handler with body of effect <E | eff> and clauses for every op of E produces result of effect eff; continuation k in each op clause carries effect eff. Return clause transforms body's result type A into handler's result type B. Proposal 13 additions: (1) return clause is optional — defaults to identity 'return x => x' when omitted, reducing boilerplate in the common case where the handler transforms ops but not the result; (2) nested handlers handle innermost-first — an operation performed by the body goes to the innermost lexically-enclosing handler that clauses that effect; operations not covered propagate outward (matches Koka / Multicore OCaml / Effekt / Frank universal consensus); (3) return-keyword ambiguity note — handler 'return' clause and function 'return' statement share the keyword; parser disambiguates by context (handle_expr vs fn_body).

closest_prior_art.approach: Deep handlers by default; continuation k has return type D (handler result) not C (body result); handlers in action (Kammar/Lindley/Oury ICFP 2013) give the canonical typing rule
closest_prior_art.reference: Kammar, Lindley, Oury 'Handlers in Action', ICFP 2013

closest_prior_art.approach: Continuation k has return type C (body result); handler doesn't self-reinstall; used for catamorphism-unfriendly patterns like mutual recursion
match/effect syntax with one-shot linear continuations; return clause optional; runtime exception if unhandled

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Typing rule is stated (many tutorial intros skip it). Explicit separation of return-clause (A -> B) from op-clauses mirrors the well-studied Kammar et al. presentation.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chapter commits to deep handlers (k returns B) but doesn't mention shallow handlers. Doesn't discuss scoped vs unscoped handlers (Koka's handle vs handle-with). Doesn't discuss parameterized handlers (Kammar et al. extension — folds with a state). The example uses 'state.set(new_s); k(())' which requires state outside the handler's monadic scope — this is exactly the scope-leak problem Koka solves with scoped resources. Run_state example relies on mutable state declared above handle — fine operationally but doesn't show how a handler-local state dimension interacts with §19 mutation.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Shallow handler variant. Parameterized handlers (state-threading). Scoped handlers. Handler composition rules (multiple effects in body, handled by nested handles). Runtime cost of handler invocation (allocation? stack copy? tail-call?). Handler subsumption (can a handler that removes Exn be typed as removing Exn+Div?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add shallow variant with its own typing rule and concrete use case (mutual recursion over effect-producing tree). Add parameterized handlers. Specify scoping rules. State compilation strategy (evidence passing / handler specialization / fibers).

analysis.what_fx_does: One-shot (linear) continuations by default — direct jumps, no heap allocation; opt-in 'multishot' marker on operation requires all captured values @[copy].

closest_prior_art.approach: One-shot only; multi-shot via ocaml-multicont library which deep-copies stack segments per resume — 'heap to stack conversion' optimization becomes unsound with multishot; enforces linearity dynamically
closest_prior_art.reference: Sivaramakrishnan et al. 'Retrofitting Parallelism onto OCaml', ICFP 2020

closest_prior_art.approach: Seminal PLDI 1996 paper showing one-shot continuations eliminate copying overhead inherent in multi-shot
closest_prior_art.reference: Bruggeman, Hieb, Dybvig 'Representing Control in the Presence of One-Shot Continuations', PLDI 1996

closest_prior_art.approach: Supports both via explicit 'ctl' (control) vs 'fun' (tail-resumptive) vs 'brk' (one-shot); evidence-passing compilation preserves tail calls on Pure path
closest_prior_art.reference: Xie, Leijen 'Generalized Evidence Passing for Effect Handlers', ICFP 2021

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Requires @[copy] on captured values for multishot — this is the SAFETY discipline OCaml had to add dynamic checks for because it lacks linear types. FX's §7 linearity makes this structural, not runtime. Clean integration with the type system.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chapter is 25 lines; doesn't specify what happens with linear (non-copy) values in the lexical scope but not captured (safe?). Doesn't address the 'mixed one-shot + multishot nested' footgun documented in the multicont library. Doesn't discuss how a handler USE SITE knows whether an operation is one-shot (does the type carry it?).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Exact rule for 'captured' (lexical vs by-reference). Interaction with async/await (await is one-shot continuation). Can a multishot operation be converted to one-shot or vice versa by a wrapper? Runtime cost of multishot (stack clone? CPS?). Effect type marker for multishot (so callers know).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Koka's three-way distinction (fun/brk/ctl) or Effekt's handler specialization. Add a type-level marker in the effect row so 'operation is multishot' propagates. Ensure the one-shot compilation strategy (direct jump) is documented so agents can reason about runtime cost.

analysis.what_fx_does: Generators are sugar over Yield<y:type> effect with fn yield(y):unit; fibonacci example uses while-true with ref(0)/ref(1), yield, then for-loop consumes via iterate(generator).

closest_prior_art.approach: 'gen { ... }' blocks with yield keyword ship in Rust 2024 edition; generators lower to state machines (stackless coroutines); 'gen' and 'async gen' compose naturally
yield keyword produces generator objects with send/throw/close — stackless, iterator-valued, multi-yield with resume-value type
yield is sugar over Yield effect with 'iterate' consumer — exactly FX's approach

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Effect-based generators unify with the rest of the effect system — a fn can yield AND perform IO AND be cancellable with the same handler machinery. Rust's gen-block yield-only is less uniform. No separate iterator protocol — just a Yield effect and an iterate consumer.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chapter doesn't address the 'first-yield-vs-subsequent-yield' typing asymmetry (Python's send(None) on first call; withoutboats identifies this as needing two different yield-point types). Doesn't address async generators (AsyncIterator) — Rust cares about this, FX example uses while-true which diverges (where's the Div effect marker?). The fib example has a subtle bug: while true is Div without a decreases — should have 'with Yield(i64), Div' not just 'with Yield(i64)'.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Resume-with-value (generators with inputs — Python's send, Rust's resume_arg). AsyncIterator/async generators. Termination — does iterate recognize 'end' or does the generator need to return a specific value? Interaction with for-loop termination (break triggers what in the generator?). The Div effect should be declared in the fib example but isn't.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Show a bi-directional generator (yield value, resume with value of different type) to match Rust's stackless coroutine. Fix the Div annotation in the example. Add an async-generator example. Specify what for-over-iterate does on break (sends a Drop signal to the generator? Cancels it?).

analysis.what_fx_does: await and spawn_in are operations of Async effect; task_group guarantees all spawned tasks complete before group exits (structured concurrency).

closest_prior_art.approach: withTaskGroup/withThrowingTaskGroup with group.addTask; parent awaits all children before returning; cancellation propagates; the canonical structured concurrency API
coroutineScope{...}/supervisorScope{...}; Job hierarchy; async{...}/launch{...} are children of scope; failure cancels siblings
Nathaniel J. Smith's original structured concurrency in Trio with async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery; inspired Swift/Kotlin

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unifies structured concurrency with the algebraic effect system — task_group is a handler scope for the Async effect. Linear types ensure spawned futures are awaited (no Swift-style 'detached task' footgun). Integration with §7 ownership means futures can pass linear resources.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chapter is 26 lines; doesn't discuss cancellation propagation (Swift/Kotlin spend pages on this). Doesn't discuss supervisor scopes (failure isolation between siblings). Doesn't discuss async-sync boundary (can Tot fn call await? Obviously no, but what's the error message?). The task_group example has 'futures |> map(await)' — unclear whether this awaits them in order (sequential) or concurrently; if concurrently, does order matter?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Cancellation semantics — what happens when task_group exits due to exception? Supervisor vs cascading failure. Shielding (Trio-style). Timeouts. Deadlines. Structured cancellation across FFI (Swift has this). Interaction with select (§11.9). Interaction with session types (§11) — can a spawned task own a channel endpoint?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Trio-style shielding primitives. Specify cancellation model (CancellationError exn? Async cancel() op?). Distinguish task_group (fails-all) from supervisor_group (isolates failures). Add deadline/timeout primitive. Specify 'futures |> map(await)' semantics (I think this reads as sequential awaits — for parallel start needed spawn_in which already happened; clarify).

analysis.what_fx_does: Platform table restricts available effects: x86/ARM gets all 9; GPU excludes Exn/IO (keeps Tot/Div/Ghost/Alloc/Read/Write/Async); FPGA gets Tot/Alloc(Stack)/Read/Write only. Using unavailable effects is compile error.

closest_prior_art.approach: Kernel code cannot call host stdlib; compile errors at HIP/NVCC from forbidden intrinsics; vendor-specific diagnostics not type-level
Pure functional language where 'side effects are tightly controlled: there are none'; targets GPU; rejection of retrofitting an effect system is explicit
Effect system for accumulators in for-loops; polymorphic in underlying effects; targets GPU
closest_prior_art.reference: Paszke et al. 'Getting to the Point: Index Sets and Parallelism-Preserving Autodiff for Pointful Array Programming', ICFP 2021

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Genuinely novel — no mainstream language makes platform availability part of the type/effect system as a first-class dimension. CUDA/OpenCL errors are vendor-specific, post-hoc, at the kernel compile step; FX catches at source compile time. Dex has an effect system but for accumulators, not for platform restrictions. Futhark rejects effects entirely. Reveals the shape of what a platform effect-restriction type would look like.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: 15 lines is not enough. Real GPU programming needs Shared/Local/Global memory spaces (not just 'Alloc'). FPGA needs bounded recursion depth (FX has decreases but FPGA also needs bounded loops). No story for multi-target binaries (compile same source to x86 AND GPU — which effects are checked against which target?). No story for kernels that offload: main fn is IO, kernel fn launched from it is restricted — how is the boundary typed? GPU lacks Exn but what about cooperative matmul that needs synchronization primitives?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Memory spaces within GPU (Shared/Local/Global/Constant). Bounded-depth recursion for FPGA. Multi-target compilation model. Kernel-launch boundary effect. Synchronization primitives (__syncthreads) — a new effect? Warp-level primitives. How compile error is raised — at fn definition or at call site?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add GPU memory-space effect parameters (Alloc(Shared/Local/Global)). Add FPGA-specific effects for synthesis targets (LUT/BRAM budget as an effect). Specify cross-target binary model. Add a concrete example showing the x86→GPU kernel-launch boundary and effect filtering. Cite Dex and Futhark explicitly as prior art.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter §10 Verification bundles refinement types, pre/post/decreases, assert/calc/verify blocks, sorry/axiom, trust-boundary runtime validators, compiler error taxonomy, proof stability tracking, incremental verification, and a catalog of auxiliary proof tools.

closest_prior_art.approach: Refinement types + SMT + tactics + effect system; the closest surface-level ancestor to FX's verification story
requires/ensures/decreases + assert by + calc + opaque/reveal + counterexamples; most mature auto-active verifier
Decidable-fragment refinement typing with liquid inference and refinement reflection

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Tries to integrate verification into the language from day one (not a bolt-on), unifies trust-boundary validators with contracts (§14), and ties proof obligations into the 21-dimension grade system rather than treating refinements as a separate phase.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: §10 is an enumerated feature list, not a full specification: no semantics for quantifier triggers, no SMT encoding of user-defined functions (reflection vs axiomatization is undecided), no termination metric algebra (lexicographic? set-theoretic?). (The visibility/opacity model is now specified in §10.15: opaque-by-default with `@[transparent]` and `@[no_inline]` attributes and `reveal f;` inside verify blocks — this earlier gap is closed.)
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Encoding of user-defined functions to SMT (now partially addressed by §10.16 @[reflect] opt-in), measure syntax beyond a bare `decreases expr`, interaction of refinements with effects/lifetimes, how triggers are inferred, and how proof caching works across the 21 dimensions. SMT theory set is now specified (§10.16: QF_UFLIA + QF_BV + QF_NRA + QF_FP + bounded quantifiers with E-matching).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Spec each subsection with concrete semantics: (1) SMT encoding contract (QF_UFLIA + limited nonlinear + reflection on marked functions); (2) termination measures as well-founded relations with a small library of built-in orderings; (3) tie §10 explicitly to §6 graded types. (Opaque-by-default with reveal is now specified in §10.15, addressing the visibility concern from the earlier analysis.)

analysis.what_fx_does: Refinement types attach a base-type predicate (e.g., `int { x > 0 }`) checked by SMT at compile time, or by auto-generated runtime validators at trust boundaries.

closest_prior_art.approach: Restricts refinements to an SMT-decidable fragment (QF-UFLIA) and infers them via liquid typing; refinement reflection extends to user functions
Full dependent refinements checkable by SMT + tactics; no decidability restriction, but requires manual hints when SMT fails
Liquid refinements over Rust types indexed by ownership; soundness relies on stacked borrows
Subset types like `type Pos = int | 0 < x witness 1` with mandatory witness for non-emptiness

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Combines refinement types with ownership/effect dimensions uniformly (§6), and formally separates static proof from runtime validation at trust boundaries (§10.8). The three-line example is cleaner than LH/F* syntax.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No statement about decidable fragment vs undecidable; no witness requirement for inhabitation (Dafny requires `witness`, FX doesn't mention it — could produce uninhabited refinement types that vacuously typecheck); `where Ord(a)` constraint interaction with the predicate `is_sorted(x)` is unspecified — how is `is_sorted` encoded to SMT? LH's refinement reflection addresses this; FX doesn't.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Decidability guarantee or boundary; inhabitation/witness requirement; encoding rule for user functions in refinements (reflect vs axiomatize); interaction with laziness (LH needed stratified termination); refinement polymorphism/abstract refinements (Vazou et al.).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Mandate witness for non-trivially-inhabited refinements (Dafny style), declare the decidable fragment explicitly, and specify refinement reflection semantics for `is_sorted`-style user predicates. Import LH's abstract refinements for parametricity.

analysis.what_fx_does: `pre` preconditions, `post r =>` postconditions with programmer-bound result name, `decreases` termination measure, with multiple independent `post` clauses verified and reported separately.

closest_prior_art.approach: requires/ensures/decreases/modifies on methods; multiple ensures verified independently
`with Pre =>`, `with Post =>`, `'Result` and `'Old` attributes; class-wide Pre'Class/Post'Class
`requires` / `ensures` on `Stack`/`ST`/`Pure` effects; termination via `decreases`
requires/ensures/assignable, heavy runtime checking focus

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Letting the programmer name the return value (`post r =>`) instead of a magic `result`/`'Result` is a small but real ergonomic win. Independent verification of each `post` clause with clause-level error reporting matches Dafny and is better than JML. Proposal 10 added `old(expr)` as a spec-language primitive valid in two-state contexts (post/invariant/verify over two-state machines) — matches Dafny/SPARK/JML; R003 error on misuse outside two-state context. Ghost-graded and erased at compile time. Lemma declarations are first-class via §10.7 `lemma` keyword with fn grammar + dedicated declaration.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No `modifies`/`reads` frame clause — how does FX scope side-effect reasoning? Effect dimension handles some of it, but aliasing is underspecified. No `decreases` well-foundedness proof obligation story (lexicographic? multiset?).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Frame specifications (modifies/reads), pre/post on lambdas, whether `pre` can reference other functions' postconditions at call sites. Decreases measure algebra.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify termination measure algebra (well-founded relation library). Add explicit frame clauses for side-effect reasoning beyond the effect row. `old(expr)` is resolved; `lemma` declaration is resolved per Proposal 14.

analysis.what_fx_does: `verify ... exports` blocks isolate proof work and name which facts escape into the surrounding context (intermediate assertions hidden, only `exports` list visible afterward).

closest_prior_art.approach: `assert P by { ... }` scopes intermediate proof facts to the by block; only P escapes
`abstract ensures { P } body end` isolates proof with only `ensures` exported
`have` and `show` with local contexts; `moreover`/`ultimately` to compose facts
No native exports-style block; uses `let _ = _ in` with `assert` and relies on Z3's unsat core; query splitting via --split_queries

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Explicit `exports` list gives programmer precise control over what leaks — sharper than Dafny's single-assertion `assert by` and more declarative than Why3's single-ensures `abstract`. Enables multiple facts to be exported atomically.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Only one 14-line example; no grammar for `exports` list; no specification of how `hint` inside `verify` differs from `assert using` in §10.4; no rule for whether exported facts are assumed or proved from the block; does Dafny's brittleness problem (facts leaking from preconditions still available; Leino's note) carry over? (Interaction with opaque definitions is now specified in §10.15: `reveal f;` inside a verify block locally unfolds an opaque function, with body facts not escaping unless listed in `exports` — this part of the earlier critique is closed.)
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Grammar for `hint` vs `assert`, semantics of `exports` (axiom vs proved lemma), interaction with quantifiers/triggers, whether `exports` can name ghost variables, and how it composes with `verify...exports` nesting. Still missing: a `hides f, g;` negative form for locally opacifying otherwise-transparent functions.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify `exports` list as a proof obligation (compiler proves each exported fact from local assumptions then hides everything else). Add a negative form `hides f, g;` to locally opacify transparent functions inside a verify block — the positive `reveal f;` is already specified in §10.15.

analysis.what_fx_does: `assert P;` proves P from context; `assert P using lemma(args), lemma2(args);` names lemmas to be made available for the proof of P.

closest_prior_art.approach: `assert P by { Hint; }` where Hint is a full statement block (lemma calls, subordinate asserts)
`assert_norm P`, `assert P by (tac)` with Meta-F* tactics, explicit lemma calls as `FStar.Tactics.smt_sync ()`
`have P by (auto simp: lem1 lem2)` or `have P using lem1 lem2 by auto`
`have h : P := by apply lem1; exact lem2`

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Comma-separated `using lemma1, lemma2` is readable and matches Isabelle's `using` sugar. Named assertions are cleaner than F*'s verbose tactic invocations.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Strictly less expressive than Dafny's `assert by { ... }` which takes an arbitrary statement block (can include loops, lemma calls with arguments, local bindings) — FX's `using` is just a flat list. No tactic combinator (F* Meta-F*, Lean tactics) — no way to chain `rewrite; auto` style steps. No `assume` equivalent for debugging.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Tactic combinators, `assume P` for debugging, interaction between `using` and `calc` (do lemmas flow into calc steps?), scoping of `using` (single assertion only, or rest of block?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Promote `assert using` to a full tactic block: `assert P by begin rewrite comm_add; apply lem1; linarith end;`. That matches Dafny/Lean power without losing the flat-list ergonomic for simple cases.

analysis.what_fx_does: `calc` blocks chain equational reasoning with per-step `by { tactic }` justifications, terminated by `end calc`.

closest_prior_art.approach: `calc` environment supporting mixed relations (`=`, `≤`, `<`) via `Trans` instances; usable in tactic and term mode; `_` placeholders; works with `by rw`, `by linarith`, `by ring`
`calc { expr1; { hint } expr2; ...; }` with default operator `==`, optional operator overrides per step; supports `greatest` and coinductive proofs
`also` / `finally` chains composing `have P = Q by ...` steps via transitivity

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Nothing beyond matching the state of the art. The syntax is compact and readable.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No support for mixed relations shown (Lean `calc a = b < c ≤ d`); no discussion of custom transitivity instances; no interaction with `with` effect rows; the single 5-line example omits Dafny-style auto-discharge of simple steps and omits Lean's `_` placeholder pattern.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Mixed relation support, per-step auto-discharge, `_`-placeholder for missing steps, interaction with `sorry`, whether `by` can contain arbitrary tactics or only named tactics from §10.7, custom transitivity via type class.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Lean 4's `Trans` type class so users can define calc chains over `<=`, `<`, domain-specific preorders (e.g., `refines`, `subtype`). Permit `sorry` in any step for partial proofs. Document that steps can be auto-discharged when trivial.

analysis.what_fx_does: `sorry` is a placeholder that compiles in dev and is rejected in release; the compiler prints Goal/Context/Candidates/Suggestion on every sorry. `axiom` is a declared assumption, always compiles, tracked in the trust dimension — trust propagates as minimum through the call graph so release requires Verified.

closest_prior_art.approach: `sorry` inserts `sorryAx`; `#print axioms thm` transitively enumerates all axioms/sorries a theorem depends on; can be disabled via compile flag
`Admitted` (full proof admission), `admit` (tactic), `Print Assumptions thm` for transitive axiom list
`admit()` and `assume` primitives; `--admit_smt_queries true` for bulk admission; trust tracking less formalized
`assume P` (axiomatic); `{:axiom}` attribute; no release-vs-dev distinction

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Structured diagnostic printed at sorry sites (Goal/Context/Candidates/Suggestion) is sharper than Lean's bare `sorry` placeholder. Release-vs-dev distinction with automatic rejection of sorry in release is cleaner than Lean's manual `#print axioms` discipline. Trust as a first-class dimension (§6.3 dim 9) with min-propagation semantics is genuinely more formal than Lean/Coq's ad-hoc axiom listing.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No `#print axioms`-style command described for listing transitive sorry dependencies. Interaction between `axiom` and `sorry` is one paragraph — how are Assumed and Sorry distinguished in reports? What happens when an `axiom` is later proved — does its trust graduate automatically? Lean supports `@[axiom]` or theorem replacement; FX silent. Classical axioms (LEM, choice) vs domain assumptions not distinguished.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Command to enumerate trust dependencies (what calls sorry transitively), axiom taxonomy (classical vs domain), semantics of `axiom` with refinements, graduation path from `sorry`→`axiom`→`proved`, handling of `sorry` inside `comptime`.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add `#trust fn_name` query (equivalent to Lean `#print axioms`) showing transitive sorries/axioms; classify axioms (`classical`, `domain`, `extensional`) for more granular trust; link `sorry` to ProofState server endpoint (§24.7) so agents can enumerate unfinished work.

analysis.what_fx_does: `#show` displays proof state at program point (Known / Goal); `#plan` generates a proof skeleton by decomposing the goal and searching lemma database; built-in tactics `ring`/`field`/`linarith`/`omega`/`decide`/`simp`/`norm_num` usable in `by` clauses.

closest_prior_art.approach: Live display of proof state (hypotheses + goal) at cursor; `#check`, `#print`; tactics ring/linarith/omega/norm_num/simp all in core or Mathlib
`Show`, `Show Proof`, `Info` commands; tactics `ring`, `lia` (linarith), `nia`, `lra`, `ring_simplify`
Panel shows proof state; `sledgehammer` for automation; `simp`, `auto`, `arith`
No built-in named tactics — verification is monolithic SMT call; LLM-driven tools like Laurel fill gaps

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Having `#plan` that synthesizes a skeleton with sorry-at-leaves is genuinely useful for agentic workflows (matches FX's stated LLM-first ethos) and not offered by Lean/Coq/Dafny in that form. REST-accessible via §24.7 is novel.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The tactic names are listed but not specified: what is the decidable class of `decide`? Is `ring` over commutative rings only (Lean splits ring/abel/linear_combination)? `linarith` — linear over ordered rings? Integers and reals? `omega` — Presburger over integers and naturals, but does it handle division/modulo like Lean's omega does? None of these are specified — an implementer cannot build this section. Far less detail than Lean's `Mathlib.Tactic.Ring` docs.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Precise decidable class for each tactic, how tactics compose (chain, semicolon, first), tactic failure semantics, how `simp` rewrite set is specified, `norm_num` numeric tower coverage (only ints? decimals? fractions?), whether proof state includes all 21 dimensions. Agent-API integration now covered by §10.7 canonical ProofState schema (known/goal/suggestion) served by `GET /proof-state`.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify each tactic with decidable class reference (e.g., `omega = Presburger QFLIA as in Pugh 1991`), give grammar for tactic composition, and define the `#show` data structure to be the exact schema served by `GET /proof-state` (§24.7). Document that `#plan` decomposes by goal shape (conjunction→split, forall→generalize, etc.).

analysis.what_fx_does: Trust boundaries are classified by origin (literal, verified return, pattern branch, IO/FFI/deserialization); at boundaries where decidable refinements cannot be proven statically, the compiler auto-generates runtime validators; runtime checks exist only at two places — trust boundaries and external interactions.

closest_prior_art.approach: Smart constructors `refineV[NonEmpty](name)` perform runtime validation and lift into refinement-typed values
F* compiles refinement-typed APIs; runtime validation at session endpoints from Scribble protocol
Runtime validators generated from type definitions; compile-time inference of the TS type from the validator
Blame-tracking contracts at module boundaries; runtime checking
