analysis.where_fx_is_better: The origin-based classification table (literal/verified-return/pattern-branch/IO/FFI — full or none) is crisp and more formal than Scala `refined`'s ad-hoc smart constructors or Racket's blame tracking. Tying runtime validators to contract system (§14) unifies two usually-separate mechanisms.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Only `decidable` refinements auto-generate runtime validators — no story for undecidable ones (crash? error effect? skip?). No blame tracking (who violated the contract — caller, callee?). No discussion of validator cost / when to inline vs hoist. No runtime representation of the refinement witness (does the validator just check, or does it return proof evidence?).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Blame semantics, undecidable-refinement fallback, validator cost model, how `tainted` (§12.3) taints interact with trust boundaries, whether validators run in a specific effect (`Exn`?), error reporting when validation fails.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Racket-style blame with `positive`/`negative` parties so violation reports point at the right module. Define an error effect `Validation(E)` for validator failures. Allow user-specified validator (for undecidable refinements: `with validator my_fn`).

analysis.what_fx_does: The compiler automatically proves refinements following from arithmetic, pattern matching, and just-called postconditions; manual lemma names are required for non-trivial cases (reverse-of-sorted, existential witness).

closest_prior_art.approach: Liquid inference computes the strongest refinement typing via qualifier-guided abstract interpretation; preconditions must be stated, postconditions often inferred
Liquid inference synthesizes loop invariants including quantified ones about container contents
SMT auto-discharges simple VCs from arithmetic/pattern matching; manual `assert (…)` with lemma invocations for harder goals
Auto-discharge via Z3 for arithmetic and simple quantifiers; manual `calc`, `assert by`, or lemma calls for harder goals

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The `// Automatic` vs `// Manual` comment convention giving a rule of thumb (arithmetic / pattern / postcondition = auto; existentials and inverses = manual) is more honest than F*/Dafny docs which leave users to guess.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: 3 example lines is not a specification of inference. Liquid Haskell has liquid inference as a concrete algorithm (Cosman/Jhala 2017) that synthesizes qualifiers by abstract interpretation — FX has no algorithm description. Flux synthesizes loop invariants. FX doesn't mention loop invariants at all in §10.9. No qualifier inference, no strongest-postcondition computation.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Loop invariant inference (major gap — not even mentioned in §10), qualifier/predicate inference algorithm, refinement polymorphism inference (abstract refinements from Vazou), when SMT gives up and what the fallback is, cost model for inference.
analysis.could_we_do_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 1 + rigor-first] Verdict raised from 'weak' to 'adequate' after Proposal 1 adopted Dimension 20 (Size) with 'with Productive' effect + guardedness check for §3.5 codata, plus the tier-system refactor of §6.3 that classifies each dimension as Tier S (semiring), L (lattice+validity), T (typestate), or F (foundational). Rigor-first eliminates all inference sites with explicit surface syntax per Appendix E. Earlier note preserved below.

Specify a liquid-style qualifier database + inference algorithm (or refinement reflection a la LH), explicitly include loop invariant inference (compiler infers from pre/post + body), and document the decidable-fragment boundary.

analysis.what_fx_does: Every compiler error follows a fixed format (code, file:line:col, source line with underline, Goal/Have/Gap, Suggestion with concrete FX code, --explain link); a taxonomy of prefixes (T/R/E/M/S/I/P/N/W) classifies errors by dimension.

closest_prior_art.approach: E0001 etc., --explain CODE extended docs, structured JSON output for IDEs, suggested fix with rustfix
Educational error messages, full-context underline, sample fix, hint-driven diagnostic language
Explicitly inspired by Elm; structured suggestions API; header + context format
Error messages tied to InfoView state; proof-goal-centric diagnostics

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The Goal/Have/Gap structure is specific to verification (Rust/Elm don't have that because they don't verify proofs). The dimension-based taxonomy (R=refinement, M=mode, S=session, I=info flow, P=proof) maps directly to the 21 dimensions and is more informative than Rust's monolithic E-numbering. 'Every suggestion is valid FX code' is a firm commitment matching rustfix. Proposal 14 added rustfix-style applicability grades (machine_applicable / has_placeholders / maybe_incorrect) via `fxc annotate --format=json`.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No JSON machine-readable format documented (Rust has --error-format=json); interaction with agent daemon (§24) underspecified — is the structure the same? No multi-span diagnostics (Rust shows multiple source locations with labels), no reference to fix applicability levels (MachineApplicable, MaybeIncorrect — Rust has a graded scale).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: JSON schema for errors, fix applicability levels, multi-span support, localization story, internationalization of error messages, how error code IDs are assigned and kept stable across versions.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt rustfix's graded applicability (MachineApplicable / HasPlaceholders / MaybeIncorrect), commit to a stable JSON schema that agent daemon serves, add multi-span labels like Rust's `primary`/`secondary`, and document error-code allocation policy so new codes don't break external tooling.

analysis.what_fx_does: Compiler validates specifications before proof attempts, warning on vacuously-true specifications, unrealizable (contradictory) specifications, and specifications weaker than the implementation guarantees.

closest_prior_art.approach: Kupferman/Vardi formalization; detect when part of spec is irrelevant (e.g., antecedent never true)
Mutate specs, check if tests still pass (meaning spec is redundant) — MCoq for Coq, specification mutation thesis
No automatic weak-spec detection; users manually audit; tools like Laurel auto-generate missing assertions but don't validate specs

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Bundling vacuity + unrealizability + weakness checks into the compiler itself is genuinely novel for a production language — model checkers do vacuity, but not at compile time for general refinements. The 'weaker than implementation' check (body proves stronger fact than spec claims) is particularly useful and I can find no language that offers it as a built-in warning.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Section is 8 lines. No specification of the algorithm: how is vacuity detected (Kupferman/Vardi uses subformula-disabling; FX doesn't say)? Unrealizability — how is contradiction detected (SMT asks `exists r. post(r)` — what if SMT can't decide)? Weaker-than-implementation — compiler would have to infer the strongest post (§10.9 already handwaves about inference). These are hard research problems handled in one line each.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Algorithms, completeness/soundness of each check, false-positive rate, whether these are warnings or errors in release builds, how they integrate with incremental verification, treatment of intentionally-weak specs (abstraction).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify vacuity algorithm (subformula disabling a la Kupferman/Vardi), unrealizability via SAT check on `exists inputs satisfying pre, exists output satisfying post`, weakness detection by comparing inferred strongest-post to declared post. Provide suppression syntax (`@[allow(weak_spec)]`) for intentional abstraction.

analysis.what_fx_does: Compiler tracks per-obligation verification time across runs; obligations taking >80% of timeout flagged as unstable (W001); budget mode gives best-effort results within a time limit reporting partial progress.

closest_prior_art.approach: Retries each proof with N different random seeds; proof flagged unstable if any seed fails
Formalizes proof instability metric; quantifies butterfly effect in real projects (2.6% of queries unstable in Dafny suites)
rlimit (resource limit) instead of wall-clock; brittleness guide recommends opaque/reveal/assert-by
Resource units (not wall time) for reproducibility

analysis.where_fx_is_better: W001 unstable-proof warning as a standard compiler diagnostic (not a separate tool like Mariposa) is useful. Budget mode with partial-success reporting (`partial (4/7 obligations)`) is clearer than Dafny's `--resource-limit` which just fails or succeeds.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Wall-clock time (80% of timeout) is exactly what Mariposa and F* show is unreliable for stability — Z3 resource-limit (rlimit) is stable across machines; wall clock is not. FX doesn't mention rlimit. No quake-style multi-seed retry. No trigger selection analysis (Dafny does this). No unsat-core minimization (Mariposa shows 96-99% of context is irrelevant and a main cause of instability).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: rlimit-based metric, multi-seed quake testing, context minimization via unsat cores, trigger selection/analysis integration, per-proof historical trend tracking (not just last run), CI integration for stability regression detection.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Switch from wall-clock to Z3 rlimit for W001. Add `fxc verify --quake N` for multi-seed retry (adopt from F*). Record proof stability as a first-class artifact (`.fxs` file) that CI compares across commits. Auto-suggest trigger patterns when matching loops detected.

analysis.what_fx_does: Each `;` is an implicit verification checkpoint and each `verify...exports` is explicit; on edit, only affected checkpoints are re-verified; compiler reports per-checkpoint CACHED vs RE-VERIFY with timing.

closest_prior_art.approach: Persistent `.hints` file stores Z3 query/proof hints per function; incremental on function level
Per-method verification caching; recent work on progress reporting (Issue #5150); no fine-grained per-statement checkpoints shipped
Per-definition `.vo` compilation cache; per-proof-command replay via CoqIDE but no automatic fine-grained cache
Per-theory session cache via sessions; no fine-grained incremental

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Per-semicolon checkpointing is genuinely finer-grained than any production verifier. If implemented correctly, a one-line change would re-verify only that one line plus downstream facts — dramatically better than F* which re-verifies per function. The CACHED/RE-VERIFY progress output matches what Dafny users have been asking for (Issue #5150).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No specification of the cache key: is it the AST hash? Does it include context (which facts are available)? Mariposa shows that irrelevant context changes destabilize proofs — does FX invalidate the cache on any context change, or is it smart about relevance? How is staleness detected across module boundaries? What happens when SMT solver version changes? F*'s hints file has concrete answers; FX section is handwave.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Cache-key structure, granularity of invalidation across modules, staleness detection (solver version, axiom changes), interaction with `axiom`/`sorry`, parallelization (can 1000 checkpoints verify in parallel?), disk layout and cache persistence.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify cache key as Merkle hash over: (AST of checkpoint, transitive closure of facts in scope, solver version, axiom set). Persist to a `.fxv` cache directory (like F*'s `.hints` but finer). Allow parallel verification of independent checkpoints. Document the invalidation frontier explicitly.

analysis.what_fx_does: Catalog of auxiliary proof tools: counterexample generation from SMT model, lemma database search (`fxc search`), proof obligation tree, proof diff on breakage, automatic lemma extraction, proof minimization, specification coverage analysis.

closest_prior_art.approach: F7 in VS Code extracts SMT model as counterexample (experimental, re-labelled); Laurel uses LLMs + lemma-similarity to generate missing assertions
Lemma search by constant, name substring, pattern (`_ * (_ ^ _)`), conclusion shape (`|- tsum _ = _ * tsum _`)
Stores per-function proof hint; on breakage reports which hint diverged
Session file tracks which obligations discharge under which prover; proof-state diff on re-run

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Single unified toolbox accessed via `fxc` CLI + agent REST (§24) rather than IDE-specific features. `fxc search` matches Loogle's ergonomics. Proof obligation tree (pass/fail per branch) is available in Why3 but not packaged as cleanly. Specification coverage analysis is mostly unique — closest is Dafny's research on mutation testing (not shipped).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Six tools in 30 lines: many are handwave-level. 'Automatic lemma extraction when Z3 proves same fact repeatedly' — this is a research problem (common-subgoal detection), not an off-the-shelf technique; no implementation strategy given. 'Proof minimization' (remove redundant hints) — this is NP-hard in general; minimized vs optimal? 'Specification coverage' — what's the metric? (mutation-based? taxonomy-based? it's an open research area). Counterexamples from SMT models are notoriously unreliable (Dafny re-labelled theirs experimental because Z3 models can't be trusted as true counterexamples) — FX doesn't mention this caveat.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: For each tool: algorithm, complexity, soundness/completeness, false-positive/negative rates. Integration with agent daemon API. Interaction with incremental verification cache (does proof diff use the §10.13 cache?). Concrete example of automatic lemma extraction heuristic.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Split this section into subsections with proper specs: counterexample generation with explicit Dafny-style 'may be unsound' warning; lemma search with Loogle-style pattern language; proof diff as structured output from the §10.13 cache; specification coverage defined via mutation testing (one mutation per spec clause; coverage = killed mutants / total). Identify which tools are research-grade vs shipping.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter §11 packages session types, channels, task groups, parallel map, select, memory ordering, deadlock freedom via priority, and multi-mode pipelines (eager/lazy/stream/par/gpu/distributed) into one chapter on concurrency.

closest_prior_art.approach: Splits concerns across libraries: Tokio for async/select, Rayon for data parallelism, Ferrite/session-types for session types; no unified language-level chapter
CSP-style channels + select as first-class, no session types, no structured concurrency, no data-parallel DSL
Dedicated session type languages/toolchains but no integrated memory-ordering or GPU/distributed pipelines

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Ambitious in collapsing seven normally-disjoint research areas (session types, MPST, structured concurrency, relaxed memory, deadlock freedom, pipeline fusion, distributed dataflow) under one grading discipline. No other production language places all of these at language level.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chapter is only ~315 lines for seven major topics — each subsection receives less depth than a single paper on any one of them. Rust/Tokio, Akka, Spark each spend entire books on one slice of §11.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No chapter-level overview showing how these features interact (e.g., can a session-typed channel be used inside a Stream pipeline? Can par()ed pipelines emit to a channel? Do priority annotations flow through task_group?). Cross-cutting semantics are undefined.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add a preamble showing the interaction matrix between sessions, tasks, streams, memory ordering, and pipelines — a 7x7 table of 'what happens when feature X meets feature Y'. Without this, implementers will produce incompatible combinations.

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines session types as protocol types built from send(T), receive(T), branch/select, recursion (rec X), and end — 2-endpoint (binary) form, inline in normal type grammar.

closest_prior_art.approach: Binary session types as language primitives with linear channels, static checking via linear type system extension
Intuitionistic linear logic (SILL_S) session types via judgmental embedding, shared sessions via acquire/release modalities; ECOOP 2022
Global combinators EDSL in OCaml, no external Scribble toolchain, static linear channel checking
Typestate-based session type checking for Java protocols
closest_prior_art.reference: Kouzapas, Dardha, Perera, Gay — Typechecking protocols with mungo and StMungo

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Session types inhabit the same grammar as normal types (no separate typestate DSL or external Scribble tool). Linear usage piggybacks on FX's existing usage-grade semiring so no new mechanism is needed for linear channels. Branch/select keywords (not & and ⊕ symbols) are readable by LLMs and humans.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No concrete semantics given for the session calculus: no typing judgment, no reduction rules, no statement of fidelity/progress theorem. Ferrite, Links and ocaml-mpst each ship mechanized or publication-level formal systems; FX §11.1 just shows three syntax examples. No mention of intuitionistic vs classical linear logic foundation.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Asynchronous vs synchronous semantics; subtyping (Gay-Hole covariant branches? contravariant selects?); delegation (sending a channel over a channel); shared/replicated sessions (Ferrite's contribution); context-free session types; higher-order channels; connection establishment/termination semantics.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Pick a foundation explicitly: classical (Wadler's GV/CP) vs intuitionistic (Ferrite/SILL). Cite the meta-theory. Specify async vs sync. Add subtyping per Gay-Hole with stated variance. Add a 'delegation' example since it's the distinguishing capability.

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines duality inductively: dual(send)↔receive, dual(select/+)↔branch/&, dual(rec X.S) = rec X.dual(S), dual(end)=end; channel creation produces the dual pair.

closest_prior_art.approach: Coinductive subtyping reconciling duality with recursive session types; covariant in branch labels, contravariant in select
closest_prior_art.reference: Gay & Hole, 'Subtyping for session types in the pi calculus', Acta Informatica 42(2/3), 2005

closest_prior_art.approach: Opposite variance from Gay-Hole — process-substitutability view vs channel-substitutability view
Higher-order contracts model with alternative duality notion handling recursion and subtyping uniformly

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The dual rule for recursion (dual(rec X.S) = rec X.dual(S)) matches the standard inductive definition and avoids the 'dual of bound variable' trap that naive definitions fall into.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Duality on recursive session types is subtle — naive substitution-based duality is unsound in some presentations (Bernardi-Hennessy); FX gives no formal argument that its rule preserves the session-type invariants. No subtyping is defined at all, so safe substitution (the point Gay-Hole addresses) is impossible. No statement of the tension between Gay-Hole and Honda-variant duality.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Proof (or citation) that duality is an involution: dual(dual(S)) = S. Treatment of polymorphic/parameterized sessions. Relation to subtyping. Treatment of message-type duality (is dual(send(T)) receive(T) for the same T? what about subtyping on T?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: State explicitly which duality convention is used (channel vs process view per Gay 2016), prove involutivity, and either add Gay-Hole subtyping or justify its absence. Otherwise recursive session types may break compositional reasoning.

analysis.what_fx_does: Channels are linear values; each send/receive advances the protocol state and consumes the old channel type, producing the continuation type; post-end use is a compile error.

closest_prior_art.approach: Linear channel variables enforced via Rust type system; channels must be used exactly once, cannot discard without explicit termination
Channel use corresponds to proof steps; linearity falls out of linear logic cut rule
Classical linear logic encoding, affine (not truly linear) because Rust drops are tolerated

analysis.where_fx_is_better: [Partially resolved per Proposal 5/6] Verdict raised from 'adequate' to 'strong'. §11.3 now specifies the stdlib primitive cancel(ch) : unit with Fail(Cancelled) with EGV semantics (Fowler-Lindley-Morris-Decova POPL 2019) as the canonical cleanup for in-flight channels on abort paths. Combined with §7.11 errdefer, every linear channel has a named termination path on every abort site (M011 / gap #103). The peer observes a typed Cancelled message at the earliest point the session type permits — no silent channel abandonment. True linearity (not affine) via FX's built-in linear default — unlike Rust crates which compromise because Rust values are affine. Progress of the protocol falls out of the same usage semiring as other linear resources; no new mechanism.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Async reduction semantics still unspecified — is send blocking or does it return a future? What is the type of send() exactly? Ferrite and Links spell this out; FX §11.3 still doesn't. The cancel primitive is specified but its formal semantics is cited (EGV) rather than re-derived in FX.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Formal integration of EGV semantics into FX's reduction rules. Channel closure semantics for the 'end' state (still implicit). Pattern-matching on a channel's current state. Support for 'forward' / channel-forwarding between tasks. Timeout (distinct from cancel).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add a type for the bare send/receive primitives: send<T, S>(ch: channel(send(T).S), msg: T) : channel(S). Specify sync vs async (async is what makes sense with Async effect). Integrate EGV's reduction rules directly into §11 rather than citing the paper.

analysis.what_fx_does: Branching side uses branch { label => cont; ...} to offer labeled alternatives; selecting side uses select(ch, label) to pick; match receive_branch(ch) pattern-matches the received label.

closest_prior_art.approach: External choice (branch, partner decides) vs internal choice (select, this side decides); symbols from linear logic additive connectives
'select EHLO c' syntax to select a branch on a channel, 'offer' on the dual side
Choose<Left, Right> with .left()/.right() methods and Offer<Left, Right> with .offer() returning Branch::Left/Right

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Labeled (not binary) branching with keyword syntax (not & / ⊕). Pattern match integrates with the rest of the language's exhaustiveness checking. Labels are identifiers, not bitstrings.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No subtyping means you cannot refine a branch (offer fewer alternatives than the dual side selects from). Gay-Hole subtyping lets an offer 'have more labels than select needs'. In Rust session-types, choose/offer are binary and must be nested for n-ary — FX gets n-ary right at least.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Exhaustiveness check semantics: if select(ch, lbl) passes a label not in the branch set, is that a compile error or runtime? What is the type of receive_branch — an enum, a label, a handle? Can branches carry their own recursion variables? No spec of how labels are wire-encoded (relevant at TCP/MPST boundary).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Require select's label to be a static constant from the session's branch set (compile check). Specify receive_branch's return type formally. Add subtyping at branches (covariant in offered labels, contravariant in selected labels per Gay-Hole).

analysis.what_fx_does: Multiparty: uses global_session with 'Role1 -> Role2 : label(T)' declarations; each participant sees a projected view. 12 lines, one example (auction).

closest_prior_art.approach: Full protocol description language with projection, well-formedness checks, endpoint API generation; choreographies with recursion, recur/continue
Complete, sound, PSPACE projection via automata-theoretic construction — first decidable complete projection operator
Global types as EDSL combinators in OCaml, projection into channel vectors at compile time
Top-down MPST with CFSM generation, static linearity via Rust types

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Integrated at language level with the same grade/linearity infrastructure, avoiding Scribble's separate toolchain/language-independence problem (ocaml-mpst's motivation).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: 12 lines is too thin. Scribble's spec is dozens of pages. No projection algorithm, no well-formedness check, no decidability claim, no mention of whether FX uses the 2023 PSPACE-complete projection or a syntactic (incomplete) one. No recursion, no choice at the global level, no connection actions.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Projection operator definition; well-formedness conditions (projectability); handling of mixed choice (who decides and informs whom); asynchronous vs synchronous; connection/disconnection actions (real-world protocols need them per Hu-Yoshida 2016); nested sessions; delegation across parties.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Promote §11.5 to a full section — at minimum: projection algorithm (cite Li-Stutz-Wies-Zufferey 2023 for completeness), well-formedness, recursion, choice, and connection actions. Currently this is too thin to implement.

analysis.what_fx_does: Sending an owned value through a session channel transfers ownership to the receiver, consuming it at the sender per the linear usage semiring.

closest_prior_art.approach: Send/receive naturally transfer linear resources; the linear cut rule expresses ownership transfer
Channel values typed linearly; sending a linear value consumes both the send permission and the value
Move semantics naturally transfer ownership through channels; no session types but ownership transfer is explicit

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Because FX's usage-grade semiring uniformly governs all values, channel-based ownership transfer requires no special treatment — the linear grade decrements at send. This is cleaner than Rust's move+channel composition.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Section is 10 lines with one toy example. Doesn't discuss serialization semantics (does sending 'own buffer' mean zero-copy in-memory or does it serialize?). For distributed/multi-process channels, 'transfer ownership' can't mean the same thing as intra-process. Rust crates like tokio-serde make this explicit; FX glosses over it.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Intra-process vs inter-process semantics; zero-copy vs serialize behavior; interaction with classified/secret values (does sending a secret over a channel retain the classification?); delegation (sending a channel over a channel) is not mentioned.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify: same-address-space channels transfer the linear pointer (zero-copy); inter-process channels use the message's Contract format; crossing process boundary elides the linear dimension but the receiver gets a fresh linear owner. Mention delegation explicitly.

analysis.what_fx_does: task_group(fn(group) => ...) creates a scope; spawn_in(group, ...) spawns a child; the scope guarantees all children complete before it exits — classic structured concurrency nursery pattern.

closest_prior_art.approach: async with trio.open_nursery() — level-triggered cancellation, hierarchical nesting, exception group propagation (the original nursery pattern, Smith 2017)
Structured scope with cooperative cancellation at suspension points; supervisor variant keeps siblings running on failure
2021 structured concurrency; async let also provides implicit scoping
Edge-triggered cancellation and PEP 654 exception groups, inspired by Trio; native to asyncio

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Because FX tracks Async as an effect and task groups produce typed futures, the type system can express 'all children have joined' as a post-condition without runtime checks. Closure+linearity means a future cannot escape the scope (Trio/Kotlin enforce this at runtime).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Only 9 lines of example with no discussion of cancellation semantics, error propagation (exception groups?), supervision mode (like Kotlin's supervisorScope), timeouts on the group, or whether the group itself is linear. Trio, Kotlin and Swift each have multiple pages on these corners.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Cancellation model (level vs edge triggered); if one child fails, are siblings cancelled by default? Supervisor/non-propagating variant? Structured timeouts (Trio's move_on_after). Does spawn_in return a future or a handle? Is the group value linear?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Trio's level-triggered model explicitly (it's better than asyncio's edge-triggered per the community). Provide a supervisorGroup variant. Define exception aggregation (every child's error surfaces as a set). Make the group linear.

analysis.what_fx_does: parallel_map uses task groups to spawn one task per element then awaits all. items.par() converts a list to a parallel source, and pipeline stages on .par() require Tot-effect (pure) closures.

closest_prior_art.approach: Work-stealing fork-join; closures must be Fn+Send+Sync (pure / shared immutable capture); divides work adaptively, not one-task-per-element
Uses purity (no effects) for safe parallelism; sparks are scheduled by runtime, not one-task-per-element
Parallel collections; withdrawn because pervasive parallel collection invocation caused overhead and non-determinism
STL algorithms take std::execution::par; compiler/impl decides parallelization; no purity check

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Requiring Tot for .par() closures is stronger than Rayon's Fn+Send+Sync (Rayon allows shared atomics to be mutated) — FX guarantees observationally-pure which rules out both data races AND ordering-dependence. Effect system makes the check compositional.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The example parallel_map spawns one task per element (spawn_in per item) — this is massively worse than Rayon's work-stealing. For a million-element list, that's a million tasks. Rayon adapts granularity. If this is just an illustrative example, that's fine; if it's the intended implementation, it's naive. Also, requiring Tot may be too restrictive — Rayon happily allows Alloc inside the closure (heap allocations), FX's Tot rejects them (requires Alloc).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Granularity control (Rayon's with_min_len, Haskell's chunking). Whether .par() allows Alloc effect (since allocation is thread-safe). Cost model (is overhead per-element or amortized?). Parallel reduce/fold semantics (associativity requirements).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Require the closure to be in an effect subset of {Tot, Alloc, Read(immutable), ghost} rather than strictly Tot. Specify work-stealing semantics not per-element spawn. Provide a granularity hint. Look at Scala's deprecation reasons to avoid the same mistakes.

analysis.what_fx_does: select { msg from ch1 => ...; msg from ch2 => ...; _ from done => break; timeout 5000 => log(...) } waits on multiple channels with optional timeout.

closest_prior_art.approach: Language-level select with channel cases and default case; no timeout primitive (uses time.After channel)
Pattern-matching on future completion; cancellation-safety concern — dropped branches may lose state; biased vs random polling
Pattern matches on process mailbox; unmatched messages remain in mailbox; selective receive semantics differ significantly from channel select
Original external choice construct from which Go's select derives

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Direct timeout syntax (timeout N ms) is cleaner than Go's time.After hack. No macro like Tokio — it's a real statement. Can be combined with FX's exhaustiveness and effect checking.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No discussion of cancellation safety (Tokio's biggest design challenge). No fairness guarantee (Tokio is unfair, requires manual biased); Erlang's receive is even quite different because it's persistent-mailbox. No discussion of how select interacts with session-typed channels (is selecting on a session channel legal? what about the protocol state?).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Cancellation safety semantics — when one branch fires, what happens to the pending state of the other branches? Fairness guarantee. Interaction with session types (selecting on a session channel advances which protocol state?). Priority between branches. Default/non-blocking case.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify fairness (e.g., random selection among ready). Define behavior when multiple branches are ready. State cancellation safety requirements on branch bodies (all must be cancel-safe, or doc the hazard). Define session-typed select semantics (it's safe only if all channels are at a receive_branch state).

analysis.what_fx_does: Compiler infers minimum memory fences from access patterns; defaults atomics to SeqCst; explicit weaker orderings (Release/Acquire) via with MemoryOrder(field: Order) effect.

closest_prior_art.approach: 6 orderings (relaxed, consume, acquire, release, acq_rel, seq_cst); manually annotated per operation; famously hard to reason about; POPL 2024 retrospective acknowledges serious problems
Automatic inference of maximally-permissive fence placements from safety spec; on finite-state programs
Dynamic synthesis of fences for real-world concurrent C programs under TSO/PSO
NP-hard optimal fence synthesis for C11; provably optimal for straight-line programs
Resisted acquire/release until Java 9; prefers sequential consistency

analysis.where_fx_is_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 6] Verdict raised from 'adequate' to 'frontier'. FX's memory model is defined bottom-up by per-architecture emit tables (§20.5) rather than top-down by an abstract specification. The approach exploits FX's full control of source-to-bytes translation (no LLVM) — for every (operation, ordering, width, arch) tuple, the emitted instruction sequence is fixed and published, and the source semantics refine against the ISA's formal model (x86-TSO, ARM Flat, RVWMO, MIPS RC — Appendix G). No language ships this: C++/Rust/Swift all negotiate their memory model with LLVM, inheriting its underspecifications. Source types: atomic<T>, atomic_wide<T>, atomic_counter<T>, seqlock<T>. Per-operation @Ord arguments (not a per-function effect) match C++/Rust/Java VarHandle idioms. Default @SeqCst everywhere — free on x86, cheap on ARM64 LSE, one fence extra on RV64. DRF-reject theorem (linearity + atomic = only shared-mutable, races are compile errors, not UB) is the payoff of graded types meeting hardware. Thin-air freedom inherited for free because the compiler doesn't speculate across atomics (the C++ freedom that created thin-air doesn't exist in FX). Earlier analysis preserved below.

SeqCst default matches the POPL 2024 consensus that weak orderings no longer pay on modern hardware and are too hard to reason about. Automatic inference (§21.2) aligns with the Kuperstein/Vechev/DFence/FenSying research line. Making weak orderings an effect makes them visible in signatures — C++ buries them as function call arguments.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse:
[Partially mitigated by Proposal 6] Fence-inference is now a concrete downgrade optimization (§21.2) with five named conditions and @[no_downgrade] escape for CT crypto — no longer hand-wavy. Still open: the 120-lemma refinement proof (emit sequence ⊆ source semantics under M_arch) is stated but not yet mechanized; RC11/promising-semantics integration is deferred; MIPS64 remains behind a legacy profile; ARMv8.0 (pre-LSE) is embedded-profile-only, which means FX won't run on Raspberry Pi 3 class hardware without opt-in. Earlier analysis preserved below.

Fence-inference research shows the problem is NP-hard in general (FenSying) and requires whole-program finite-state analysis (Fender). FX's claim that the compiler 'inserts the minimal synchronization needed' is plausible locally but undecidable globally — how does FX handle this in practice? No concrete algorithm cited. Also, FX still inherits C++-style orderings (Release/Acquire/SeqCst) with all their baggage including the out-of-thin-air problem.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Mechanized refinement proof of the emit tables against each ISA's formal model (x86-TSO in HOL4, ARM Flat in the rmem tool, RVWMO axiomatic). Published the algorithm for happens-before inference driving downgrade; stated as five conditions but formal soundness proof pending. Support for cache control intrinsics (CLFLUSH, DC ZVA, CBO.FLUSH) — deferred to arch-specific intrinsic modules but not specified. Verified ABA-resistant patterns in seqlock<T>. Interaction with verification (Iris/FCSL-style fractional ghost state over atomic cells) — deferred to v2.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Mechanize the 120-lemma refinement proof — ~30 per arch, each small, eminently tractable. Ship a 'memory-model fuzzer' that runs litmus tests (2+2W, IRIW, MP, SB) against every supported target and checks the emit sequence matches the refinement claim. Integrate with §18.12 hardware refinement for fx-chip so the same proof framework covers SW atomics → RV64 asm → RTL implementation. Consider RC11 or promising semantics as the v2 source-level model if RC11/OOTA-freedom becomes load-bearing for verification.

analysis.what_fx_does: Channel operations carry @[priority(n)] annotations; the compiler rejects programs that wait on a lower-priority channel while holding a higher one — Kobayashi-Padovani style priority-based deadlock freedom.

closest_prior_art.approach: Lock levels / priorities assigned to channel operations; ordering constraints enforced by type system; allows cyclic interconnections with acyclic dependencies
closest_prior_art.reference: Kobayashi, 'A type system for lock-free processes', Information and Computation 2002

closest_prior_art.approach: Priorities enforce deadlock freedom for session types with cyclic process structure
Asynchronous priority-based classical processes — extends priority-based deadlock freedom to asynchronous sessions with delegation and recursion
Deadlock freedom by construction via acyclic process tree (no priorities needed, but rules out cyclic networks)
Deadlock freedom solely by linear typing and n-ary fork — no priorities needed

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Priority annotations are integrated into the existing attribute system rather than requiring a separate levels layer. Compatible with session types and channels uniformly.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: §11.11 is five lines. No semantics of priority arithmetic: how do priorities compose through recursion? Across multiparty? How does priority interact with the select statement (§11.9)? Priority Sesh and APCP papers are dense precisely because this is subtle. MPGV shows that priorities may not even be needed — FX doesn't argue why priorities over MPGV-style n-ary fork. No decidability claim.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Formal priority assignment rules; interaction with recursive session types (dynamic priority families); completeness (do all deadlock-free programs typecheck?); interaction with multiparty; interaction with task_group spawning; what values n can take (naturals? partial order?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Cite Kobayashi 2002 or Padovani's priority system. State completeness/soundness properties. Consider adopting the MPGV approach (no priorities) for simplicity if cyclic topologies can be avoided. Specify how priority arithmetic composes across parties.

analysis.what_fx_does: Single pipeline syntax |> dispatches to different execution modes based on source type: list=eager fused, Query=lazy plan with pushdown, Stream=backpressured, .par()=parallel, .gpu()=GPU kernel, distributed via group_by_key/reduce_by_key.

closest_prior_art.approach: Same query syntax dispatches to IEnumerable (eager), IQueryable (lazy SQL-translated), AsParallel (PLINQ); built-in Where+Select fusion; Nessos LinqOptimizer compiles to imperative loops
Lazy DAG; Catalyst does predicate/projection pushdown, join reordering; Tungsten does whole-stage code gen; distributed execution model
Parallel pipeline with work-stealing, type-level parallel iterator trait; no GPU/distributed modes
Source/Flow/Sink with dynamic push-pull backpressure; windowed, batching; 5 overflow strategies (backpressure, drop head/tail/buffer, fail)
Academic unified pipeline IRs that compile to CPU/GPU/distributed backends from a common representation

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Ambitious scope — covers six execution modes (eager, lazy, stream, parallel, GPU, distributed) with one surface syntax. LINQ covers 1-3, Spark covers 2 and 6, Rayon covers 4, Akka covers 3, Weld attempts most but is research. FX places pipeline fusion on a linear-types foundation which none of LINQ/Spark can: they're stuck with copy semantics. Overflow strategies match Akka's but add spill(path) which Akka lacks natively. Distributed mode with contract-enforced serialization at shuffle is novel.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: §11.12 is ~85 lines for six execution models — Spark's Catalyst alone is a 100+ page document. No query optimizer architecture, no cost model for parallel scheduling, no GPU kernel compilation pipeline detailed (just 'compiled to GPU kernel'), no distributed execution model (how is data partitioned? what's the shuffle protocol?). The 'same syntax, different semantics based on source type' risks surprising users: a .par()-vs-list semantics difference may break equational reasoning.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: The lazy optimizer: which rewrites, which cost model, what's the plan IR? GPU compilation: CUDA? Vulkan? SPIR-V? Kernel fusion? Host-device transfer semantics? Distributed: partitioning strategy? Fault tolerance? Checkpointing? Stragglers? Backpressure semantics in detail (credit-based? window-based?). Interaction between modes: can you mix .par() with Stream? Can .gpu() feed into .par()?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Either restrict scope (pick 3 of 6 modes and do them well — Spark spent a decade on just the distributed+lazy subset) or promote §11.12 to its own chapter with substantial depth. Specify the plan IR. Pick a GPU backend. Define data partitioning and shuffle contracts. Add composition rules between modes.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for Information Flow: FX enforces noninterference with classified-by-default labels, taint tracking, declassification policies, constant-time execution effect, and linear-erasure for secrets.

closest_prior_art.approach: Decentralized Label Model (DLM) extension of Java, principals own confidentiality/integrity policies, declassification mediated by static authority
closest_prior_art.reference: https://www.cs.cornell.edu/jif/ ; Myers et al., 'Jif: Language-based Information-flow Security in Java' (arXiv 1412.8639)

closest_prior_art.approach: Dynamic IFC library with floating current label + clearance, privilege-gated declassification
closest_prior_art.reference: https://hackage.haskell.org/package/lio ; Stefan et al., 'Flexible Dynamic Information Flow Control in the Presence of Exceptions' (JFP 2017)

closest_prior_art.approach: Full static IFC in ML-family languages with polymorphic security types
closest_prior_art.reference: Pottier & Simonet, 'Information Flow Inference for ML' (POPL 2002)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Classified-by-default inverts Jif's explicit-label discipline: programmer must actively declare `unclassified`, which eliminates entire class of 'forgot to label' leaks (Twitter/Facebook/Google password-in-log incidents). IFC unified with the other 18 dimensions in one semiring, not bolted on. Classified + linear = automatic secure-zeroing at drop without annotation.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No DLM-style multi-principal labels visible here — FX collapses to two-level `secret`/`unclassified`, which cannot express Jif-style 'Alice allows Bob to read' delegations nor Civitas-style voting semantics. No robust declassification against active attackers (Cecchetti 'Nonmalleable IFC' 2017) discussed. Chapter does not address progress-sensitive vs. progress-insensitive noninterference; most practical systems including Jif enforce only PINI and FX inherits this without saying so.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No explicit security lattice specification (only two levels shown); no principal algebra; no robust/nonmalleable declassification; no mention of covert channels (timing, termination, progress); no semantic definition of noninterference theorem FX claims to enforce.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt DLM-style principals with conjunctive/disjunctive composition even if defaults remain two-level. State the exact noninterference theorem proven (PINI, TINI, or full TSNI) with citation. Add robust-declassification guarantee in the Trust dimension.

analysis.what_fx_does: Three-level label system with `secret` (explicit high), `unclassified` (explicit safe), and default-classified, where default data cannot flow to IO/log/unclassified output without `declassify`.

closest_prior_art.approach: Explicit lattice labels required on each field; default is the join of all constraints; principals own policies
closest_prior_art.reference: Myers & Liskov, 'A Decentralized Model for Information Flow Control' (SOSP 1997); https://www.cs.cornell.edu/jif/doc/jif-3.3.0/dlm.html

closest_prior_art.approach: Wrapper type makes values non-Loggable/non-Display; must explicitly unwrap
Flow-locks and role-based declassification on Java
closest_prior_art.reference: Broberg & Sands, 'Paralocks: Role-Based Information Flow Control and Beyond' (POPL 2010)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Classified-by-default is strictly safer than explicit-label systems for the real-world failure mode (forgotten annotations leak). Integrates with the Trust/Usage/Effect dimensions so a `secret`+linear+`with IO` function gets erasure and flow check simultaneously. Only three keywords vs. Jif's complex principal/policy syntax — lower cognitive load.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Two-level lattice is strictly less expressive than Jif's DLM; no way to express 'read by Alice only' or 'endorsed by Bank' — forces everything into the binary `secret/unclassified` with declassification escape hatch, which does not scale to multi-tenant systems. Classified-by-default imposes migration tax on porting existing code (every return type must be audited).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of what `classified` joins with in the semiring table beyond §6.1's 2-element example; no integrity labels (Jif has both confidentiality and integrity); no mention of endorsement (integrity declassification); nothing on storage vs. termination channels.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Allow user-defined label lattices (as a `grade_dimension`) so projects that need multi-level MAC or DLM can specify them while keeping the classified-by-default default. Add integrity labels dual to confidentiality with `trusted`/`untrusted` parallel to `unclassified`/`secret`.

analysis.what_fx_does: Type system enforces noninterference including implicit flows through control structure — branching on secret that controls unclassified result is a compile error; result of branch on secret must itself be secret.

closest_prior_art.approach: pc label tracks security level of control context; assignments to low variable require pc=low
closest_prior_art.reference: Volpano, Smith, Irvine, 'A Sound Type System for Secure Flow Analysis' (JCS 1996)

closest_prior_art.approach: Continuations carry pc label; noninterference proven via stack discipline
Guard type σ persists in sequencing to prevent implicit flow via scheduling

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Implicit-flow ban shown by example — matches standard Volpano-Smith discipline. Composes with session types (§12.5) and effect system so implicit flow through channel sends is also rejected. Result-type upgrading (`let secret result = if flag;`) gives standard escape hatch.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Spec is 8 lines. Does not state which noninterference (PINI, TINI, TSNI) is enforced. No treatment of termination channels (`if secret; loop() else ()`) which is a known Volpano-Smith weakness — Russo-Sabelfeld 2010 shows flow-sensitive systems can leak via termination. Doesn't mention fixing the known unsoundness of naive implicit-flow typing under exceptions (Stefan et al. 2012 LIO solved this dynamically).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No pc label mechanics described; no semantic noninterference statement; no handling of exceptions/panics in secret-guarded contexts; no treatment of concurrent implicit flows via synchronization or scheduling; no discussion of progress channels.
analysis.could_we_do_better: State the noninterference theorem precisely with proof reference. Specify whether FX is progress-sensitive (rejecting `while secret > 0;`) or progress-insensitive. Treat exceptions formally — in §9's Fail/Exn effect, does a secret-guarded `fail` leak? Add test_theory cases for each known attack.

analysis.what_fx_does: Orthogonal taint classes (SQLi, XSS, PathTraversal, user-defined) track untrusted data from sources; `sanitize(Class, value, escaper: fn)` clears one class at a time; multi-class values carry multiple taints.

closest_prior_art.approach: Pluggable Java type system with @Tainted/@Untainted qualifiers and inference; subtyping @Untainted ≤ @Tainted
closest_prior_art.reference: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.18529 'Practical Type-Based Taint Checking and Inference' (2024)

closest_prior_art.approach: Runtime single-bit taint on all tainted-origin values; interpreter-level checks
Static data-flow analysis with source/sanitizer/sink specifications per rule

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Multiple orthogonal taint classes with independent tracking is a feature Perl/Ruby taint mode lacks (single bit). First-class language keywords (`taint_class`, `sanitize`) make sanitizers explicit and auditable vs. CodeQL's external rule files. Compile-time enforcement beats TaintTyper's 'eschews strict soundness' tradeoff.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No mention of implicit flows for taint (if x is tainted and if-branch assigns y, is y tainted?). No subtyping/widening rules. Sanitizer's correctness is trusted — `escape_sql` could be wrong but compiler accepts it blindly (TaintTyper has same limitation; CodeQL lets you write sanitizer specs but still relies on user correctness). No mechanism for declassifying taint analogous to `declassification_policy` for secrets.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Semantics of how taint propagates through ADTs, closures, and effects; interaction with `secret` (is tainted secret data 'double bad'?); how taint interacts with generic/polymorphic functions; no way to express partial sanitization (e.g., escape-for-HTML-attribute vs. escape-for-HTML-text); no verification that sanitizer function actually clears the specified class.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Require sanitizer functions to carry a refinement-typed postcondition proving the output is safe for the named sink class. Support taint declassification policies analogous to §12.4. Add `sanitize_via` where the escape function is part of a verified sanitizer library. Specify propagation through all dimensions.

analysis.what_fx_does: Declassification via named policy type with principal, what-description, audit flag; `declassify(value, policy: P)` performs the downgrade; policy types are decorator-annotated.

closest_prior_art.approach: declassify(value, new_label) requires static authority containing principals whose policies are weakened
Dynamic capability tokens unlock declassification of specific labels; untrusted code cannot forge
Who/What/Where/When framework for classifying declassification policies
closest_prior_art.reference: Sabelfeld & Sands, 'Dimensions and Principles of Declassification' (CSFW 2005)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Named-policy approach combined with `audit: true` flag hooks directly into Trust/Provenance dimensions — every declassification auditable by construction. Typed policy (not a label literal) means renaming/refactoring tools can track all uses. The `what:` description becomes part of the proof obligation documentation.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Only the 'who' and 'what' dimensions appear — no 'where' (line-granularity) or 'when' (temporal) policy forms. No robust declassification against active adversaries (Chong-Myers 'Nonmalleable' 2017). Authority mechanism is implicit: who is permitted to invoke `CardDisplay`? Jif's static authority computation is precise; FX's `principal: 'payment_service'` is a string — not enforced by the type system without more machinery.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: How principal strings are checked/granted; no `where`/`when` dimensions; no nonmalleable declassification; no relationship between declassification and the provenance/trust dimensions it presumably uses; no specification of what `audit: true` emits and where.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Turn `principal:` into a typed principal (module-level capability) rather than a string. Support policy composition (`allows X and Y then P`). Add where-clauses (`at call_site: payment_endpoint`) per Sabelfeld-Sands. Formally bind the audit flag to the Observability dimension so auditing becomes a grade.

analysis.what_fx_does: `with CT` effect forbids branches, match, array indexing, and variable-time ops on secret-graded values; compiler statically rejects violations; composes with refinement-typed postconditions (e.g., `aes_spec`).

closest_prior_art.approach: Secret types s32/s64 + type system preventing timing-variant operations; mechanized soundness proof in Isabelle; V8 implementation
closest_prior_art.reference: Watt, Renner, Popescu, Cauligi, Stefan, 'CT-Wasm: Type-Driven Secure Cryptography for the Web Ecosystem' (POPL 2019, arXiv:1808.01348)

closest_prior_art.approach: DSL with CT-aware transformation; compiles to LLVM; integrated into OpenSSL/libsodium
closest_prior_art.reference: Cauligi et al., 'FaCT: A Flexible, Constant-Time Programming Language' (SecDev 2017)

closest_prior_art.approach: High-assurance compiler from structured assembly; verified constant-time preservation; Spectre-resistant extensions
closest_prior_art.reference: Almeida et al., 'Jasmin: High-assurance and high-speed cryptography' (CCS 2017)

closest_prior_art.approach: End-to-end verified CT from Wasm through Cranelift to DIT-aware assembly with Ghidra dataflow verifier

analysis.where_fx_is_better: [Partially resolved per Proposal 5/6] Verdict raised from 'adequate' to 'strong'. Three cross-dimension rules (§6.8) formalize CT's interaction with other dimensions: E044 (CT × Async contradictory — matches universal practice in crypto libraries), I003 (fail on secret-dependent condition in CT is forbidden — specializes §12.5's branch rule to the control-flow effect Fail), I004 (classified data over async session requires CT or declassify). @[no_downgrade] attribute on atomic accesses (§21.2) pins the emit sequence for CT-critical code — no auto-sync optimization can weaken the ordering. The `fxc dump --atomics` audit trail gives crypto reviewers concrete visibility into the instruction sequence. CT as an effect composes with refinement types (`post r => aes_spec(key, plaintext, r)`) — FaCT/CT-Wasm don't compose with functional-correctness proof. If FX's full compilation pipeline (§20) is CT-preserving end-to-end, this matches ct-Wasmtime's ambition.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: CT preservation through the compiler's downgrade optimization pass (§21.2) relies on @[no_downgrade] being placed correctly — a programmer error that omits it on a secret-dependent atomic could cause a timing leak. Mechanized CT preservation across the full §20 pipeline is still pending. Jasmin's Spectre-v1/v2 resistance still has no FX analog. DIT mode requirements on ARMv8.6/Intel are not specified. The constant-time mask trick shown (`mask = 0u64 - widen<u64>(cond)`) is now emitted as explicit widen<u64>, which helps the CT audit but doesn't prove LLVM-style branch reintroduction can't happen — FX's own backend controls emit so this risk is lower than C/C++ but not zero until mechanized.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: CT preservation theorem through the compilation pipeline (end-to-end, including §21.2 downgrade). Spectre/speculative leakage treatment. Hardware DIT mode (ARM FEAT_DIT, Intel DOITM). Cache-oblivious memory access requirement (CT-Wasm forbids secret-indexed loads completely). Timing of division and multiplication. Interaction with the Observability dimension beyond the current qualitative treatment.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Require CT preservation as part of the §20 end-to-end compiler correctness theorem. Add `with CT, SpectreResistant` as a more restrictive effect. Specify that CT division is forbidden (or that the compiler emits constant-time software division). Publish a CT auditor tool that reads fxc dump --atomics output and flags any emit sequence whose runtime varies with secret inputs.

analysis.what_fx_does: Under classified-by-default, any `log(...)` of a classified value is a compile error; `log(f"user_id={user.id}")` is allowed iff `user.id` is unclassified.

closest_prior_art.approach: TypeScript branded type; Secret<string> is non-serializable and non-loggable unless explicitly unwrapped
Runtime sanitization and redaction in logging libraries (e.g., Log4j filters, ESAPI logger)
Secret<T> implements Debug/Display as '[REDACTED]'; macros can't print without unwrap

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Compile-time enforcement strictly stronger than runtime redaction regex (which misses new fields / international formats — exactly the Twitter/Facebook/Google scenarios). Zero annotation overhead at the call site; works by defaulting. Catches accidental `{user}` where user is a record with a secret field — regex-based and Display-based approaches miss struct-level leaks unless every field is individually wrapped.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Section is 5 lines with one example. Does not handle format-string injection (CWE-117 / log4shell category) — `log(f"{attacker_controlled}")` may still be sanitized wrt newlines/CRLF? Not stated. No integration with structured logging (JSON fields). No notion of log level, so audit-level logs cannot access more than info-level logs.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: CRLF/log-injection sanitization; log-level-based declassification; structured-logging contract; redaction-as-declassification policy; what happens with `Debug`/`Display` / `to_string` — does the type class's default leak? A classified type's `to_string` must itself be classified — stated nowhere.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Require `log` to be an effect capability with format-string refinement forbidding unsanitized untrusted concatenation. Add `LogLevel` as an Observability grade so debug-level allows more declassification than info. Make `Display` on classified types return a classified string automatically.

analysis.what_fx_does: Classified + linear = compiler automatically inserts secure_zero before deallocation with no programmer annotation; `drop(key)` triggers zero-then-free sequence.

closest_prior_art.approach: Manual #[derive(Zeroize)] + explicit zeroize() call; uses write_volatile + SeqCst fence to defeat DSE
Wrapper type auto-zeroes on Drop; still requires manual wrap at source
Standard-library functions the compiler is forbidden to elide

analysis.where_fx_is_better: No-annotation claim is strictly better than Rust (which requires #[derive(Zeroize)] or wrapping in Secret<T>) AND than C (which requires manual memset_s call). Because linearity forces each secret value to be consumed exactly once at a known point, the compiler has a precise drop site — no DSE can elide it because it is a semantic drop, not a dead store from the compiler's POV. Integrates with the compilation pipeline's `secure_zero` intrinsic at §20.2.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Does not address what zeroize cannot: register spills, stack copies on move, cold-boot attack on DRAM. rust-lang/rust#17046 (open since 2014) proves even language teams find this hard. FX implies these are solved but does not discuss: when a secret `key` is passed by value through a `Tot` function, the caller's stack slot and intermediate registers are not zeroed unless the compiler is explicitly asked. Microarchitectural side channels (Spectre leaking 'zeroized' secrets from L1) not covered.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Register clearing on return from secret-handling functions; stack copy elimination or clearing; specification of what happens when a secret value is moved through a closure capture; handling of `panic`/`fail` mid-function (does the partial state get zeroed?); interaction with `defer` (§7.11, already flagged gap #101).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify a §20-level theorem: 'At any post-drop point, no memory or register contains a copy of any part of the dropped secret.' Require callees handling secrets to clear callee-saved registers on return. Integrate with `with CT` to also clear speculative state (fence after drop). Treat fail/panic paths explicitly.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header: `machine` unifies FX's dimensions (type, ownership, effect, refinement, session, security) onto typed state machines where current state is in the type and invalid transitions are compile errors.

closest_prior_art.approach: Each state is a distinct type parameter; compile-time transition enforcement; no built-in statechart features
closest_prior_art.reference: https://stanford-cs242.github.io/f19/lectures/08-2-typestate.html ; Strom & Yemini 1986 'Typestate: A Programming Language Concept for Enhancing Software Reliability' (TSE)

closest_prior_art.approach: Hierarchical parallel state machines with broadcast, runtime interpreter, visual editor
closest_prior_art.reference: https://stately.ai/docs/xstate ; Harel 1987 'Statecharts: A Visual Formalism for Complex Systems' (Science of Computer Programming)

closest_prior_art.approach: State-based formal specification languages with invariant/refinement verification
closest_prior_art.reference: ABZ conference proceedings (Springer LNCS 10817); Leslie Lamport, 'Specifying Systems' (2002)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unifies typestate-compile-time-safety (Rust can do this but only with phantom types and boilerplate) with Harel hierarchical composition (XState runtime) with TLA+/Event-B temporal properties — three ideas in one construct. No other mainstream language has all three.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: XState has production runtime + graphical editor + actor model ecosystem; FX is a language spec only. TLA+ has TLC, Apalache, and decades of verified distributed systems (AWS S3, CosmosDB). FX's claim to verify the union is ambitious but unproven — the integration is the hard part, and §13 is ~50 pages implying it's not yet done.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Chapter-level integration theorem linking machines to the 21 dimensions; mapping to known model-checking backends (Proposal 2 added a §13.5 decidability table covering Vardi-Wolper for finite-state, @[cutoff(n)] Emerson-Kahlon for parameterized, SMT + invariant for infinite-state); bisimulation/refinement theory foundation.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Explicitly position machines as 'typestate + statecharts + TLA+' and cite each tradition. Commit to a model-checking backend (Apalache for symbolic, SPIN for LTL).

analysis.what_fx_does: `machine` block with `state S of { fields }`, `transition t : From -> To requires P; ensures Q;`, `initial` and `terminal` markers; state is threaded through the type (`ConnectionState(Connected)`); invalid transitions are compile errors.

closest_prior_art.approach: Separate struct per state; methods consume self and return next-state struct
Runtime JS/TS statechart with states/transitions/guards/actions; no compile-time type threading
State variables + actions with guards (ENABLED) and updates via primed variables; properties checked by TLC/Apalache
Machine with events = guard + action; refinement proof obligations auto-generated

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Single language construct vs. Rust's N types + impl blocks + phantom data dance. Compile-time state-in-type AND runtime behavior AND temporal verification unified (XState has none of this; TLA+ has the last two but no types).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Event-B's refinement obligations and Apalache's symbolic execution are mature; FX §13.1 is declaration syntax only. TLA+ has `UNCHANGED` and frame semantics for what isn't modified — FX §13.1 does not say what happens to unchanged state fields between transitions. State typing notation `ConnectionState(Connected)` is not obviously compatible with §3 types — `Connected` is a data constructor with fields; how does `(Connected)` without payload work?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Grammar for state-parameterized types; frame rules (what fields are implicitly unchanged); how `initial`/`terminal` compose with sub-machines; what 'connect(host: host, attempt: 0)' method on Disconnected state is (generated from transition declaration?); concurrent/interleaved transition semantics.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Ground the state-indexed type in §3.2's refinement machinery: `ConnectionState { state == Connected }`. Specify frame conditions per Event-B. Borrow TLA+'s `UNCHANGED` for fields not modified. Grammatically integrate into fx_grammar.md (currently a §13 gap).

analysis.what_fx_does: Eleven transition modifiers: `requires`, `ensures`, `with`, `inverse`, `timeout`, `retry`, `atomic`, `idempotent`, `commutative`, `monotonic`, `emits`, `on_fail` — composable on one transition.

closest_prior_art.approach: Events have guard + action; refinement adds proof obligations; atomic by construction
closest_prior_art.reference: Abrial, 'Modeling in Event-B: System and Software Engineering' (2010)

closest_prior_art.approach: Actions are ENABLED + next-state relation; atomicity implicit; fairness separate
Activities with retry policies, timeouts, compensation handlers at the runtime level
Types carry algebraic laws (commutativity, monotonicity); compiler/runtime exploits for replication
closest_prior_art.reference: Shapiro et al., 'A Comprehensive Study of Convergent and Commutative Replicated Data Types' (INRIA TR 2011)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Collects annotations that normally live in disparate places (TLA+ guard, Cadence retry policy, CRDT law, Event-B invariant) into one transition declaration. `commutative`/`monotonic` as language-level laws (with SMT verification via §16.6 algebraic structures) is more principled than Temporal's runtime labels.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Eleven modifiers is a lot. No orthogonality analysis: does `atomic` + `idempotent` + `retry` interact consistently? Temporal's retry-with-backoff is battle-tested in distributed production; FX offers `retry n strategy` without specifying what strategies exist. `emits Event(data)` — where does the event go, with what delivery semantics (at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once)? Unspecified. `on_fail` with three modes is thin compared to Saga's pivot/compensable/irreversible classification (AWS).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Interaction matrix between modifiers; retry strategy catalog; event delivery semantics and ordering guarantees; semantics of `atomic` across distributed machines (is it 2PC? Paxos? handwaving?); precedence when `idempotent(key: k)` and `commutative` conflict; whether `monotonic` requires a state lattice declaration.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Reduce to orthogonal primitives: `guard` (requires), `action` (ensures+with), and annotations with explicit algebraic law semantics (`commutative`/`monotonic` → structure instances per §16.6). Lift retry/timeout/on_fail into a separate Saga-layer construct so core transitions stay simple. Borrow AWS's compensable/pivot/irreversible classification for on_fail.

analysis.what_fx_does: Each transition can declare an `inverse t'` that the compiler proves semantically restores the pre-state; on runtime failure, the compensation chain is derived automatically from inverse declarations.

closest_prior_art.approach: Manually-coded compensating transactions per forward step; orchestrator executes chain on failure; no compile-time inverse verification
Every instruction has a reverse; execution trace is invertible by construction
closest_prior_art.reference: Yokoyama & Glück, 'A Reversible Programming Language and Its Invertible Self-Interpreter' (PEPM 2007)

closest_prior_art.approach: Workflow with compensation activities; runtime durability; no static proof of inverse correctness
Compensable / Pivot / Irreversible transaction types; explicit operator responsibility

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Compiler-proved inverse is novel at the language level — Saga implementations rely entirely on programmer correctness (AWS explicitly says 'inverses are not automatic rollbacks, they are domain-level operations'). Auto-derived compensation chain eliminates the most bug-prone manual orchestration step. Integrates with the linear/ownership system so resource restoration is typed.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Inverse transitions work only when operations are truly reversible — but real systems have pivot points (after email send, payment capture) and irreversible operations. FX example (BankTransfer) uses `ensures balance == old + amount` which is only provable if no concurrent actor modified the account between operations — Saga literature explicitly warns about this 'dirty read' problem. FX §13.3 does not address: (a) concurrent modification invalidating inverse preconditions, (b) irreversible external effects (sent email, wire transfer), (c) failure of the compensation itself. No ordering guarantee when multiple inverses must run.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Pivot/irreversible transition classification; semantics when compensation itself fails; interaction with real external IO (inverse of `send_email`?); concurrent-safety of the compensation chain; what 'restores original state' means under interference from other machines.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt AWS's compensable/pivot/irreversible classification explicitly. Require inverses carry `with Compensation(level: Strict|Weak)` to indicate whether exact restoration or semantic correction is intended. Add `on_compensation_failure` escalation per Temporal. Prove compensation soundness under linearity: if two machines concurrently ran forward, the inverse chain cannot always restore.

analysis.what_fx_does: Six composition operators: `*` product (parallel independent), `*sync(events)` synchronized product, `>>` sequence, `match` choice, `*{ while cond }` loop, hierarchical nest.

closest_prior_art.approach: Process-algebra operators on statecharts: sequence ⇒, choice |, quantified choice |x, Kleene ⋆, guard, parallel |[A]|, interleave, quantified sync
closest_prior_art.reference: Frappier et al., 'Extending statecharts with process algebra operators' (Innovations Syst Softw Eng 4, 2008); https://gric.recherche.usherbrooke.ca/en/algebraic-state-transition-diagrams/

closest_prior_art.approach: Sequential (;), choice (□, ⊓), parallel (∥A), interleave (|||), hiding, renaming
AND-states for orthogonal parallel regions with broadcast; OR-states for hierarchy
Composite, orthogonal, history, final states

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Six orthogonal operators is comparable to ASTDs' seven and richer than vanilla Harel (which has only hierarchy + AND/OR). Sequence `>>` with typed handoff of terminal data to initial data of successor is more type-aware than CSP's untyped ';'.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Missing ASTDs' quantification (`|x`, quantified parallel) — FX cannot parametrize a machine over an arbitrary number of instances without leaving the operator algebra. No hiding / internalization of events (CSP's backslash operator) — relevant for abstracting internal transitions in refinement. `*sync` syntax shown with named events but §13.4 does not specify whether it is CSP-style synchronization on event labels or Lotos parameterized synchronization. No semantic definition (operational, denotational) for the six operators.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Quantified composition (per-instance dynamic machine sets — partially addressed in §13.13 machine_set but not as a composition operator); event hiding; formal operational semantics; algebraic laws (is `>>` associative? does `*` distribute over `match`?); precedence and parsing.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add quantified operators from ASTDs explicitly. Give operational semantics (SOS rules) so the operators compose predictably. State algebraic laws as `test_machine` properties so users can rely on them. Add event hiding for refinement. Cite ASTDs as the closest prior art and explain divergences.

analysis.what_fx_does: Language-level property keywords: structural (`reachable`, `unreachable`, `deadlock_free`, `deterministic`, `terminating`, `bounded`), safety (`always`, `never`), liveness (`eventually`, `leads_to`, `responds ... within`), fairness (`fair_leads_to`, `progress`); decided by model checking for finite-state, induction+SMT for parameterized.

closest_prior_art.approach: Temporal properties in TLA (linear-time) verified by TLC explicit model checker or Apalache symbolic SMT backend
closest_prior_art.reference: Lamport, 'Specifying Systems' (2002); Konnov et al., 'TLA+ Model Checking Made Symbolic' (OOPSLA 2019)

closest_prior_art.approach: Explicit-state LTL model checker via Büchi automata; mature, industrial (Mars Pathfinder, Curiosity)
Symbolic branching-time model checker using BDDs/BMC
closest_prior_art.reference: Cimatti et al., 'NuSMV: A New Symbolic Model Verifier' (CAV 1999)

closest_prior_art.approach: Verify a property for all N processes by finite cutoff or regular abstraction
closest_prior_art.reference: Emerson & Namjoshi, 'Reasoning about Rings' (POPL 1995); Bloem et al., 'Decidability of Parameterized Verification' (Morgan & Claypool 2015)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Property keywords as first-class language forms (not Promela-style side-files, not TLA+'s parallel universe). Integration with refinement types and effect system means a property can quantify over refinement-typed state. `responds P Q within d` with explicit time bound is more operationally useful than LTL's leads-to.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: TLA+ and Promela have decades of mature tooling, counterexample visualization, distributed proof-reuse (AWS, MongoDB), and textbook treatment. FX §13.5 is 20 lines declaring the keyword exist. For parameterized machines, 'proved by induction with SMT' is not automatic — decidability of parameterized verification is well-known to be hard (undecidable in general; see Bloem-Jacobs-Khalimov-Konnov-Rubin-Veith-Widder); FX provides no hint on fragment or proof technique. Fairness keywords `fair_leads_to`/`progress` are not defined — which fairness (weak/strong/just)?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Temporal-logic fragment specification (LTL? CTL? TLA? propositional mu-calculus?); semantics of each keyword; which fragment is decidable; fairness flavors; how `within d` duration interacts with logical time; counterexample generation; connection to §13.18 temporal-logic-for-hardware (`G`, `F`, `X` used there — two different temporal sub-languages in one chapter).
analysis.could_we_do_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 2] Verdict raised from 'weak' to 'strong' after Proposal 2 adopted past-free propositional LTL over sized streams (§3.5, dim 20) as the single temporal logic for both machine (§13.5) and hardware (§13.18) properties. Four primitives (G, F, X, U) live as ordinary FX functions in std/ltl imported via open; structural predicates (deadlock_free, deterministic, bounded_size, reachable) are library functions in std/machine_props; fairness uses weak_fair/strong_fair as predicates composed via implication. All prior English-keyword sugar (always, never, leads_to, eventually, responds within, fair_leads_to, progress, plus the structural keyword list) is deleted — 13 contextual keywords removed from §2.3 machine block list. Property blocks use uniform assert expr; statements (§10.4 mechanism). Decidability table explicit: finite-state -> Vardi-Wolper automaton product; parameterized with @[cutoff(n)] -> Emerson-Kahlon symmetry reduction; infinite-state -> user-supplied inductive invariant. Earlier note preserved below.

Commit to one temporal-logic fragment (likely TLA or LTL for linear-time, since §13.18 uses LTL operators) and cite the mechanized semantics. Specify fairness as weak-fair by default with `strong_fair` opt-in. Identify the decidable parameterized fragment (e.g., `Lossy Channel Systems`, `WSTS`). Integrate `responds ... within` with the Complexity and Lifetime dimensions.

analysis.what_fx_does: Refinement declarations with explicit state-mapping functions that prove an implementation machine's paths project onto a specification machine's paths.

closest_prior_art.approach: Refinement via gluing invariants and discharged proof obligations; supports superposition and data refinement; 'elaborates' relations map concrete to abstract events
Trace-based refinement via state-mapping (refinement mapping) with liveness reasoning via temporal logic; Lamport's primed/unprimed variable style
Trace subsetting refinement formalized via mapping to Event-B

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Refinement is a first-class language construct, not a side artifact: the mapping function lives in the source file and is discharged by the compiler rather than a separate Rodin/TLAPS toolchain. FX integrates refinement with effect/security/usage dimensions (§13.8) which Event-B/TLA+ cannot express at all.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The spec is silent on whether this is forward simulation, backward simulation, trace refinement, or failure-divergence refinement — critical distinction (cf. TLA+ debates on downward/upward simulation). Silent on stutter/tau/internal steps (so caching with no spec-observable effect is unspecified). No gluing invariant; the mapping is purely functional and cannot express relations richer than total functions. Event-B can do partial mapping; FX example cannot. No explicit liveness/fairness refinement as TLA+ provides.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No semantics for the refinement proof obligation. No handling of stuttering (multiple impl steps mapping to one spec step is implicit in the match arms but never formally defined). No guidance when specification is nondeterministic. No mechanism for gluing invariants in the sense of Event-B. No support for liveness refinement. No compositional refinement (refinement of refinement, n-ary chains).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Event-B's full refinement discipline: gluing invariant + simulation proof obligations (INV, GRD, SIM, FIS, WFIS), distinguish superposition from data refinement, support partial mapping where concrete state includes ghost history for intermediate steps, and require explicit specification of stuttering behavior. Add liveness refinement via TLA+-style WF/SF annotations.

analysis.what_fx_does: Applies the graded type system semiring (§6) to state machine paths, summing grades across transitions and verifying linear resources reach grade 0 at terminal states.

closest_prior_art.approach: Encode state transitions via phantom state parameter, linear consumption prevents state reuse
Graded modal types with semiring-indexed grades; uses Z3 to discharge grade constraints

closest_prior_art.system: Rust typestate crate / F* effectful typestate
