analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Associated types vs functional dependencies choice, multi-parameter type classes, HKT (alluded in §3.13 but not connected here), type-family style computation, specialization mechanism.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Commit to associated types (Rust-style) rather than FunDeps; spell out how coherence interacts with graded dimensions (security, lifetime). Add Racordon/Flesselle/Pham 2025 discussion of Swift-style witness tables.

analysis.what_fx_does: Single surface syntax for runtime polymorphism: explicit existential records on the kernel Sigma type (Appendix H.3). Form: `type Closeable = exists (T: type), { val: own T; close: fn(own T) -> unit with IO };`. The record's fields ARE the vtable — no compiler-generated dispatch table, no hidden trait-object metadata. Construction builds the record directly (`Closeable { val: c, close: Connection.close }`); consumption calls fields directly (`item.close(item.val)`). Multi-method composition is field aggregation. Kernel translation: nested Sigma types per Appendix H.3. Proposal 12 explicitly removed `dyn Trait` and `Any` from the language.

closest_prior_art.approach: exists/forall via GADTs or ExistentialQuantification: `data Widget = forall a. Widget { state :: a, draw :: a -> IO () }`; records carry the methods as fields exactly like FX's approach
First-class modules + packaged existentials via GADTs; `(module S with type t = ...)` packs type AND values AND substructures; the most expressive prior art
`dyn Trait` (pointer + vtable) hides the vtable behind a trait declaration with dyn-compatibility rules (no Self in return, no generic methods); trait_upcasting stabilized 1.86 (2024)
Refinement types + path-dependent types from DOT calculus simulate existentials via structural subtyping; bulky but expressive
closest_prior_art.reference: DOT calculus papers, Amin et al. OOPSLA 2016

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Vtable layout is visible at the type level — readers see exactly which methods the existential supports with full signatures including modes, effects, and refinements. Linear `own T` state composes with destruction semantics naturally: the `close: fn(own T) -> unit with IO` field consumes the packed value, and linearity discipline prevents use-after-close. No dyn-compatibility restrictions: methods can return `Self`-shaped types, carry generic parameters, and involve linear types uniformly. Zero compiler-generated metadata — the record layout is all there is. Kernel stays tiny (just Sigma). Method resolution lattice simplifies to Levels 0-2 (impl > instance > class default) — fully static.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Verbosity at definition sites: ~60 characters to write `exists (T: type), { val: own T; close: fn(own T) -> unit with IO }` vs ~12 for `dyn Closeable`. Named existentials at stdlib level amortize this for common cases but new abstractions pay the full cost. No automatic derivation from type classes: a trait `Closeable<T>` does not auto-produce an existential `Closeable` — users write both. Multi-method existentials that share methods with a `+`-composition idiom require aggregate-by-copy at the record type (no structural subtyping over existentials). Upcasting from `{ val; method1; method2 }` to `{ val; method1 }` is not automatic; users must repack.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Stdlib catalog of named existentials for common patterns (Closeable, Readable, Iterable, Drawable, Serializable). Compiler sugar for type-class-to-existential packing — e.g., `class.pack(x)` producing the canonical existential for a trait. Upcasting semantics: when can an `exists T. { f: ..., g: ... }` flow to an `exists T. { f: ... }`? Variance of existentials in generic contexts. Performance validation that the emitted code matches Rust's `dyn Trait` fat pointer.
analysis.could_we_do_better: (1) Add `@[existential]` attribute on type classes to auto-generate the canonical existential form. (2) Specify upcasting as a field-subset coercion between existential types (decidable at compile time since both types are records). (3) Ship a stdlib catalog of named existentials alongside the trait definitions. (4) Document migration patterns for Rust developers: when to use closed enum (finite variants), contract (boundary), existential (open polymorphism with dispatch).

analysis.what_fx_does: Algebraic structures (Monoid, CommMonoid, Group, Ring, Lattice, etc.) declared as ordinary type classes marked with the '@[structure]' attribute. Proposal 12 part 2 rewrote §16.6 from the old 'structure CommMonoid(T) ... end structure' form to '@[structure] class CommMonoid<T: type> ... end class' form matching §2.3 keyword commitment. No separate 'structure' keyword exists — the @[structure] attribute signals that 'law' clauses in the class body are optimization-relevant (SMT-verified against each instance, exploited for parallel reduction / factoring / short-circuit / deduplication). Laws are propositions the compiler checks at instance-declaration site — P006 on instance failing a declared law. Hierarchy (Semigroup → Monoid → CommMonoid → Group → AbelianGroup → Semiring → Ring → CommRing → Field; PartialOrder → TotalOrder → Lattice → BoundedLattice) lives in Std.Algebra as cascading @[structure] class declarations.

closest_prior_art.approach: Algebraic class hierarchy with proved laws; AMO-Lean converts Mathlib lemmas to equality-saturation rewrite rules for verified C/Rust codegen
Locale hierarchy for algebraic structures; more general than type classes, multi-instantiation allowed
Factories/builders; canonical structures + unification hints

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Explicit cradle-to-grave: declare law, SMT verifies, optimizer exploits. AMO-Lean does this manually (handcrafting rewrite rules from Mathlib theorems); FX promises automation. Tight integration with 21-dim grade system lets e.g. CommMonoid+Tot auto-parallelize.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: SMT cannot close most non-trivial algebraic laws (e.g., monoid laws over inductive types need induction, not SMT). Lean 4 grind+ring tactics use a mix of decision procedures and inductive proofs; FX's 'SMT-only' claim is dubious for real algebra. Mathlib contains millions of lines of non-SMT-decidable algebra; FX hand-wave of 'law verified by SMT' undersells this.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: How structures compose (is Ring = Monoid + Group? multiple-inheritance diamond problem à la Mathlib's IsDomain dependency ordering?). No mention of canonical structures or unification hints for hierarchy navigation. No statement of which laws actually go to SMT vs need inductive proof.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt HB-style (Cohen/Sakaguchi/Tassi 2020) factories+builders for hierarchy evolution without breakage. Allow law proofs via tactics beyond SMT (induction, ring, field, calc). Reference AMO-Lean as concrete proof-carrying optimizer precedent.

analysis.what_fx_does: Declaration-site variance annotations on type parameters (+a covariant, -a contravariant, no mark = inferred from usage: read=covariant, write=contravariant, both=invariant).

closest_prior_art.approach: Declaration-site variance (+A, -A), but NO inference — user must annotate
No user-visible variance annotations; variance inferred structurally per type parameter from uses in fields, checked by compiler
Optional variance annotations (+'a, -'a) with inference otherwise

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Mixing declaration-site annotation with structural inference matches OCaml best practice; Rust-style pure inference surprises users, Scala-style pure declaration is verbose. FX gets both worlds.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Description is 6 lines and gives no algorithm. How does inference work for mutually recursive types? Recursive type variance (FX's codata streams, §3.5)? GADTs (§3.3 pattern matching mentions GADT patterns)? OCaml needed 15 years to stabilize its variance-inference algorithm (see Pottier's type inference papers) — FX dismisses in a paragraph.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Algorithm for variance inference (positivity-check, fixpoint). Interaction with higher-kinded types (§3.13 mentions `f: Type -> Type`). Interaction with refinement types (does `list<+a>` require monotone refinement?). Variance of existentials (§16.5).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the actual inference algorithm (OCaml's or Scala 3's) with a worked example of mutual recursion. Address variance of dependent types.

analysis.what_fx_does: Explicit table of 16 features rejected from FX (classes, virtual, protected, abstract classes, multiple inheritance, implicit ctors/dtors, operator overloading, implicit this mutation, null, downcasting, friends, implicit conversions, template metaprogramming, syntax macros, custom operators), each with a 'use instead' column.

closest_prior_art.approach: Similar exclusions; no inheritance, no null, no operator overloading (traits like Add are structured); no ad-hoc macros (declarative macros + proc macros)
No inheritance, no generics in original design (added 2022), no operator overloading, no exceptions; composition + embedding
No operator overloading, no hidden control flow, no implicit conversions, comptime replaces macros

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Zig-level philosophical discipline (no hidden control flow, no ambient conversions) combined with Rust-level safety (no null, no downcasting) and explicit substitutes. The table format with 'use instead' is pedagogically strong.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Some exclusions are weakly defended: 'operator overloading → unreadable' is folkore, not a research-grounded claim (Scala, Haskell, C++ disagree with concrete counter-cases). 'Custom operators → unreadable in unfamiliar code' applies equally to FX's custom type class methods. 'Implicit conversions → silent bugs' but FX widens u8→u16 silently (§3.8).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Rationale for allowing type-class method dispatch (which IS a form of ad-hoc polymorphism not so different from operator overloading). No discussion of variadic functions or currying.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Either allow operator overloading for types implementing algebraic structures (Lean/Coq/Haskell style, constrained), or state why named methods are unambiguously better (with citation to e.g. Scala experience reports).

analysis.what_fx_does: Methods taking 'self' (consuming) and returning the same type enable chaining. Proposal 12 part 2 updates: (1) spread syntax fix — body uses '{ ...self, field: value }' not '{ self with field: value }' (Proposal 4 committed 'with' to effect-annotation-only); (2) 'own self' builder pattern is the ONLY supported form — the 'ref mut self -> ref mut Self' alternative is compile error M013 (§16.1). Each method call consumes the old value and produces a new one; the compiler generates in-place update via linearity so the runtime cost matches imperative mutation while the observable semantics are 'one new value per method'. The one place FX systematically hides mutation behind immutable semantics — sound because linearity guarantees a single owner.

closest_prior_art.approach: Consuming builder (self -> Self) vs mut-ref builder (&mut self -> &mut Self); typestate pattern with PhantomData for compile-time required-field checking
Fluent builders + @DslMarker for DSL scoping; builder classes with receivers
UFCS + auto-return-type inference makes every free function chainable

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Linear `self` consumed at each step gives FX compile-time guarantee of single-use builder (can't accidentally reuse after .send()) — superior to Rust's Drop-based runtime checks.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No typestate pattern shown — Rust builders use typestate (phantom types) to statically forbid .build() without required fields. FX's example cannot (as written) enforce 'url must be set before send'. No mention of DSL-style builders (Kotlin @DslMarker, Swift @resultBuilder).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Typestate encoding for required fields. Interaction with immutability (can a @[copy] builder also chain?). Auto-impl for common patterns. No mention of error accumulation in chains.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add a typestate example showing how FX's refinement types can encode required-fields statically (e.g., HttpRequest { url : string { length > 0 } } requires url be set before build). Reference the typed-builder Rust crate.

analysis.what_fx_does: NEW SECTION added under Proposal 12 part 2. Closed stdlib-fixed catalog of five derivable traits via @[derive(...)] attribute: Eq (structural equality), Ord (total order + derived eq/neq/lt/le/gt/ge from compare), Show (to_string), Hash (for maps/sets), Default (default value from field defaults). Each derive generates the canonical unambiguous implementation from the type's shape; each requires the same trait on every field. Parameterized types REQUIRE explicit 'where' bounds — T065 on @[derive(Eq)] type pair<a> { x: a; y: a } without 'where Eq(a)'; rigor-first, no auto-bound inference. Closed catalog: user packages cannot add new derivable traits (T066 on @[derive(UnknownTrait)]); custom code generation uses comptime explicitly at the call site. Excluded from catalog because dedicated mechanisms exist: Copy (@[copy] attribute), Send/Sync (not FX concepts), Arbitrary (@[arbitrary] per §23.2), Serialize (contracts §14).

closest_prior_art.approach: Most mature deriving system with multiple strategies (stock, newtype, anyclass, via) plus GHC Generics for shape-based derivation
'#[derive(Eq, Ord, Hash, Default, Debug, Clone)]' with procedural macros allowing user-defined derives via the syn/quote crates
'derives' clause backed by compiler-generated Mirror instances exposing type-level structure; intentionally low-level
'data class' auto-generates equals/hashCode/toString/copy/componentN; not extensible to user-defined derivations
'Codable' protocol uses compiler synthesis for Encodable/Decodable conformance; Equatable / Hashable auto-derivable for structs/enums of conforming types

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Closed catalog eliminates the Rust procedural-macro supply-chain risk where '#[derive(MyCustom)]' ships arbitrary code generation that an agent reading the type cannot trace. The five stdlib-fixed derives have unambiguous semantics from the type's shape — a reader knows exactly what @[derive(Eq)] generates without consulting a derive-macro implementation. Explicit 'where' bounds (rigor-first) match FX's philosophy of stated-at-binding-site rather than inferred; cleaner for LLM-first primary user. Exclusion of Copy / Send / Serialize from the derive catalog is principled — each has a dedicated FX mechanism with better properties than a generic derive (Copy has its own attribute, Serialize uses contracts with boundary validators, etc.).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No user-defined derives means libraries providing custom traits (say, a serde equivalent) cannot ship their trait with automatic derivation — users must call comptime code generation explicitly at each site. More verbose. Rust's ecosystem benefits enormously from serde / clap / strum / etc. all being derivable. FX's equivalent ecosystem must live in the closed catalog — stdlib-maintainer responsibility. Explicit 'where' bounds mean verbosity on parameterized types; Rust's auto-bound is often the ergonomic default. Narrow catalog means features like Clone (distinct from Copy via deep clone) aren't derivable — stdlib must provide them separately.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Precise syntactic rules for @[derive(Show)] — what does to_string format look like for records, variants, tuples, nested? Conventions for @[derive(Ord)] on variants with mixed field types (lexicographic order of declared variants? alphabetical?). Interaction with comptime-generated types (does @[derive] work on types defined via staged programming §17.2?). Escape hatch for manual instance that overrides a derived one — is this allowed, or does derive produce the final word?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the canonical format for each derive's output (JSON-like for Show, lexicographic-variant-order for Ord, commutative hash for Hash, etc.). Consider adding @[derive(Equiv)] for custom equivalence classes (quotient types §3.7). Document the stdlib trait signature for each derivable trait alongside §16.10 so readers can trace from 'derives Eq' to 'stdlib Eq class definition'.

analysis.what_fx_does: NEW SECTION added under Proposal 12 part 2. Specifies two-tier operator dispatch model: (1) arithmetic and bitwise operators (+, -, *, /, %, &, |, ^, ~, <<, >>) are compiler INTRINSICS defined only for built-in numeric / bool / bits(n) / signed_bits(n) / trits(n) types — user types cannot extend them and must use named methods (Matrix.add(a, b) rather than overloaded +); (2) comparison operators (==, !=, <, <=, >, >=) DISPATCH through stdlib Eq and Ord type classes — user types participate via @[derive(Eq)] / @[derive(Ord)] or manual instance. Boolean not/and/or intrinsics; constructor-test 'is' intrinsic; pipe '|>' syntactic desugar; range '..'/'..=' produce range<T>. Rationale: under LLM-first primary-user constraint, 'a + b' must have locally-knowable effects (Tot for numerics); allowing user-type overloading would allow arbitrary code with arbitrary effects, regressing readability. Comparisons are the exception because they are structural (Eq / Ord classes have Tot-only methods by convention).

closest_prior_art.approach: Dispatches ALL operators through type classes — (+) has type Num a => a -> a -> a, resolved at compile time via dictionary passing or specialization
Dispatches via std::ops traits (Add, Sub, Mul, etc.); only trait-backed operators can be overloaded; no custom operators
'operator fun' modifier on specifically-named member/extension functions (plus, minus, times, etc.)
Allows both overloading existing operators and defining entirely new custom operators with custom precedence groups; maximally flexible
Dual-dispatch via __add__ / __radd__ etc. magic methods; every operator user-overloadable
No operator overloading whatsoever; user types use named methods

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The two-tier split (intrinsic-only for arithmetic, class-dispatched for comparison) is locally readable: 'a + b' on FX cannot mean 'I/O, allocation, or fail'; 'a == b' can go through user-defined Eq but Eq has Tot-only convention. Consistent with §16.8's 'no operator overloading' philosophy without sacrificing derive(Eq) / derive(Ord) ergonomics. Easier for agents: the +/-/* operators in FX source are fixed semantics; only comparison requires checking the Eq/Ord instance. Go's approach (no overloading at all) is the alternative; FX's exception for comparisons preserves the ergonomic use of sort/hash/map without losing the 'operators are intrinsics' readability property.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Users porting math-heavy code from Haskell / Python / Swift / C++ lose the ability to write 'a + b' for matrices, complex numbers, vectors, fractions. Must use Matrix.add(a, b), Complex.mul(a, b), etc. This is a real ergonomic regression for numerical-libraries authors. Mitigation: stdlib provides named-methods conventions; pipe-based chaining 'a |> Matrix.add(b)' is idiomatic. Swift's approach (full custom operator support) gives math-DSL authors a better story but at the cost of readability elsewhere.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Precise list of which built-in types each arithmetic intrinsic supports (e.g., does '%' work on f32? on bits(n)? on trits(n)?). Interaction with overflow-mode declarations (§3.1): does '+' respect with overflow(wrap) on the enclosing function? Should probably be specified as 'intrinsics inherit the enclosing function's overflow mode'. Detailed specification of the Eq / Ord classes in stdlib including total-order laws.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the arithmetic-intrinsic table explicitly: for each operator, list which built-in types it applies to, what overflow mode applies, what CT behavior on secret-graded arguments. Consider a 'Plus' class for user types that want + (Swift-style) but restrict to Tot + @[structure] + no-overflow semantics to preserve readability. Evaluate later if this lands or if named methods suffice — likely named methods suffice.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter declaring §17's scope: compile-time evaluation via comptime, staged code(T), decorators, custom attributes, physical units, and codata operations.

closest_prior_art.approach: comptime keyword, no separate generics system, full compile-time execution of arbitrary code
Typed multi-stage programming with brackets/escapes; code(T) is the staging type
const fn + proc macros + declarative macros (three separate systems)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unified vocabulary (comptime, code(T), decorators) subsuming Rust's three-system split (const fn, decl macro, proc macro) while being typed (unlike Template Haskell's untyped default and Rust's proc macro token streams).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chapter is a 1-line header with no metatheory. Zig's comptime is well-specified after ~8 years of field use; TH/MetaOCaml have formal type theories (Davies/Pfenning 2001; Xie/Pickering/Löh/Wu/Yallop/Wang POPL 2022). FX must pick one.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Relationship between comptime and staged code(T) (Zig comptime evaluates, code(T) defers). Ordering semantics (when do comptime blocks run relative to each other?). Effect restrictions on comptime (no IO to the FS, presumably — not stated).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Cite λ○ (Xie/Wang 2025 ICFP splice variables) and Layered Modal Type Theory (Hu/Pientka ESOP 2024) for the staged type system.

analysis.what_fx_does: comptime marker forces compile-time evaluation of an expression/function/block; used for sizeof, lookup tables, platform-conditional SIMD dispatch; if not evaluable at compile time, compile error.

closest_prior_art.approach: comptime keyword; subsumes generics, macros, reflection; runs any pure code at compile time with same syntax as runtime
CTFE (Compile-Time Function Evaluation) + static if + template mixins
#run directive for compile-time execution, build process steering via compile-time code

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Platform-conditional dispatch `comptime if platform.simd_width >= 512` is exactly Zig's strongest pattern; FX inherits it. 'Compile error if not evaluable' matches Zig's strict semantics, avoiding D's constexpr vs non-constexpr split.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No mention of comptime-visible reflection (Zig's @typeInfo, @typeName, @Type for synthesizing types). No mention of comptime allocation (Zig has comptime allocator discipline). No story for comptime effects (IO-in-comptime, compile-time file reads — Zig allows @embedFile). Jai's 'compile-time metaprogramming can drive the build itself' is a much stronger claim than FX makes.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Type-level comptime (synthesize types at compile time, Zig: `fn Pair(comptime A: type, comptime B: type) type`). Reflection/introspection API. Comptime-only effect (Ghost effect partially covers this but isn't linked). Restrictions on comptime (can it call FFI? read env vars?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Explicitly specify: (a) comptime can synthesize types (Zig pattern), (b) comptime has a Ghost-like effect row restricting side-effects, (c) @typeInfo-like reflection API. Cite Kelley's 'Zig blurs the line' blog post.

analysis.what_fx_does: Typed staged programming: code(T) is the type of fragments producing T; `(expr) quotes, ~expr splices; supports power-function unrolling, regex precompilation. Subsumes macro-like codegen.

closest_prior_art.approach: .<e>. bracket, .~e escape; typed multi-stage; recent additions: val_code subtype, let-insertion, scope-extrusion detection
[||e||] typed quote, $$(e) typed splice; Code monad with Quote type class for stage-polymorphism
inline + quotes ('{...}, ${...}); typed splices; integration with type-level programming
let-splice bindings; sharing and reuse of splice computations; inspired by typed TH target calculus

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Syntax `(~rest)(x)` and `run(...)` is cleaner than Template Haskell's `$$(...)` or MetaOCaml's `.<...>.`. By adopting typed quotation from the start, FX avoids the TH untyped-by-default legacy.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Only *two* stages (comptime vs runtime) apparent — MetaOCaml and typed TH allow arbitrary N-stage. No mention of cross-stage persistence (CSP) — the 20-year problem that MetaOCaml just solved (Kiselyov 2024). No scope-extrusion handling. No stage-polymorphism (Quote type class). Doesn't address Xie et al. POPL 2022 result that typed TH + polymorphism + type classes = impredicativity headaches.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: N-stage generality, scope extrusion detection, cross-stage persistence rules, stage polymorphism, code pattern matching (λ○pat), integration with type classes (Xie POPL 2022).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Xie/Wang λ○ (ICFP 2025) let-splice bindings for splice-sharing. Specify scope extrusion detection (MetaOCaml 2024). Decide on N-stage vs 2-stage up front and cite rationale.

analysis.what_fx_does: Decorators are type-safe function transformers: @[cached] wraps fn f(x)=>... in memoization; decorators compose right-to-left as @[cached, retry(3)].

closest_prior_art.approach: Decorators with ParamSpec/Concatenate for signature-preserving transformers; runtime execution
#[derive(...)], #[attribute] procedural macros operating on token streams, compile-time
inline methods + @main annotation + metaprogramming via macros (not decorators per se)
Annotations + bytecode weaving (AspectJ, ByteBuddy) for runtime decoration

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Type-safe by construction (signature preserved by type system), compile-time (no Python runtime overhead), composable (not token-stream-fragile like Rust proc macros). Declaratively named (not ad-hoc like #[attribute]).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Example decorator uses a mutable `ref(empty_map())` cache — in FX's linear-by-default setting, this needs `with Alloc`, `Write`, and probably `with Reentrant`. Not shown. The 'composes right-to-left' is Python's convention but NOT mentioned how it interacts with effects (does `@[cached, io_log]` apply cache-then-log or log-then-cache — and which effects does the result have?). No story for decorator introspection of the wrapped function's signature (PEP 612 ParamSpec/Concatenate).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Decorator type signature's own grade row (can @[cached] change the effect row? must it be effect-polymorphic?). How decorators see/modify their wrapped function's pre/post/decreases clauses. Decorator hygiene (symbol conflicts). Decorator visibility (pub fn vs private).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify decorator as a function `code(T->R) -> code(T->R)` in staged programming terms (unifying §17.2 and §17.3). Require effect-polymorphism (Concatenate-style). Reject mutation state inside decorator without explicit @[stateful].

analysis.what_fx_does: Custom attributes are pure metadata (@[route(...)], @[table(...)], @[json(rename_all: camelCase)]) never changing parsing/typechecking; read by comptime generators; code compiles with or without them.

closest_prior_art.approach: Inert attributes carry metadata read by derive/attribute macros; cfg/cfg_attr are active
@Annotation with RetentionPolicy (SOURCE/CLASS/RUNTIME); runtime reflection available
[AttributeUsage(...)]; Assembly/Type/Method metadata with runtime reflection

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Principled invariant 'No attribute can make invalid code compile or valid code fail' is stronger than Java/C# (runtime reflection changes behavior) and matches Rust inert attribute ethos. Makes LSP/agent tools reliable — attributes cannot change semantics, so refactoring is safer.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The claim 'never change parsing or type checking' conflicts with @[copy] (§5.4 — changes type's mode to unrestricted), @[priority(1)] (§13.15 — affects preemption), @[declassification_policy] (§12.4 — enables declassify), @[event_sourced] (§13.23 — generates code). Either these are NOT 'custom attributes' (then state that), or the claim is false. Needs crisp distinction.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Who owns attribute namespaces (crate/module level? global?). Attribute argument type-checking (are `@[route("GET", "/api/users")]` arguments typed?). Attribute inheritance through traits/impls. Attribute scoping (function/type/parameter/expression).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Split attributes into two formal categories: (a) language-reserved attributes (copy, priority, route) with defined semantics, (b) user custom attributes (pure metadata, ignored by compiler). Specify typed-argument rules (like Rust's attribute proc macros).

analysis.what_fx_does: Physical units as first-class types via `dimension Length; type meters = qty(Length); type velocity = qty(Length/Time);` with compile-time dimension algebra; runtime is bare f64 (zero overhead); mismatched dimensions are compile errors.

closest_prior_art.approach: Built-in [<Measure>] type; inference of units in expressions; supports rational exponents; widely used
Library-based; phantom types + macros; zero runtime cost; integer exponents only
Header-only template library; compile-time dimensional analysis; C++20/23 concepts
Type-level naturals for dimensions; GHC type families

analysis.where_fx_is_better: First-class language support (like F#) not library (unlike Rust uom, C++ mp-units). Syntactic `qty(Length/Time^2)` matches mathematical notation directly. Zero overhead explicit (bare f64). Integrates with FX's existing refinement system for bounded physical quantities.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: F# has been production-ready since 2007 with rational exponents; FX's section is 6 lines and doesn't say whether exponents can be rational (Planck-scale physics needs square-root-of-units). No mention of unit systems (SI, CGS, imperial), conversions, or polymorphic units (F#'s `'u -> 'v`). No story for how to add new base dimensions without recompilation. No story for multidimensional tensors with units (critical for §3.6).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Rational exponents (sqrt(Length)). Unit conversions between systems. Polymorphic unit parameters (generic over dimension). Integration with tensors (§3.6). Scaling prefixes (kilo, mega). Non-dimensional constants (π, e).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Support rational exponents (Haskell dimensional, Kenniston Quantity). Define unit conversion as algebraic isomorphism. Allow dimension polymorphism. Reference F# as the ergonomic target.

analysis.what_fx_does: take(n, s) consumes n elements of a stream by inductive recursion on nat; zip_with constructs via unfold with head/tail clauses — coalgebraic destructor-based definition.

closest_prior_art.approach: Stream defined by its observations head/tail via copattern matching; coverage checker verifies exhaustive destructor cases; sized types guarantee productivity
CoFix + cofix; guardedness checker — historically problematic, leading to copattern-like refactoring
Lazy streams (infinite lists) by default; productivity not statically checked; bottom is allowed

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Decreases-annotated take with explicit unfold (§3.5 codata constructor) matches the Abel/Pientka POPL 2013 copattern design — the modern solution. Terminal consumer paired with coalgebraic constructor is the cleanest story in the coinduction literature.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Six lines, two code snippets, and zero discussion of: (a) productivity checking (guardedness vs sized types vs copatterns — gap #172 in FX's own gaps.json mentions this for §3.5), (b) subject reduction under coinduction (the bug that forced Agda's musical notation and later copatterns), (c) η-rules for codata (Agda explicitly BANS eta for coinductive records because it loops the type-checker). Kiselyov-tier caveats entirely absent.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Productivity check algorithm. Guardedness rule. Interaction with codata + mutation + security grades (can you have a secret stream?). Bisimulation proofs (§13.19 mentions this for hardware — no general codata bisimulation). Eta-rules (or explicit ban). Corecursive principles (e.g., paramorphism on codata).
analysis.could_we_do_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 1 + rigor-first] Verdict raised from 'weak' to 'adequate' after Proposal 1 adopted Dimension 20 (Size) with 'with Productive' effect + guardedness check for §3.5 codata, plus the tier-system refactor of §6.3 that classifies each dimension as Tier S (semiring), L (lattice+validity), T (typestate), or F (foundational). Rigor-first eliminates all inference sites with explicit surface syntax per Appendix E. Earlier note preserved below.

Cite Abel/Pientka POPL 2013 copatterns, adopt sized types for productivity (reduces to type-checking), explicitly ban eta for codata (Agda precedent), add bisimulation principle as a built-in (not just for hardware).

analysis.what_fx_does: §18 chapter: declares FX has native bit-level types (bits/signed_bits/trits), bit formats with virtual fields, register access modes, clock-domain tracking, a synthesizable subset, pipeline sugar, and ISA-refinement proofs — all in the same language used for software.

closest_prior_art.approach: Single language for HW design + verification via guarded atomic actions, strong type system for bit manipulation, pure combinational expressions separated from Action types
Two-language stack: Sail for ISA spec, Chisel for RTL, separate refinement tooling via JasperGold/SymbiYosys
Haskell-derived HDL with full synthesizing compiler to Verilog/VHDL and type-safe clock domain crossing

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unifies software, ISA spec, RTL, and drivers in one language with one type system; the graded type system natively represents clock domains, linear register access (RC reads), and information-flow security — which are bolt-ons in Chisel/Bluespec/Sail. Co-verification from a single register_file definition is genuinely novel.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Bluespec's GAA model provides proven compositional semantics for multi-rule pipelines that FX's pipeline sugar handwaves. Sail has a mature RISC-V model adopted by RISC-V International with HOL4/Isabelle/Coq/Lean backends. Chisel has the battle-tested FIRRTL/CIRCT compiler and a large production ecosystem (Rocket, SiFive chips). FX's §18 is 370 lines vs Sail's ~6000-page manual plus years of industrial shakeout.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No treatment of: asynchronous logic, multi-cycle primitives (BRAM, DSP), area/timing/power analysis, Moore vs Mealy machine distinction, inout/tristate nets, simulation semantics, testbenches, waveform dumping, FPGA-specific primitives (LUTs, carry chains, hard IP), technology mapping, place-and-route integration, floorplanning.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Chapter is reasonably scoped but underbaked relative to 20+ years of HDL evolution. Could adopt Bluespec-style rule semantics as the underlying formal model for machines, reference Koika/PLDI20 for formal rule scheduling, specify interface to LLVM CIRCT for codegen, and add an explicit non-synthesizable simulation-only subset like SystemVerilog's distinction.

analysis.what_fx_does: Declares `format` types as named bit layouts over `bits(n)` with MSB-first fields, compiler-generated extraction/insertion, and virtual fields that reassemble scattered bits (e.g., RISC-V B-type immediate) with zero-gate dot access.

closest_prior_art.approach: Packed structs give named bitfield access; packed unions give overlapping views; no native 'virtual field' but equivalent via union members
Bitvector concatenation patterns with `@` operator, bidirectional encode/decode `<->` mappings generate both encoder and decoder from one definition
Procedural-macro based packed structs with getters/setters, enum-in-field support, compile-time size check
Bit-precise words with polymorphic width, reverse/join/split for rearrangement, compiles to VHDL/Verilog

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Virtual fields that automatically reassemble scattered bits (RISC-V B/J immediates) are more declarative than SV packed unions (which only overlay contiguous layouts) and more elegant than Rust bitvec manual shifting. Sail requires explicit `@` concat in the mapping; FX's `virtual imm = bits{imm_12, imm_11, imm_10_5, imm_4_1, 1b0}` reads like the ISA manual.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Sail's encode/decode mappings are bidirectional in one declaration — FX doesn't specify whether `format` supports symmetric decode→encode. No treatment of big-endian vs little-endian field order, mixed-endianness layouts, non-byte-aligned offsets across byte boundaries, or fields with type-parameterized width. Modular-bitfield has first-class enum-in-field support; FX's `bits(n)` fields are only numeric.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Formal specification of: virtual field write semantics (how does assigning to .imm update the five scattered bit positions?), overlap detection between virtual and real fields, alignment constraints, parameterized-width formats, inheritance/composition between formats, endianness at format level, how `insn as R` handles invalid bit patterns (is it total?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Sail-style bidirectional `<->` mappings so `format` declarations generate both decoder and encoder with provable round-trip. Add explicit `format extends` for RISC-V base+extensions. Specify virtual-field write semantics formally. Add enum-valued fields like modular-bitfield's BitfieldSpecifier.

analysis.what_fx_does: Allows pattern matching on format fields with literal bit constants and `.field` to access the matched format's fields, with compiler-verified non-overlapping patterns for decoder exhaustiveness.

closest_prior_art.approach: Match on literal bitvectors with exhaustiveness checking; mappings via `<->` give bidirectional encode/decode; widely used in the official RISC-V Sail model
Wildcard matching on bit patterns with `?` don't-care bits; `case inside` for ranges
ASCII-string regex-like bit patterns with capture groups, portable C89
Native pattern matching with bit-width annotations and capture

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Combining format types with nested pattern matching and `.field` shorthand is more ergonomic than SV casez (which requires manual slicing) and more typed than bitmatch's stringly-typed patterns. Overlap detection is decidable via QF_BV (see §18.6) — this is genuinely strong versus riscv-formal which doesn't check decoder exhaustiveness.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Sail's bidirectional encdec mappings are more powerful — one declaration produces both encoder AND decoder, proven inverse. FX's match is unidirectional. No don't-care bits syntax (`0b0110_????` in SV casez is natural; FX would force enumerating variants). No priority ordering specification when patterns legitimately overlap (e.g., microcode fallbacks). Erlang bit syntax has been production-proven for 20+ years.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Syntax for don't-care bits in patterns, handling of priority-ordered patterns, guards on field patterns (e.g., `R{...} when funct7 != 0`), nested format matching (matching a field that is itself a format), catch-all fallback semantics beyond `_`, how patterns interact with `as` casts on invalid encodings.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add Sail-style bidirectional mapping form `mapping decode : bits(32) <-> decoded` to generate encoder+decoder together. Add don't-care `?` in bit literals. Specify guard clause interaction. Specify error behavior when no pattern matches a format cast.

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines 8 register access modes (RW/RO/WO/W1C/W1S/RC/RS/RSVD) as compile-enforced semantics; treats RC reads as linear operations and generates correct bit-write patterns for W1C.

closest_prior_art.approach: XML schema with `<access>` (read-only/read-write/write-only/writeOnce/read-writeOnce) crossed with `<modifiedWriteValue>` (oneToClear/oneToSet/oneToToggle/zeroToClear/...) and `<readAction>`
Native `intr`, `nonsticky`, `sw`/`hw` access properties, sticky bits, counter/interrupt/alias special registers, UDP-extensible
Typed PAC with read()/write()/modify() proxy objects, enforces field-level access at Rust type level
Supports R/W, RO, WO, W1C, W0C, WRC, WSRC, WOC, WOS and many more (~25 access kinds)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Making RC a LINEAR read is genuinely novel — no other system captures read-to-clear as resource consumption in the type system. Compile-enforced W1C bit-write generation (not RMW) eliminates a classic driver bug class. RSVD writes as compile error is stronger than IP-XACT (which just documents).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Only 8 modes versus SystemRDL's extensible set and UVM RAL's ~25 modes. Missing: W0C (write-0-to-clear), W0S, WO1 (write-once), WSRC/WCRS (write-set/clear + read-set/clear composite), WOC (write-anything-to-clear), sticky bits, shadow registers, FIFO registers, counter registers, alias registers, TMR. No hw-side access mode (SystemRDL's `hw=rw` property). No modeled side-effects (e.g., register write triggers DMA).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Composite/sticky semantics, hw-vs-sw access split (hardware may write while software reads RO), asynchronous hardware updates, lock registers, interrupt enable+status pair patterns, register aliasing, shadow registers, partial-word writes (byte enables), swdword-wide register partitioning, read side-effects beyond RC (e.g., FIFO read pops next element).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Extend to SystemRDL 2.0 parity: add W0C/W0S/WO1/WSRC/WCRS/sticky/nonsticky/intr/counter/alias/shadow/FIFO modes. Split access into `sw:` and `hw:` dimensions like SystemRDL. Add byte-enable semantics. Model register side-effects explicitly (register write as an effect operation).

analysis.what_fx_does: Specifies `virtual` cross-register reassembly (e.g., 64-bit dma_addr from hi/lo 32-bit regs), `write_order hi then lo` constraints, field-level `requires` preconditions, and register-level `where` invariants tied to dependent register values.

closest_prior_art.approach: Architecture-specific barriers (writel has ordering, __raw_writel does not); mmiowb() before spin_unlock on ia64/PowerPC; read-before-write as barrier idiom
Register groups, alias registers, and UDP-extensible dependency declarations
Interface invariants and access protocols specified in DSL, compiled to C with ordering constraints enforced

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Compile-enforced write ordering (`write_order hi then lo`) is genuinely better than Linux's hope-and-barrier pattern — FX makes out-of-order writes a type error. `requires` preconditions on WO bits (e.g., `start` requires `dma_addr != 0 and dma_len > 0`) catch a classic driver bug class (starting DMA with stale descriptors).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No specification of MEMORY ordering between MMIO writes and prior memory stores (the tg3-Ethernet bug class from LWN). No treatment of write-combining regions, mmiowb-style spinlock barriers, or hw-side ordering guarantees (does device observe in program order?). No specification of what happens across context switches, interrupts, or CPU migration during a mandated write sequence. SystemRDL+UVM RAL have formal testbench support for dependency verification; FX §18.4 doesn't address the verification side.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Memory-barrier semantics (FX relies on §11.10 memory ordering but §18.4 doesn't cite it), write-combining MMIO regions, interruption/preemption during mandated write sequences, read-ordering constraints (FIFO data registers), how hardware's view of ordering is specified (does the type system prove the DEVICE sees correct order, or just the CPU issues it in order?), posted-writes vs non-posted on PCI, address-space safety (§19.7 has phys/virt/bus addr types but §18.4 doesn't integrate).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Integrate with §11.10 memory ordering explicitly. Specify that `write_order` inserts mmiowb-class barriers. Add read_order for FIFO-style registers. Model posted-vs-nonposted writes (relevant for PCIe). Add context-safety annotation (must be atomic w.r.t. interrupt?). Specify hardware-observable ordering as a refinement property.

analysis.what_fx_does: Couples a state machine directly to register values via `driven_by portsc` with `when <predicate>` state definitions, and proves state exclusivity/exhaustiveness via QF_BV SMT — so device state is the combination of register bits, not a separate abstract model.

closest_prior_art.approach: Concurrent assertions with temporal operators verify register-state transitions, one-hot encoding, protocol correctness
Register model includes mirror values and expected-state tracking; state machines are separate from register layout
Device spec as a state machine + register interface, generates driver code from specification

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Deriving device state from register bit predicates (via `when power and connect and enable`) is architecturally superior to SystemRDL's separate state machine model or UVM RAL's mirror values — it eliminates the synchronization problem between the register model and the abstract state model. SMT-proven exclusivity/exhaustiveness is stronger than SVA assertions (which only check runtime).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The USB PortState example is under-specified: no treatment of concurrent register writes from hardware side (a hardware-driven status bit change can cause a state transition without any SW action), no model of transition latency (`after 10ms` appears but isn't grounded in a temporal logic), no composition with SVA-style past/future operators. SVA has 20+ years of tool-chain maturity (JasperGold, VC Formal, OneSpin) that FX would need to reinvent.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Hardware-driven state transitions (spontaneous register changes from device side), transition timing semantics (`after 10ms` — is this a real-time constraint proved by SMT, a hint, or a simulation delay?), state observability when multiple software threads read the register, race conditions between SW write and HW update, formal semantics of `driven_by`.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add `hw_transition` clauses for device-initiated changes. Specify the temporal logic for `after` with clear reference to §13.18. Integrate with §11.10 memory ordering. Provide a semantic rule for what `driven_by` means when the driving register has W1C/RC fields whose read/write has side effects.

analysis.what_fx_does: Claims that bit-vector arithmetic is decidable in SMT (QF_BV), so every bit-field refinement, cross-field dependency, register state predicate, format exhaustiveness, and virtual field reassembly is 'automatically provable' with 'no fuel, no tactics, no manual lemmas'.

closest_prior_art.approach: Bit-blasting to SAT; also word-level simplification, preprocessing, and eager/lazy solvers
Specialized QF_BV solvers with word-level reasoning before bit-blasting
Quantified bit-vector solving via BDDs instead of quantifier instantiation

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Framing the entire bit-level spec so that verification obligations land in QF_BV is architecturally correct — decidable fragment means no user-visible tactics or fuel tuning. Matches the best practice of modern SMT-based verification tools.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The claim is overstated. QF_BV is decidable but NP-hard, and NEXPTIME-hard when widths are binary-encoded. A 1024-bit comparison or multiplication can cause Z3 to blow up. Bit-blasting produces propositional formulas whose size is proportional to bit-width × operation complexity, and multiplications/divisions are exponentially painful. 'Always gets a definitive answer' ignores SMT timeouts in practice. Quantified bit-vector (UFBV) — which shows up for parameterized-width formats — is a significant complexity jump.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Performance characterization: which bit-widths are tractable in which time budgets? Handling of timeouts (§10.12 mentions budgets but §18.6 claims 'always'). Non-linear bit-vector multiplication and division — these scale poorly. Parameterized-width formats trigger quantifiers. Interaction with quantifier instantiation when a register state predicate uses a universal over bit positions.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Replace 'always gets a definitive answer' with 'decidable in principle, with practical performance envelope documented per operation class'. Add a performance table: 64-bit ops tractable, 512-bit multiplications warn, 1024-bit non-linear requires manual hints. Specify interaction with §10.12 proof budgets. Mention that Bitwuzla or specialized QF_BV solvers may be preferred over Z3 for large bit-widths.

analysis.what_fx_does: Treats `Sync(clk)` as an effect in the semiring where mixing `sync(a) * sync(b)` with different clocks is a compile error, requiring `sync_2ff(x, from: clk_a, to: clk_b)` for explicit crossing.

closest_prior_art.approach: Type-safe clock domain crossing with domain-parameterized Signals; CDC requires explicit synchronizer primitives, all checked at Haskell type level
Runtime `withClock(clk)` blocks, AsyncQueue from rocket-chip for multi-bit CDC, but not enforced at type level

closest_prior_art.system: SystemVerilog Clocking Blocks + SVA for CDC
closest_prior_art.approach: Clocking blocks define sampling/driving edges; SVA multi-clock assertions verify metastability settling; static CDC analysis via separate tools
Formal specification of CDC verification methodology

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Clock domain as a first-class effect in the semiring is cleaner than Chisel's runtime `withClock` and competitive with Clash's type-level domains. Compile-time rejection of cross-domain arithmetic is the only sound approach — SV requires separate structural CDC tools.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Only specifies 2FF synchronizer. Missing: the critical rule that only ONE BIT at a time may cross a synchronizer (multi-bit needs gray coding / async FIFO / MCP). Missing: handshake synchronizers (open-loop vs closed-loop), AsyncQueue/AsyncFIFO equivalents, Gray-code converters for counters. Clash has far more elaborated domain-parameterized libraries. No treatment of reset-domain crossing (RDC) which is a growing concern. No specification of the statistical MTBF model (metastability is probabilistic, not guaranteed).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Multi-bit CDC rule (only one bit at a time via simple 2FF), async FIFO for multi-bit data, handshake protocols (open/closed loop), gray-code converters, reset domain crossing, glitch detection on combinational paths crossing domains, clock-domain relationship metadata (related/unrelated clocks), frequency ratios, MTBF analysis parameters.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add rule that `sync_2ff` accepts at most 1 bit — multi-bit crossings require `async_fifo`/`handshake_sync`/`gray_sync` primitives. Specify RDC separately from CDC. Add `clock_relationship(clk_a, clk_b)` meta-declarations (sync/async/ratio). Reference Clash's domain-parameterization pattern. Document MTBF/settling-time assumptions for 2FF.

analysis.what_fx_does: Lists the synthesizable subset as Tot/Sync(clk)/bits(n)/bounded loops/format/machine with Sync, and non-synthesizable as Div/IO/Alloc/Exn/String/list/int.

closest_prior_art.approach: Whole Chisel is synthesizable by construction (no Scala runtime survives), but Chisel generators are elaborated — Scala side is arbitrary
Haskell subset — any lazy evaluation, any type, but recursion must be bounded/structural; compiler rejects non-synthesizable
Scala library generating Verilog; anything in Scala elaboration side is fine, generated HDL is always synthesizable
IEEE 1800 + 1364.1 define the synthesizable vs simulation-only subset (no $display, no `#delay`, no fork/join in synth)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Framing synthesizability as an effect constraint (only Tot/Sync(clk)/Alloc(Stack) allowed) is cleaner than SV's syntactic subset specification. The same source file can contain both synthesizable and non-synthesizable code with the compiler tracking the boundary via effect typing — no separate simulation-only-vs-synthesizable file split.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The table is 8 lines. It misses: simulation primitives (force/release/display), testbench constructs (transactor, scoreboard), dynamic arrays/queues (useful in testbenches even if non-synth), DPI-C/FLI foreign interfaces, initial blocks, fork/join concurrency for testbenches, waveform dumping, timescale. Chisel vs FX: Chisel allows arbitrary Scala generators in elaboration that compute to synthesizable HDL — FX `comptime` is similar but interaction with `hardware` modules is not specified.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Testbench constructs (non-synthesizable but necessary), force/release, simulation-only assertions, delays/#10, initial blocks, DPI/FFI to simulator, waveform output, coverage collection, mixed synth+simulation-only code organization, how `comptime` elaboration integrates with `hardware` modules, whether a `hardware fn` can call a comptime function for generating configurations.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Distinguish THREE subsets, not two: synthesizable (gates), simulation-only (testbench), and compile-time (generation). Add explicit testbench facilities (force/release, waveform, coverage). Specify the comptime-to-hardware boundary. Reference IEEE 1800 synthesizable subset as a baseline.

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines `hardware fn` as a pure function on bits that synthesizes to combinational logic, with match on opcode, bit concatenation, and tuple returns.

closest_prior_art.approach: Pure value expressions are combinational; Action/ActionValue types separate state-changing logic; type-enforced split
Scala functions returning `UInt`/`Bits` nodes build combinational circuits; `Wire` and `:=` express assignment
Any Haskell pure function over BitVector/Signed/Unsigned is combinational; top-level `Signal dom` adds clock

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Directly inheriting FX's function syntax (including `match`) for combinational logic is more ergonomic than Chisel's `when/elsewhen/otherwise` or SV's case. Tuple return for `(result, flags)` is cleaner than SV output ports. The Tot effect requirement enforces purity cleanly at the type level.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Bluespec enforces combinational purity via Action/ActionValue types with more decades of shakeout. Chisel and Clash have mature combinational libraries (Mux, Cat, PriorityEncoder). §18.9 shows only an ALU example; no treatment of combinational feedback loops (illegal), combinational cycle detection, fanout limits, or synthesis-time cost modeling. `carry_out`/`overflow_detect` used in example aren't defined as primitives.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Combinational cycle detection (must be DAG), fanout bounds, latency/gate count hints, where arithmetic primitives come from (is `+` on bits(w) a built-in gate or library function?), how match exhaustiveness interacts with synthesis (inferred `X` for missing cases?), inout/tristate support, parameterized arithmetic widths resolved.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Explicit combinational-cycle rejection rule. Specify arithmetic primitives (ripple-carry vs carry-lookahead selection). Add cost model hints (decreases gates). Reference Chisel's Mux/Cat/etc. as a stdlib. Specify behavior on non-exhaustive match (synthesis implication).

analysis.what_fx_does: Specifies `on rising(clk) ... end on` as the block for clocked register updates, with `reg name : bits(w) = 0;` declaration and `.set()` for sequential assignment; `let` outside `on rising` creates combinational wires.

closest_prior_art.approach: `always_ff @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) q <= d;` — dedicated keyword for sequential, tool-checkable intent
`process(clk) begin if rising_edge(clk) then q <= d; end if; end process;`
`val q = RegNext(d, init)` — register is a first-class value, clock inherited from Module
Reg interface with `._read` and `._write`, updated atomically at rule fire

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Syntactic distinction between `let` (wire) and `.set()` inside `on rising` (register) is clearer than SV where the same `<=` means different things in `always_ff` vs `always_comb`. The `on rising(clk)` block is a less error-prone formulation than SV's sensitivity list (no accidental `always @(*)` vs `always @(posedge clk)` confusion).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Chisel's `RegNext(d)` is arguably cleaner still — no block needed at all; register is a value. Bluespec's Reg interface composes better with functional abstractions. No treatment of asynchronous reset vs synchronous reset, falling-edge triggers, multi-clock registers, initial values beyond `= 0`, enable signals (Chisel `RegEnable`), clock gating, retention registers for power.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Asynchronous reset (`on rising(clk) or reset`-style composite), falling-edge registers, enable signals (RegEnable equivalent), clock gating, ICG (integrated clock gating cells), retention registers, shift register inference, memory inference (when does `reg` array become SRAM vs flip-flops?), multi-port register files, reset value beyond literal 0.
analysis.could_we_do_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 1 + rigor-first] Verdict raised from 'adequate' to 'strong' after Proposal 1 adopted Dimension 20 (Size) with 'with Productive' effect + guardedness check for §3.5 codata, plus the tier-system refactor of §6.3 that classifies each dimension as Tier S (semiring), L (lattice+validity), T (typestate), or F (foundational). Rigor-first eliminates all inference sites with explicit surface syntax per Appendix E. Earlier note preserved below.

Add `on rising(clk) with reset(r)` for sync reset, `on rising(clk) async_reset(r)` for async. Add `enable(e)` clause. Specify memory inference rules (large reg arrays → SRAM). Add clock-gating primitive. Reference Chisel RegEnable pattern.

analysis.what_fx_does: Introduces pipeline sugar with named stages (fetch/decode/execute/memory/writeback), automatic pipeline register insertion, `stall when`/`flush when` clauses, `emit` for stage outputs, `prev.` for previous-stage data — the compiler generates forwarding muxes, stall logic, flush logic, and valid-bit tracking.

closest_prior_art.approach: Rules with guards compose into pipelines via FIFOs between stages; scheduler resolves conflicts automatically; formal semantics via Koika (PLDI 2020)
Manual pipeline registers + hand-written forwarding/stall logic; pipeline is structural not declarative
High-level pipeline DSLs with automatic timing balancing (Filament uses timeline types to prove pipeline safety)
MEMOCODE 2022 paper derives pipeline models from Chisel/FIRRTL, handling RegNext and forwarding

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Declarative pipeline with automatic forwarding/stall/flush generation is far better than Chisel's structural approach (hand-written registers + hazard logic). The claim of verified refinement against ISA spec is stronger than any mainstream HDL provides. Native syntax for `stall when`/`flush when` is cleaner than Bluespec's rule-scheduling approach for in-order pipelines.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Only supports in-order, simple pipelines per the example. No treatment of: out-of-order execution, register renaming, reservation stations, reorder buffers, superscalar issue, branch prediction state, multi-cycle operations (multipliers, dividers), pipeline with variable-latency units (cache miss), exceptions/interrupts crossing pipeline stages, speculation recovery. Bluespec's GAA generalizes better to irregular pipelines. Filament's timeline types prove stricter properties. The claim of auto-generated forwarding + verified refinement is aspirational without a paper-level proof.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Out-of-order pipelines, variable-latency stages (cache misses), speculation, branch prediction state, exception handling mid-pipeline, multi-issue superscalar, pipeline with back-pressure, memory ordering between pipeline stages, sleep-and-retry semantics, formal semantics of `prev.` across multi-cycle stalls.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Start with in-order as baseline; add explicit extension for out-of-order with reservation stations/ROB. Reference Koika semantics for the formal model. Add variable-latency stage primitive. Specify exception propagation. Reference Filament for timeline-type-verified pipelines. Note that 'verifies pipeline refines ISA' is a research-grade claim needing citation (currently only CHERIoT-Ibex/Flute have done this at production scale).

analysis.what_fx_does: Specifies the ISA as a Tot function `step : arch_state -> arch_state`, proves the pipeline refines it via `refinement pipeline_correct` with an abstraction function and a correspondence property, and extracts to Verilog via `fxc --target verilog`.

closest_prior_art.approach: ISA spec in Sail, adopted by RISC-V International; generates HOL4/Isabelle/Coq/Lean definitions; refinement verified against CHERIoT-Ibex and Flute via Jasper/SystemVerilog translation
Coq DSL for both spec and implementation; refinement proofs in Coq; generates Verilog
SVA-based with RVFI interface; compares processor core against Spike via SymbiYosys/SBY; widely adopted (Ibex, VexRiscv, PicoRV32)
Multi-backend spec in Haskell via hs-to-coq, with Clash-generated single-cycle Verilog for cross-check

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Having spec AND implementation in the same language with the SAME type system eliminates the translation layer that Sail needs (Sail-to-SMT-to-SystemVerilog is a research prototype). The refinement declaration syntax is more concise than Kami's Coq and more rigorous than riscv-formal's assertion-based comparison. Abstraction function inline is cleaner than Kami's simulation relation.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Sail has been adopted as THE official RISC-V spec by RISC-V International — FX would need to persuade the community to switch. CHERIoT-Ibex and Flute verification against Sail are published results with thousands of pages of machine-checked proofs. FX claims 'verifies pipeline refines ISA' but shows 5 lines of refinement block; the actual proof work (e.g., register-file invariants, memory-ordering correspondence, commit semantics, speculation recovery) is handwaved. Extraction to Verilog is a one-line mention without specifying the backend (does FX reimplement CIRCT?).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Memory-model correspondence (RVWMO or TSO alignment), speculation/commit boundary semantics (what does 'commits(tick(p))' actually mean?), exception/interrupt correspondence, instructions with side effects (CSRs, fences, AMO), handling of implementation-defined behavior, specification coverage tracking (Sail measures this), test-stream tandem verification (Sail has TestRIG integration), how `fxc --target verilog` compares to FIRRTL/CIRCT.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Cite CHERIoT-Ibex methodology explicitly. Specify memory-model correspondence separately. Detail the `commits()` predicate formally. Adopt Sail-style specification coverage reporting. Specify the Verilog backend (build on CIRCT? port FIRRTL?). Add tandem/differential testing infrastructure like TestRIG.

analysis.what_fx_does: Claims that the same `register_file` definition serves RTL, ISA spec, and driver simultaneously, so changing layout triggers compile errors in all three.

closest_prior_art.approach: Single SystemRDL source generates synthesizable RTL, UVM verification environment, C headers/HAL, and documentation
IEEE 1685 XML drives RTL generation, register-map headers, UVM RAL models, software APIs
Single DSL spec → C driver + specifications for device behavior

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Having ONE language and ONE type system for RTL + spec + driver is architecturally cleaner than SystemRDL's XML-based multi-generator approach (where each target has separate tooling). The compile-time coupling is stronger than SystemRDL's documentation-level consistency. Atomic re-compilation across all three consumers is a genuine win.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Section is 5 lines. No treatment of: partial updates (what if driver is in a different repo/version?), ABI stability for the C-accessible driver interface, versioning across hardware revisions (older/newer drivers on new/old hardware), how the software driver gets typed access (FFI, direct use?), integration with Linux/kernel build systems, how RTL and driver can disagree intentionally during transitions. SystemRDL 2.0 has an entire spec around versioning and backward compatibility.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Concrete example showing the shared register_file used by all three consumers, versioning across hardware revisions, ABI stability for driver C-interop, kernel module integration, intentional asymmetry (driver workarounds for hardware bugs), multi-tenant drivers where multiple versions of hardware need to coexist.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand section with a concrete example (USB port used by controller RTL + USB-ISA spec + usb driver). Specify versioning semantics. Integrate with §14 contracts for cross-revision compatibility. Specify C-ABI generation rules. Address the bug-workaround case.

analysis.what_fx_does: Provides a table mapping FX constructs to hardware primitives: let → adder+wires, if → mux, match → mux tree, reg → flip-flops, on rising(clk) → register update, format → wire selection (zero gates), machine → FSM, pipeline → pipeline regs + hazard logic, for → unrolled copies, bits{} → concatenation.

closest_prior_art.approach: IEEE 1364.1 and vendor-specific (Synopsys DC, Xilinx Vivado) specify synthesis mapping with technology-library awareness
FIRRTL compiler has well-defined lowering passes; MFC (MLIR-based) and SFC (Scala) implementations; CIRCT provides modern backend
Documented compilation pipeline from Haskell core to synthesizable HDL
C/C++/SystemC → RTL with scheduling/allocation/binding stages

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The table is a concise, agent-readable contract that ties source constructs to hardware — more transparent than SV's informal synthesis guidelines scattered across vendor manuals. Zero-gate claim for format/bit-slicing/concatenation is correct (wire selection truly has no logic cost).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The table is 13 lines. Missing: technology mapping to FPGA primitives (LUTs, carry chains, DSPs, BRAMs), ASIC standard cells, retiming, register placement optimization, clock tree synthesis, scan insertion, power-gating, memory inference rules (when does `reg array` become BRAM vs distributed RAM vs flip-flops?), handling of unrolled `for` with large bounds (compile-time blow-up). Chisel/FIRRTL has a 10-year-mature lowering pipeline (30+ passes in CIRCT); FX would need to build this.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Memory inference rules (which reg arrays become SRAM), FPGA primitive mapping (LUT6/carry chain/DSP48), ASIC cell libraries, retiming/register-balancing, timing closure, clock tree, scan/DFT insertion, power optimization, area/timing/power reporting, cost model for unrolled loops, handling of large bits(n) (128? 1024?) mapping to hardware, IP-vendor primitive instantiation.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand table with FPGA/ASIC mapping layer. Specify memory inference rules. Add retiming/pipelining hints. Integrate with CIRCT/FIRRTL backend explicitly (rather than inventing new). Add cost model (gates/LUTs/FFs/memory per construct). Reference IEEE 1364.1 as baseline.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter-level header for Systems Programming, consolidating FX's approach to kernel/driver code via effects (IRQ context), linear types (DMA), machines (lifecycle), lock orders, stack budgets, inline asm, address-space types, and C interop.

closest_prior_art.approach: microkernel + userspace drivers, per-driver verification with separate logic frameworks
type-safe kernel code within the Linux kernel itself, abstractions are libraries not language features
managed-code OS with typed channels, contract-verified drivers, SIP isolation

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unifies context, DMA ownership, lock ordering, stack bounds, and MMIO under one type system in the same source language that also writes ISA and RTL — seL4/sDDF uses separate toolchains (C, CapDL, Pancake, Viper); Singularity is dead; Rust for Linux lacks a sleep-safe effect (see Klint). FX treats systems concerns as first-class language features rather than library idioms.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: 268 lines covering an enormous scope that took seL4 a decade and Dingo/Termite a PhD thesis to partially solve. No discussion of hardware interrupt vector tables, preemption count tracking, RCU, per-CPU data, softirq tasklets, MSI-X, workqueues, page fault paths, or SMP memory barriers in driver context. Singularity and Dingo both had full PhD-level formalisms; FX has code sketches.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Interaction between effect hierarchy and RCU/preemption/softirq; (b) scheduler integration; (c) how cross-driver shared state is typed; (d) dynamic IRQ registration — the example shows a static handler, not request_irq flow; (e) NMI context; (f) virtualization boundaries (VMX root, guest drivers); (g) SMMU/IOMMU integration.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Split §19 into four chapters: execution context model (IRQ/softirq/RCU/NMI/preempt-count), DMA+MMIO, locking+memory ordering, driver lifecycle. Each needs full metatheory treatment comparable to §6. Cite sDDF as architectural baseline and Klint as the specific prior art for the IRQ-context type rule.

analysis.what_fx_does: Models execution context as a totally-ordered effect hierarchy HardIRQ < SoftIRQ < Dispatch < Passive where calling a less-restricted function from a more-restricted one is a compile error, blocking e.g. mutex_lock from HardIRQ.

closest_prior_art.approach: static preemption-count tracking via per-function adjust/expected-range properties — detects atomic context violations at compile time
runtime detection of sleep-in-atomic, relies on CONFIG_DEBUG_ATOMIC_SLEEP annotations
finitized kernel interface verified by Z3 at LLVM IR level, avoids interrupt/preemption complications by design (xv6 scope)
no shared interrupt context — each SIP has its own address space and scheduler view

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Compile-time enforcement in a language first-class effect, not a lint or debug config. Unlike Klint (which pragmatically sacrifices soundness), FX claims full soundness via the effect semiring. Unlike lockdep, no runtime cost and no need to hit the offending path. The hierarchy is totally ordered and composable with allocation effects (§19.11).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Linux reality is not a 4-level chain: RCU read-side, preemption disabled, interrupts disabled, bh_disable, migrate_disable, NMI, spinlock_t vs raw_spinlock_t under PREEMPT_RT all compose independently — a 4-level lattice cannot express this. Klint's preemption-count model is strictly more expressive (integer counter + range). The FX text uses 'MMIO' as an effect but never defines it.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Where does NMI fit? (b) How does PREEMPT_RT behavior change the hierarchy at compile time? (c) Interaction with local_irq_save/restore as RAII regions? (d) How is context inferred for function pointers passed across the kernel? (e) Threaded IRQs (request_threaded_irq) blur HardIRQ and Passive.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Replace the 4-point chain with a lattice: {preempt_count: nat, irq_disabled: bool, rcu_depth: nat, in_nmi: bool}. Effects track deltas rather than absolute levels. This matches Klint's soundness goal and Linux reality, at the cost of more complex inference.

analysis.what_fx_does: Tracks CPU-vs-device ownership of DMA buffers via linear types: dma_map_single consumes the buffer producing a dma_token, dma_unmap_single+reclaim returns ownership to CPU, preventing CPU access while device owns memory.

closest_prior_art.approach: convention-based ownership with runtime CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG checker; dma_sync_single_for_cpu/for_device enforces at runtime
capability-based device frames distinguished from RAM in the proof, IOMMU enforces at hardware level
verified DMA descriptor rings via Hoare logic annotations, data integrity across transfers proved
affine buffer handles via CoherentAllocation<T>, subject of ongoing Linux maintainer dispute (Ted T'so, 2024)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Clean pair-of-functions signature encoding the entire linear discipline in the type, no runtime check, no IOMMU dependency. Compared to Linux DMA debug (runtime) and Rust's in-progress DMA traits (disputed), FX's linear type is the most concise expression. Unlike seL4 which relies on IOMMU hardware, FX is a pure software property.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Doesn't address cache coherency (sync_for_cpu/sync_for_device across partial buffer regions), bidirectional DMA, scatter-gather with variable entries, shared dma-buf across multiple devices (Linux dma-buf framework), or what happens if the device never raises completion (DMA timeout handling). The example shows single-buffer single-direction — far from NVMe/GPU reality. Pancake already verified a real NIC driver with full DMA; FX shows one snippet.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Cache coherency model — is dma_sync needed? (b) Scatter-gather tables; (c) Shared DMA buffers (dma-buf exporter/importer with 3+ consumers); (d) Bounce buffers / swiotlb; (e) DMA direction BIDIRECTIONAL; (f) IOMMU mapping vs direct physical; (g) What happens under timeout / device error — is the linear token leaked?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Introduce fractional permissions (§6.4 already mentions Frac) for dma-buf sharing: a buffer can be shared-readable (Frac 1/n) across n importers. Model cache coherency as a separate dimension (Coherent < NonCoherent with explicit sync transitions). Specify timeout semantics — a dma_token must participate in a typed timeout handler or be consumed on error paths.

analysis.what_fx_does: Driver initialization/cleanup expressed as a state machine where every resource-acquiring transition declares an `inverse`; compiler auto-generates compensation chains for every failure path and the remove transition from any state.

closest_prior_art.approach: formal FSM protocol language for drivers, Termite synthesizes drivers from device+OS specs — driver generated automatically
runtime cleanup stack: devm_kzalloc, devm_request_irq etc. automatically freed on probe failure or remove
linear Drop trait runs destructors in LIFO scope order, guard objects for resources
components with explicit init/teardown contracts, verified per-component

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Explicit inverse declaration unifies setup/teardown pairing at the type level, avoiding Rust-RAII's LIFO-only discipline (which forces fixed teardown order even when physical cleanup has dependencies). Unlike devres, which is runtime and only for `probe` failures, FX covers arbitrary transitions. Machine-based model generalizes cleanly to partial-state removal.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Termite synthesizes the entire driver from spec — strictly stronger than FX, which only verifies. Linux devres already provides the cleanup chain for 90% of drivers with zero type theory. The `always (state is Removed) ==> pci_released and ...` invariant requires proving that compound conjunction — unclear how this interacts with §10 SMT when predicates cross multiple resources. No discussion of asynchronous teardown, which is the hard case (device stuck; firmware crashed).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) What happens when `inverse` itself can fail? (b) Async teardown (firmware unresponsive); (c) Hot-unplug events mid-init; (d) Shared resources across drivers (shared IRQ line); (e) Device power states (runtime PM, suspend/resume are orthogonal state machines); (f) How does the compiler order the compensation chain when inverses have side-effect dependencies?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Borrow Termite's idea and have the compiler synthesize the `remove` transition automatically from the sequence of forward transitions, rather than requiring each one to declare `inverse`. Add explicit failure modes to inverses: `inverse unmap_bars on_fail force_unplug`. Model power states as an orthogonal machine product (§13.4 `*sync`).

analysis.what_fx_does: Declares a global strict total order over lock classes (SpinIRQ < DeviceLock < SubsystemLock < GlobalLock) that the compiler checks statically at every lock acquisition.

closest_prior_art.approach: runtime BFS over observed lock-class dependency graph, detects cycles; achieves 'mathematical closure' over observed sequences only
static summary-based analysis on Linux 5.10, found 54 real deadlocks with 17% false positives
GUARDED_BY/ACQUIRED_BEFORE attributes, compile-time order checking, used in Chromium and LLVM
compile-time lock-order validation in C++ using template metaprogramming

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Strict total order at language level gives zero false positives (within the declared universe) and zero runtime cost, unlike lockdep which is runtime and cannot rule out yet-unobserved orderings. Compared to Clang Thread Safety (attribute-based, partial), FX's order is a first-class declaration. Compared to DLOS (17% FP rate), FX is sound by construction.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Real kernels don't have a total order on locks — they have a partial order with mutually incomparable families (per-CPU spin locks, per-object mutexes, RCU read-side). A total order is an oversimplification. The section is 13 lines; no discussion of how _nested variants (§ mutex_lock_nested) fit, how lock ordering interacts with IRQ context (an irq-safe lock must precede non-irq-safe), or how to prove a dynamic lock's class at allocation site (sparse has __acquires/__releases annotations). Fuchsia already has compile-time validation with richer model.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Partial-order lock graphs (only chain shown); (b) Nesting within same class (mutex_lock_nested); (c) Per-object lock identity vs class; (d) Dynamic lock allocation — how is the class known at compile time? (e) Interaction with condition variables / completions / DMA fences (DEPT deals with this); (f) Reader-writer locks; (g) Dropping locks in non-LIFO order.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Generalize to partial order (DAG of lock classes) with subclassing for nesting. Add a typed lock-class parameter: `let _g = spin_lock<DeviceLock>(ref dev.lock)`. Integrate DEPT's dependency-tracker notion: not just locks but any blocking event (folio_lock, completion, DMA fence).

analysis.what_fx_does: Stack allocation effect carries a comptime bound (Alloc(Stack, bound: 2048)) that the compiler verifies by summing frame sizes across the entire call tree reachable from the annotated function.

closest_prior_art.approach: static WCSU analysis using compiler stack-usage data, per-subprogram and global worst-case, handles indirect calls via user-provided refinement, TQL-5 qualifiable for DO-178
GCC emits .su files with per-function stack usage; Linux kernel has scripts/checkstack.pl for reporting
fixed 512-byte stack, path-sensitive bounds-check via abstract interpretation over all paths
no static stack bound; convention + guard pages

analysis.where_fx_is_better: First-class language-level stack budget integrated with the effect system — GNATstack is a separate analyzer with external call data files; GCC -fstack-usage is a post-hoc scan. FX integrates with the function signature and can reject code at use site, not just at a late analysis pass.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Four lines of text. GNATstack has 20+ years of production experience handling recursion, indirect calls, dispatching, C/C++/Ada, variable-sized frames — GNATstack explicitly documents all the failure modes FX glosses over.
