No discussion of recursive functions, dynamic alloca, C calls, VLA, longjmp, OS signal stacks, or how the bound composes under function-pointer callbacks (indirect calls). eBPF verifier enforces 512 bytes; Linux kernel enforces 8K/16K thread stacks — real-world numbers.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Recursion (bound=infinity without `decreases`?); (b) Indirect calls / function pointers / closures; (c) VLA / alloca; (d) Interrupt stack separate from thread stack; (e) Stack growth direction / guard pages; (f) How does Alloc(Stack, bound:N) propagate across ABI boundaries to C callees with unknown usage?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Reference GNATstack explicitly, adopt its model: per-entry-point analysis with bounded recursion via `decreases` (§5.2), supplement files for external C calls, and a visible `+?` uncertainty marker in diagnostics. The four-line section needs 2-3 pages of semantics to be operational.

analysis.what_fx_does: Typed inline assembly block (asm x86_64 with string instruction plus typed in/out/clobbers clauses) that binds assembly register names to FX values of declared bit-vector types.

closest_prior_art.approach: typed operands with register classes, lateout, inout, clobber_abi, options (pure, nomem, nostack), whitelist of types per register class
AT&T syntax with typed outputs via -> T, comptime-known assembly strings; active proposal for dialect-based parser integration
untyped constraint strings (=r, m, etc.), clobbers list, memory barrier; ubiquitous but error-prone
full assembly parser in the D compiler for x86, expression-embedded operands

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Compared to GCC extended asm (raw constraint strings with no types), FX's typed operands are cleaner. Compared to Zig (still string-based), FX appears to have structured in/out clauses.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: One 12-line example. Rust's asm! is a mature, stabilized feature with register classes, lateout, inout, clobber_abi directives, and per-register-class type whitelists — FX's example shows none of this complexity. No treatment of: memory clobbers, flag registers, reserved registers (LLVM reserves e.g. ebx), labels, sym operands for constants, naked functions, ABI-preserving clobbers, or volatility semantics. Zig has been debating the right model for years (issues #5241, #10761); FX presents a sketch with no evidence of having addressed the same problems.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Memory clobber semantics (may read/write global memory); (b) Flag-register effects; (c) Reserved/frame-pointer registers; (d) Naked functions; (e) ABI preservation (clobber_abi equivalent); (f) Target-arch dispatch (multiple ISAs in same source); (g) Compile-time operand validation; (h) Interaction with the Privileged effect — what is Privileged?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Rust's asm! model wholesale: register classes, lateout/inout, options {pure, nomem, nostack, preserves_flags}, clobber_abi("C"). Add FX-specific `with MMIO, Clobbers(memory)` to tie clobbers into the effect system. Explicitly specify which instructions are allowed (no jumps out of asm block — violates control-flow invariants).

analysis.what_fx_does: Declares three distinct nominal types (phys_addr, virt_addr, bus_addr) as bits(64) aliases to prevent mixing — functions like ioremap and phys_to_bus have signatures that make address-space conversions explicit.

closest_prior_art.approach: GCC-invisible annotations treated by sparse as distinct address spaces, used with make C=1 since kernel 2.6

closest_prior_art.system: Rust for Linux UserSlice / IoMem abstractions
closest_prior_art.approach: typed wrappers with unsafe internals; __iomem equivalence via read_volatile/write_volatile wrappers
capability-system distinction between device-backed and RAM-backed frames, enforced in kernel proofs

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Nominally distinct types enforced by the core type system with zero annotation overhead (no __CHECKER__ conditional compilation). Unlike sparse (optional checker), FX's check is part of the language. The distinction between phys/virt/bus covers IOMMU-capable systems, which sparse does not.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: This is a 7-line section that re-invents what sparse has done for 22 years. Sparse has 5+ canonical address spaces (kernel, user, iomem, percpu, rcu) plus __force escape hatch; FX shows 3 with no RCU/percpu/user distinction. No `ptr` type — the example uses raw bits(64), losing pointer-ness. No discussion of casts, `__force`-equivalent escape hatches, array/offset arithmetic, or how this interacts with the Representation dimension (§6.3 dim 10).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) User-space addresses (__user); (b) Per-CPU address space; (c) RCU-protected pointer spaces; (d) Cast operators (phys_to_virt, with what effect?); (e) Pointer arithmetic within an address space; (f) Sub-spaces (e.g. IOMMU DMA window vs bus); (g) How does a machine with multiple IOMMUs express multiple bus_addr spaces?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Make address space a first-class dimension (20th?) with its own semiring, parameterized by a label: `ptr<space: addr_space, T>`. Then `ptr<__iomem, u32>` and `ptr<__user, u8>` are type-incompatible. Provide typed conversion functions that require the MMU/IOMMU effect. Integrate with sparse-style __force for audit-tagged escapes.

analysis.what_fx_does: Linear tracking of pending_io across an NVMe queue pair: submit moves the io into the tracker array slot, complete uses .take() to consume it; double-completion is a compile error because .take() on None fails linearly.

closest_prior_art.approach: lockless per-core queue pairs with tracker/request arrays, convention-based exclusivity (undefined behavior if violated), C code
Rust ownership model for per-queue-pair thread exclusivity, affine tracker slots
full formal verification of DMA descriptor ring protocol with 4 properties including data integrity
behavioral/protocol model of submission-completion with CID reuse invariants

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The `tracker[cpl.cid].take()` idiom statically prevents double completion — SPDK documents this as undefined behavior but enforces at runtime only. The linear discipline also prevents CID reuse before completion, which is a real NVMe driver bug class. More concise than Pancake's Viper annotations.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The example is 45 lines but omits: SQ/CQ phase bit inversion edge cases, head/tail wraparound correctness (modular arithmetic), multiple completions per doorbell write, admin queue vs I/O queue, SR-IOV, controller reset / shutdown invariants, CID collision on timeout+retry. SPDK is production code with millions of IOPS; FX has a code sketch. Pancake has a machine-checked Hoare-logic proof of the NIC driver's protocol; FX has a type signature.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Timeout handling — who consumes the pending_io on timeout? (b) Controller reset (all outstanding IOs must be reclaimed); (c) CID exhaustion; (d) Admin vs I/O queue differences; (e) SGL vs PRP handling (payload DMA); (f) Completion-side concurrent access by multiple threads; (g) Interaction with the DMA buffers — each pending_io owns one or more dma_buf; are those also linearly tracked?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Pair submit with a typed timeout handler that consumes the pending_io (mandatory — no orphaning). Add a `reset` transition to the queue pair machine that drains all trackers. Tie each pending_io linearly to its DMA buffers so a leak in the buffer is also a leak in the io. Provide a proof obligation that phase-bit inversion is correct (currently the example uses `not self.cq_phase.get()` but nothing proves this is right).

analysis.what_fx_does: WiFi connection modeled as a machine where Handshake/Connected states carry secret-graded crypto keys; disconnect transition consumes keys, combining linear + classified to guarantee secure erasure on drop.

closest_prior_art.approach: C-based state machine with manual memset_explicit for key erasure, ad hoc classification
verified crypto primitives in F* with secret-independence (constant-time) proofs, separate from state machine
symbolic protocol verification of 4-way handshake, does not address key erasure
runtime Drop-invoked memset_volatile to zero secrets; no linear enforcement, can be forgotten

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Combines three properties in one type: state-valid-key invariant, linear consumption, classified/zeroed-on-drop (§12.7). Rust's zeroize is opt-in; in FX it's automatic for linear+classified. EverCrypt verifies the crypto primitive but not the surrounding driver state; ProVerif verifies the protocol but not the implementation. FX unifies these in a single machine declaration.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: 14 lines for a 4-way handshake protocol that has been the subject of KRACK, FragAttacks, and Dragonblood vulnerabilities. No treatment of: key hierarchies (PMK/PTK/GTK/IGTK), rekeying, roaming (BSS transition with key migration), replay counters, EAPOL state machine proper, firmware-side key installation atomicity (`keys_installed(dev.fw)` is an invariant with no semantics), multiple authenticators, PMF. The `on_event FwNotify.AuthOK` mechanism is hand-waved — FW notifications can be spoofed or lost.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Key hierarchy modeling; (b) Rekeying / GTK update; (c) Replay counter state; (d) FW-CPU boundary trust (FW could lie about AuthOK); (e) What if firmware fails to erase keys — is the `keys_installed` invariant vacuous? (f) MLO / multi-link; (g) Handshake timeouts; (h) Cross-session key confusion.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Use §14 contracts to formalize the CPU-FW boundary: firmware notifications are tainted, require trust validators. Model key hierarchy as a separate linear resource family with the PMK at the root. Tie the `keys_installed(dev.fw)` invariant to a ghost reference that only firmware-authenticated messages can advance — this requires a crypto-authenticated trust-boundary mechanism that §10.8 doesn't fully specify.

analysis.what_fx_does: Kernel modules declare @[abi(C, linux_kernel)] and @[link_section] to produce standard object files linking with GCC-compiled kernel; extern "C" declares C functions with typed FX signatures.

closest_prior_art.approach: bindgen + wrapper crate for kernel inline functions/macros, asmlinkage/calling-convention compatibility, LLVM/GCC ABI coexistence flagged as risk by Kroah-Hartman
direct C header parsing to Zig types at comptime, no separate bindgen step
straight C ABI declarations, link-section attributes via pragma(linkerDirective)

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Claims same object files + GCC linkage in a language with 21 type dimensions — if true, this is stronger than Rust for Linux, where bindgen cannot see through kmalloc's __always_inline definitions and asmlinkage calling convention has caused problems. FX's attribute-based declaration is concise.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Nine-line section making a very strong claim. Rust for Linux spent years on: (a) regparm(0) asmlinkage on x86-32, (b) __always_inline kmalloc being invisible to bindgen (requires kmalloc_for_rust wrapper), (c) preprocessor macros like module_init(), (d) LLVM-vs-GCC ABI subtleties (bitfield struct returns). FX handwaves all of this. 'Same calling convention as C' is ISA-dependent; 'same object files' requires matching debug info, section flags, relocation handling, ELF symbol visibility — none of this is addressed. 'Links with GCC output' — which version? Which flags? kernel-CFI signing?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) Preprocessor macro handling; (b) __always_inline / inline functions; (c) asmlinkage / custom calling conventions; (d) Bitfield ABI; (e) Kernel CFI / kcfi / return address signing; (f) module_init/module_exit macro linker magic; (g) Per-arch quirks (ARM32 eabi, PowerPC function descriptors); (h) GCC plugin passes (kernel sometimes relies on these).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Reference Rust for Linux's specific workarounds as required features: provide a `comptime` macro expander equivalent to bindgen, specify per-arch calling conventions explicitly via @[abi(C, linux_kernel, arch="x86_64", calling_convention="sysv64")], state which ABI version is targeted (stable kernel API is an oxymoron — Linux has no stable internal ABI). Document which C features are NOT supported (VLAs, _Generic, variadic macros as function-like).

analysis.what_fx_does: Allocation effect carries a flag (Kernel vs Atomic) constrained by the execution-context effect — Passive + Alloc(Kernel) → GFP_KERNEL allowed, HardIRQ + Alloc(Kernel) is a compile error, HardIRQ + Alloc(Atomic) → GFP_ATOMIC.

closest_prior_art.system: Linux GFP flags + ISP RAS RuleDB 010/043
closest_prior_art.approach: kernel code chooses GFP flag per-call-site, runtime detection via might_sleep and atomic_sleep debug, static rules codified but not enforced by compiler
static preemption-count property tracking, including for allocator calls; integrates with kernel's existing GFP flag machinery
'callers keep track of whether they are running in a schedulable context and inform callees' — the textbook rule FX is automating

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Compile-time elimination of GFP_KERNEL-in-atomic bugs — currently a runtime-only check in Linux (might_sleep) requiring CONFIG_DEBUG_ATOMIC_SLEEP. The effect-carries-flag encoding mechanizes Morton's manual convention. Cleaner than Klint's pragmatic preempt-count tracking: FX's mapping is a direct semiring product.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The actual GFP flag space is vast: __GFP_NOWAIT, __GFP_NOIO, __GFP_NOFS, __GFP_HIGH, __GFP_DIRECT_RECLAIM, __GFP_KSWAPD_RECLAIM, __GFP_MEMALLOC, __GFP_ZERO, __GFP_HARDWALL, __GFP_NOFAIL — dozens of orthogonal bits. FX's binary Kernel/Atomic split is a toy model. Missing: GFP_NOIO for filesystem-reclaim loops, GFP_NOFS for filesystem code that might recurse, GFP_USER vs GFP_HIGHUSER for user-destined pages, PF_MEMALLOC tasks. Linux LWN 2023 article documents 5000+ GFP_ATOMIC call sites and dozens of flag compositions.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (a) GFP_NOIO / GFP_NOFS recursion prevention (held fs/io locks); (b) Migration context (__GFP_HARDWALL / NUMA); (c) __GFP_NOFAIL semantics; (d) Zone preferences (DMA, DMA32, NORMAL, HIGHMEM); (e) Memory reclaim task state (PF_MEMALLOC avoids reclaim recursion); (f) How does this compose with Spinlock effect — holding a spinlock also forbids sleep; (g) MMIO allocations (kmalloc with DMA flag).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Replace binary Alloc(Kernel)/Alloc(Atomic) with a bitfield-style Alloc(zone, reclaim_ok, io_ok, fs_ok, fail_ok) and model each constraint independently. Tie directly to the execution-context lattice: Alloc(io_ok) forbidden under FilesystemReclaim context, Alloc(reclaim_ok) forbidden under PF_MEMALLOC, etc. This matches Linux's actual flag space.

analysis.what_fx_does: FX compiles through four binary IRs (FXCore/FXLow/FXAsm/Object), with source as the only human-readable artifact, and claims end-to-end verified correctness via simulation relations across layers.

closest_prior_art.approach: 11 intermediate languages in 4 phases with formal Coq proofs linking each layer; 20 verified compilation passes bridging C to assembly
closest_prior_art.reference: Xavier Leroy, 'Formal verification of a realistic compiler', CACM 2009; https://compcert.org/

closest_prior_art.approach: Arbitrary number of dialect-based IR levels with progressive lowering; extensible and domain-specific unlike CompCert's fixed chain
closest_prior_art.reference: https://mlir.llvm.org/; Lattner et al., 'MLIR: Scaling Compiler Infrastructure for Domain Specific Computation', CGO 2021

closest_prior_art.approach: Verified compiler from ML to multiple machine codes via sequence of intermediate languages with end-to-end machine-checked correctness
closest_prior_art.reference: https://cakeml.org/; Tan et al., 'A New Verified Compiler Backend for CakeML', ICFP 2016

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's 21-dimension erasure story is broader than CompCert (which erases nothing special) or CakeML (which erases types/proofs). The binary-only IR format with checksums is a practical hygiene choice. The ISA formalization reuse (codegen + hardware verification + peephole) is architecturally elegant.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: CompCert has 20 YEARS of mechanized proofs and has been qualified for DO-178C avionics (March 2026). CakeML has POPL 2014 Most Influential Paper award. FX has zero implemented verification. The gap between the claim and reality is enormous. Also, FX's 4-layer pipeline is thin compared to CompCert's 11 languages — the jump from 'typed, monomorphic SSA' to 'flat virtual registers' is doing too much work in one pass.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of how the formal proofs would actually be constructed — in what proof assistant? What logic? CompCert uses Coq, CakeML uses HOL4. FX says nothing. No discussion of compilation time targets. No incremental compilation strategy for the binary IRs. No discussion of debug info propagation through the layers.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Consider MLIR's dialect approach — let each of the 21 dimensions have its own dialect/lowering pass rather than one monolithic FXCore→FXLow jump. This would make verification tractable (smaller simulation relations per pass) and match CompCert's lesson that more intermediate languages = easier proofs. Also specify the proof framework explicitly (§31.8 commits to Lean 4).

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines four compilation layers: FX (source) → FXCore (typed monomorphic SSA) → FXLow (flat virtual registers) → FXAsm (target instructions, physical registers) → Object (.o files), all binary except source.

closest_prior_art.approach: Uses 11 intermediate languages (CompCert C → Clight → C#minor → Cminor → CminorSel → RTL → LTL → Linear → Mach → Asm) with formal semantics for each; more fine-grained decomposition aids verification
closest_prior_art.reference: https://compcert.org/compcert-C.html; Leroy, 'A formally verified compiler back-end', 2009

closest_prior_art.approach: Single monolithic SSA IR with ~67 opcodes; GlobalISel adds SelectionDAG → MachineIR → target instructions as additional layers for codegen
Core → STG → Cmm → native code; GRIN project proposes alternative with explicit laziness representation for whole-program optimization
closest_prior_art.reference: https://grin-compiler.github.io/; Podlovics et al., 'A Modern Look at GRIN', Acta Cybernetica 2020

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Binary-only intermediates with checksums prevent accidental hand-editing and ensure integrity — pragmatic choice. The 4-layer count is reasonable for a language that erases so much at the FX→FXCore boundary.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: CompCert's 11 languages prove that finer granularity makes each step easier to verify. FX's FXCore→FXLow jump does ADT lowering, closure conversion, allocation resolution, and synchronization insertion in one pass — each of these is a separate CompCert language boundary. LLVM's GlobalISel adds MachineIR between IR and target instructions, providing a better instruction selection framework than FX's direct FXLow→FXAsm mapping.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No formal semantics given for any of the intermediate representations. CompCert's key innovation is that EACH intermediate language has formal semantics — FX just lists op names. No binary format specification. No discussion of how binary IRs are versioned across compiler versions. No parallel compilation story (can different modules be compiled to FXCore independently?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Split FXCore→FXLow into at least 3 passes: (1) ADT lowering + pattern match compilation, (2) closure conversion + defunctionalization, (3) allocation strategy resolution + synchronization insertion. Each with its own intermediate language and formal semantics. This directly enables CompCert-style per-pass correctness proofs.

sections.name: FX to FXCore: Verification and Erasure

analysis.what_fx_does: FX→FXCore is claimed to contain 90% of compiler intelligence: parsing, desugaring, elaboration of all 21 dimensions, monomorphization, construct compilation (machines→enums, contracts→validators), erasure of 12+ dimensions, insertion of drop/secure_zero/volatile, and high-level optimization.

closest_prior_art.approach: F* uses KreMLin to extract Low* (a C-like subset) to C; erasure removes Ghost computations and proof terms; semantics preservation proved for the extraction to C via lambda-ow*
closest_prior_art.reference: Protzenko et al., 'Verified Low-Level Programming Embedded in F*', ICFP 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1703.00053

closest_prior_art.approach: Certified erasure pipeline from Coq to OCaml via lambda-box (untyped lambda calculus); erasure of types and proofs formally verified in Coq itself; used by CompCert and ConCert
closest_prior_art.reference: Forster, Sozeau, Tabareau, 'Verified Extraction from Coq to OCaml', POPL 2024; https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3656379

closest_prior_art.approach: Progressively transforms high-level dependent type theory to low-level C with reference counting; optimization passes integrated into lowering; compilation strategy quite different from Coq extraction

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Erasing 12+ dimensions (security labels, protocol states, provenance, trust, observability, clock domains, complexity/precision/space bounds, usage grades, mutation markers, reentrancy) is far more ambitious than F* (erases Ghost + proofs) or Coq (erases types + proofs). The secure_zero insertion for classified data and volatile markers for MMIO show practical systems awareness absent from academic verified compilers.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The MetaCoq team took years to formally verify just type/proof erasure in Coq. FX proposes erasing 12+ dimensions with zero formal treatment of correctness. The 2024 verified extraction paper (Forster/Sozeau/Tabareau) shows how hard even simple erasure is to verify. FX's monomorphization strategy is not discussed beyond 'generics instantiated at concrete types' — Rust's experience shows monomorphization causes severe code bloat and compile time issues. Go's GC-shape stenciling hybrid exists precisely because full monomorphization doesn't scale.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No formal erasure correctness statement. No discussion of monomorphization bloat mitigation (polymorphic recursion? code sharing for same-layout types?). No specification of what 'high-level optimize' means formally — inlining and DCE after erasure could break the simulation relation if not done carefully. No discussion of separate compilation — does every module get fully monomorphized independently? What about cross-module generic instantiation?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Go's hybrid approach for monomorphization: use GC-shape stenciling to share code across types with identical representation, dictionary-passing for the differences. This directly addresses code bloat. For erasure correctness, define a formal erasure function and state the theorem that erased programs preserve observable behavior — even if the proof is future work, the theorem statement constrains the design. Consider splitting this mega-pass into sub-passes with explicit intermediate forms.

analysis.what_fx_does: Type-directed lowering from ~50 typed FXCore ops (carrying monomorphic types, linearity, regions, effects, source locations) to ~100 flat FXLow ops (sized values, virtual registers, SSA with Phi nodes); ADTs become tagged memory, pattern matches become decision trees, closures become structs+function pointers.

closest_prior_art.approach: ~67 SSA opcodes covering terminators, binary/unary ops, memory, casts, aggregates, vectors, atomics, exception handling; well-proven op set after 20+ years of evolution
Graph Reduction Intermediate Notation for functional languages; makes laziness/memory explicit; whole-program heap points-to analysis enables aggressive unboxing and eval elimination
closest_prior_art.reference: https://grin-compiler.github.io/; Podlovics et al., 'A Modern Look at GRIN', Acta Cybernetica 2020

closest_prior_art.approach: Sequence of 12+ intermediate languages for incremental feature elimination; closLang for closure conversion, bvlLang for value numbering, dataLang for data representation, wordLang for word-level operations
closest_prior_art.reference: https://cakeml.org/jfp19.pdf; Tan et al., 'The Verified CakeML Compiler Backend', JFP 2019

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The op set is well-organized with explicit categories for linear resource management (Drop, SecureZero, Move, Clone), contract operations (Validate, Encode, Decode), and ghost operations. LLVM has nothing comparable for linear resources. The metadata annotations (linearity, region, effect set, source location) per op are richer than LLVM's metadata system.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: ~50 FXCore ops vs LLVM's ~67 — missing: vector insert/extract/shuffle (crucial for SIMD), exception landing pads, freeze (for undef handling), getelementptr (FX uses Project but GEP is more general for nested structures). The closure conversion example shows naive struct+indirect call — no defunctionalization analysis or lambda lifting. GRIN's whole-program heap points-to analysis can eliminate most indirect calls in functional code; FX mentions defunctionalization in §20.2 but shows no evidence of it in the lowering. CakeML splits closure conversion into its own language (closLang) — FX lumps it into one pass.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No vector/SIMD operations at FXCore level — they appear only in FXLow. This means SIMD decisions are made late, missing optimization opportunities visible at the typed level. No exception/effect handler lowering shown — how do delimited continuations become FXLow ops? No discussion of decision tree compilation quality (GHC's pattern match compiler is >5000 lines of sophisticated code). No treatment of how contract Validate/Encode/Decode operations lower to FXLow — they just disappear from the op list.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add explicit SIMD/vector ops at FXCore level (like MLIR's vector dialect) so type-directed vectorization can happen before lowering. Split closure conversion and ADT lowering into separate passes with intermediate representations (following CakeML's closLang/bvlLang split). Add a whole-program analysis pass (like GRIN's heap points-to) before closure conversion to enable defunctionalization where possible. Show how effect handler lowering works — this is a critical missing piece.

sections.name: FXLow to FXAsm: Instruction Selection and Register Allocation

analysis.what_fx_does: Instruction selection maps FXLow ops to target instructions via ISA semantics modules; register allocation uses graph coloring (or linear scan for fast mode); linearity improves liveness analysis by freeing registers at consumption points rather than last-use points.

closest_prior_art.approach: Backtracking register allocator with live-range splitting; 10-20% faster compile time and up to 7% better runtime than previous allocator; heavily fuzz-tested (10K+ lines of dense Rust)
Iterated Register Coalescing (IRC) algorithm verified in Coq; produces competitive code quality with full formal correctness guarantee
closest_prior_art.reference: Leroy, 'A formally verified compiler back-end', 2009; https://compcert.org/

closest_prior_art.approach: Greedy register allocator with live-range splitting, rematerialization, and register hints; GlobalISel framework for instruction selection replacing SelectionDAG

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The linearity-improves-liveness insight is genuinely valuable and novel — when a value is linearly consumed, the register allocator KNOWS it's dead at that point, not just 'might be dead'. This is tighter than conservative liveness analysis. No other production compiler exploits linear types for register allocation.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Graph coloring is the classical approach but modern compilers have moved on. Cranelift's regalloc2 uses backtracking with live-range splitting and achieves better results than graph coloring for JIT scenarios. LLVM's greedy allocator with rematerialization beats textbook graph coloring. The section says nothing about live-range splitting, rematerialization, or register coalescing — all critical for competitive code quality. The instruction selection description is extremely thin — no mention of pattern matching, tree tiling, or BURG-style code generation.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No instruction scheduling (crucial for out-of-order processors and especially for in-order ARM cores). No discussion of how the ISA semantics module drives instruction selection — is it pattern matching? Declarative rules? No register coalescing strategy. No discussion of how spill code is generated or optimized. No ABI specification for function calls. Frame layout is mentioned in one sentence with no detail.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Cranelift-style backtracking allocator with live-range splitting as the primary allocator, enhanced with the linearity information. Add instruction scheduling as a separate pass. Specify instruction selection as declarative rewrite rules over ISA semantics (similar to LLVM's TableGen patterns) rather than the vague 'maps to target instructions via ISA semantics'. Add rematerialization support — linearity tells you when NOT to rematerialize (consumed values cannot be recomputed).

analysis.what_fx_does: Per-architecture emit tables defining the instruction sequence for every (atomic operation, ordering, width) tuple on x86-64, arm64, rv64, mips64. The tables are the operational semantics of atomic operations — not a suggestion to a backend. Source DRF-SC semantics refine against each target's formal memory model. Sub-tables for: load/store, atomic RMW, CAS/cas_weak, fences, double-word atomics (CMPXCHG16B / CASP), seqlock<T> fallback protocol, per-arch constraint summary, auto-sync downgrade rules.

closest_prior_art.approach: Verified compiler for concurrent C under x86-TSO; instruction sequences for atomics proved to refine source semantics; precedent for per-arch emit tables as memory model
closest_prior_art.reference: Sevcik, Vafeiadis, Nardelli, Jagannathan, Sewell, 'CompCertTSO: A Verified Compiler for Relaxed-Memory Concurrency', J.ACM 2013

closest_prior_art.approach: Per-target assembly generation with verified semantics; x86-64, ARMv8, MIPS, RISC-V; no explicit atomic memory-model refinement but the approach is the right shape
closest_prior_art.reference: Fox & Myreen, 'Verified Compilation of CakeML to Multiple Machine-Code Targets', CPP 2017

closest_prior_art.approach: Per-target AtomicExpandPass lowers LLVM's abstract atomic model to target instructions; well-tested but not formally verified; thin-air problem inherited from C++11 source model
Official mapping from C/C++11 primitives to RISC-V RVWMO instruction sequences; FX's rv64 emit table is derived from this
closest_prior_art.reference: RISC-V Unprivileged ISA Specification, Appendix A.6

closest_prior_art.approach: Machine-checked HOL4 model of x86-TSO; FX's x86-64 emit table refines against this
closest_prior_art.reference: Sewell, Sarkar, Owens, Nardelli, Myreen, POPL 2009

closest_prior_art.approach: Simplified ARMv8 concurrency model with multi-copy atomicity; FX's arm64 emit table refines against this
closest_prior_art.reference: Pulte, Flur, Deacon, French, Sarkar, Sewell, POPL 2018

analysis.where_fx_is_better: No existing production language publishes per-arch emit tables as its memory model. C++/Rust/Swift negotiate their memory model with LLVM, inheriting LLVM's underspecifications and the C++ thin-air problem. CompCertTSO and CakeML achieve verified per-target emit but target only x86-TSO or a single chosen ISA; FX commits to four ISAs with explicit baselines. The ARMv8.1+ LSE baseline is a meaningful commitment — excludes legacy embedded ARMv8.0 by default but makes LSE's single-instruction atomics the assumed path. The auto-sync downgrade pass (§21.2) is concrete — five named conditions, five-way lattice, @[no_downgrade] escape for CT code. `fxc dump --atomics` audit trail is best-in-class for security review and LLM-generated code inspection.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The 120-lemma refinement proof (each (op, ord, width) cell × each ISA → formal model) is stated but not yet mechanized. CompCertTSO's whole refinement is machine-checked in Coq over ~8000 lines; FX's equivalent is pending. The table covers 30+ cells per arch × 4 arches; some edge cases (sub-word atomics on rv64 without Zabha, atomic_wide on rv64, mixed-width atomic RMW) are handled by fallback logic whose correctness argument is prose-only. MIPS64 is included but marked legacy — the emit table is correct but will not be test-exercised heavily. Interaction with §18 hardware refinement (fx-chip pipeline correctly implementing RVWMO atomics) is acknowledged but not worked out.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Mechanized refinement proof per cell × ISA. Automated fuzzer that runs litmus tests (2+2W, IRIW, MP, SB, WRC, ISA2) against the emitted code on real hardware and verifies the model's allowed outcomes. Cost numbers per instruction sequence per target — the prose gives approximate cycle counts but no pipeline-accurate model. Interaction with §18.12 hardware refinement for fx-chip. Handling of atomic operations to memory-mapped I/O (MMIO) regions — is an atomic store to MMIO semantically different from normal memory?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Mechanize the 120-lemma proof (~30 lemmas per arch × 4 arches). Each lemma is small: prove the instruction sequence satisfies the source operation's semantics under the formal ISA model. Ship a litmus-test framework that runs canonical tests (Sewell's lib) on every supported target and validates the emit sequences. Integrate with §18.12: fx-chip pipeline must correctly implement RV64 atomics, which is an extension of the existing ISA-refinement theorem. Add cost tables per microarchitecture (Skylake, Zen3, Apple M1, Cortex-A76, SiFive U74, Ampere Altra) to guide auto-sync heuristics and cost-model-driven optimization.

analysis.what_fx_does: One-pass encoding of target instructions to bytes using format types from section 17.1, with verified property decode(encode(insn)) == insn; outputs ELF/Mach-O/PE.

closest_prior_art.approach: Assembles and generates byte-level outputs for each ISA within the verified part of the compiler; low-level machine models include encoding; verified for x86-64, ARMv6, ARMv8, MIPS-64, RISC-V
closest_prior_art.reference: https://cakeml.org/cpp17.pdf; Fox & Myreen, 'Verified Compilation of CakeML to Multiple Machine-Code Targets', CPP 2017

closest_prior_art.approach: CSLED framework automatically generates verified instruction encoders and decoders from declarative ISA specifications; applied to complete X86 and RISC-V assembly in CompCert
Assembly languages modelled at AST level — assembling/encoding done OUTSIDE the verified part; known weakness vs CakeML
closest_prior_art.reference: Leroy, 'Formal verification of a realistic compiler', CACM 2009

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Using the same format types (section 17.1) for both hardware bit-field descriptions and instruction encoding is elegant — one mechanism serves two purposes. The decode(encode(insn))==insn property is the right correctness criterion. CakeML does this but CompCert historically did not (assembly was outside the trusted base).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: CakeML and the CSLED framework have ACTUAL verified assemblers with machine-checked proofs. FX states the property but provides no proof mechanism. The section is only 4 lines long — no discussion of how relocations work, how the ELF/Mach-O/PE format differences are handled, how linking interacts with verification. CompCert's recent work on verified assemblers for the ELF format shows this is a substantial engineering challenge.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of relocations, symbol tables, or linking. No specification of object file format details. No treatment of position-independent code (PIC). No debug information (DWARF) generation. No discussion of how separate compilation + linking preserves the end-to-end theorem. The CSLED approach of generating encoders/decoders from declarative specs is more principled than FX's hand-wavy 'uses format types'.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt the CSLED approach: define instruction encodings declaratively using section 17.1 format types, then auto-generate verified encoder/decoder pairs. Explicitly address relocations and linking — CompCert's recent work on verified ELF assemblers shows this is tractable. Add DWARF debug info generation (essential for any practical compiler). Specify how separate compilation interacts with the correctness theorem.

analysis.what_fx_does: Each target ISA is an FX module defining machine state (registers, memory, flags) and instruction semantics as a pure function; the same module serves codegen verification, hardware verification (section 17.12), and optimizer peephole rewriting; microarchitecture cost models are separate.

closest_prior_art.approach: Domain-specific language for ISA semantics; formal models for ARMv8-A (auto-translated from Arm's ASL), RISC-V (adopted by RISC-V International), MIPS, CHERI; generates theorem prover definitions (Isabelle/Coq/Lean), executable emulators, SystemVerilog reference models
closest_prior_art.reference: https://github.com/rems-project/sail; Armstrong et al., 'ISA Semantics for ARMv8-A, RISC-V, and CHERI-MIPS', POPL 2019

closest_prior_art.approach: Arm's internal Architecture Specification Language; authoritative source for ARMv8-A semantics; auto-translated to Sail for formal reasoning
Formal RISC-V specification adopted by RISC-V International; used as golden reference for hardware verification via ISA-Formal methodology; Pydrofoil for fast execution

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Unifying codegen verification, hardware verification, and peephole optimization in ONE specification is architecturally superior to Sail's approach where the same spec is translated to different forms for different uses. The separation of correctness (ISA semantics) from performance (microarch cost models) is clean and principled.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Sail has ACTUAL complete formal models for ARMv8-A (thousands of instructions), RISC-V (adopted by the standards body), and MIPS. FX shows a toy 3-instruction x86_64 example. The Sail RISC-V model is the official formal spec used by industry. Sail's type system was specifically designed to handle ISA-level complexity (bitvector-length-dependent types, exceptions, complex control flow). FX's general-purpose type system may struggle with the accidental complexity of real ISAs. ARM's ASL→Sail pipeline demonstrates that ISA specs need tooling for auto-translation from vendor pseudocode — FX has no story for this.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No story for how to actually write complete ISA specs for x86-64 (2000+ instructions), ARM (similarly complex), or even full RISC-V. No discussion of ISA extensions, optional features, or privileged instructions. No treatment of memory models (Sail integrates with relaxed memory model tools). No story for vendor adoption — Sail succeeded because it could auto-translate from ARM's existing ASL. The x86_64 example shows only Add/Call/Ret — the real challenge is SIMD, floating point, system instructions, memory ordering.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Be honest about bootstrapping: initially target only RISC-V (simplest ISA, good Sail reference to validate against). For x86-64 and ARM, plan to auto-translate from Sail specifications rather than hand-write FX modules. Add relaxed memory model integration (Sail does this). Consider whether FX's type system can actually handle the complexity of real ISA specs — Sail needed custom bitvector dependent types for this.

analysis.what_fx_does: States an end-to-end compiler correctness theorem: observable behavior of compiled binary equals observable behavior of source evaluation; proof chains simulation relations through erase_correct → lower_correct → select_correct → alloc_correct → encode_correct.

closest_prior_art.approach: End-to-end semantic preservation proved in Coq for 20 compilation passes; correctness stated via trace inclusion (observable events); handles undefined behavior gracefully since 2010; qualified for DO-178C avionics in 2026
closest_prior_art.reference: Leroy, 'Formal verification of a realistic compiler', CACM 2009; https://compcert.org/

closest_prior_art.approach: End-to-end correctness theorem in HOL4: compiled machine code has identical semantics to source up to out-of-memory errors; covers compilation to 6 target architectures with byte-level verified encoding
closest_prior_art.reference: https://cakeml.org/; 'The Verified CakeML Compiler Backend', JFP 2019

closest_prior_art.approach: Verified compiler for lazy functional language; correctness proved in three stages matching three semantic layers; first verified compiler implementing laziness

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The theorem structure is clean and the simulation chain is well-decomposed. If FX could actually prove this for a language with 21 type dimensions, it would be far beyond CompCert (C subset) or CakeML (ML variant). The ambition is extraordinary.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: CompCert's proof took person-decades of effort for a MUCH simpler language. CakeML similarly required enormous effort. FX's theorem is stated but there is zero discussion of proof methodology, proof effort estimation, or which parts might be proved first. The theorem ignores resource exhaustion (CakeML explicitly accounts for out-of-memory). The theorem ignores undefined behavior (CompCert handles this since 2010). The 'observable' function is undefined — CompCert uses traces of I/O events; what does FX use? The theorem also ignores concurrency — CompCertTSO needed substantial additional work to handle concurrent C under TSO.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Definition of 'observable'. Treatment of resource exhaustion (stack overflow, OOM). Treatment of concurrent programs. Treatment of floating-point — does 'equals' mean bit-identical across platforms? Estimation of proof effort. Incremental verification strategy — which passes to verify first? No mention of CompCertTSO's work on verified fence elimination, which is directly relevant to FX's automatic synchronization inference (section 20.2).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Define 'observable' precisely (trace of I/O events, as in CompCert). Add explicit treatment of resource exhaustion and nontermination. For concurrency, reference CompCertTSO's approach. Estimate proof effort honestly — even for a subset of FX (say, the Tot fragment without effects), the proof would take years. Consider a phased approach: (1) verify encoding correctness (smallest, most tractable), (2) verify register allocation (CompCert has done this), (3) verify lowering for the pure fragment, (4) extend to effects. State which parts are aspirational vs planned.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter covering optimization strategies: pipeline fusion via linear types, automatic synchronization inference, aggressive optimization (superoptimization, specialization, verified loop transforms, refinement-guided data structure selection), binary size minimization, tail call guarantees, memoization/idempotency annotations, and partial failure semantics.

closest_prior_art.approach: Stream fusion, deforestation, rewrite rules, inlining heuristics, strictness analysis, demand analysis — decades of optimization work for a lazy functional language
closest_prior_art.reference: Coutts et al., 'Stream Fusion: From Lists to Streams to Nothing at All', ICFP 2007

closest_prior_art.approach: Comprehensive optimization pipeline with 100+ passes including auto-vectorization, loop transformations, interprocedural optimization, PGO, LTO
Minimal runtime, direct syscall emission, comptime evaluation, no hidden allocations — achieves tiny binary sizes in practice

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The integration of refinement types as optimization input is genuinely novel — no other compiler uses statically verified constraints to drive data structure selection and specialization. The combination of linear types for fusion safety + effect system for parallelization safety is architecturally elegant.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Many claims are research aspirations not engineering reality. Superoptimization has never scaled beyond tiny kernels in any production compiler. LLVM's 100+ passes represent decades of engineering. GHC's fusion system, despite decades of work, still has known failure cases. FX's optimization chapter reads more like a research wishlist than a compiler specification.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No profiling-guided optimization (PGO) story. No link-time optimization (LTO) discussion. No auto-vectorization strategy beyond vague references. No inlining heuristics. No aliasing analysis (crucial for C-competitive optimization). No discussion of optimization stability — will the same source produce the same binary?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Separate proven optimization techniques (fusion, DCE, constant propagation, inlining) from research aspirations (superoptimization, refinement-guided data structure selection). Provide clear optimization levels like LLVM's O0-O3 with defined behavior at each level. Add PGO and LTO as first-class features.

analysis.what_fx_does: Adjacent map/filter/map operations on eager sources fuse into a single pass with no intermediate allocations; linear types prove intermediates are consumed once, making fusion safe.

closest_prior_art.approach: Transforms list operations to stream operations that GHC's inliner can fuse; handles zips, left folds, nested lists, comprehensions; relies on general-purpose compiler optimizations to eliminate intermediate values
closest_prior_art.reference: Coutts et al., 'Stream Fusion: From Lists to Streams to Nothing at All', ICFP 2007; https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1291220.1291199

closest_prior_art.approach: Indexed stream fusion for collections with mechanized proof of correctness in Lean; tested in Lean, Morphic, and Rust; requires specialization and linear update guarantees from host language
closest_prior_art.reference: Ji et al., 'Fast Collection Operations from Indexed Stream Fusion', 2025; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.06456

closest_prior_art.approach: Iterator trait with lazy chaining; LLVM optimizes away intermediate state in release mode; no formal fusion guarantee but works well in practice due to inlining

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Using linear types as the PROOF that fusion is safe is genuinely novel. GHC's stream fusion relies on fragile inlining heuristics — it can silently fail to fuse. Rust's iterator fusion depends on LLVM's optimizer. FX's approach makes fusion a TYPE-LEVEL guarantee, not an optimizer heuristic. The 2025 indexed stream fusion paper confirms that linear update + specialization are the right ingredients.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: GHC's stream fusion handles much more complex cases: zips, left folds, nested lists, comprehensions. FX shows only the trivial map/filter/map case. The 'Stream Fusion, to Completeness' paper (Kiselyov et al., POPL 2017) achieves guaranteed fusion for a much wider class of operations using staging. FX's approach as described may not handle zip, flat_map, or fold fusion. Staged fusion (Andras Kovacs's typed Template Haskell approach) gives stronger guarantees.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: What happens with zip? flat_map? fold/reduce? These are the hard cases where GHC's fusion often fails. No discussion of when fusion should NOT happen (fusing a very large pipeline into one function can cause register pressure issues). No formal specification of which operations are fusible. No benchmarks or performance claims. The section is only ~15 lines — far too thin for a critical optimization.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify exactly which operations are fusible and prove a completeness result (all fusible pipelines WILL be fused). Consider staged fusion (Kiselyov et al., POPL 2017) for guaranteed completeness. Address zip, flat_map, scan, fold explicitly. Discuss fusion inhibitors and provide diagnostics when fusion fails. The linearity insight is strong — develop it into a proper formal framework with a fusion theorem.

analysis.what_fx_does: Compiler analyzes data flow in concurrent code, builds happens-before graph from access modes and data dependencies, and inserts minimal synchronization: nothing (for channel-transferred linear values), fences (weakest sufficient), atomic ops (for single-word RMW), or locks (for multi-step mutual exclusion); does NOT synthesize lock-free algorithms from sequential code.

closest_prior_art.approach: Verified fence elimination optimizations for x86-TSO; proved correct in Coq as part of a full verified compiler for concurrent C; uses non-standard global simulation argument
closest_prior_art.reference: Sevcik et al., 'Verifying Fence Elimination Optimisations', ESOP 2011; https://fzn.fr/readings/fenceelim.pdf

closest_prior_art.approach: Prototype tool for automatic fence insertion under TSO/PSO/VIPS memory models; sound and complete fence insertion procedure for concurrent finite-state programs; counter-example guided
closest_prior_art.reference: https://github.com/memorax/memorax; Abdulla et al., 'Memorax, a Precise and Sound Tool for Automatic Fence Insertion under TSO', TACAS 2013

closest_prior_art.approach: Verification-based approach to memory fence insertion in relaxed memory systems; starts from sequentially-correct program and inserts minimal fences for TSO/x86-TSO safety
closest_prior_art.reference: Linden & Wolper, 'A Verification-Based Approach to Memory Fence Insertion in Relaxed Memory Systems', SPIN 2011; https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-642-22306-8_10

analysis.where_fx_is_better:
[Resolved per Proposal 6] Verdict raised from 'adequate' to 'strong'. The section is now concrete: source defaults to @SeqCst everywhere; the compiler downgrades per a specific lattice (SeqCst → AcqRel → Acquire/Release → Relaxed) with five named conditions (channel-established ordering, join-point ordering, data-independent use, no cross-thread read before join, single-threaded execution). The @[no_downgrade] attribute is the explicit escape for CT crypto. `fxc dump --atomics` prints the emit sequence per target, giving a concrete audit trail. Linear types make 'nothing needed' the dominant case — channels and ownership establish happens-before without any synchronization. Pattern recognition for lock-free idioms (Treiber stack, M&S queue) reuses stdlib-verified templates rather than hand-rolling. Earlier analysis preserved below.

The disclaimer that the compiler 'does not synthesize lock-free algorithms from sequential code' is honest and important — it takes code with concurrent INTENT and makes it correct. The integration with linear types (channel-transferred values need no synchronization) is a genuine improvement over prior art that treats all shared access uniformly. The four-level hierarchy (nothing/fence/atomic/lock) with automatic selection is cleaner than manual annotation.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse:
[Partially mitigated] The downgrade algorithm's five conditions are stated; soundness of each condition under happens-before analysis is argued but not mechanized. Lock-free pattern recognition is restricted to stdlib templates — not extensible; programmers with novel patterns must write explicit atomics. CompCertTSO-style mechanized correctness for the downgrade pass is not yet present. Earlier analysis preserved below.

Memorax and related tools are VERIFIED to insert correct and minimal fences for specific memory models. FX provides no correctness argument. The academic fence insertion literature assumes finite-state programs for decidability — FX's programs are not finite-state, so the analysis is necessarily conservative. CompCertTSO showed that even REMOVING redundant fences requires subtle global simulation arguments — FX's INSERTION of fences is at least as hard. The section doesn't specify which memory models are targeted (TSO? ARM's relaxed model? RISC-V RVWMO?).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Mechanized correctness for the downgrade pass. Extensible lock-free pattern library (user-declared templates that the compiler matches). Perf counters showing real-world downgrade hit rates. Integration with §10 verification: prove a user's concurrent code satisfies its ensures clause under the downgraded emit sequence.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Mechanize each downgrade condition as a lemma over the emit tables and the formal ISA model. Publish benchmark numbers (% of @SeqCst ops downgraded in typical concurrent programs). Consider a user-facing diagnostic: 'fxc explain-atomic L42 Server.fx' shows the HB graph, the chosen ordering, and which downgrade condition fired.

analysis.what_fx_does: Under --release=aggressive: superoptimization (enumerate all instruction sequences for small kernels, check equivalence via SMT), whole-program specialization (generate versions per call-site refinements), verified loop transformations (tiling, fusion, vectorization, parallelization), refinement-guided data structure selection (e.g., map with key<256 becomes array), partial evaluation, rewrite templates (proved-equivalent pattern/replacement pairs), refinement suggestions, and compile time budgets.

closest_prior_art.approach: Stochastic superoptimizer for x86-64; MCMC-based randomized search over instruction sequences; sound loop superoptimization achieved 25% median speedup on libc string functions
closest_prior_art.reference: Schkufza et al., 'Stochastic Superoptimization', ASPLOS 2013; Churchill et al., 'Sound Loop Superoptimization for Google Native Client', ASPLOS 2017

closest_prior_art.approach: LLM-based superoptimization (SuperCoder: 95% correctness, 1.46x speedup) and caching-based superoptimization (EPSO: 795 rewrite rules, 68.87% size reduction vs Clang)
closest_prior_art.reference: SuperCoder, OpenReview 2025; EPSO via Dataflow-based pruning, OOPSLA 2020

closest_prior_art.approach: Semi-automatic data type refinement environment; selects efficient implementations of high-level data types (sets, sequences) based on program analysis; closest existing work to FX's refinement-guided data structure selection

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The integration of refinement types as optimization input is genuinely novel and potentially powerful — no other compiler uses statically verified constraints like 'key: nat{<256}' to automatically select data structure implementations. The refinement suggestion feature ('adding this pre enables this optimization') creates a feedback loop between programmer and optimizer that doesn't exist elsewhere. Compile time budgets are practical.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Superoptimization has NEVER been practical in a production compiler. STOKE (2013) worked on tiny kernels. Even the 2024-2025 work (SuperCoder, EPSO) is research, not production. Sound loop superoptimization (Churchill 2017) was limited to libc string functions. FX claims superoptimization as a compiler feature but provides no discussion of search space bounds, timeout behavior, or fallback. Whole-program specialization conflicts with separate compilation. Verified loop transformations are an entire PhD thesis each (tiling alone has a huge literature). The section bundles together 7 different research programs as if they're features to be implemented.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No profile-guided optimization (PGO) — this is the single most impactful optimization in practice and FX doesn't mention it. No auto-vectorization strategy (mentioned only as 'vectorization' in one line). No cost model for when to apply each optimization. No discussion of phase ordering (the order of optimizations matters enormously). No intermediate representation for the rewrite template database. The 'refinement-guided data structure selection' table shows 6 examples but no algorithm for how the compiler decides.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Separate the section into 'implemented optimizations' (fusion, DCE, constant propagation, inlining — these are table stakes) and 'research directions' (superoptimization, verified loop transforms). Add PGO as a first-class feature. For data structure selection, define the algorithm: what refinements trigger what selections, how are conflicts resolved, what happens when refinements are too weak? Drop superoptimization from the compiler spec and instead provide a standalone tool (like STOKE) that can optimize hot loops offline.

analysis.what_fx_does: No mandatory runtime; binary contains service modules only for declared effects; direct syscall emission (no libc/PLT/GOT/dynamic linker); hello world ~250 bytes, bare-metal LED blink ~40 bytes, full web service ~200KB; effect-driven linking excludes unused service modules.

closest_prior_art.approach: Minimal runtime, ~2KB hello world out of box, ~200-700 bytes with direct syscalls + LTO + sstrip; no hidden control flow, no hidden allocations, comptime evaluation
With no_std + no_main + custom panic handler + direct syscalls, can reach sub-KB sizes; default hello world is much larger due to libstd; requires significant effort for minimal binaries
Benchmark comparing Hello World binary sizes across 18 languages; C with musl achieves ~14KB static, Zig ~2KB, assembly ~200 bytes

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Effect-driven linking is genuinely novel — excluding service modules based on EFFECT declarations rather than symbol references is more precise and compositionally sound. The graduated size table with clear effect-to-size mapping is excellent engineering communication. The claim of ~250 byte hello world is competitive with hand-optimized assembly.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Zig ACTUALLY achieves ~200-700 byte hello worlds today, with a real compiler. FX's claims are unverified. The ~250 byte figure is suspiciously optimistic — a minimal ELF with write syscall + exit is ~170 bytes (the stated overhead), leaving only ~80 bytes for the actual print logic including the string, which is tight but possible. The ~40 byte bare-metal LED blink is plausible for raw binary format. However, the ~200KB 'full web service + DB' is extremely optimistic — Zig's equivalent would be much larger. The direct syscall approach means no compatibility across kernel versions (syscall numbers can change, though in practice they rarely do on Linux).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of dynamic linking (needed for plugins, shared libraries). No treatment of how stdlib modules affect binary size (section 25 describes a HUGE stdlib). No discussion of debugging support cost (debug info can be 10x the binary). No mention of compiler-RT equivalent for soft-float, 128-bit math, etc. No cross-platform story for macOS (Mach-O) or Windows (PE) direct syscalls. The binary size claims should be backed by actual measurements from an implemented compiler.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Provide a formal model of effect-driven linking with a proof that it's sound (no runtime failures from missing service modules). Add opt-in dynamic linking for plugin scenarios. Distinguish between release binary sizes and debug binary sizes. Be honest that the size claims are targets, not measurements, until the compiler exists. Consider providing a 'binary size budget' tool that shows per-function contribution.

analysis.what_fx_does: rec functions default to requiring tail calls (with TailCall effect); non-tail recursion must opt in via 'with StackRecursion'; this makes tail call optimization the default rather than optional.

closest_prior_art.approach: Tail call elimination is REQUIRED by the language standard; the spec precisely defines which syntactic forms allow tail position; all conforming implementations must support proper tail calls
Explicit tail call instructions (return_call, return_call_indirect) added as extension; became Baseline across all browsers December 2024; toolchain responsible for emitting tail vs non-tail calls
@tailrec annotation causes compile error if function is not tail-recursive; similar idea of making tail call a checked property

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Making tail calls the DEFAULT for recursive functions is a stronger guarantee than Scheme (which requires it globally but gives no diagnostic if you accidentally write non-tail code) or Scala (which requires manual annotation). The opt-in 'with StackRecursion' for non-tail recursion makes the stack usage cost visible in the type signature.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Scheme's guarantee applies to ALL calls in tail position, not just self-recursive calls. FX's 'rec' modifier only applies to explicitly recursive functions — mutual tail calls, continuation-passing, and general tail positions are not addressed. WebAssembly's explicit tail call instructions provide hardware-level support that FX would need to target. The section doesn't discuss mutual recursion tail calls (the 'and' keyword from section 5.2).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Does tail call guarantee extend to mutual recursion via 'and'? Does it apply to indirect calls (calling a function value in tail position)? How does this interact with effect handlers (calling a continuation in tail position)? No discussion of how tail calls interact with debugging (tail calls destroy stack frames, making debugging harder). No mention of whether the guarantee extends to the WebAssembly target.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Extend the guarantee to all calls in tail position (Scheme-style), not just self-recursive calls with 'rec'. This is strictly more powerful and eliminates a class of bugs where programmers forget that mutual recursion or CPS-style code needs tail calls. Add a diagnostic mode that shows which calls are in tail position and which are not. Address the debugging story (some implementations offer 'tail call with shadow stack for debugging').

analysis.what_fx_does: Annotations for cache semantics: NeverMemoize (crypto-safe, prevents caching), Memoize (declares referential transparency); for distributed systems: Idempotent(key: ...) for retry safety, Commutative for reordering safety, Reversible(undo: ...) for compensation; compiler verifies properties (e.g., migrate; rollback = identity).

closest_prior_art.approach: Type system that checks algebraic properties (commutativity, associativity, idempotence) of CRDT merge functions; automated inductive theorem prover verifies properties
closest_prior_art.reference: https://propel-prover.github.io/; 'Type-Checking CRDT Convergence', Programming Group UniSG, 2023

closest_prior_art.approach: Row-polymorphic effect types track side effects; type-directed compilation of algebraic effects; no specific support for commutativity/idempotency but effect system could encode it
closest_prior_art.reference: Leijen, 'Koka: Programming with Row-polymorphic Effect Types', 2014; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1406.2061

closest_prior_art.approach: Conflict-free Replicated Data Types require merge to be commutative, associative, idempotent; no existing production language tracks these properties in the type system

analysis.where_fx_is_better: No production language currently tracks commutativity, idempotency, or reversibility as type-level properties. Propel (the closest work) is a research prototype for CRDTs only. FX's approach of making these properties visible in function signatures for distributed systems is genuinely novel. The NeverMemoize annotation for crypto is a practical safety feature that doesn't exist elsewhere. The compiler verification of Reversible (migrate; rollback = identity) is a strong correctness guarantee.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The properties are stated as annotations but the VERIFICATION story is unclear. Proving commutativity (f(a,b) = f(b,a) for all a,b) requires inductive proof over all inputs — how does the compiler do this? SMT? Property-based testing? The Propel paper shows this requires specialized theorem proving. 'Idempotent' for distributed systems is typically implemented with idempotency keys in middleware, not enforced at the type level — FX conflates the concept with the implementation. 'Commutative' as a type annotation provides no mechanism for the runtime to actually exploit commutativity.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: How does the compiler verify commutativity? SMT can't prove it for arbitrary functions over unbounded domains. What happens at the distributed system boundary — does the Idempotent annotation generate middleware? How does Reversible interact with effects (rollback_migration presumably has effects)? No discussion of performance implications of idempotency checking (maintaining key→result maps). The Memoize annotation is just referential transparency — how does it differ from Tot? No discussion of cache invalidation semantics.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Separate the ANNOTATION from the VERIFICATION. Annotations are cheap; verification of commutativity/idempotency is hard. Specify exactly which verification strategies apply: SMT for algebraic types, property-based testing for complex functions, manual proof for the rest. For distributed systems, generate actual middleware from the Idempotent annotation (like a deduplication layer). Connect Commutative to CRDT literature and provide standard CRDT building blocks. Define Memoize as 'Tot + @[copy] on arguments and return type' to make it precise.

analysis.what_fx_does: Effect annotations distinguish all-or-nothing writes (with IO, Atomic — all bytes written or error) from partial writes (with IO, Partial — returns count of bytes written); postconditions can constrain partial results.

closest_prior_art.approach: Returns number of bytes written (partial write); atomicity only guaranteed for pipes/FIFOs up to PIPE_BUF; no type-level distinction between atomic and partial
write() may write partial data; write_all() loops until complete or error; distinction is at the API level (different methods), not the type level

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Making the partial-vs-atomic distinction visible in the effect system is genuinely useful — callers can see from the type whether they need to handle partial writes. This prevents a common class of bugs where programmers assume write() writes all data. No other language makes this distinction at the type level.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The section is only 10 lines of code with no explanation. It doesn't explain HOW atomic writes are implemented — does the compiler generate a retry loop? Use a different syscall? Buffer the entire output? The POSIX semantics of write() are inherently partial for most file descriptors — claiming 'Atomic' requires either buffering (which can fail for different reasons) or using specific syscalls (writev with specific flags). Rust's approach of providing write() and write_all() as separate functions is simpler and equally clear.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Implementation strategy for Atomic writes. Error semantics when Atomic write partially fails (is the state rolled back?). Interaction with non-blocking I/O. Interaction with async effects. What about reads — is there Atomic read? What about other I/O operations (network send, file seek)? The section is far too thin to be a specification — it's two code snippets with no prose.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand significantly. Define the semantics of Atomic precisely: does it buffer and write at once? Retry on partial? Use specialized syscalls? Specify error recovery: if Atomic write fails after writing some bytes, what state is the file in? Generalize beyond write: Atomic should apply to any I/O operation that can partially complete (network send, database insert batch). Consider adding 'Resumable' as a third option (like Rust's write() which returns bytes written for caller to loop).

analysis.what_fx_does: FX introduces sketch mode — a project profile (not a dialect) that uses the same syntax and parser but defers proof obligations to runtime, relaxes ownership/effects/security to warnings, and runs on a bytecode VM with sub-100ms startup, bridging the gap between rapid prototyping and full verification.

closest_prior_art.approach: Gradual strictness via tsconfig flags (strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, etc.) that can be enabled incrementally; TypeScript 6.0 makes strict the default. Migration tools like @ts-migrating allow per-line fallback.
Formally derived gradual verification system where imprecise specifications are filled optimistically and verified at runtime; pay-as-you-go cost model from static to dynamic checking.
closest_prior_art.reference: Gradual C0: Symbolic Execution for Gradual Verification, TOPLAS 46(4), 2025

closest_prior_art.approach: Supports varying strictness levels including decreases * to skip termination checks, and progressive verification via the assures keyword.
closest_prior_art.reference: Towards Progressive Program Verification in Dafny, Figueroa et al., SBLP 2018

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's sketch mode is more comprehensive than TypeScript's strict mode because it spans all 21 type dimensions, not just null checks and implicit any. The idea that the same source file can move along a spectrum from fully dynamic to fully verified without changing syntax is stronger than Dafny's approach where the specification language is always present. The integration with bytecode VM, record/replay, and hot reload makes the sketch-to-verified gradient practical rather than theoretical.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: TypeScript's gradual typing has been validated at massive scale (Bloomberg: 50M lines, 2000+ engineers; GitHub: 2.64M monthly contributors as of 2025). FX's sketch mode is entirely aspirational with zero implementation experience. CMU's gradual verification has formal metatheory (AGT methodology) proving the gradual guarantee — FX does not describe any formal property that sketch mode preserves. The claim that sketch mode is 'comparable to TypeScript in strictness' is unsupported; TypeScript's structural type system with union types, conditional types, and mapped types is far more sophisticated than basic type checking.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No formal gradual guarantee (adding annotations should not introduce new errors in working code). No description of how runtime checks interact with linear ownership — if ownership is relaxed to warnings, what prevents double-free or use-after-free at runtime? No migration tooling beyond 'fxc annotate' — TypeScript ecosystem has sophisticated per-line, per-file, per-package migration strategies. No description of sketch mode performance characteristics beyond '100ms startup'.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt the AGT (Abstracting Gradual Typing) methodology from Garcia et al. 2016 and Aldrich's gradual verification work to formally derive the sketch-to-normal transition. Define a gradual guarantee: if a sketch-mode program runs without warnings, adding type annotations will not cause it to fail. Specify how linear resources are handled at runtime in sketch mode — likely need a reference-counting GC as fallback.

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines a comprehensive fatal/warning/silent categorization across all 21 type dimensions for sketch mode, covering ~80 specific diagnostic cases spanning core types, refinements, ownership, effects, security, protocols, lifetimes, provenance, trust, representation, observability, clock domains, complexity, precision, space, overflow, FP order, mutation, reentrancy, size, and version.

closest_prior_art.approach: Individual compiler flags (strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictFunctionTypes, etc.) that can be enabled independently, plus @ts-expect-error and @ts-ignore for per-line suppression. TypeScript 6.0 makes all strict flags default.
mypy has --strict flag plus individual toggles (disallow_untyped_defs, warn_return_any, etc.). Pyright has off/basic/standard/strict/all modes. Meta 2024 survey: 67% use mypy, 38% Pyright.
Sound gradual typing with runtime contracts at typed/untyped boundaries. Compositional type narrowing tracking through program execution — something no other gradual system does.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: This is the most detailed sketch/gradual mode specification in any language design document. TypeScript has ~15 strict-mode flags; FX enumerates ~80 specific diagnostic behaviors across 21 dimensions. The three-tier fatal/warning/silent classification is cleaner than TypeScript's ad-hoc flag accumulation. The per-dimension categorization means the sketch profile is systematically derived from the type system rather than being an afterthought.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: TypeScript's strict mode has been battle-tested at massive scale and the flag interactions are well-understood through years of production use. FX's 80+ warning categories create a combinatorial explosion of potential interactions — how do relaxed ownership warnings interact with relaxed effect warnings? Typed Racket's approach is formally sound (runtime contracts guarantee type soundness); FX's sketch mode has no soundness guarantee. The 'convenience relaxations' (mutable references without ceremony) are dangerous — silently wrapping in ref could mask serious ownership bugs.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No prioritization or grouping of warnings for migration — which warnings should be fixed first? No per-file or per-function opt-in/opt-out (TypeScript allows per-file tsconfig, per-line @ts-expect-error). No description of warning interaction semantics — e.g., if ownership is relaxed AND effects are relaxed, can a sketch program silently perform IO through a supposedly-pure function that also double-uses a linear resource? No runtime cost model for the ~30 categories that become runtime assertions. The 'convenience relaxations' section (mutable refs without ceremony, prelude) is a significant design decision buried in 2 lines.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Define warning groups that can be enabled together (ownership-group, security-group, effects-group) for staged migration. Add per-function @[sketch] and @[strict] annotations for gradual file-level migration. Specify which runtime assertions are O(1) vs O(n) — a runtime check for 'index might be out of bounds' is O(1) but 'is_sorted(result)' is O(n). Define interaction semantics formally: what invariants hold when multiple dimensions are relaxed simultaneously?

analysis.what_fx_does: Sketch mode compiles to a stack-based bytecode VM with no monomorphization, no native codegen, no SMT. Claims sub-100ms startup for files under 10K lines, sub-10ms cached reload via content-hash .fxb files. The VM loads native-compiled modules as shared objects for stdlib/dependencies. Three execution modes: default (sketch=bytecode, imports=native), trace-module (force specific module to bytecode), full-vm (all bytecode).

closest_prior_art.approach: Zig compiles to bytecode for a VM that evaluates comptime. Known performance bottleneck — ~20x slower than interpreted Python for complex comptime. Zig 0.15.1 achieved 275ms hello-world compilation with new x86 backend.

Self-optimizing AST interpreters with JIT compilation via partial evaluation. Achieves near-native performance for dynamic languages. Requires JVM startup overhead but reaches peak performance through specialization.
Simple stack-based bytecode VM. Python 3.13 introduced free-threaded mode and JIT compilation. Startup is ~30-50ms for simple scripts. Well-understood performance characteristics.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The three execution modes (default/trace-module/full-vm) are a thoughtful design for debugging — you can selectively force modules to bytecode for trace visibility while keeping stdlib native. The native module interop via shared objects is practical. Content-hash caching for sub-10ms reload is a strong development experience target.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Sub-100ms startup for 10K lines is a claim without implementation backing. Zig's comptime VM, which is simpler (no type inference, no effect tracking), is acknowledged as a major performance bottleneck. FX's sketch VM must do type inference, effect inference, and convert proof obligations to runtime checks — this is strictly harder than Zig's comptime. GraalVM Truffle achieves good performance only through sophisticated self-optimizing AST rewriting and JIT compilation — FX describes a 'simple stack-based VM' which will be far slower. The section gives no bytecode instruction set, no performance model, no memory management strategy for the VM.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No bytecode instruction set specification. No GC strategy for the VM (the native FX has no GC — what does the sketch VM use?). No description of how type inference works at bytecode level. No benchmark data or complexity analysis supporting the 100ms claim. No description of FFI between bytecode and native beyond 'platform calling convention'. No error handling for when native modules and bytecode disagree on types.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Consider using a Truffle-style self-optimizing AST interpreter instead of a simple stack-based VM — it would achieve much better peak performance with less implementation effort. Alternatively, consider compiling sketch mode to WASM or an existing VM (JVM, CLR) rather than building a custom bytecode VM from scratch. At minimum, define the bytecode instruction set and provide a realistic performance model.

analysis.what_fx_does: The sketch VM records every execution step to .fxr files containing effect logs (IO results replayed on re-run), periodic VM state snapshots (stack, heap, bytecode offset), and full instruction traces. Supports resuming at any bytecode instruction by loading nearest snapshot and replaying forward. Provides REPL-style interaction at crash points with :rewind capability.

closest_prior_art.approach: Records all kernel inputs and nondeterministic CPU effects (rdtsc, performance counters) for deterministic replay of native Linux processes. Provides reverse execution via GDB. Pernosco builds omniscient database from rr traces for instant time navigation and dataflow tracking.

Built on rr recordings, pre-computes omniscient database of all CPU-level states via binary instrumentation in parallel. Web-based UI with instant time navigation, dataflow visualization. Users report 100x speedup in debugging.
Records deterministic browser session captures for JavaScript. Analyzes recordings to identify root causes. Focused on frontend debugging with time-travel capabilities.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's record-and-replay is deeply integrated with the language's effect system — pure functions are re-executed, effectful calls replayed from logs. This is semantically richer than rr's blind recording of syscalls. The integration with the REPL and the ability to evaluate arbitrary expressions at any recorded point is more ergonomic than rr's GDB interface. The snapshot-and-replay-forward strategy is simpler to implement than rr's hardware performance counter approach.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: rr works on native compiled code at full speed with ~1.2x recording overhead; FX's approach only works on bytecode VM, which is already orders of magnitude slower. Pernosco pre-computes ALL states and provides instant time-travel with dataflow tracking — FX must replay forward from the nearest snapshot, which is slow for distant points. rr has been battle-tested on Chrome, QEMU, LibreOffice; FX's approach is entirely theoretical. The effect log approach breaks down for non-deterministic native calls that aren't tagged as effectful.
