analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of recording overhead (space and time). No description of snapshot frequency strategy (every N instructions? adaptive?). No handling of non-deterministic native calls beyond effects (e.g., thread scheduling, memory allocation addresses). No compression strategy for potentially massive instruction traces. No description of .fxr file format. The example shows :rewind by instruction count — no way to rewind to a specific source line or condition.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Consider adopting Pernosco's approach of deferred omniscient database construction from recordings — run the program once, then pre-compute all states in parallel for instant navigation. Add conditional rewind (rewind to where x changed) rather than just instruction counts. Specify recording overhead budget (e.g., 2x slowdown target) and adaptive snapshot frequency based on heap size.

analysis.what_fx_does: Provides 'inspect;' statement that pauses execution and drops into a REPL, and 'checkpoint(name)' for agent-driven non-interactive checkpointing. Agents can load checkpoints and evaluate expressions via 'fxc replay file.fxr --eval expression'.

closest_prior_art.approach: Built-in breakpoint() function (Python 3.7+) that drops into pdb by default but is configurable via PYTHONBREAKPOINT env var. Any debugger can hook into it. Simple and well-adopted.

Everything is a live object in a running image. Inspector, Browser, Debugger are all Smalltalk objects. Can edit code mid-exception, restart from any call stack frame, inspect/modify any object state. The gold standard for live programming.
@infiltrate macro pauses execution and opens a REPL-like mode. JuliaInterpreter.jl provides full debugging with breakpoints. Revise.jl enables code reloading.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The checkpoint() for agents is a genuinely useful innovation — no other language explicitly designs mid-program interactivity for non-human consumers. The combination of inspect (interactive) and checkpoint (programmatic) covers both human and agent workflows. The integration with the replay system means checkpoints are reusable across sessions.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Smalltalk's live programming is far more powerful — you can edit code mid-exception and restart execution, modify any object's state, recompile individual methods. FX's inspect is read-only (evaluate expressions but cannot modify state or patch code at the inspect point). Python's breakpoint() is simpler and already universally adopted. The section is only ~30 lines — very thin compared to the depth needed.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Can you modify variables during inspect? Can you call functions with side effects? Can you resume with modified state? Can you set conditional breakpoints (inspect when loss < threshold)? What happens to linear ownership during an inspect REPL session — is the inspected state borrowed? Can checkpoint() be used in production builds or only sketch mode? No specification of REPL capabilities (tab completion, history, multi-line input).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Allow state modification during inspect (at least for ref bindings). Add conditional inspect: 'inspect when condition;'. Allow patching functions during inspect (Smalltalk-style). Define clearly what expressions are evaluable in the REPL — can you allocate? perform IO? The agent checkpoint workflow is novel and should be expanded with more examples and capabilities (e.g., checkpoint diffing, checkpoint branching).

analysis.what_fx_does: A trace reconstructor reads .fxr instruction logs and produces human-readable listings showing every function call inlined, every loop iteration, every intermediate value with inferred types and all 21 type dimensions. Supports verbosity levels: --values, default (types+values), --full (all 21 dimensions), --show specific dimensions.

closest_prior_art.approach: Go's runtime/trace package produces traces of goroutine execution viewable via go tool trace or gotraceui. Shows goroutine scheduling, GC events, syscalls. Per-goroutine timeline visualization.
Free online step-by-step visualization of program execution for Python/Java/C/C++/JS. Shows stack frames, heap objects at each step. Educational focus.
Combines notebook experience with systems code — uses visualization language (Python) to translate program states into visual representations. LaTeX and HTML frontends.
closest_prior_art.reference: Literate Tracing, Matthew Sotoudeh, Onward! 2025, ACM SIGPLAN

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's trace is unique in showing all 21 type dimensions per value — ownership state (own/consumed), effect purity, security classification, provenance, version, size, etc. No other trace tool shows this level of semantic richness. The CONSUMED annotation for linear values is particularly useful for understanding ownership flow. The selective dimension display (--show mode,security) is practical.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Go's execution traces are for real compiled code at production scale; FX's traces are only available for bytecode VM execution. Python Tutor provides visual interactive stepping; FX provides text-only output. TReX allows custom visualization via Python code; FX's trace output is fixed-format. For large programs, the text trace will be enormous and unusable — no interactive navigation, filtering, or search described.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No interactive trace viewer (only text output described). No filtering by function, variable, or condition. No diff between two traces. No integration with the concurrent trace format (.fxt). No trace size management — a 10K-line program with loops could produce millions of trace lines. No visual timeline or graphical representation. No integration with IDE/editor.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add an interactive trace viewer (web-based like Perfetto UI or terminal-based). Support trace queries: 'show me everywhere x was modified' or 'show the ownership chain of this value'. Add trace diffing for comparing two runs. Integrate with .fxt concurrent traces for a unified view. Consider TReX-style programmable trace visualization.

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines .fxt binary format for concurrent traces with 64-byte event records containing Lamport clock timestamp, thread/task ID, event type (12 types), source line, bytecode PC, and variable-length data. Events with equal timestamps on different threads are genuinely concurrent. Provides 8 rendering views: per-thread timeline, multi-column interleaving, sync points, shared state accesses, coroutine suspend/resume, data races, lock cycles, and aggregate stats.

closest_prior_art.approach: End-to-end request tracing across microservices via OpenTelemetry. Jaeger v2 (Nov 2024) uses OpenTelemetry at its core. Spans model causality via parent-child relationships. Timeline, graph, table views.

JSON-based trace format with process/thread IDs, timestamps, duration events. Now backed by Perfetto's protobuf format. Widely adopted as interchange format for profiling tools. Microsecond wall-clock timestamps.
O(n) space per event (n = number of processes) but can detect concurrent events, unlike Lamport clocks which only provide partial ordering. Used in distributed systems for causal consistency.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The 8 specialized rendering views are more comprehensive than Jaeger's single timeline+graph view. The dedicated --races and --deadlocks views are unique — most tracing tools require separate analysis tools for these. The fixed 64-byte event record is space-efficient and cache-line-aligned. Event types cover the full spectrum from instructions to concurrency primitives.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: FX uses Lamport clocks, which CANNOT detect concurrent events — they only provide happens-before ordering. The spec claims 'events with equal timestamps on different threads are genuinely concurrent' but this is WRONG for Lamport clocks. Equal Lamport timestamps do NOT imply concurrency — you need vector clocks for that. Jaeger and OpenTelemetry are production-grade distributed tracing systems used at massive scale; FX's .fxt is theoretical. Chrome Trace Format is a widely-adopted standard with rich tooling; FX invents a custom binary format with no ecosystem.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: CRITICAL BUG: The concurrency claim about Lamport clocks is incorrect. Either switch to vector clocks (O(n) space per event instead of O(1)) or weaken the claim to 'events without causal ordering MAY be concurrent.' No description of how the 8 rendering views are implemented or displayed. No integration with standard tracing formats (OpenTelemetry, Chrome Trace). No scalability analysis — how many events per second can be recorded? No export format for external analysis tools.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Replace Lamport clocks with vector clocks or hybrid logical clocks (HLC) that combine wall-clock and logical ordering. Support export to Chrome Trace Format or OpenTelemetry for compatibility with existing visualization ecosystems (Perfetto UI, Jaeger UI). Add a web-based trace viewer. Define maximum recording overhead budget.

analysis.what_fx_does: In REPL or at inspect breakpoint, :reload recompiles changed functions to bytecode and patches the VM's function table. Existing data stays in memory. If reloaded function's inferred type conflicts with live values, VM warns and offers to discard affected bindings. Native modules can also be reloaded from build cache.

closest_prior_art.approach: Production-grade hot code swapping supporting 2 module versions simultaneously. Includes sys:suspend/sys:change_code/sys:resume protocol, code_change callbacks for state migration, and full OTP release upgrade (relup) system. Used at Ericsson for telecom-grade zero-downtime upgrades.

Image-based: edit code mid-exception, recompile individual methods while system runs, automatic object layout migration when instance variables change. The entire running environment IS the development environment.
Module-level replacement with state preservation. React Fast Refresh preserves component state across reloads. Sub-second feedback loops. Widely adopted for frontend development.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's type-checking on reload is better than Erlang's (Erlang's code_change callback is manual and error-prone — Ericsson reportedly spends as much time testing relups as testing applications) and better than JavaScript HMR (which has no type-aware reload). The 'warn and offer to discard' strategy for type conflicts is practical. Supporting both bytecode and native module reload is flexible.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Erlang's hot code loading works in production systems with zero downtime and handles state migration via explicit code_change callbacks — FX's approach is development-only (REPL/inspect). Smalltalk allows editing code mid-exception and resuming — far more powerful than FX's function-table patching. JavaScript HMR handles UI state preservation with framework integration (React Fast Refresh) — FX has no UI framework integration described. FX's 'discard affected bindings' is crude compared to Erlang's explicit state migration protocol.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No state migration protocol — what happens when a function's closure captures values of the old type? No handling of in-flight computations (what if a background task is running the old version?). No versioning — Erlang keeps 2 versions simultaneously to handle in-progress calls. No hot-reload for type definitions (adding/removing fields). The section is short (~20 lines) for a complex feature. No description of what 'patches the VM's function table' means precisely — is this atomic? What about concurrent access?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add Erlang-style state migration callbacks for data that needs transformation. Support type definition reload with automatic field migration (Smalltalk does this). Specify atomicity guarantees for the reload operation. Distinguish development-only reload from production hot-patching (which would need Erlang-level rigor). Add reload hooks for cleanup/initialization.

analysis.what_fx_does: Defines a three-stage gradient from sketch (infer all, runtime checks, mutable default, bytecode, 100ms startup) to normal (annotated signatures, compile-time checks, immutable default, native, seconds compile) to aggressive (annotated refinements, compile-time proofs, native+superopt, minutes compile). Transition via 'fxc annotate' which reports needed annotations, suggested refinements, and linearity opportunities, with --apply for automatic insertion.

closest_prior_art.approach: Formally derived gradual verification from static verification using AGT methodology. Pay-as-you-go cost model. Gradual C0 implementation with symbolic execution. Published in TOPLAS 2025 and POPL 2024.
closest_prior_art.reference: Sound Gradual Verification with Symbolic Execution, Zimmerman et al., POPL 2024

closest_prior_art.approach: Incremental flag enabling per-package, @ts-expect-error for per-line suppression, ts-migrating plugin for gradual upgrade. TypeScript 6.0 makes strict the default.
Supports decreases * to skip termination checks, {:verify false} to skip verification of specific methods, and progressive verification via assures keyword.
closest_prior_art.reference: Towards Progressive Program Verification in Dafny, Figueroa et al., SBLP 2018

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The three-stage gradient with concrete tooling (fxc annotate --apply) is more practical than CMU's gradual verification which remains academic. The automatic annotation suggestion with line-by-line diffs is better than TypeScript's migration tools which only flag errors. The gradient spans from fully dynamic to fully verified — wider range than TypeScript (untyped to typed) or Dafny (unverified to verified).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: CMU's gradual verification has formal metatheory guaranteeing the gradual guarantee (adding specs doesn't break working code). FX provides no such guarantee. The three stages are coarse — TypeScript allows per-flag granularity. The 'mutable by default' in sketch mode contradicts FX's core philosophy of 'deny by default' — this inconsistency could cause confusion. The fxc annotate example shows 14 annotations + 3 refinements + 2 linearity changes for one file — this could be overwhelming for non-trivial codebases.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No formal gradual guarantee. No intermediate stages between sketch and normal (TypeScript has ~15 independent flags). No description of what happens when fxc annotate cannot infer a needed annotation. No batch processing across a codebase (annotate all files). No metrics for migration progress (e.g., '73% of functions annotated'). No rollback if annotations break something. The aggressive stage is described in one line — what does 'superopt' cost in practice?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Define intermediate strictness levels between sketch and normal (e.g., ownership-strict but effect-relaxed). Provide a formal gradual guarantee. Add migration progress dashboards. Support per-function strictness override (some functions strict, others sketch, in the same file). Provide cost estimates for the aggressive stage (how many minutes per function?).

analysis.what_fx_does: FX provides six built-in test constructs as language keywords (not library macros): unit tests, property-based tests, machine tests, contract tests, benchmarks, and type theory tests. The compiler understands them, verifies their properties, and generates efficient test runners.

closest_prior_art.approach: test keyword is a language primitive. Tests live alongside code with no external framework. Compiler understands tests natively.
#[test] attribute integrated with Cargo. Rich macro-based assertions, doc tests, integration tests. Benchmark tests via #[bench] (nightly) or criterion.rs.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Six specialized test kinds vs. Zig's one (unit only) and Rust's two (unit + benchmark). Machine tests and type theory tests are genuinely novel constructs that no other language offers as built-in. The unified test runner across all kinds is cleaner than Rust's fragmented ecosystem (cargo test + criterion + proptest as separate dependencies).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Rust and Zig's testing is implemented and battle-tested. FX's six test kinds are purely aspirational. The breadth is impressive but untested — implementing six different test generation/execution strategies is a massive engineering effort. Having testing as language keywords means any evolution requires language changes, unlike library-based approaches.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No test isolation strategy described. No mocking/stubbing mechanism. No test fixtures or setup/teardown. No snapshot testing. No coverage reporting. No flaky test detection.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Consider making at least some test kinds library-based rather than language keywords — this allows evolution without language changes. Add test fixtures, mocking, and coverage as built-in capabilities if testing is truly first-class.

analysis.what_fx_does: Unit tests use 'test name ... end test;' syntax with explicit effect declarations (test name with IO, DB). Assertions include assert expr, assert expr == expected, assert_err expr is Variant, assert_raises ExnType { body }, assert_within(tolerance) a b. Tests with effects must declare them.

closest_prior_art.approach: Built-in test declarations with std.testing module. Tests live alongside source code. expectError() for error testing. No external framework needed.
#[test] attribute on functions. assert!, assert_eq!, assert_ne! macros. #[should_panic] for expected failures. Integrated with cargo test. Doc tests from documentation comments.
Convention-based: TestXxx functions in _test.go files. t.Error/t.Fatal for failures. Table-driven tests as idiomatic pattern. Subtests via t.Run.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Effect declarations on tests (test name with IO, DB) is unique — no other language tracks test effects, which enables the compiler to verify test purity and categorize tests by their resource needs. The assert_within(tolerance) for floating-point comparison is built-in rather than requiring a library. The typed closer (end test) is consistent with FX's syntax.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Rust's doc tests (code in documentation comments that are compiled and tested) are missing. Go's table-driven test pattern and subtests via t.Run enable systematic parameterized testing that FX lacks. The assertion set is basic — no assert_contains, assert_matches_regex, assert_throws_with_message, assert_eventually (for async). No test helper functions or test context object equivalent to Go's *testing.T or Rust's test infrastructure.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No test fixtures or setup/teardown. No test helper functions (Go's t.Helper()). No test timeout. No test filtering by tags. No parallel test execution within a test (Go's t.Parallel()). No doc tests. No table-driven or parameterized test syntax. No test output capture. No snapshot testing. No mocking framework integration. The section is only ~15 lines of examples — very thin.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add parameterized tests (test name<params> with table of inputs). Add doc tests from /// comments. Add test fixtures via before/after blocks. Add test timeout: test name timeout 5s. Add @[parallel] for tests that can run concurrently. Define a test context type for logging, cleanup, skip.

analysis.what_fx_does: Property-based tests are ordinary boolean-returning fns marked with @[property] (Proposal 14): `@[property] fn sort_preserves_length(xs: list(nat)) : bool = length(sort(xs)) == length(xs);`. No new keyword or grammar production — attribute-based dispatch. Compiler generates random inputs, runs properties, and shrinks failing cases. Configurable via @[samples(N)] and @[max_size(N)]. Custom generators via 'instance Arbitrary for T' with an arbitrary method using an Rng.

closest_prior_art.approach: Original PBT framework. Type-class-based generators and shrinkers (Arbitrary class). Separate generator and shrinker per type. Foundation of all subsequent PBT tools.

Core is a structured fuzzing library (Conjecture). Integrated shrinking (no separate shrinker needed). Strategy-based generators. Diverges significantly from QuickCheck — recommended by QuickCheck author as superior design.
PBT for proof assistants. Plausible checks properties by random generation up to a bound. Chamelean extends with automatic generator derivation for inductive relations. Inspired by Coq's QuickChick.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The @[property] attribute on an ordinary fn is cleaner than QuickCheck's library approach or Hypothesis's decorator pattern and avoids a new keyword per the rigor-first 'no keyword proliferation' principle. Integration with FX's type system means generators can leverage refinement types (generate nat { x < 100 } directly). The Arbitrary instance approach is well-understood from QuickCheck. rng.string_matching(regex) for generating strings matching a pattern is a nice built-in.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Hypothesis's integrated shrinking (no separate shrinker) is strictly better than QuickCheck's approach — FX appears to follow QuickCheck (separate Arbitrary instance). The Hypothesis author explicitly recommends Hedgehog over QuickCheck for Haskell because of this fundamental limitation. QuickerCheck (2024) shows that QuickCheck's algorithm is inherently sequential — no parallelization strategy described for FX. Lean's Plausible struggles with stack overflow at 10K examples — what's FX's scaling story? No coverage-guided fuzzing integration.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No integrated shrinking (Hypothesis-style). No coverage-guided property testing (hybrid fuzzing+PBT as recommended by recent research). No stateful property testing (QuickCheck state machine testing, Hypothesis stateful testing). No filtering/assuming (Hypothesis's assume() or QuickCheck's ==>). No parallel property evaluation (QuickerCheck addresses this). No deterministic replay of failing inputs. No timeout per property. The section doesn't describe the shrinking algorithm at all.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt Hypothesis/Hedgehog-style integrated shrinking rather than separate shrinkers. Add stateful property testing for state machine interaction testing. Integrate with coverage-guided fuzzing for deeper exploration. Support parallel property evaluation. Add assume/filter for preconditions. Specify shrinking algorithm (integrated, internal, or type-directed).

analysis.what_fx_does: test_machine keyword triggers compiler-driven exhaustive state space exploration: explores all reachable states, tests all transitions, verifies invariants at every state, checks deadlock freedom. For parameterized machines: @[depth_range(1, 16)]. For infinite-state: @[bound(100)] with @[induction].

closest_prior_art.approach: External specification language with exhaustive BFS state exploration. Used at AWS for S3, DynamoDB, EBS, Aurora. TLC generates all initial states then explores all transitions. Significantly slower than SPIN due to interpretive execution but more expressive.

State-machine-based programming language with built-in model checking for distributed systems. Used across AWS flagship products. Communicating state machines as first-class construct. More accessible to developers than TLA+.
Explicit-state model checker for Promela specifications. Bit-state hashing for near-100% coverage. Faster than TLC for simple systems. Used for protocol verification.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Language-integrated model checking is genuinely novel — TLA+/P/SPIN are all external tools with separate specification languages. FX machines ARE the program code, so there's no specification-implementation gap. The @[induction] annotation for infinite-state machines bridges model checking and theorem proving — TLA+ can only model-check finite instantiations. The @[depth_range] for parameterized machines is practical.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: TLA+ is the gold standard used at AWS scale (S3, DynamoDB, EC2) with decades of tooling and research. SPIN has sophisticated state-space reduction techniques (partial order reduction, bit-state hashing). FX describes zero state-space reduction strategies — naive exhaustive exploration will hit state explosion on any non-trivial machine. The P language already provides developer-accessible model checking of communicating state machines — FX's contribution over P is integration into a general-purpose language, not novel model checking. The section is only 6 lines.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: CRITICAL: No state-space reduction techniques (partial order reduction, symmetry reduction, abstraction). No state explosion mitigation strategy. No counterexample generation or visualization. No compositional verification for composed machines. No bounded model checking depth specification beyond @[depth_range]. No progress reporting during exploration. No integration with test_contract or property tests. The section is dangerously thin — 6 lines for a feature that TLA+ and SPIN devote entire textbooks to.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify state-space reduction techniques. Add counterexample trace generation with visualization. Support compositional model checking for machine compositions (product, sequence). Add explicit state bound with automatic abstraction refinement (CEGAR). Integrate with property-based testing for state exploration heuristics. This section needs 10x more detail.

analysis.what_fx_does: test_contract keyword auto-generates tests for: round-trip (encode/decode/compare), version migration (random old-version data, migrate, validate), invalid input (random bytes produce typed errors, not crashes), and access rules. Supports multi-format and version matrix testing.

closest_prior_art.approach: Consumer-driven contract testing via example interactions. Pact V4 specification supports multiple interaction types. Bi-directional testing via PactFlow compares consumer pacts against provider OpenAPI specs. Used widely in microservice architectures.

buf lint and buf breaking detect backward-incompatible schema changes. Wire format compatibility guaranteed by protobuf encoding rules. Schema evolution rules (don't reuse field numbers, only add optional fields).
Schema-based validation with test generation from schemas. Tools like json-schema-faker generate valid/invalid test data. Ajv for runtime validation.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Auto-generated tests from contract declarations are more comprehensive than Pact's manual example-based approach. Version migration testing (generate random old data, migrate, validate) is unique — Pact tests individual interactions, not migration paths. Invalid input testing that guarantees typed errors (not crashes) leverages FX's type system. Multi-format testing (json, protobuf in one declaration) is cleaner than separate Pact contracts per format.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Pact is battle-tested in production microservice architectures at scale (Netflix, Atlassian, etc.). Pact's consumer-driven approach ensures actual consumers define the contract — FX's contracts are provider-defined with no consumer-driven testing described. Pact's broker (PactFlow) manages contract versions across teams and CI/CD — FX has no distribution/coordination story. The section is only 10 lines.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No consumer-driven testing (Pact's core innovation). No contract broker or coordination across teams/services. No CI/CD integration for contract verification. No test generation strategy described (how are random old-version data generated?). No performance testing of serialization/deserialization. No fuzz testing of wire format parsers. No description of what 'typed errors, not crashes' means operationally.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add consumer-driven contract testing where consumers declare which contract features they use. Add a contract broker for multi-team coordination. Integrate with fuzz testing for wire format robustness. Add performance benchmarks for serialization round-trips. Specify the random data generation strategy for migration testing.

analysis.what_fx_does: bench keyword for benchmarks with @[compare] for side-by-side cases. Output: median, p95, p99, allocations, throughput. Regression detection via --save-baseline and --compare-baseline.

closest_prior_art.approach: Statistics-driven microbenchmarking with confidence intervals, regression detection, HTML reports with plots. Parameterized benchmarks, throughput measurement. black_box() to prevent optimization. The standard for Rust benchmarking.

OpenJDK-developed benchmark framework. Handles JVM warmup, dead code elimination, constant folding automatically. Fork-based isolation, @Param for parameterization, confidence intervals and standard deviations. Gold standard for JVM.
Built-in benchmark support via testing.B. b.N auto-scaling, b.ResetTimer(), sub-benchmarks. Simple but effective. Mean only, no confidence intervals by default.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Built-in language keyword (vs. external library for Criterion.rs and JMH). The @[compare] syntax for side-by-side case comparison is more ergonomic than Criterion's parameterized groups. Reporting median+p95+p99+allocations+throughput in one output is comprehensive. Baseline regression detection is built-in rather than requiring CI setup.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Criterion.rs provides statistical confidence intervals, HTML reports with plots, and sophisticated statistical analysis — FX mentions only median/p95/p99 with no confidence intervals or statistical tests. JMH handles JVM-specific pitfalls (warmup, dead code elimination, constant folding, forking) — FX doesn't describe any warmup or optimization-prevention strategy (what about the native compiler's optimizer?). Go's sub-benchmarks and b.N auto-scaling are simple but effective patterns not described in FX. No mention of black_box or equivalent.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No statistical confidence intervals or significance tests. No warmup handling. No dead-code-elimination prevention (black_box equivalent). No HTML/visual report generation. No parameterized benchmarks. No benchmark isolation (JMH's forking). No memory profiling integration. No flame graph generation. No asymptotic complexity benchmarking. The section is only ~15 lines.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add statistical confidence intervals and significance testing (Criterion-level). Add warmup handling and dead-code prevention. Support parameterized benchmarks with automatic scaling. Add visual report generation. Add asymptotic complexity benchmarking (O(n) verification by running at multiple sizes). Integrate with FX's cost dimension for automatic complexity validation against declared bounds.

analysis.what_fx_does: test_theory keyword for testing the type system itself — each known type theory bug from published literature becomes a permanent test. Tests check expect_compile_error with message matching and expect_accepted. test_metatheory keyword enables property-based metatheory fuzzing: generates random well-typed terms via bidirectional generation, checks preservation/progress, shrinks counterexamples. A single corpus regression blocks all releases.

closest_prior_art.approach: Domain-specific language for operational semantics with built-in randomized testing. redex-check generates random terms to falsify properties. Automated testing benchmark with manually injected bugs. Well-typed term generators derived from typing judgments.

Property-based testing for proof assistants. QuickChick in Coq auto-derives generators for inductive predicates. Chamelean extends Lean's Plausible with automatic derivation. Tests theorems by random generation.

Rustlantis (OOPSLA 2024) generates random MIR for Rust compiler differential testing. TyMut (OOPSLA 2025) fuzzes C++ type systems via type-driven mutation. CSmith generates random C programs for compiler testing.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Making type theory testing a language-level keyword is genuinely novel — no other language has test_theory or test_metatheory as built-in constructs. PLT Redex is an external tool; QuickChick/Plausible are libraries. FX integrates this into the compiler's own test infrastructure, meaning the compiler tests itself using its own test framework. The 'single corpus regression blocks all releases' policy is strong. Bidirectional well-typed term generation is the right approach (matches PLT Redex's best practice).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: PLT Redex has a published benchmark with manually injected bugs and quantified effectiveness — FX has no validation data. Rustlantis generates MIR directly (bypassing the parser/typechecker) for differential testing — FX tests at the source level which may miss internal compiler bugs. CSmith found hundreds of real compiler bugs over years — FX's approach is unproven. Plausible in Lean 4 struggles with scaling (stack overflow at 10K samples) — FX claims @[samples(100000)] without addressing scalability.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No description of the well-typed term generation algorithm beyond 'bidirectional generation'. No coverage metric for how much of the type system is exercised. No differential testing against a reference implementation. No description of what happens when a counterexample is found (how is the bug localized?). No performance targets for term generation rate (TyMut generates 22K programs/second). No integration with coverage-guided fuzzing. No description of how the 'corpus' is maintained or grown.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Publish a benchmark with injected bugs (like PLT Redex's benchmark) to validate effectiveness. Add differential testing against a reference semantics. Add coverage tracking for which typing rules are exercised. Integrate with coverage-guided fuzzing for deeper exploration. Specify term generation rate targets. Add grammar-based fuzzing for parser testing in addition to well-typed term generation for type system testing.

analysis.what_fx_does: Unified test runner via 'fxc test' with flags for each test kind (--unit, --property, --machine, --bench, --theory), name pattern filtering, parallel execution (--jobs N), and machine-readable output (--format json). Tests stripped from release builds.

closest_prior_art.approach: Unified test runner for unit tests, integration tests, doc tests, and benchmarks. Pattern filtering, parallel execution, --format json (unstable). Tests compiled out of release builds via cfg(test).
Built-in test runner for unit tests, benchmarks, and fuzzing (Go 1.18+). Pattern matching, parallel execution via -parallel flag, -json output. Tests in _test.go files.
Built-in test runner that discovers test blocks in source files. Outputs results directly. No external test runner needed.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Covers more test kinds under one runner than any other language (6 kinds vs. Rust's 3 or Go's 3). The --machine and --theory flags are unique. Stripping tests from release builds is good practice, consistent with Rust/Zig.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The section is 14 lines — purely a CLI reference. No description of test discovery, execution order, or isolation. Rust's cargo test has sophisticated features: test output capture, --nocapture, --test-threads, --ignored for skipped tests, #[ignore] attribute, compile_fail doc tests. Go has -count, -timeout, -cover for coverage, -fuzz for fuzzing, -race for race detection. FX describes none of these.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No test discovery mechanism described. No test timeout. No test coverage reporting. No test skip/ignore mechanism. No test output capture. No watch mode (rerun on file change). No test ordering guarantees (random? deterministic?). No integration with CI systems beyond --format json. No race detection flag. No fuzz testing mode (Go has built-in fuzzing since 1.18). No test caching (Go caches passing test results).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add --timeout, --coverage, --watch, --race flags. Add @[ignore] or @[skip(reason)] for skipping tests. Add test caching for unchanged code. Add --fuzz mode that combines property and contract tests with coverage-guided fuzzing. Add --report for HTML test reports. Specify test execution order and isolation guarantees.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header introducing the FX compiler as a persistent HTTP REST daemon that agents interact with via structured JSON calls, covering discovery, incremental checking, auto-fix, refactoring, proof assistance, and health analysis — designed for agentic LLM consumers rather than human IDE users.

closest_prior_art.approach: JSON-RPC-based bidirectional protocol between editors and language servers; 150+ language implementations; LSP 3.17 is the dominant standard for tooling integration
Exposes IntelliJ's refactoring/analysis engine via Model Context Protocol for AI agents, bridging IDE-internal APIs to external HTTP/REST; emerged 2024-2025
LSP-based verification server with incremental checking, auto-insertion of proof constructs (forall/calc statements), integrated into VS Code and IntelliJ

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX is the only language spec that designs the compiler-agent protocol as a first-class chapter of the language definition rather than an afterthought. The REST API is explicitly designed for LLM agent workflows (discovery, check/fix cycles, what-if previews), which LSP was never designed for. The self-describing root endpoint and phased error reporting are genuinely novel for a language spec.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: LSP has 10+ years of ecosystem maturity with 150+ implementations and universal editor support. Choosing REST over LSP (or alongside it) means FX must maintain two protocols. The spec mentions LSP on the same port but provides zero detail on the LSP implementation. REST has higher per-request latency than persistent JSON-RPC connections. No mention of WebSocket or streaming for long-running verification operations.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of authentication/authorization for the daemon. No rate limiting or multi-tenancy model for shared daemon access. No specification of how the REST protocol handles concurrent modification conflicts beyond 'mutations serialized per file'. No versioning of the REST API itself. No discussion of how to handle verification operations that take minutes (streaming progress, cancellation).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Define a concrete protocol specification (OpenAPI 3.1) rather than example-only documentation. Support Server-Sent Events or WebSocket for long-running verification progress. Specify how the REST API relates to LSP concretely (shared state, different views?) rather than hand-waving 'same port'. Consider adopting MCP (Model Context Protocol) as the agent-facing layer since it is becoming the standard for AI-tool integration.

analysis.what_fx_does: The compiler runs as a persistent HTTP daemon (fxc serve --port 9100), loads the project once, keeps elaborated state in memory, watches files, and serves queries with warm incremental checking (cold start 2-5s, warm recheck 50-200ms).

closest_prior_art.approach: Persistent language server using demand-driven incremental computation (Salsa); targets sub-100ms autocomplete latencies; uses JSON-RPC not REST
Persistent server with incremental elaboration; supports interactive proof development; integrates with Loogle for lemma search via VS Code extension
Custom JSON-based client-server protocol over stdio; incremental checking of file suffixes; bridges to VS Code via fstar-vscode-assistant LSP wrapper

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The self-describing root endpoint that lists all available operations is a better experience for programmatic consumers than LSP's capability negotiation, which was designed for human-driven editors. The warm recheck target of 50-200ms is competitive with rust-analyzer's goals. Exposing both REST and LSP from the same warm state is a good pragmatic choice.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: rust-analyzer's Salsa framework provides formally specified demand-driven incremental computation with fine-grained dependency tracking. FX's spec says nothing about the incrementality algorithm — just 'incrementally rechecked'. The 50-200ms claim is aspirational with no implementation backing. F*'s incremental checking already works but is known to be slow on large files; FX needs to specify what granularity of incrementality is targeted. Lean 4's server handles interactive proof state management, which FX's REST API does not address.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of the incremental computation framework (Salsa-like? Adapton? Query-based?). No specification of what 'warm recheck' means — recheck of what granularity (file? function? expression?). No mention of progress reporting for long verifications. No specification of how proof state is maintained across REST requests. No discussion of memory pressure from keeping elaborated state warm.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the incremental computation model explicitly (query-based a la Salsa is the SotA). Define the granularity of incrementality: per-semicolon checkpoints from section 10.13 should be referenced here. Add streaming progress for verification operations. Specify memory budget management for the warm state.

analysis.what_fx_does: GET /symbol/{name} returns full metadata (signature, effects, pre/post, trust, call graph). GET /search supports name search, type search, and goal-directed lemma search (e.g. search?goal=is_sorted(append(_, _))).

closest_prior_art.approach: Pattern-based lemma search by constant, name substring, subexpression, type signature, and conclusion pattern; web API, VS Code integration, in-code #loogle command; developed by Joachim Breitner at Lean FRO
Search by function name or approximate type signature; JSON API with ?mode=json; embeddable; command-line and web interface; library-level Haskell API
Natural language semantic search over Mathlib using embeddings; complements Loogle's pattern-based approach

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX unifies name search, type search, and goal-directed lemma search in a single endpoint with a consistent JSON response format. Loogle and Hoogle are separate tools with different APIs. The integration of call graph data (called_by/calls) and trust level in the symbol response goes beyond what Hoogle/Loogle provide.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Loogle's search syntax is far more sophisticated: it supports non-linear pattern variables, conclusion-only patterns (|- prefix), filtering definitions vs theorems, and combined multi-filter queries. Hoogle supports approximate type matching which FX does not mention. Neither FX's spec nor examples show how goal search handles unification, backtracking, or ranking of results. Lean's ecosystem also has Moogle for natural language queries — FX has no semantic/embedding-based search.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of the search algorithm (unification-based? fuzzy matching? indexed?). No ranking or relevance scoring described. No pagination for large result sets. No specification of how goal search handles polymorphism, higher-kinded types, or partial matches. No natural language search capability. No specification of search index construction or update strategy.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the search algorithm concretely — at minimum, unification-based type search a la Hoogle with anti-unification for partial matches. Add result ranking. Support Loogle-style conclusion patterns explicitly. Consider adding embedding-based natural language search as a future extension. Add search index strategy for large codebases.

analysis.what_fx_does: POST /check returns phased error reports (parse/names/types/modes/effects/verify), with later phases blocked until earlier ones clean. POST /auto-fix applies trivial fixes, GET /errors?root=true returns root errors with concrete fix diffs, POST /what-if predicts whether fixes work without writing files, and POST /edit applies fixes and rechecks.

closest_prior_art.approach: Continuous verification as you type; auto-insertion of proof constructs (forall/calc statements) for failing assertions; LSP-based error reporting; but no structured auto-fix API or what-if prediction
Custom JSON protocol for incremental checking of file suffixes; provides type-at-point and autocompletion; no auto-fix or what-if capabilities
Clippy provides 800+ lints with auto-fix via `cargo clippy --fix`; rust-analyzer provides LSP code actions with fix suggestions; but no phased checking or what-if prediction

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The phased error pipeline (parse -> names -> types -> modes -> effects -> verify) with explicit blocking is a genuinely novel design for agent consumption — no other system structures errors this way for programmatic consumers. The what-if prediction endpoint is unique: no LSP implementation offers 'apply this fix hypothetically and tell me the result'. The combination of auto-fix + root-cause analysis + what-if + apply-and-recheck in a single coherent API is beyond what any current system provides.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Clippy has 800+ concrete, battle-tested lint rules with auto-fix; FX has zero implemented. Dafny's auto-insertion of proof constructs is a working feature, not a spec. The 'every error has a fix field' claim is extraordinarily ambitious — most compilers have <50% auto-fixable errors. The spec does not address what happens when auto-fix suggestions conflict or when what-if predictions are wrong (false positives from incomplete analysis).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of what classes of errors are auto-fixable vs not. No error taxonomy with fix coverage expectations. No specification of what-if prediction accuracy or limitations. No discussion of how phased blocking interacts with partial compilation (can the agent get partial type information while modes phase is blocked?). No specification of fix conflict resolution when multiple fixes interact.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Define the auto-fix taxonomy: which error codes have machine-generated fixes, which require human/agent intervention. Specify what-if semantics precisely — is it full recheck with virtual edits, or heuristic prediction? Add a confidence score to what-if predictions. Allow partial results from blocked phases (e.g., partial type info even when modes phase has errors).

analysis.what_fx_does: Beyond errors, the daemon suggests improvements on compiling code: effect narrowing (unnecessary effect declarations), pipeline sugar (lambda-to-dot-shorthand), stronger spec (implementation proves more than spec requires), and parallel opportunities (Tot functions on .par()). Categories: ownership/mode, specification, sugar/style, effects, types/contracts, performance, security. Filterable by kind/severity/file.

closest_prior_art.approach: 800+ lints in categories: correctness, suspicious, style, complexity, perf, pedantic, restriction, nursery, cargo; auto-fix via --fix; configurable per-lint via attributes and clippy.toml
Pluggable linting framework with hundreds of rules, auto-fix, shareable configs, and custom rule development; widely adopted

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's 'stronger_spec' suggestion (implementation proves more than spec requires — suggest strengthening the spec) is genuinely novel and unique to verification-aware languages. The 'parallel_opportunity' suggestion (detecting Tot functions eligible for .par()) leverages FX's effect system in a way no existing linter can. Effect narrowing is also unique to languages with declared effect systems.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Clippy has 800+ concrete, categorized, community-vetted lint rules with years of false-positive tuning. FX lists 4 example suggestions with zero specification of the full catalog. ESLint has a mature plugin ecosystem with shareable configs. FX provides no mechanism for user-defined suggestions or third-party suggestion plugins. The filtering API is specified but the suggestion catalog is not.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No catalog of concrete suggestions (how many? what are they?). No specification of false positive rates or suppression mechanisms. No user-defined suggestion rules. No configuration for suggestion severity thresholds. No specification of how suggestions interact with decorators (@[cached] etc). The section is 20 lines — thin for what should be a rich subsystem.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Define a concrete suggestion catalog with at least 30-50 categories across the 7 suggestion domains listed. Add suppression mechanisms (inline @[allow(suggestion_name)] like Clippy). Consider allowing user-defined suggestions via comptime analysis plugins. Specify false-positive handling and confidence scoring.

analysis.what_fx_does: POST /rename, /extract-fn, /inline — all return preview edits with contract impact analysis. The agent reviews and applies via POST /edit.

closest_prior_art.approach: LSP-based rename with cross-file edits; extract function assist with generics support (improved 2022); inline assist; challenges with ownership/lifetimes make extract-method non-trivial
Programmatic refactoring via RefactoringFactory; recently exposed to AI agents via MCP server; extensive refactoring catalog (rename, extract, inline, move, change signature, etc.)
Academic tool proving that extract-method for ownership/lifetime languages is significantly harder than for GC languages; builds on IntelliJ Rust plugin

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The preview-before-apply pattern with contract impact analysis is better than rust-analyzer's immediate-apply model. Adding contract impact to refactoring previews (e.g., 'rename causes MINOR version bump') is unique to FX. The REST API makes refactoring accessible to non-IDE agents without editor plugins.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: IntelliJ offers 20+ refactoring types (move, change signature, pull up/push down, introduce parameter, safe delete, etc.). FX specifies exactly three. rust-analyzer's extract function handles generics and lifetime inference, which is directly relevant to FX's ownership model — the spec says nothing about how extract-fn handles modes/lifetimes. REM's OOPSLA paper demonstrates that extract-method for ownership languages is fundamentally harder than FX acknowledges.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Only 3 refactorings specified (rename, extract-fn, inline). No move-declaration, change-signature, introduce-type-alias, extract-trait, or safe-delete. No specification of how extract-fn handles linear values, borrowing modes, or effect annotations. No discussion of the inherent complexity of refactoring ownership-typed code (see REM paper). The section is 12 lines — far too thin.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand to at least 10 refactorings. For extract-fn specifically, address how the extracted function's mode annotations, effect annotations, and specification clauses (pre/post) are inferred. Reference the REM paper's challenges for ownership-aware extraction. Add safe-delete with usage check and move-declaration with re-export.

analysis.what_fx_does: GET /health returns sorry count, unstable proofs, module error counts. GET /impact returns transitive callers, affected tests, affected contracts. GET /dead-code finds unused pub functions, imports, unreachable branches. GET /coverage?kind=spec returns specification coverage percentage.

closest_prior_art.approach: Centralized code quality dashboard with multi-language support; technical debt tracking; code smell detection; security vulnerability scanning; but not compiler-integrated
Cloud-based coverage dashboards with CI integration, historical trends, PR annotations; focused on test coverage, not specification coverage

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Specification coverage (spec_coverage: what fraction of pub fn have postconditions) is unique to verification-aware languages and genuinely useful for tracking verification progress. The /impact endpoint combining callers + tests + contracts in a single query is more integrated than separate tools. sorry_count tracking as a first-class health metric is novel and directly useful for verification workflows.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: SonarQube provides historical trends, technical debt estimation, security scanning, and multi-project dashboards — FX provides a snapshot API with no history. Code Climate tracks code health over time with team-level analytics. FX's /health is a single-point-in-time query with no historical comparison, trend tracking, or regression detection. The /dead-code endpoint exists but dead code detection in the presence of decorators, comptime, and module functors is non-trivial and unspecified.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No historical trending or regression detection. No technical debt estimation. No complexity metrics (cyclomatic, cognitive). No specification of how dead-code detection handles comptime code generation, decorator transformations, or conditional compilation. No dashboard — just raw API endpoints. No team-level analytics. The section is 15 lines with JSON examples only.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add /health/history for trend tracking. Define concrete metrics beyond the four examples shown (e.g., verification time per module, grade usage distribution, effect footprint). Specify dead-code detection semantics in the presence of comptime and decorators. Consider adding a /tech-debt endpoint that estimates verification effort remaining.

analysis.what_fx_does: GET /proof-state returns known facts, goal, and suggestion at a specific source location. POST /proof-plan returns proof obligations, strategies, and a proof skeleton for a function.

closest_prior_art.approach: Lean-auto translates Lean 4 goals to monomorphic HOL and dispatches to ATPs (Vampire, E, Z3); solves problems beyond Aesop's rule-based search; integrated via tactic syntax; presented 2025

Hammer tactic for Coq: finds appropriate lemmas from all accessible lemmas, translates to FOL, dispatches to ATPs (Vampire, E, Z3, CVC4), reconstructs proofs in Coq; general-purpose, no domain-specific config
Automatic insertion of proof constructs (forall statements, calc blocks) for failing assertions; auto-active means programmer provides key annotations and solver fills gaps; integrated in IDE

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The /proof-plan endpoint that generates a proof skeleton from goal structure is a useful API for agent workflows — more structured than Dafny's auto-active approach. Returning known facts + goal + suggestion in a JSON-consumable format is better for LLM agents than Lean's tactic mode or Coq's interactive proof state.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Lean-auto and CoqHammer dispatch goals to external ATPs (Vampire, E, Z3) and reconstruct proofs — this is battle-tested proof automation. FX's spec describes returning 'suggestions' but does not specify what proof search strategies are available. The section mentions 'strategies' in /proof-plan but lists zero concrete strategies. CoqHammer handles lemma selection from thousands of lemmas; FX says nothing about lemma selection algorithms. Lean 4 also integrates LLM-based proof search (various papers, 2024-2025) which FX does not mention.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of proof search strategies (ATP dispatch? tactic search? LLM-assisted?). No specification of lemma selection algorithm. No specification of proof reconstruction from external solver results. No discussion of hammer-style automation. No mention of integration with external ATPs. The 'suggestion' field in the example is a single string — no structured representation. Section is 14 lines including JSON — far too thin for the complexity of proof assistance.
analysis.could_we_do_better: This section needs major expansion. Specify: (1) which ATPs/SMT solvers are dispatched to and how, (2) lemma selection algorithm (premise selection), (3) proof reconstruction strategy, (4) the concrete list of available 'strategies' in /proof-plan, (5) integration with LLM-based proof search as a future direction. Reference Lean-auto and CoqHammer as the SotA to exceed.

analysis.what_fx_does: Demonstrates a complete 12-call agent workflow: orient (GET /), understand module, read existing API, write code, check, auto-fix, get errors with fixes, what-if preview, apply fixes, check suggestions, apply suggestions, verify impact, check health — from 'I know nothing' to 'feature complete, verified, contract-compatible' in ~2s compiler time.

closest_prior_art.approach: LLMs autonomously plan, execute, and interact with compilers/debuggers/VCS; 152 academic papers 2022-2025; most use ad-hoc tool integration, not structured compiler APIs
Universal open standard for AI agent-tool communication; used by IntelliJ MCP Server and others; standardizes tool discovery and invocation but not compiler-specific workflows

analysis.where_fx_is_better: No other language specification includes a complete worked example of an agent-compiler interaction session as part of the language definition. The workflow is concrete, achievable, and well-structured. The 2-second total time target for a complete check-fix-verify cycle would be genuinely transformative for agentic development if achieved.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: This is a worked example, not a specification — it demonstrates what the API can do but does not define protocol semantics, error handling, or failure modes. The 2-second claim is aspirational with no implementation. Real verification of non-trivial properties (SMT solver calls) routinely takes 10-60 seconds; the example conveniently picks a scenario where verification is fast. Current agentic tools like Cursor/Claude Code achieve similar workflows through ad-hoc integration without needing a dedicated compiler protocol.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No error handling in the workflow (what if auto-fix fails? what if what-if prediction is wrong?). No specification of how the agent handles verification timeouts. No discussion of the workflow for proof-heavy functions where verification takes minutes. The example is cherry-picked for a simple scenario — no complex case study.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add a second worked example showing a harder case: proof-heavy function with verification timeout, incorrect auto-fix, agent-driven proof construction using /proof-plan. Show the failure path, not just the happy path. Include timing breakdown showing what takes how long.

analysis.what_fx_does: CLI fallback (fxc check --agent) outputs structured line-oriented text with PHASE/ERROR/SUGGEST prefixes, error codes, source locations, and fix diffs — for agents without HTTP access.

closest_prior_art.approach: OASIS-standardized JSON format for structured compiler/analyzer diagnostics; supported by MSVC (2022 v17.8+), proposed for Clang, C++ standards paper P3358R0 (2024); hierarchical diagnostics with fix objects
Machine-readable JSON diagnostic output with error codes, spans, suggested replacements; used by cargo, rust-analyzer, and CI tools; pre-RFC for full structured i18n-aware diagnostics
Adding machine-readable diagnostics to the OCaml compiler; presented at OCaml 2024 workshop; aims for tooling-consumable output beyond human-readable messages

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's CLI output includes fix diffs inline (not just error locations), which is more actionable for agents than rustc's JSON format. The PHASE/ERROR/SUGGEST taxonomy is clearer for phased processing than flat error lists.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: SARIF is an OASIS standard with broad tooling support (VS Code, GitHub Code Scanning, many static analyzers). FX invents a custom line-oriented format instead of adopting SARIF. Rust's --error-format=json is widely consumed by tools. FX's custom format requires custom parsing — no standard tooling ecosystem will support it. The format shown is informal (no BNF or formal spec).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No formal specification of the output format (BNF, field types, escaping). No machine-readable JSON alternative (only line-oriented text). No SARIF compatibility. No specification of how multi-line fix diffs are represented in line-oriented output. The section is 11 lines — barely specified.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt SARIF as the structured output format — it is the emerging standard and already has tool support. Offer both SARIF JSON and the compact line format. Formally specify the line format with a grammar. Add --error-format=json like rustc.

analysis.what_fx_does: Daemon watches files via OS notifications, incrementally rechecks in background, serves read-only queries concurrently, serializes mutation queries per file. Multiple agents share warm state. Check IDs are snapshots; file changes invalidate affected IDs. Can expose both REST and LSP on different ports from the same warm state.

closest_prior_art.approach: Client manages server lifecycle (initialize/shutdown); no native daemon mode; persistent server mode requested but not standardized (github issue #1182)
Terraform language server used as daemon with --port for multiple editor windows; demonstrates desire for persistent language servers beyond single-editor sessions

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The snapshot-based check IDs with staleness detection are well-designed for concurrent multi-agent access. Running REST + LSP from the same warm state is pragmatic and avoids redundant computation. Serializing mutations per file while allowing concurrent reads is a sensible concurrency model.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: LSP has a well-defined lifecycle protocol (initialize/initialized/shutdown/exit) with capability negotiation. FX specifies zero lifecycle management: how does the daemon start? How does it shut down gracefully? What happens on crash/recovery? How are warm state snapshots persisted? The section is 8 lines for what should be a critical operational subsystem.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No startup/shutdown protocol. No crash recovery strategy (is warm state lost?). No memory pressure handling (what if warm state exceeds available RAM?). No specification of OS notification mechanism (inotify/FSEvents/kqueue). No specification of how incremental background rechecking is prioritized. No specification of how multiple agents discover/connect to a running daemon.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the daemon lifecycle: startup options, graceful shutdown, crash recovery (persist warm state to disk?), health monitoring. Add memory budget configuration. Specify daemon discovery (well-known port, PID file, socket). Define background recheck priority (open files first? recently changed?).

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for distribution: package identity, version resolution (Minimal Version Selection), security (content-addressed + signed + transparency log), automatic semver computation, supply chain defenses, project profiles, and the no-dialect rule.

closest_prior_art.approach: Cargo.toml manifest, SAT-based version resolution, crates.io registry, cargo-semver-checks for automated semver validation, trusted publishing via OIDC, no_std for embedded profiles
go.mod manifest, Minimal Version Selection, checksum database (sum.golang.org), module proxy protocol, no build scripts in Go modules themselves

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX combines MVS (from Go) with contract compatibility checking, automatic semver computation, and effect-based supply chain auditing in a single coherent system. No existing package manager integrates type-level contract checking with version resolution.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Cargo and Go Modules are battle-tested with millions of packages. FX's distribution system is entirely on paper. The combination of all these features creates a complex system where interactions (contract checking + MVS + effect auditing) may have surprising failure modes that only emerge at ecosystem scale.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of registry protocol (HTTP? Git?). No mirror federation protocol. No offline/air-gapped mode specification. No specification of how contract compatibility checking interacts with version resolution (can it cause resolution failure? backtracking?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the registry protocol concretely. Address the interaction between contract compatibility checking and version resolution — in particular, what happens when the only compatible version fails contract checks. Define the offline/air-gapped workflow.

analysis.what_fx_does: Packages identified by namespace/name with verified namespaces (email domain, GitHub org, DNS record). std/ reserved. Manifest is package.fx.toml. No build scripts, no install hooks. Code generation via comptime in sandbox (no IO, no network).

closest_prior_art.approach: Domain-verified namespaces via DNS TXT records; io.github.<username> auto-verified via GitHub OAuth; most mature verified-namespace system in production
Module paths are domain-based (e.g., github.com/user/repo); implicit namespace verification via domain ownership; no build scripts in module system; go.sum for integrity
Scoped packages (@scope/name); scopes tied to npm organizations; no domain verification — scope ownership is first-come-first-served within npm

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Verified namespaces via multiple mechanisms (email, GitHub, DNS) is better than npm's first-come-first-served scopes and crates.io's flat namespace. The complete prohibition of build scripts eliminates the xz-utils attack vector by design. Sandboxed comptime code generation is a principled alternative to build scripts.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Maven Central's domain verification is battle-tested at massive scale. Go's domain-based module paths provide natural verification. FX lists three verification mechanisms but specifies none in detail — what DNS record? What email verification flow? What GitHub OAuth scope? The no-build-scripts stance is bold but may be impractical for FFI-heavy packages (bindgen, protoc, etc.) — what replaces build.rs/Makefile for native code compilation?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of the actual verification protocols (DNS TXT record format, OAuth flow, email verification). No specification of namespace transfer or revocation. No specification of how native code compilation (FFI) works without build scripts. No specification of package yanking semantics beyond 'downloadable by hash'. No specification of how the registry handles namespace disputes.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the verification protocols concretely (DNS TXT record value, GitHub OAuth flow). Address the FFI compilation question — how do packages that wrap C libraries build without build scripts? Consider adopting Trusted Publishing (OIDC from CI/CD) like PyPI/crates.io. Specify namespace dispute resolution.

analysis.what_fx_does: Lock file (fx.lock) committed to VCS with exact versions, content hashes, trust levels, effect summaries. Resolution via Minimal Version Selection: collect min constraints, pick max of mins. Deterministic, O(n), no SAT. Plus contract compatibility check on resolved versions.

closest_prior_art.approach: Original MVS proposal by Russ Cox; only >= constraints; deterministic O(n) resolution; no SAT; module compatibility guaranteed by import path major versioning
SAT-based version resolution with range constraints (^, ~, >=, <); Cargo.lock for reproducibility; more expressive constraints but NP-hard in theory (fast in practice)
Critical analysis showing MVS trades ecosystem-wide testing benefits for simplicity; the NP-hardness argument against SAT is weak since modern SAT solvers handle package resolution efficiently

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Adding trust levels and effect summaries to the lock file is unique and valuable — no other package manager tracks trust propagation or effect footprint at the version resolution level. The contract compatibility check during resolution catches semantic breakage that semver alone misses.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: MVS has known drawbacks: it selects older (potentially unpatched) versions by default, reducing ecosystem-wide CVE patching velocity. matklad's 2024 analysis argues the NP-hardness argument against SAT is practically irrelevant — SAT solvers handle real-world package resolution efficiently. MVS prevents expressing upper-bound constraints (< 2.0) which are sometimes needed for known-broken versions. Users may be confused when go.mod says v1.2.3 but build uses v1.5.0 due to transitive requirements.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of how contract compatibility checking interacts with MVS — if the minimum version passes semver but fails contract checks, does resolution try a newer version? Or fail? This interaction is critical and unspecified. No specification of how trust levels aggregate during resolution (min? max? per-dependency?). No yanked-version handling in resolution. No specification of lock file format.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the contract-check + MVS interaction precisely. Consider allowing 'exclude' constraints (like Go's exclude directive) for known-broken versions. Specify trust level aggregation: is project trust the min of all dependency trust levels? Define the lock file format as a stable contract. Consider allowing optional upper-bound constraints for security-critical scenarios.

analysis.what_fx_does: Three-layer security: discovery (replicated registry index), storage (content-addressed SHA-256), trust (Ed25519 author signatures + append-only Merkle tree transparency log). Published packages immutable. Yanked versions remain downloadable. Version timeline append-only.

closest_prior_art.approach: Keyless signing via short-lived OIDC certificates + Rekor transparency log; eliminates key management burden; adopted by npm, PyPI, Kubernetes; CNCF graduated project
Auditable transparency log for Go module checksums; clients verify against tamper-proof tree; no author signatures but strong integrity guarantees
SLSA-compliant provenance attestations linking packages to source repos and CI builds; Sigstore-based signing; transparency log integration

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's three-layer separation (discovery/storage/trust) is cleaner than Sigstore's more complex architecture. Ed25519 is a good choice for long-lived author signatures. The explicit statement that 'yanked versions remain downloadable by hash' is stronger than npm's approach where yanked packages can disappear.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Sigstore's keyless signing eliminates key management entirely — developers authenticate via OIDC and get short-lived certificates. FX requires developers to manage Ed25519 keypairs, which is a significant usability burden. Sigstore is a CNCF graduated project with production deployment at npm, PyPI, and Kubernetes scale. FX's transparency log is unspecified beyond 'Merkle tree, independently witnessed'. npm's provenance links packages to specific CI builds (SLSA provenance), not just author identity — FX does not mention build provenance.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of key management (how are Ed25519 keys generated, stored, rotated, revoked?). No specification of the transparency log protocol (gossip? witnesses? auditor API?). No build provenance / SLSA integration. No keyless signing option (Sigstore-style). No specification of what 'independently witnessed' means concretely. No specification of certificate/key revocation. No specification of how the registry handles compromised author keys.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Adopt keyless signing via Sigstore/OIDC as the primary mechanism — it eliminates key management burden. Keep Ed25519 as an option for environments without OIDC. Add SLSA build provenance attestations. Specify the transparency log protocol concretely (Trillian? custom? witness protocol?). Define key compromise recovery procedures.

analysis.what_fx_does: Compiler computes semver from contract diff (added function = MINOR, removed = MAJOR, etc.). Author confirms. Overstating allowed, understating blocked by registry. Auto-generated changelog and migration guide for MAJOR bumps.

closest_prior_art.approach: Lints crate API for semver violations; 120 lints as of end 2024 (doubled from 57 in 2023); being merged into cargo publish workflow; detects sealed traits, feature breakage, manifest changes
Built-in automatic semver enforcement based on API diff; prevents publishing with incorrect version bump; pioneered automatic semver computation in PL ecosystems

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX goes beyond cargo-semver-checks by computing semver from contract diffs, which include serialization format changes, access mode changes, and version compatibility declarations — not just API surface. The auto-generated migration guide for MAJOR bumps is a feature no current tool provides. Blocking understating at the registry level (like Elm) but allowing overstating is a pragmatic design.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: cargo-semver-checks has 120 concrete, tested lint rules covering subtle breakage patterns (sealed trait changes, feature gate changes, manifest incompatibilities). FX's semver computation is described in ~8 lines with a few examples. Elm's system is actually implemented and used by thousands of packages. The section references section 14.10 but the actual computation rules (contravariant inputs, covariant outputs) need more formalization for edge cases (associated types, higher-kinded type changes, effect changes).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of how effect changes are classified (adding IO to a function = MAJOR? What about effect variables?). No specification of how type class instance changes affect semver. No specification of how machine state changes propagate. No specification of changelog generation format. Very thin section — 15 lines.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand the semver computation rules to cover all 21 dimensions — effect changes, mode changes, proof obligation changes, trust level changes, version labels. Define edge cases (associated type changes, default method additions). Specify the changelog generation format (Conventional Commits? custom?). Reference cargo-semver-checks' lint taxonomy as inspiration for breadth.

analysis.what_fx_does: No build scripts (xz-utils vector eliminated). Effect transparency (secret IO/Network additions change contracts, caught by semver). Classified-by-default (exfiltration requires visible declassify). No raw pointers. fxc diff-audit shows new effects and trust changes between lock file versions.

closest_prior_art.approach: Default-deny for file/network/env/subprocess access; --allow-read, --allow-net flags; npm postinstall scripts blocked by default (--allow-scripts to opt in); sandbox for untrusted code
2+ year social engineering campaign; malicious build scripts in tarballs reassembled backdoor during compilation; compromised liblzma/OpenSSH; CVSS 10; prevented by eliminating build scripts entirely

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's defense is structural, not policy-based: build scripts simply do not exist in the language, so the xz-utils attack vector is eliminated by construction rather than by permission flags (Deno) or trust (npm). Effect transparency making secret IO additions visible via contract/semver changes is a novel multi-layered defense. The diff-audit tool combining effects + trust is more informative than any existing audit tool.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Deno's sandbox is a working, deployed system with real security audits and CVE handling. FX's defenses are on paper. The 'no build scripts' stance may be impractical for real-world packages that need native compilation, code generation from external schemas (protobuf, flatbuffers), or platform-specific configuration. Deno discovered that their sandbox has real bypasses (symlinks, subprocesses) — FX's sandboxed comptime may have similar issues that only emerge in implementation.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of what comptime sandbox permits and forbids beyond 'no IO, no network'. Can comptime read files? Access environment variables? Measure time? Allocate unbounded memory? Each of these is a potential side channel. No specification of how FFI libraries (C bindings) are built without build scripts. No specification of how fxc diff-audit handles transitive dependency changes. No threat model document.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Write a formal threat model covering the attack surface. Specify the comptime sandbox precisely (what syscalls are blocked, what resources are bounded). Address the FFI build question explicitly — perhaps via a separate, auditable 'native build manifest' that is not Turing-complete. Add dependency confusion attack prevention (namespace verification + registry priority rules).

analysis.what_fx_does: Profiles restrict the language per project — allowed effects, allocation strategies, stack budget, overflow default, sorry handling. Modules cannot opt out. Module-level pragmas can tighten but never loosen. Profiles affect type checking, never parsing.

closest_prior_art.approach: no_std removes libstd, restricting to core + alloc; editions (2015/2018/2021/2024) change language semantics while maintaining inter-edition compatibility; cfg attributes for conditional compilation
Four build modes (Debug/ReleaseSafe/ReleaseFast/ReleaseSmall) with different safety check levels; comptime for conditional compilation; no separate profile system but build.zig controls everything

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX profiles are far more granular than Rust's no_std binary: you can restrict specific effects, set per-function stack limits, control overflow behavior, and ban sorry — all independently configurable. The monotonic tightening rule (modules can only further restrict) is cleaner than Rust's cfg-based conditional compilation which can add or remove anything. The guarantee that profiles never affect parsing is a strong invariant.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Rust's edition system enables backward-incompatible language changes while maintaining cross-edition interoperability — different crates in the same project can use different editions. FX's profiles only restrict, never change semantics, which limits their ability to evolve the language. Rust's no_std has a massive ecosystem of compatible crates; FX's profile system will need similar ecosystem support to be useful.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of how profiles interact with dependencies (must all deps satisfy the project profile? can a dependency have a looser profile?). No standard set of profiles (only one example: embedded_driver). No specification of how profiles are distributed or shared across projects. No specification of profile inheritance or composition. No specification of what happens when a dependency violates the project profile.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Define 5-10 standard profiles (embedded, crypto, web-service, kernel, proof-library) as starting points. Specify dependency-profile interaction: transitive profile enforcement vs per-boundary checking. Allow profile composition (embedded + crypto). Define what error messages look like when a dependency violates a profile.

analysis.what_fx_does: FX forbids syntax extensions, custom operators, custom keywords, #lang directives, compiler plugins that change parsing/type checking, implicit conversions, and template metaprogramming. Code generation uses sandboxed comptime. Hard rule: every .fx file parseable by any FX tool without knowing project config.
