closest_prior_art.approach: Every module specifies its language via #lang; enables entirely new syntaxes and semantics per file; macro system can redefine core constructs; module-scoped to limit blast radius; #lang protocol itself is fixed/rigid

Implicit conversions enabled 'dialect-like' behavior per library; large-scale study (7280 projects) found widespread abuse hurting readability; Scala 3 redesigned implicits to given/using to reduce abuse potential
No macros, no operator overloading, no generics (until 1.18), minimal syntax; gofmt enforces one style; every Go file readable by anyone; deliberate uniformity over expressiveness

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The Scala study (Krikava et al., 2019) empirically demonstrates the readability damage from implicit dialect creation — FX avoids this by design. Unlike Go, FX achieves uniformity without sacrificing expressiveness (it has generics, type classes, comptime, 21 dimensions). The guarantee that every .fx file is parseable without project config is stronger than Racket's #lang (which requires knowing the language to parse) and Rust's macro system (which can generate arbitrary syntax).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Racket's extensibility has produced genuinely useful DSLs (Typed Racket, Scribble, Slideshow, educational languages). FX's prohibition may force verbose encodings for domains that would benefit from syntactic sugar (e.g., linear algebra notation, regex DSLs, SQL embedding). Rust's proc macros, despite their downsides, enable derive, serde, and tokio macros that dramatically reduce boilerplate. FX must demonstrate that comptime + decorators + contracts can cover the same use cases without syntax extension.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No analysis of which common macro/extension use cases are covered by comptime + decorators and which are not. No specification of what comptime code generation can produce (arbitrary declarations? only expressions?). No discussion of how FX handles domain-specific notation needs (mathematical formulas, hardware description, query languages) without syntax extension. The section does not acknowledge the tension between uniformity and expressiveness.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add a subsection showing how comptime + decorators + contracts handle the top-10 use cases for macros/syntax extensions in Rust/Scala/Racket (derive, serde, async, SQL embedding, regex, test frameworks, builder patterns, CLI parsing, logging, error handling). Demonstrate that the same expressiveness is achievable. Acknowledge the expressiveness trade-off honestly and explain why uniformity wins for an agent-first language where every consumer must parse every file.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for the standard library design: FX ships a large stdlib justified by contract-verified evolution, effect-driven tree shaking, and profile-based restriction replacing no_std.

closest_prior_art.approach: Batteries-included philosophy with backward compatibility guarantee; Go 1.22+ added pattern routing in http.ServeMux, 1.21 added log/slog — stdlib evolves conservatively under full-time core team
Intentionally small std; 2025 Extended Standard Library (ESL) proposal to certify curated crates with 'blessed' badge; blessed.rs as interim curated list

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's three-pillar argument (contracts for safe evolution, effects for tree shaking, profiles for subsetting) is more principled than Go's conservative manual curation or Rust's community-driven blessed.rs. The contract-verified evolution mechanism theoretically solves the 'stdlib rots because you cannot remove anything' problem that killed Python's urllib lineage.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The argument is entirely theoretical — no stdlib exists yet. Go's stdlib succeeds because a well-funded, long-tenured team maintains it, not because of a type-system feature. Rust's ecosystem succeeds because crates can evolve independently and die gracefully. FX's contracts may slow stdlib evolution by requiring formal migration proofs for every change.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of stdlib governance (who decides what goes in?), no staffing model, no timeline, no priority ordering among the ~20 planned module groups. No discussion of how comptime code generation interacts with stdlib contracts.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add a concrete governance model: define a stdlib RFC process, priority tiers (P0: core data structures and IO; P1: serialization and crypto; P2: networking and DB; P3: domain-specific), and acceptance criteria. Address the staffing elephant: contracts help evolution but someone still has to write and maintain the code.

analysis.what_fx_does: Argues FX can ship a large stdlib where others cannot, via three mechanisms: contract-verified evolution (compiler-computed semver diffs), effect-driven tree shaking (unused modules cost zero), and profile-based restriction (replaces no_std with continuous effect-based subsetting).

closest_prior_art.approach: Batteries included with backward compatibility guarantee, maintained by full-time Google-funded team. Works because Go has narrow 'sweet spot' (network servers) and fearlessly assertive maintainers. No formal versioning mechanism — just discipline and compatibility promise.

Small std, rich third-party ecosystem. ESL proposal (2025) to certify curated crates. Key tension: 'whatever you integrate now into std will be the wrong decision in 2 years' vs decision paralysis from too many crate choices.
Very large stdlib that accumulated mistakes (urllib -> urllib2 -> urllib3 -> requests). Demonstrates exactly the problem FX claims to solve via contracts.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The three mechanisms are individually sound and compose well. Effect-driven tree shaking is genuinely novel for stdlib subsetting — no other language has this. Profile-based restriction is more principled than Rust's no_std binary split. Contract-verified evolution addresses the Python urllib problem at the type level.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Contract-verified evolution requires the contracts to actually be written and maintained — this is enormous work that the spec hand-waves. Go succeeds with a human governance model, not a formal one. The argument assumes FX's contract system works as specified (it has no implementation). Rust's small-std + crates model has a different advantage: libraries can die without breaking the language itself.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No cost analysis of writing contracts for every stdlib API. No discussion of whether effect-driven tree shaking works for transitive dependencies within the stdlib. No comparison with Rust's feature flags for conditional compilation. No discussion of stdlib versioning policy (does the stdlib version independently from the compiler?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Quantify the contract maintenance burden: estimate how many contract declarations a module like std/http would need. Consider a hybrid model: core modules (data structures, basic IO) are stdlib with contracts; everything else is a blessed-crate equivalent with lighter governance. This gets the benefit of contracts where it matters without drowning in formalism.

analysis.what_fx_does: Lists ~20 module groups spanning data formats (JSON/YAML/TOML/CSV/XML/MsgPack/Protobuf/CBOR), text processing, time, cryptography (SHA-2/3/BLAKE3/ChaCha20/AES-GCM/Ed25519/X25519/RSA/TLS 1.3/X.509), networking (HTTP/1.1+2/WebSocket/gRPC/DNS/QUIC), database clients (PostgreSQL/SQLite/Redis), storage/compression, image codecs (PNG/JPEG/WebP/AVIF), numerics (tensors/BLAS/FFT/stats/RNG), parsing, system, observability, and formalized mathematics (ported from Lean 4's Mathlib).

closest_prior_art.approach: Covers HTTP, crypto, JSON, XML, compression, testing, but not DB clients, image codecs, gRPC, or formalized math. Scope is narrower but achievable by a funded team.
Over 2 million lines, 210,000+ theorems, 100,000+ definitions. Took a large community 10+ years to build. Porting from Lean 3 to Lean 4 alone was a 10-month community effort for ~1M lines.

Broadest stdlib of any mainstream language, but still delegates crypto (cryptography), HTTP (requests), DB (SQLAlchemy), image (Pillow) to third-party. Even Python's batteries-included model didn't attempt verified crypto or formalized math.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The ambition to have all formats share a contract-based API (switching JSON to MessagePack as a one-line change) is genuinely novel. Verified crypto with constant-time guarantees in the stdlib would be a major differentiator if achieved.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The scope is absurdly ambitious. Lean's Mathlib alone is 2M+ lines maintained by a large community over a decade — 'ported from Lean 4's Mathlib' is a multi-year project by itself. Verified TLS 1.3 took Project Everest (Microsoft Research, INRIA, CMU, MSR-INRIA) years with dozens of researchers. Verified image codecs, verified TCP, verified crypto — each is a research-group-scale effort. Listing them as stdlib modules with no implementation plan is aspirational to the point of being misleading.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No implementation priority or timeline. No staffing estimate. No acknowledgment that Mathlib porting is a massive multi-year effort (it has never been successfully ported to any language other than Lean 4 from Lean 3). No discussion of what 'verified' means for each module — are these proved in FX itself or wrapped verified C code?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Be honest about what ships at v1.0 vs what is aspirational. Define three tiers: (1) ships at launch: core data structures, basic IO, JSON, basic crypto primitives; (2) ships within 1 year: HTTP, DB clients, more formats; (3) long-term research: verified TLS, verified image codecs, Mathlib. The current presentation reads like a wish list, not a plan.

analysis.what_fx_does: map(K, V) promises behavior (insert/lookup/delete/iterate) but not implementation; compiler picks optimal backing structure based on refinement information (e.g., small key range -> direct array, few entries -> flat sorted array, many entries -> Swiss Table). Allocator is an effect, not a type parameter, so list(int) is always list(int) regardless of allocation strategy.

closest_prior_art.approach: C++ locked unordered_map to node-based storage via iterator-stability guarantees. Google's absl::flat_hash_map is 2-5x faster but incompatible. Every major company ships its own hash map. FX's critique is well-founded.

Jai provides automatic Structure-of-Arrays conversion and compile-time metaprogramming for data layout, but the programmer still chooses the container type explicitly — no compiler-selected implementation switching.
Rust's allocator API makes the allocator a type parameter (Vec<T, A>), which FX explicitly avoids. Rust's approach is more flexible but infects the type signature.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The critique of C++ unordered_map is precise and well-articulated. Decoupling allocator from type is a genuine improvement over Rust's approach for ergonomics. Compiler-selected implementation based on refinement is novel — no production language does this today. The SoA optimization guided by pipeline access patterns is clever.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Compiler-selected implementations create reproducibility and debugging challenges: when the compiler silently switches from flat array to Swiss Table after a refactor widens a key range, performance characteristics change invisibly. No other production system has attempted this — the lack of prior art is a warning, not just an opportunity. How does the user debug performance regressions when the compiler changed the data structure? How does the compiler decide 'with 12 entries' — is that a comptime-known bound or a runtime observation?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of the decision criteria for implementation selection. No discussion of how the user overrides when the compiler makes a bad choice. No discussion of what happens when refinement information is partial or absent. No specification of iterator invalidation semantics (if FX promises none, that constrains implementations just like C++). The 'with 12 entries' example is hand-wavy — how does the compiler know the entry count?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Define explicit selection criteria (e.g., 'if key type has refinement bounding to < 256, use direct array; if comptime-known size < 64, use flat array; otherwise Swiss Table'). Add @[map_impl(flat)] override annotation. Specify iterator invalidation rules clearly — if FX promises no invalidation, it will face the same C++ problem. Consider exposing the implementation choice in compiler diagnostics so users can understand performance.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for metatheory: FX maintains correctness through a corrected Lam rule (Wood/Atkey 2022), a known-unsoundness corpus that grows and never shrinks, and a five-layer defense strategy spanning smoke tests, fuzzing, cross-referencing mechanized proofs, self-verification, and formal review.

closest_prior_art.approach: Lean verifies its own typechecker in Lean, found at least one soundness bug through the verification process. This is the closest real-world equivalent to FX's Layer 4 self-verified metatheory.

Rust has ~84 open I-unsound issues tracked on GitHub as of 2024. Pragmatic approach: known bugs documented but not necessarily blocked on fix. Contrasts with FX's 'corpus regression blocks all releases'.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The five-layer defense is more systematic than any existing language's approach. The 'corpus never shrinks' policy is stronger than Rust's pragmatic acceptance of known unsoundness. Cross-referencing every rule with mechanized proofs is ambitious and valuable.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Lean 4 actually has a verified typechecker (Lean4Lean); FX has a design document. The gap between describing a five-layer defense and implementing one is enormous. Rust has 84 known unsoundness bugs after years of real-world use; FX has 7 catalog entries from textbooks. Real systems find bugs through deployment, not just literature review.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of the cost of the five-layer defense on development velocity. No timeline for self-verified metatheory (Layer 4). No discussion of how the 21-dimension interaction multiplies the soundness surface area.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Acknowledge that the five layers are aspirational targets, not current capabilities. Prioritize: Layer 1 (smoke tests) at launch, Layer 2 (fuzzing) within 6 months, Layer 3 (cross-references) ongoing, Layer 4 (self-verification) as a multi-year research goal. Layer 5 (formal review process) can start immediately.

analysis.what_fx_does: FX uses Wood/Atkey 2022 context division in the Lam rule to prevent linear variables from being captured and used multiple times in replicable closures. The canonical witness: fn(x) => f(f(x)) is rejected when f is linear because 1/w = 0 erases f from the closure's context.

closest_prior_art.system: Granule language (Orchard, Liepelt, Eades 2019; Wood & Atkey 2022)
closest_prior_art.approach: Granule implements graded modal types with the corrected Lam rule. Uses Z3 for constraint discharge. Published soundness proof formalized in Agda (Abel et al. framework). OOPSLA 2024 paper on fractional uniqueness for borrowing/lifetimes.

closest_prior_art.system: Wood & Atkey 'A Framework for Substructural Type Systems' (ESOP 2022)
closest_prior_art.approach: The original paper defining context division (G/p) for the Lam rule. Provides the theoretical foundation FX builds on. Also published 'A linear algebra approach to linear metatheory' (arXiv 2020) connecting grade checking to linear algebra.

QTT is the predecessor with the known-broken Lam rule. Atkey's original QTT paper gives CwF semantics and resource-correctness proof but with the unsound rule. FX correctly identifies this as broken and uses the fix.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX correctly identifies and adopts the fixed rule, with a clear and correct canonical witness. The presentation is accessible — the 1/w = 0 explanation is clearer than the formal paper. FX's ambition to scale this to 21 dimensions goes well beyond Granule's scope.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Granule has an actual implementation with a soundness proof formalized in Agda. FX has a design document. The section is thin — it presents one witness but doesn't discuss how context division interacts with the other 18 dimensions. Granule's CSL 2025 and OOPSLA 2024 papers push the theory further than what FX describes.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of how context division extends to dependent types (Atkey's QTT is dependently typed but the division rule has subtleties there). No mention of the Granule project at all, which is the primary implementation of these ideas. No formalization plan for the correctness of FX's specific instantiation.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Cite Granule explicitly as the primary implementation of graded modal types. Discuss how context division interacts with dependent types (FX is dependently typed, so this matters). Add a second witness showing the rule's interaction with effects or security grades, not just usage.

analysis.what_fx_does: Maintains a catalog of known type theory bugs from published literature, organized by category: grade/linearity (Atkey 2018 Lam, session aliasing, ML value restriction), dependent types (Type:Type / Girard's paradox), information flow (implicit flow, CT violation), concurrency (fractional permission overallocation). Each becomes a permanent test_theory case; corpus never shrinks; regression blocks releases.

closest_prior_art.approach: Academic workshop dedicated to cataloging soundness bugs across languages and formalisms. Found novel Java unsoundness, documented OCaml/Scala GADT bugs. Key insight: 'many soundness bugs found across languages share fundamental characteristics'. This is the closest academic analog to FX's corpus.

84+ open soundness issues tracked via GitHub labels. Includes trait system holes (GATs + ATIT #135011), implied bounds + variance (#25860 open since 2015), auto trait selection (#84857). Pragmatic: bugs documented but not all fixed. Much larger corpus than FX's 7 entries.

Found a soundness bug 'entirely theoretically, by failing to prove a theorem and working backwards to an actual exploitable soundness bug'. Also found native_decide leakage. Demonstrates that building proofs finds bugs that testing alone misses.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The 'corpus never shrinks, regression blocks releases' policy is stronger than any existing language. The categorization by dimension (grade, dependent type, info flow, concurrency) is systematic. The inclusion of the Atkey Lam bug shows genuine awareness of the graded types literature.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Seven catalog entries is extremely thin compared to Rust's 84+ known soundness issues or the breadth of bugs discussed at the UNSOUND workshop. The corpus covers textbook bugs but misses categories that real implementations hit: variance + lifetime interaction, higher-rank type inference unsoundness, GAT normalization bugs, coinduction/guardedness checker bugs, universe polymorphism bugs. FX with 21 dimensions should have a much larger anticipated bug surface than what's listed. (§6.8 composition rules catalog pairwise collisions but are not themselves soundness bug entries.)
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No bugs from the intersection of multiple dimensions (e.g., security + session types, effects + linearity). No mention of the UNSOUND workshop or its findings. No bugs related to the specific novel features FX introduces (machine state typing, contract verification, hardware synthesis). No discussion of how new bugs are discovered and added — the corpus should grow from fuzzing (Layer 2) and self-verification (Layer 4), not just literature review.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand the corpus significantly: add dimension-interaction bugs (security x linearity, effects x sessions, lifetime x ownership), add bugs from the UNSOUND workshop proceedings, add anticipated bugs from FX's novel features (machine refinement, contract migration, hardware clock domain mixing). Define a bug bounty or submission process. Cross-reference with Rust's I-unsound tracker for structural parallels.

analysis.what_fx_does: Five-layer defense: (1) known-witness smoke tests on every build, (2) property-based metatheory fuzzing nightly (generate random well-typed terms, check preservation/progress), (3) cross-reference every typing rule with published mechanized proofs, (4) self-verified metatheory (preservation/progress as FX theorems proved by induction, part of build), (5) formal review process requiring provenance, positive/negative tests, metatheory re-proof, fuzz run, corpus check for every new rule.

closest_prior_art.approach: Verifies Lean 4's typechecker in Lean itself. Equivalent to FX's Layer 4 but actually implemented. Found a real soundness bug through verification. Covers type checking, not the full metatheory.

PLT Redex (Felleisen et al.) enables random testing of reduction semantics. αCheck (nominal logic bounded model-checker) automates metatheory bug finding. FuzzChick (Coq) does coverage-guided fuzzing of properties. These are the practical implementations of FX's Layer 2.

closest_prior_art.system: Agda formalization of graded type theory (Abel et al.)
closest_prior_art.approach: Granule's metatheory is formalized in Agda with soundness proof via logical relations. This is the standard for Layer 3 cross-referencing — FX's rules should cite such formalizations.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The five layers are well-structured and each addresses a different failure mode. No existing language has all five. Lean4Lean achieves Layer 4 but lacks Layers 2 and 5 as formal policy. The requirement that every new rule needs a full re-proof is extremely rigorous.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Lean4Lean exists and works; FX Layer 4 is aspirational. PLT Redex and FuzzChick exist and work; FX Layer 2 is aspirational. The five layers assume a mature, well-staffed project with dedicated metatheory engineers — realistic for Microsoft Research (Lean) or a well-funded team, but the spec provides no indication of how this will be staffed. Layer 3 ('every rule cites a machine-checked source') is extremely ambitious for 21 dimensions — no single mechanized proof covers the interaction of all 21.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of tool choices for each layer (which fuzzer? which proof assistant for cross-reference? Lean 4 is implied by §31.8). No estimate of the effort to achieve Layer 4 self-verification. No discussion of how to handle the combinatorial explosion of dimension interactions in fuzzing — testing preservation for 21 interacting dimensions is qualitatively harder than for a simple lambda calculus.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Be concrete about tooling: Layer 2 should use something like FuzzChick or a custom QuickCheck for FX terms. Layer 3 should start with Agda/Lean formalizations of the core 5-6 dimensions (type, usage, effect, security, lifetime) and expand. Layer 4 should target a minimal core first (simply-typed + linear), not the full 21-dimension system. Add estimated timelines.

analysis.what_fx_does: States four core meta-theorems over the §31 kernel calculus: Preservation (well-typed terms stay well-typed under β/ι/ν/η reduction), Progress (closed well-typed terms are values or reduce), Consistency (no closed term of type ∀(P : Type<0>).P), Strong Normalization (every well-typed term normalizes). Mechanization status: stated in Lean 4 reference implementation; proofs pending; cited precedents include MetaCoq (POPL 2024), Idris 2 formalization, in-progress Lean 4 kernel verification.

closest_prior_art.approach: Mechanized verification of Coq's kernel typechecker, including consistency. Shows the full trusted base can be mechanized for a production proof assistant. Reference template for FX's equivalent.
closest_prior_art.reference: Sozeau et al., 'The MetaCoq Project', JAR 2020; POPL 2024 follow-up on verified extraction

closest_prior_art.approach: Verifies Lean 4's typechecker in Lean 4 itself. Found a real soundness bug. Covers type checking; does not yet cover full strong normalization.
Graded modal type theory with mechanized preservation/progress in Agda. Covers the grade-algebra axioms FX reuses; does not address universe hierarchy or emit-table axioms.

closest_prior_art.system: Abel et al. graded type theory in Agda (ICFP 2023)
closest_prior_art.approach: Formalized erasure soundness for a graded modal dependent type theory. FX's Grade-zero axiom (erasure correctness) refines against this.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Stating the four theorems explicitly as part of the spec commits FX to a soundness proof-obligation that most languages never make concrete. The target (Lean 4 mechanization) is chosen because the kernel is already implemented in Lean 4, so the compiler and the proof share a language — no translation gap. FX's unified kernel (§31) makes the theorems coherent across all 21 dimensions: a single Preservation theorem covers types, grades, universes, and the emit-table axiom uniformly, rather than requiring separate theorems per subsystem.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The theorems are stated but unproved. MetaCoq took ~5 years with a dedicated team. Lean4Lean is mid-flight for Lean 4's simpler kernel (no graded modalities). FX's kernel is more ambitious (21 dims + emit-table axiom) and the proof effort is correspondingly larger. Until the Lean 4 proof lands, FX is 'argued-from-cited-precedent' — same status Coq occupied pre-MetaCoq. Strong Normalization for a graded modal dependent type theory with coinductive data and sized codata is research-level; no prior mechanization covers that specific combination.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: (1) Timeline estimate for each theorem's mechanization. (2) Cited-precedent-coverage matrix: for each kernel axiom, name the nearest existing mechanized-proof precedent and the gap. (3) Plan for the emit-table axiom U-emit, which is outside pure MLTT and requires a refinement argument against the ISA memory model (additional lemma burden per supported target). (4) Interaction between Strong Normalization and the emit-table axiom — U-emit is non-reducing at kernel level, so SN holds 'over the pure fragment' but the full story needs a separation argument.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Publish a proof-plan document: which Lean 4 files target which theorem, which dependencies are in Mathlib already, estimated effort per theorem. Start with the simplest (Preservation) and build up. Cite every kernel axiom's precedent and document the novel axioms (especially U-emit) as explicit proof obligations. Once Preservation + Progress land, FX can claim type-soundness rigorously.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for six reference implementations (fx-chip, fx-driver, fx-net, fx-db, fx-image, fx-numeric), each targeting a domain where FX's type-system properties provide concrete advantages.

closest_prior_art.approach: Suite of verified implementations: miTLS (TLS 1.3 in F*), HACL* (crypto in Low*/F*), EverParse (parser generators), Signal* (Signal protocol). Multi-year, multi-institution research effort with dozens of researchers. Produced production-quality C code used in Firefox, Linux, Windows.
Verified microkernel with growing ecosystem: verified Ethernet driver via Pancake, verified IPv6 stack attempts, DARPA PROVERS program (2024). Each component takes years of dedicated effort.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX proposes to unify all six domains under one language and type system, whereas Project Everest and seL4 each address only a subset. If achievable, having verified hardware, drivers, networking, database, image codecs, and tensor computation in one language would be unprecedented.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Six reference implementations is approximately six research-group-scale projects. Project Everest took 5+ years with Microsoft Research backing. seL4 verification took 20+ person-years. SiFive's Kami RISC-V spec took a dedicated team. Claiming all six as 'reference implementations that ship alongside the compiler' without any implementation is deeply unrealistic without massive funding and staffing.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No staffing plan, no timeline, no priority ordering, no acknowledgment of the scale of effort required. No discussion of what 'ships alongside' means — are these complete implementations or proof-of-concept demos?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Prioritize ruthlessly: pick ONE reference implementation as the launch showpiece (fx-chip or fx-driver are the most compelling). Designate the rest as 'future work' with honest effort estimates. Consider partnering with existing projects (e.g., contribute to Rust-for-Linux rather than building fx-driver from scratch).

analysis.what_fx_does: fx-chip: a verified RISC-V RV64GC core with ISA as total function, five-stage pipeline, refinement proof connecting the two. Target: boot Linux on FPGA with every instruction commit formally proven.

closest_prior_art.approach: SiFive's RISC-V ISA specification in Kami (Coq). Provides RV32GC and RV64GC semantics including User/Supervisor/Machine modes and Zam extension. Can output Verilog for simulation/synthesis. This is the closest existing verified RISC-V spec.

End-to-end formal ISA verification framework for RISC-V processors using bounded model checking. Verifies individual instructions against RISC-V spec. Used by multiple RISC-V implementations. Does NOT do full refinement proof — checks instruction-level properties.

Formal verification of RISC-V designs written in Chisel (Scala-based HDL). Recent work adapts formal verification to Chisel designs, but instructions specified in Verilog/SystemVerilog remain challenging to maintain.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's approach of having ISA spec, RTL, and refinement proof all in one language is genuinely novel. Kami uses Coq for spec but outputs Verilog; riscv-formal uses external tools. FX's unified language for all three eliminates the specification gap. The claim that the ISA is a Tot function usable for both verification and hardware is elegant.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: RV64GC is enormous: 'G' = IMAFD (integer + multiply + atomic + float + double), 'C' = compressed instructions. Kami took a SiFive team to specify just the ISA. A five-stage pipeline that boots Linux needs MMU, interrupt handling, CSRs, and extensive microarchitectural features. This alone is a multi-year project for a team of verified hardware engineers. NVIDIA shipped 1 billion RISC-V cores in 2024 — the bar for a usable implementation is very high.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of verification strategy for floating-point (notoriously hard). No mention of MMU/page table walker verification. No discussion of cache coherence. No acknowledgment of the scale — booting Linux requires kernel-level maturity including interrupt controllers, timers, UART, etc. No comparison with existing verified RISC-V efforts to show what's different.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Start with RV64I (integer only, no floating point, no atomics, no compressed) as the initial target. Booting Linux is a stretch goal, not a launch target. Compare explicitly with Kami and riscv-formal to position what FX adds. Specify whether the pipeline verification covers timing (cycle-accurate) or just functional correctness.

analysis.what_fx_does: fx-driver: Linux kernel drivers in FX with execution context effect hierarchy preventing sleeping-in-interrupt, linear DMA tracking preventing use-while-device-owns-buffer, and machine-based driver lifecycles generating cleanup chains. Initial targets: e1000 NIC, NVMe, XHCI USB.

closest_prior_art.system: Rust for Linux (officially permanent as of Dec 2025)
closest_prior_art.approach: Rust in Linux kernel is no longer experimental. Android 16 devices ship with Rust-written ashmem allocator. Linux 6.14 has PCI/platform device driver enablement for Rust. Real GPU drivers expected to merge within 12-18 months. Memory safety via Rust's ownership, but without formal verification or effect tracking.

Uses Rust and Verus for formally correct device drivers. Addresses verification of concurrent driver code and integration with kernel's low-level interface. Presented at 2nd Workshop on Kernel Isolation, Safety and Verification (ACM). Closest to FX's ambition of verified drivers.

closest_prior_art.system: seL4 verified Ethernet driver via Pancake (2024)
closest_prior_art.approach: Pancake language with verified compiler, integrated into Viper SMT verification. End-to-end functional correctness for a real Ethernet driver. Demonstrates that verified drivers are achievable but require significant tooling investment.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's execution context effect hierarchy (HardIRQ < SoftIRQ < Dispatch < Passive) is more principled than Rust-for-Linux's approach, which relies on API design rather than type-level enforcement. Linear DMA tracking is stronger than Rust's ownership alone. Machine-based lifecycle with automatic cleanup chain generation is novel.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Rust for Linux is shipping in production NOW — Android 16 devices, millions of users. Veld demonstrates that Rust + Verus can achieve formal verification of kernel drivers TODAY. FX has no kernel support, no driver framework, no kernel build system integration. The Linux kernel community has taken years just to accept Rust; integrating a brand-new language is unrealistic without extraordinary community buy-in. NVMe and XHCI are complex drivers — the e1000 alone is a significant project.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of kernel build system integration. No discussion of how FX drivers interoperate with C kernel code (calling conventions, locking primitives, memory model compatibility). No mention of the Linux kernel community acceptance challenge. No comparison with Veld's approach using existing Rust + Verus tooling.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Position fx-driver as targeting a microkernel (seL4) rather than Linux — the community acceptance barrier is lower and seL4's verification culture is more aligned. Alternatively, target bare-metal embedded first (no OS integration needed). If Linux is the target, start with a loadable kernel module for a trivial device (e.g., LED GPIO driver) rather than e1000/NVMe/XHCI.

analysis.what_fx_does: fx-net: verified TCP/IP stack with formal compliance to RFC 793/9293. Session types for protocol states. Linear packet buffers for zero-copy. Contracts for wire formats. Targets: UDP, TCP (no congestion), then Cubic, TLS 1.3, HTTP.

closest_prior_art.system: miTLS / Project Everest (Microsoft Research + INRIA + CMU)
closest_prior_art.approach: Verified TLS 1.3 implementation in F*, compiled to C via Low*. Part of Project Everest which built a verified HTTPS stack. HACL* and EverCrypt for verified crypto. Produced C code used in Firefox, Linux, Windows. Multi-year, multi-institution effort with dozens of researchers.

Formal specification of TCP/UDP and Sockets API in HOL4. The most comprehensive formal TCP spec ever produced. Key paper: 'Engineering with Logic: Rigorous Test-Oracle Specification and Validation for TCP/IP'. Shows how hard TCP formalization is — this was a multi-year research project just for the specification, not an implementation.
Planned correct-by-construction TCP/IP stack for seL4 via refinement. Presented at seL4 Summit 2019. Still in progress — demonstrates that even well-funded groups find this extremely challenging.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Session types for TCP state machine is elegant and well-motivated. Linear packet buffers for zero-copy is a genuine type-level advantage. The unified contract-based approach for wire formats is novel. The progression from UDP to TCP to TLS is sensible.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Project Everest took 5+ years with Microsoft Research backing to produce verified TLS. NetSem took years just to formally specify TCP (not implement it). TCP RFC compliance is notoriously difficult — RFC 793 is ambiguous and implementations diverge. FX claims 'formal compliance to RFC 793/9293' in one paragraph where NetSem needed an entire research program. Adding Cubic congestion control and TLS 1.3 makes this a decade-scale project.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of what 'formal compliance' means — against which formalization of the RFCs? No acknowledgment of TCP's notorious edge cases (TIME_WAIT, simultaneous open, RST handling). No discussion of performance targets (zero-copy is mentioned but not benchmarked against existing stacks). No comparison with Project Everest or NetSem.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Define 'formal compliance' precisely: does it mean matching NetSem's HOL4 spec, or a new formalization in FX? Start with UDP only (it's trivially formalizable). TCP without congestion control is a reasonable second step. TLS 1.3 should explicitly build on Project Everest's work (HACL*, EverCrypt) rather than starting from scratch. Reference NetSem and Everest explicitly.

analysis.what_fx_does: fx-db: SQL database from SQLite to PostgreSQL scale. Machines for transaction state with inverse chains for abort. Write-ahead log as linear monotonic structure. Query plan correctness as refinement proof. Intra-query parallelism via type system proving disjoint page access.

closest_prior_art.approach: SMT-based SQL query equivalence verification. VeriEQL proves equivalence with integrity constraints. QED translates to Q-expressions for normalization-based equivalence. These verify query transformations, not full database implementations.

Workshop on blockchain-inspired verifiable databases (Amazon QLDB, Azure SQL Ledger). Focuses on audit trails and tamper-evidence, not full implementation verification. Shows the field is active but no one has verified a full SQL engine.

Production distributed SQL database, PostgreSQL wire-compatible. Extensive testing (Jepsen) but no formal verification. Demonstrates the engineering scale: 600K+ lines of Go, hundreds of engineers over 10+ years.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The intra-query parallelism idea — compiler proving disjoint page access to enable concurrent execution — is genuinely novel and could not be done without FX's type system. Transaction machines with inverse chains for rollback is a clean formalization. WAL as linear monotonic structure leverages FX's mutation lattice nicely.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: No one has ever formally verified a complete SQL database. CockroachDB is 600K+ lines of Go with hundreds of engineers. SQLite is 150K+ lines of C. Even VeriEQL (state-of-the-art) only verifies query equivalence, not full database correctness. 'Starting at SQLite scale and growing to PostgreSQL scale' is a multi-decade project for a large team. The claim that query plan correctness is 'a refinement proof' glosses over the enormous complexity of SQL query optimization.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of SQL parsing and type checking (which SQL dialect? what subset?). No discussion of storage engine design. No discussion of concurrency control beyond the transaction machine abstraction. No comparison with any existing verified database work. No acknowledgment that a verified SQL engine would be a first-of-its-kind achievement requiring massive effort.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Start with a verified key-value store (much simpler than SQL). Then add a verified B-tree. Then a minimal SQL subset (SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on single tables). Full SQL with joins, subqueries, window functions, CTEs is years away. The intra-query parallelism idea is the most novel contribution — focus the description on that rather than the 'SQLite to PostgreSQL scale' ambition.

analysis.what_fx_does: fx-image: image loading/saving/filtering with format declarations for codec parsing. Length fields carry refinements bounding every buffer access. Buffer overflows, integer overflow in size calculations, and use-after-free structurally impossible. Targets: PNG, JPEG, WebP, AVIF.

closest_prior_art.approach: Memory-safe language for parsing untrusted file formats. Compile-time bounds checking — if it compiles, it's safe (buffer overflows, integer overflows, null derefs). Cannot make syscalls, so trivially safe against memory leaks. Transpiles to C. PNG decoder benchmarks show memory-safe implementations (Wuffs, Rust png, zune-png) now outperform C libraries (libpng, spng, stb_image).

Memory-safe PNG decoders in Rust that 'vastly outperform' C-based PNG libraries. Support APNG, auxiliary chunks, tested on 100K+ real-world images. Demonstrates that memory safety + performance is achievable without full formal verification.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's format declarations (§18.1) are more expressive than Wuffs' approach — they can encode cross-field dependencies and virtual fields with compile-time verification. The refinement-based approach (length fields bound buffer access) is more general than Wuffs' compile-time bounds checking. FX can also handle the image processing/filtering side, not just parsing.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Wuffs exists, works, and is deployed in Chrome/Skia/Fuchsia. Rust PNG decoders exist and outperform C. FX has no implementation. Wuffs' design choice to ban syscalls (no malloc) is a stronger guarantee than FX's effect-based approach. The 'structurally impossible' claims for buffer overflow and integer overflow are also true of Wuffs — this is not unique to FX. JPEG decoding involves complex floating-point DCT computation that FX's exact-decimal-by-default approach makes harder, not easier.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of performance targets (Wuffs and Rust decoders compete on performance, not just safety). No discussion of JPEG's DCT computation and how FX handles IEEE 754 in this context. No mention of Wuffs as prior art. No discussion of fuzzing strategy for codec testing (standard practice for image parsers).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Acknowledge Wuffs explicitly as the state of the art for safe format parsing. Position FX's advantage as generality (Wuffs is a DSL; FX is a general-purpose language that happens to be safe for parsing). Discuss performance: can FX match Wuffs/Rust PNG performance? Address JPEG/WebP/AVIF complexity honestly — these codecs are much harder than PNG.

analysis.what_fx_does: fx-numeric: shape-typed tensors, BLAS bindings, autodiff via staged programming (§17.2), GPU dispatch via .gpu() source type. Shape mismatches caught at compile time. Target: inference for Llama 7B with compile-time shape checking throughout.

closest_prior_art.approach: Array programming language with typed index sets for shape checking. Supports autodiff with linear types (linearize/transpose builtins). Type system statically checks array dimensions for equality. Published at ICFP. The most direct prior art for shape-typed tensors + autodiff.

Shape+dtype annotations for JAX/PyTorch tensors with runtime checking. Pragmatic approach: Float[Tensor, 'channels'] with named dimensions. Widely used in practice. Shows the demand for shape checking even without compile-time guarantees.
Deep learning in Rust with compile-time shape-checked tensors and neural networks. Includes autodiff. Demonstrates that shape typing is implementable in a systems language.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's staged programming for autodiff is more principled than Dex's approach — code(T) types allow verified code generation. GPU dispatch via source type (.gpu()) is elegant. Compile-time shape checking throughout a Llama 7B inference pipeline would be a strong demonstration.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Dex exists and has published results. jaxtyping is used in production. dfdx works in Rust. FX has no implementation. Llama 7B inference requires extensive CUDA/Metal/ROCm kernel optimization — shape typing alone does not make this competitive with TensorRT-LLM (40,000+ tokens/sec on B200) or llama.cpp. The gap between 'compile-time shape checking' and 'competitive inference performance' is enormous. No mention of quantization (critical for practical LLM inference), attention optimization (FlashAttention), or KV-cache management.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of GPU kernel code generation strategy. No discussion of quantization support (int4/int8/fp8 — essential for practical LLM inference). No mention of FlashAttention or memory-efficient attention. No performance targets. No comparison with Dex, JAX, or any existing shape-typed tensor library. 'BLAS bindings' suggests calling existing C libraries — how does this compose with FX's verification story?
analysis.could_we_do_better: Focus on what FX uniquely enables: verified shape correctness for the entire inference pipeline, verified autodiff via staging, and type-safe GPU dispatch. Drop the Llama 7B claim unless there's a concrete plan for competitive performance. Compare explicitly with Dex for the type-system aspects and with TensorRT-LLM/llama.cpp for the performance aspects. Address quantization and memory management.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for scaling properties: argues that FX's modular type checking, explicit contracts, proof composition, and absence of global state create a 'bounded blast radius' where well-typed edits cannot silently break downstream code.

closest_prior_art.approach: Modular verification where each function is checked in isolation; caches verification results per entity; changes invalidate only dependent entities
closest_prior_art.reference: Incremental Proof Development in Dafny with Module-Based Induction, Dafny Workshop at POPL 2024

closest_prior_art.approach: Automated semver violation detection via API diff analysis; RFC 1105 defines breaking vs non-breaking changes formally

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX integrates contract-based automatic semver computation (section 14.10) with modular type checking, so API stability is compiler-enforced rather than tool-enforced. Rust's cargo-semver-checks is a separate linter that can miss subtle breakage; FX claims to catch it in the type system itself.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The entire chapter is 4 subsections totaling ~70 lines with zero formalism. It reads as a set of informal claims rather than proved properties. Dafny's incremental verification has actual implementation and empirical evaluation. FX's claims about 'previously proved properties stay proved' need formal monotonicity theorems that are not provided.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No formal definition of 'blast radius' as a metric. No discussion of how diamond dependencies, type class coherence, or contract version conflicts affect modularity. No comparison with Lean 4's incremental elaboration or Dafny's modular caching. No empirical evidence or even a hypothetical benchmark.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Formalize the bounded blast radius claim as a theorem: 'If module M type-checks against interface I, and module U changes only private definitions while preserving I, then M type-checks without modification.' This is standard separate compilation theory but needs to be stated precisely for a 21-dimension system. Add a section on diamond dependency resolution.

analysis.what_fx_does: Claims that a well-typed edit that compiles cannot silently break anything outside its public surface: module M unaffected by upstream private changes, function proof obligations stable unless signatures change, trust levels monotonic.

closest_prior_art.approach: Separate compilation via signatures/interfaces; private implementation changes invisible to clients; formalized since 1994
closest_prior_art.reference: Robert Harper and Mark Lillibridge, 'A Type-Theoretic Approach to Higher-Order Modules with Sharing', POPL 1994

closest_prior_art.approach: Formal enumeration of what constitutes a breaking API change for semver purposes; cargo-semver-checks automates detection; 1 in 6 top crates has violated semver at least once despite tooling
Each method verified in isolation against its specification; changes to one entity invalidate only dependent verification results
closest_prior_art.reference: Dafny Workshop at POPL 2024, 'Incremental Proof Development in Dafny with Module-Based Induction'

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX combines modular type checking with automatic semver computation from contract diffs (section 14.10), which is more integrated than Rust's separate tooling approach. The trust dimension tracking (sorry/axiom propagation) is genuinely novel -- no other system automatically tracks proof trust through the call graph.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The claims are informal and lack proofs. ML's separate compilation has been formally verified for decades. Rust's experience shows that even with formal definitions of 'breaking change' (RFC 1105), 1 in 6 crates still violates semver -- suggesting FX's claims about compiler-guaranteed stability need stress-testing. The claim 'previously proved properties stay proved' is non-trivial for dependent types where type-level computation can change -- needs formal monotonicity proof.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No treatment of: (1) how type class instance coherence interacts with modularity (adding a new instance can break downstream code), (2) how contract version conflicts in diamond dependencies are resolved, (3) whether refinement type inference leaking implementation details can cause unexpected breakage, (4) interaction with comptime evaluation and staged programming.
analysis.could_we_do_better: State and prove the separate compilation theorem for FX's type system. Address coherence: ML's signature-based modularity is well-understood, but FX's type classes + contracts + 21 dimensions create many more surfaces for unexpected coupling. Enumerate the exhaustive list of 'breaking changes' a la Rust RFC 1105 but for FX's richer type system.

analysis.what_fx_does: Lists five reasons the bounded blast radius holds: modular checking (no global inference), explicit API stability via contracts, complete compiler feedback, proof composition (using postconditions without re-proving internals), and no silent state.

closest_prior_art.approach: Modular verification per function; but practical experience shows proof context complexity snowballs up the call graph, making higher-level proofs harder despite modularity
closest_prior_art.reference: Ranjit Jhala et al., 'On the Impact of Formal Verification on Software Development', OOPSLA 2025

closest_prior_art.approach: Modular full-system verification in C++ using separation logic; generic automation dramatically accelerates verification scaling
closest_prior_art.reference: Gregory Malecha et al., 'Modular, Full-System Verification', HotOS 2025

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The 'no silent state' argument is strong -- most languages (even Rust) allow global mutable state that can create hidden coupling. FX's effect system making all side effects explicit is a genuine advantage for modular reasoning. The integration of contracts with semver diff is also stronger than any existing system.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Dafny's experience directly contradicts the optimistic framing: even with modular verification, specification complexity snowballs up the call graph. FX's 21 dimensions likely make this worse, not better. The 'complete compiler feedback' claim is aspirational for a language that doesn't have a working compiler yet. BRiCk/Rocq have actual implementations demonstrating modular verification at scale.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of how proof obligations scale with program size in practice. Dafny research shows that modular checking does NOT mean constant-cost per function -- it can degrade. FX needs to address how its 21-dimension grade vectors affect verification scalability. Incremental checking granularity is specified (§10.13: per-semicolon checkpoint).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add concrete scaling analysis: how does verification time grow with module size, dependency depth, and dimension count? Reference Dafny's empirical findings about proof context snowballing and explain how FX's design mitigates or accepts this limitation.

analysis.what_fx_does: Honestly admits four limitations: wrong specs (compiler can't detect wrong postconditions), proofs needing human insight, performance bugs not caught without complexity annotations, and changing requirements being inherently costly.

closest_prior_art.approach: Specification errors are the fundamental limitation -- 'an implementation verification requires two versions of a design, so a bug in the specifications voids the reference model'
Real-world verified systems found bugs due to incomplete specs, incorrect modeling of syscall error codes, and resource limit assumptions -- the spec-to-reality gap is persistent
closest_prior_art.reference: Ray Wang, 'Formal Verification: The Gap Between Perfect Code and Reality', 2017, https://raywang.tech/2017/12/20/Formal-Verification:-The-Gap-between-Perfect-Code-and-Reality/

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX is unusually honest about limitations for a language spec. Most language designs either ignore formal verification entirely or oversell it. The four-point breakdown is accurate and well-calibrated.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The list is incomplete. Critical missing limitations: (1) SMT solver incompleteness and nondeterminism (Z3 can time out on queries that are trivially provable with different heuristics), (2) specification validation is mentioned in section 10.11 but not referenced here, (3) 21-dimension interaction combinatorics -- the more dimensions, the more ways specs can be subtly wrong, (4) the fundamental oracle problem: who verifies the verifier?
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No mention of: verification tool bugs (SMT solver bugs, type checker bugs), proof brittleness (small code changes causing proof obligations to become undecidable), the cost of maintaining proofs during refactoring, social/organizational barriers to formal verification adoption. The LLM-agent context makes spec quality even more critical -- LLMs are notoriously bad at writing correct formal specifications (2024 research shows LLM-generated JML/temporal-logic specs frequently fail SMT verification).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand to 8-10 limitations. Add: SMT incompleteness/nondeterminism, proof maintenance cost during refactoring, verification tool correctness (quis custodiet ipsos custodes), specification combinatorics with 21 dimensions, and the LLM-agent specification quality challenge. Reference section 10.11 (spec validation) as a partial mitigation.

analysis.what_fx_does: Proposes two metrics for measuring scaling: (1) tokens spent per verified function (agent efficiency), and (2) regression rate (how often edits cause re-verification of previously-verified functions).

closest_prior_art.approach: Standard LLM coding benchmarks measure pass@1, task resolution rate, but NOT verification-oriented metrics like 'tokens per verified function'
Measures LLM proof generation: >90% of benchmark tasks solved automatically, >50% in <30 seconds or 3 LLM calls -- closest existing metric to FX's 'tokens per verified fn'

analysis.where_fx_is_better: The 'tokens per verified function' metric is genuinely novel and well-suited to an agent-first language. No existing benchmark combines verification with LLM token efficiency. The regression rate metric is a practical and measurable property that captures modular checking quality.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Only 2 metrics is thin. No discussion of: verification time per obligation, proof stability (what % of obligations are within 80% of timeout -- mentioned in section 10.12 but not here), false positive rate of the spec validator, or user satisfaction. AutoVerus already measures similar things more concretely (% solved, time to proof, number of LLM calls).
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No target values (what's a good ratio?). No methodology for measurement. No discussion of confounders (codebase familiarity, problem difficulty, library availability). No comparison with existing developer productivity metrics (lines per hour, defect rate, time-to-market). The two metrics are interesting but insufficient for a complete evaluation framework.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand to 5-8 metrics: add verification time per obligation, proof stability ratio, spec coverage (% of public functions with pre/post), trust level distribution, and proof maintenance cost (tokens spent fixing broken proofs after edits). Set concrete targets: e.g., regression rate < 5% for private changes, < 20% for public interface changes.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header for domain archetypes: provides starter templates (ISA+RTL+refinement for hardware, lifecycle machine+registers for drivers, wire format+session types for networking, transaction machines for databases, format parsers for image codecs, shape-typed tensors for ML) showing how FX's features compose in each domain.

closest_prior_art.approach: Domain-specific patterns emerge from library ecosystems rather than language-level archetypes; no single language provides formal templates for 6+ domains
Hardware-specific design languages with formal semantics; Sail covers ISA specification with theorem-prover output; BSV covers synthesizable hardware

analysis.where_fx_is_better: No other language provides formal, verified templates spanning hardware through ML in a single language with shared type system. The breadth is unprecedented. Each archetype demonstrates multiple FX dimensions composing (ownership + effects + contracts + sessions).
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Breadth without depth. Each archetype is 30-70 lines of code that omits critical details. Domain-specific languages (Sail for ISA, Chisel for RTL, Wuffs for codecs, Dex for tensors) have years of refinement in their specific domains. FX's 'one language for everything' approach risks being adequate-everywhere but excellent-nowhere.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No archetype for: web applications (the most common domain), CLI tools, build systems, compilers, operating system kernels (beyond drivers), embedded systems (bare-metal), or distributed systems (consensus protocols). The 6 chosen domains are impressive but heavily biased toward FX's strengths.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Add at least 2-3 more archetypes covering common software: web service (HTTP + DB + auth), distributed consensus (Raft/Paxos with session types), and bare-metal embedded (no allocator, interrupt-driven). These would demonstrate FX's range more convincingly.

analysis.what_fx_does: Shows a hardware archetype in ~61 lines: ISA as pure function over arch_state, RTL as 5-stage pipeline with stall/flush/forwarding, and a refinement proof connecting them via a state abstraction function.

closest_prior_art.approach: First-order imperative language with lightweight dependent types for bitvector lengths; generates executable emulators (C, OCaml) and theorem-prover definitions (Isabelle, HOL4, Coq); official RISC-V ISA spec; used for tandem verification via TestRIG/RVFI
High-level hardware construction language embedded in Scala; BOOM is synthesizable parameterizable RV64GC core; 2024 paper on formal verification of Chisel-based RISC-V processors
closest_prior_art.reference: Shen et al., 'Formal Verification of RISC-V Processor Chisel Designs', SETTA 2024, LNCS vol 15469

closest_prior_art.approach: Formal RISC-V ISA spec that is both executable in simulation AND synthesizable to hardware; used for tandem verification against Sail; Fife and Drum pipelined designs pass rv32ui-p tests

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX unifies ISA spec, RTL, and refinement proof in one language with one type system. Sail+Chisel+Isabelle requires three separate tools. The pipeline sugar (stage/stall/flush/emit) is more concise than Chisel's Scala embedding. The refinement proof is integrated rather than requiring a separate theorem prover.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Sail has a complete RISC-V spec that boots Linux and has been tested against multiple implementations via TestRIG. FX's archetype is 61 lines covering a toy pipeline -- no evidence it scales to RV64GC. Chisel has years of silicon tapeout experience. Bluespec BSV specs are both executable AND synthesizable. FX's hardware story is entirely aspirational with zero implementation experience.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No discussion of: synthesis timing/area results, comparison with generated Verilog quality, how FX handles clock domain crossing in the pipeline, whether the refinement proof methodology scales beyond 5-stage in-order to out-of-order superscalar. The archetype doesn't show memory hierarchy, TLB, or interrupt handling -- all critical for a real CPU.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand the archetype to at least RV32I completeness (not just a sketch). Show how the refinement proof handles data hazards formally. Compare generated Verilog gate count against equivalent Chisel. Address the 'Sail can boot Linux' benchmark -- what would it take for FX's ISA spec to reach that level?

analysis.what_fx_does: Shows a kernel driver archetype in ~64 lines across 4 modules: register file with access modes (RW, W1C, W1S), lifecycle machine with inverse transitions for cleanup, interrupt handler with HardIRQ effect, and kernel module entry point with C ABI.

closest_prior_art.approach: Rust drivers accepted in kernel 6.8; ownership model eliminates 91% of safety vulnerabilities in drivers (per 2024 ACSAC study of 240 CVEs); safety documentation standard proposed; officially non-experimental as of Dec 2025
SMT-based verification of Rust code; specs and proofs written in Rust syntax; used at Microsoft and Amazon; two best papers at OSDI 2024; linear ghost types for reasoning about shared resources
closest_prior_art.reference: https://verus-lang.github.io/verus/guide/ and Lattuada et al., 'Verus: A Practical Foundation for Systems Verification', SOSP 2024

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's lifecycle machine with inverse transitions automatically generating cleanup paths is more structured than Rust's Drop trait. The execution context hierarchy (HardIRQ < SoftIRQ < Dispatch < Passive) as effect types is cleaner than Linux's runtime checks. Register access modes (W1C, W1S, RC) as types prevent entire classes of driver bugs that even Rust doesn't catch.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Rust for Linux has real drivers shipping in production kernels. FX has zero drivers. The 2024 ACSAC study shows Rust already eliminates 91% of safety vulnerabilities -- FX's additional guarantees (register access modes, lifecycle machines) address the remaining 9% but at the cost of a completely new language with no ecosystem. Verus adds formal verification to existing Rust code, which is more practical than rewriting everything in FX.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No treatment of: kernel API compatibility (how does FX interop with the existing 30M-line C kernel?), how to handle the subset of unsafe operations that Linux requires (DMA mapping, raw pointer manipulation), power management (suspend/resume state machines), hotplug, sysfs/procfs interfaces, or how FX's allocator interacts with the kernel's slab allocator.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Show a realistic comparison: take a real Linux driver bug (from the 240 CVEs studied) and demonstrate how FX's type system would have prevented it. This is far more convincing than a toy archetype. Address the Rust+Verus path as a pragmatic alternative that builds on an existing ecosystem.

analysis.what_fx_does: Shows a network protocol archetype: TCP wire format as bit-level format declaration, connection state machine with invariants (snd_una <= snd_nxt), and session types for the client-side protocol.

closest_prior_art.system: Session types for TCP (Cavoj et al. 2024)
closest_prior_art.approach: First application of multiparty session types (MPST) to transport layer; implements subset of TCP server in Rust using session types encoded as native Rust types; tested against Linux TCP stack
closest_prior_art.reference: Cavoj et al., 'Session Types for the Transport Layer: Towards an Implementation of TCP', arXiv:2404.05478, April 2024

closest_prior_art.approach: Framework for implementing and formally verifying distributed systems in Coq; includes verified implementations of Raft consensus

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX integrates wire format (bit-level parsing), state machine (connection states), and session types (protocol sequence) in one unified system. Cavoj et al.'s Rust implementation requires separate libraries for each concern. The format declaration automatically generates parser/serializer with bounds checking. The state machine invariants (snd_una <= snd_nxt) connect wire format to protocol state, which the Rust approach cannot express.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Cavoj et al. have a working implementation tested against the real Linux TCP stack. FX's archetype is ~50 lines of untested specification. The Rust MPST encoding is practical and deployable today. Verdi has mechanized proofs in Coq for distributed protocols. FX's archetype doesn't show: congestion control, retransmission, flow control, or any of the complex parts of TCP that make verification hard.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Only shows connection establishment, not steady-state data transfer. No treatment of: congestion control state machines (which interact with the connection state machine), TIME_WAIT handling, RST injection resilience, or how the session type model handles out-of-order delivery (session types assume reliable ordered channels, but TCP IS the reliability layer). The fundamental mismatch between session types (which assume ordered delivery) and transport protocols (which must handle disorder) is not addressed.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Address the session-type/transport-layer mismatch explicitly: Cavoj et al. note that 'the transport layer has different requirements and constraints compared to the application layer resulting in different requirements for verification.' FX should explain how its session types handle the unreliable network link that TCP sits on top of.

analysis.what_fx_does: Shows a database archetype: schema with refinement types, contract with access modes and SQL format binding, and a transaction state machine with atomic commit/abort and durability/visibility invariants.

closest_prior_art.approach: Verified key-value store based on B-epsilon tree in Dafny; proves functional correctness and crash safety; uses linear types for memory reasoning; 28% fewer proof lines with linear Dafny; 25x faster than BerkeleyDB on writes
closest_prior_art.reference: Hance et al., 'Storage Systems are Distributed Systems (So Verify Them That Way!)', OSDI 2020, https://www.usenix.org/system/files/osdi20-hance.pdf

closest_prior_art.approach: Compositional TLA+ specifications for distributed transactions; formal interface boundary between transaction protocol and WiredTiger storage engine; modular verification of isolation and permissiveness
closest_prior_art.reference: Schultz et al., 'Design and Modular Verification of Distributed Transactions in MongoDB', VLDB 2024/2025

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX integrates schema refinements (valid_email), access control modes, serialization formats, and transaction semantics in one declaration. VeriBetrKV verifies the storage layer but has no schema-level types. MongoDB uses TLA+ separately from implementation code. FX's contract system binding SQL format to typed schema is novel.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: VeriBetrKV is a verified, benchmarked storage engine with crash-safety proofs. FX's archetype is ~40 lines that doesn't address: B-tree/LSM implementation, crash recovery, WAL correctness, concurrency control (MVCC, 2PL), or query optimization. MongoDB's TLA+ specs cover distributed transaction isolation which FX's single-node archetype ignores entirely. The archetype is a schema definition, not a database engine.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No treatment of: storage engine implementation (page layout, buffer pool, WAL), concurrency control protocol, query plan correctness, index maintenance, recovery after crash, distributed transactions, replication. The 'transaction machine' has 3 states which is vastly simpler than real transaction lifecycle (which includes savepoints, two-phase commit, prepared states, etc.).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Expand significantly: show at least the WAL append as a linear monotonic structure (mentioned in section 27.4 but not shown here), demonstrate how FX's ownership prevents double-reads of uncommitted data, and show a 2PC machine composing with the local transaction machine.

analysis.what_fx_does: Shows an image processing archetype: pixel format as ADT, image type parameterized by format/width/height with stride refinement, and PNG decoder using format declarations with length-bounded chunk parsing and region-scoped allocation.

closest_prior_art.approach: Memory-safe language for untrusted file formats with compile-time bounds checking; no allocation capability in Wuffs code; GIF decoder ships in Chrome since 2021; PNG decoder 1.2-2.7x faster than libpng; safety checks are compile-time not runtime
Memory-safe Rust PNG decoders 'vastly outperform' C-based libraries (libpng, spng, stb_image); tested on 100K+ real-world images; full APNG support

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX's format declaration for PngChunk encodes the length-data dependency directly in the type (data: length * 8 bits), making buffer overflows structurally impossible. Wuffs achieves similar safety but through a custom language; FX integrates this into a general-purpose language. The region-scoped allocation (Alloc(Region('r))) is more flexible than Wuffs' no-allocation model.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Wuffs has a GIF decoder shipping in Chrome to billions of users since 2021 and is 1.2-2.7x FASTER than libpng. Rust png decoders 'vastly outperform' C libraries. FX has zero implementation. Wuffs' 'no allocation capability' is a stronger safety property than FX's tracked allocation. Wuffs' approach of doing ALL checks at compile time is also stronger than FX's potential for runtime validators at trust boundaries.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No treatment of: JPEG (which requires DCT and is much more complex than PNG), progressive decoding, color space conversion, ICC profile handling, EXIF metadata, error resilience for corrupted images, streaming decode, or SIMD optimization (which Wuffs and Rust crates exploit heavily). The archetype shows PNG chunk parsing but not the actual IDAT decompression which is where the real complexity (and vulnerability surface) lives.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Show how FX handles the hard parts: JPEG's Huffman + DCT pipeline, or PNG's IDAT inflate with sliding window. These are where real vulnerabilities live (CVE-heavy code paths). Compare FX's compile-time guarantees against Wuffs' on a specific vulnerability class. Address performance: can FX match Wuffs/Rust speed?

analysis.what_fx_does: Shows a tensor computation archetype: shape-typed matmul/broadcast_add, Linear layer with forward pass, comptime autodiff via staged programming, and GPU execution via .gpu() source type.

closest_prior_art.approach: Research language with typed indices as first-class types for shape safety; catches shape errors at compile time; richer than NumPy's runtime checks; supports existential types for ragged arrays
closest_prior_art.reference: https://github.com/google-research/dex-lang and 'Dex: array programming with typed indices', OpenReview 2019

closest_prior_art.approach: ATL is a verified compiler for a functional tensor language with reshape operators decoupling compute/storage order; TensorRight provides automated verification of tensor graph rewrites
Statically typed tensor language with fully typed dimensions, generic functions over tensor dim and batch dim, EinOps-style reshaping where dimensions are explicit

analysis.where_fx_is_better: FX integrates shape-typed tensors with autodiff (via staging), GPU dispatch, and the full verification stack (pre/post on tensor ops). Dex has typed indices but no formal verification. ATL has verified compilation but no GPU story. Ten has typed dimensions but no autodiff or effects. FX's comptime autodiff producing code(tensor -> tensor) is elegant.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: Dex has a working implementation with actual shape inference and execution. ATL has a mechanically verified compiler. FX's autodiff is a one-line 'differentiate_source(f)' with zero specification of how it works. The .gpu() dispatch mechanism is hand-waved. No treatment of: custom kernels, memory management on GPU, synchronization, mixed precision, quantization, or any of the practical concerns that make real ML frameworks complex.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: No specification of: what differentiate_source actually does (forward-mode? reverse-mode? source-to-source?), how GPU memory allocation works, how data transfer between CPU and GPU is tracked in the ownership system, how shape broadcasting rules are defined, how dynamic shapes are handled (batch size often runtime-determined), or how FX competes with XLA/TVM/Triton for code generation.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify the autodiff mechanism: is it source-to-source transformation via staging (like JAX's jaxpr)? If so, what are the differentiable primitives? Show how the ownership system tracks GPU memory (this is a real pain point -- CUDA's unified memory vs explicit transfers). Address dynamic shapes: most real inference has variable batch/sequence length.

analysis.what_fx_does: Chapter header introducing FX's commitment to a finite axiomatic kernel in the Metamath tradition. Every surface feature in §3-§26 reduces mechanically to kernel terms; every kernel term is checked against the Appendix H allowlist (23 canonical entries / 33 per-rule rows in leanx/AXIOMS.md). The allowlist is an upper-bound ceiling on the trusted base — the reference Lean 4 implementation currently declares 0 axioms in its trusted kernel trees, with 3 entries (U-emit, ua_wire, HIT) genuinely axiomatic when their MTT extensions land. Kernel is implemented in Lean 4 as reference stage-0 bootstrap host. Audit trail per-definition via `fxc --show-axioms`.

closest_prior_art.approach: Set.mm: ~40 axiom schemas + 300-line verifier + all of ZFC + much of mathematics. The discipline FX adopts: publish the trusted base, derive everything, make soundness auditable per-theorem.
~8K LOC C++ kernel (MLTT with universes + inductives); 1M+ theorems in Mathlib all derived. `Print Assumptions theorem_name` shows axiom dependencies. FX's direct model.
CoC with universe polymorphism; MetaCoq (POPL 2024) provides verified extraction and a partially-verified kernel. Template for FX's Lean 4 verification plan.

F*'s core calculus (POPL 2017) plus Dijkstra Monads for Free defines the trusted base. FX derives from the same tradition but re-designs the kernel with graded modalities front-and-center rather than encoded after the fact.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: No production systems language publishes its trusted base. Rust/Swift/Go rely on LLVM's semantics (itself underspecified). FX's commitment to a finite axiom list places it in the proof-assistant tradition (Coq/Lean/Agda) while targeting native-code systems programming. The discipline — every new surface feature requires a kernel translation — keeps the axiom list from drifting over time. `fxc --show-axioms` gives per-definition auditability that Rust/Swift cannot provide.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The kernel is a major commitment and will take years of spec refinement + implementation + proof mechanization to complete. Current state: kernel terms and axioms enumerated; mechanization pending. No existing language combines FX's specific constraints (21 graded dimensions + universe hierarchy + session types + memory model emit-table axiom + native codegen). Coq/Lean have mature kernels but don't do systems-programming codegen; F* does both but has an ad-hoc kernel rather than a publishable minimal one.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Lean 4 reference implementation (currently specified, not implemented). Mechanized proofs of the four meta-theorems (§27.4). Audit tooling (`fxc --show-axioms`) exists in spec only. Kernel-translation subsections are promised for every §3-§26 chapter but only three worked examples appear in §31.7 (ADT, linear, atomic). Need to systematically add translations to every surface feature.
analysis.could_we_do_better: Implement a minimal Lean 4 kernel (types, Pi, Sigma, universes only) as a first milestone; derive one surface feature (ADTs) end-to-end; publish the worked example. Then extend incrementally. Parallel track: mechanize Preservation for the minimal kernel. Once the smallest interesting core works, adding dimensions is mechanical.

analysis.what_fx_does: States four pillars of the kernel discipline: (1) finite axiom list (~33 in Appendix H); (2) derivation-only extensions — every §3-§26 feature reduces to kernel terms; (3) per-definition audit trail via `fxc --show-axioms`; (4) Lean 4 as host, aligning the compiler implementation language with the proof language.

closest_prior_art.approach: Publish the axioms; derive everything; every proof is a sequence of axiom applications. FX adopts the discipline without the specific encoding.
Standard security-engineering practice: identify the smallest set of components that must be trusted, audit them separately, minimize them. FX's kernel is the TCB for language semantics.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Combining TCB discipline with a dependently-typed programming language is unusual. Systems languages don't do TCB reasoning; proof assistants don't do native codegen. FX does both.
analysis.where_fx_is_worse: The principle is stated but the tooling is aspirational. `fxc --show-axioms` doesn't exist yet. Lean 4 compiler doesn't exist yet. Discipline without enforcement is just good intentions.
analysis.what_fx_is_missing: Concrete plan for when and how tooling lands. Policy for new-axiom proposals (when is a new axiom justified vs when does the feature need redesign?).
analysis.could_we_do_better: Specify a 'new axiom review' process: any proposal that adds to the axiom list must justify why the feature cannot be derived. Otherwise the axiom list drifts and the discipline erodes.

analysis.what_fx_does: Ten kernel term forms: var, app, lam (graded), Pi (dependent fn type, graded), Sigma (dependent pair, graded), Type<u> (universe), Ind (inductive), Coind (coinductive, sized), Id (equality), Quot (quotient). Every lambda/Pi/Sigma binder carries a 21-dim grade vector.

closest_prior_art.approach: Classical MLTT has Pi, Sigma, universes, identity, and inductive definitions. FX adds graded binders (Wood-Atkey 2022) and Coind with size-indexing (Abel-Pientka POPL 2013).
closest_prior_art.reference: Martin-Löf, 'Intuitionistic Type Theory', 1984; Wood-Atkey 'A Linear Algebra Approach to Linear Metatheory', LICS 2022

closest_prior_art.approach: Graded modal linear logic with three sample dimensions. FX's kernel generalizes to 21 dimensions via the tier system.
MLTT + multiplicity grades {0,1,w}. FX extends to arbitrary semiring/lattice grades with Wood-Atkey division.

analysis.where_fx_is_better: Ten forms is a conservative kernel — comparable to Lean 4's 11 forms or Coq's 12. Every FX surface construct reduces to these. Graded binders on Pi/Sigma/lam uniformly handle all 21 dimensions without special-casing.
